title: A TinyMLOps Framework for Real-world Applications
journal: Charting the Intelligence Frontiers Edge AI Systems Nexus
reason: The paper introduces a TinyMLOps framework for deploying and managing AI models on resource-constrained microcontrollers, directly addressing operational challenges and practices in a specific industrial/edge context.
deployment_context: TinyML/Edge AI on Microcontrollers (MCUs) in IoT environments (far-edge)
operational_constraints: ["(1) Extremely constrained computational power and storage on target devices (MCUs like Raspberry Pi Pico, ESP32, Arduino, STM32 families).", "(2) Absence of a full-fledged operating system on MCUs, requiring adapted methodologies for AI workflow orchestration.", "(3) Need to balance software efficiency (e.g., accuracy) with hardware constraints (e.g., memory, power, latency) during model optimization.", "(4) Traditional model deployment often requires hardcoding models into firmware, necessitating a complete firmware flash for updates, which reduces flexibility in real-time scenarios.", "(5) Challenges in collecting real-time performance and health metrics directly from highly constrained edge devices.", "(6) The need for co-design between software (model) and hardware (target device) to achieve optimal performance and resource utilization."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) Cloud-to-device continuum approach: Cloud resources handle heavy tasks (data labelling, model training), while microcontrollers gather real-time efficiency and hardware utilization metrics.", "(2) Model optimization for target hardware: Techniques like quantization and pruning are applied by a Model Conversion component to adapt trained models for efficient execution on specific MCUs.", "(3) Experiment tracking: An Experiment Tracker (e.g., MLFlow Tracking) stores model binaries, associated parameters (hyperparameters, training data), and performance metrics (software and hardware) to manage the optimization process and ensure reproducibility.", "(4) Dual testing environments: Models are evaluated in cloud-based Test Sandboxes for software metrics (e.g., accuracy) and on physical Test Devices for hardware-specific metrics (e.g., latency, memory usage).", "(5) Continuous feedback loop: Collected metrics from test devices are fed back to an Optimization Engine to refine hyperparameters, model configuration, and hardware selection, triggering new iterations.", "(6) Model registry: A Production Model Repository (e.g., MLFlow Model Registry) stores validated and optimized models ready for production deployment, with internal labels for target devices/scenarios.", "(7) Flexible model deployment: MicroPython-based firmware allows deploying only Python scripts and associated binary files via HTTP requests, decoupling the ML model from the firmware and avoiding full firmware flashes for updates.", "(8) Real-time monitoring: Tools like Prometheus and Grafana are used to collect and visualize real-time hardware (latency, memory, CPU) and software (errors, anomalies) performance metrics from production devices.", "(9) Automated retraining triggers: Webhooks from monitoring tools (Grafana) can automatically trigger new optimization iterations based on detected performance issues or anomalies.", "(10) Data versioning and management: Tools like Pachyderm are used for scalable data storage with version control, tracking large volumes of data throughout the ML pipeline."]
gaps_identified: ["Existing platforms like Edge Impulse require hardcoding models into firmware, which reduces flexibility in real-time scenarios due to the need for OTA updates for model changes.", "Standard MLOps methodologies are not directly applicable to low-end microcontrollers due to the absence of a full-fledged OS and severe computational/resource constraints, necessitating adapted methodologies like TinyMLOps."]
key_findings: The paper proposes a TinyMLOps framework architecture that extends traditional MLOps to resource-constrained microcontrollers. It emphasizes a cloud-to-device continuum, where heavy tasks are offloaded to the cloud, and real-time metrics are collected from edge devices. A key contribution is the decoupling of ML models from device firmware using MicroPython, enabling more flexible updates than traditional hardcoding. The framework integrates various state-of-the-art tools for data engineering, model optimization, experiment tracking, and real-time monitoring to address the unique challenges of TinyML deployment.
method: Architectural design and conceptual framework development, followed by a technology mapping to demonstrate real-world feasibility.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Data Engineering, Model Training, Model Optimization, Model Deployment, Model Monitoring, Model Governance (implicitly through versioning and registry)
future_research: The paper implicitly suggests further work in the practical implementation and evaluation of the proposed framework in diverse real-world TinyML applications, particularly focusing on the scalability and adaptability across various constrained platforms and application-specific requirements.

title: Network science and explainable AI-based life cycle management of sustainability models
journal: PLoS ONE
reason: The paper explicitly discusses the application of MLOps lifecycle framework for managing sustainability models, including model development, deployment, monitoring, and maintenance, with a focus on explainable AI and network science tools.
deployment_context: Sustainability planning and policy development, specifically for assessing and predicting Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) related to water use in Hungary. The models are deployed and maintained as a Python and Dash-based application.
operational_constraints: ["(1) Environmental systems exhibit high complexity and dynamics, with diverse interacting variables that vary over time, making models susceptible to uncertainties.", "(2) Lack of data availability and quality, as well as the need for model adaptability to local conditions (localization of model structures), pose significant challenges.", "(3) Country-specific factors (development phases, policies, databases) make it difficult to develop accurate and reliable system dynamics models.", "(4) Traditional sustainability models are often static, not continuously improved, validated, or maintained, and their structure is not prepared to integrate and track changes.", "(5) Insufficient quality of underlying data can lead to incorrect inferences about model quality and variable effects.", "(6) Different economies or environmental backgrounds may exhibit different relationships, requiring homogeneous samples and clear definition of the modeled area.", "(7) Structural errors in models can be difficult to identify and correct.", "(8) Some variables in real-world datasets may lack variance (e.g., water price, rice paddy irrigation area, internal/external renewable water resources in the Hungarian case study), rendering them ineffective as policy intervention points in the model.", "(9) The aggregated nature of SDG indicators makes direct regulation difficult, necessitating identification of effective intervention points.", "(10) Policy interventions in one sub-model (e.g., water) can have negative trade-off influences on other interconnected areas and models (e.g., food market), requiring a system-of-systems evaluation."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) Lifecycle-based model development: The paper proposes using the MLOps lifecycle framework (design, model development, operation) intertwined with CRISP-ML(Q) (business/data understanding, data preparation, modeling, evaluation, deployment/monitoring, maintenance) for continuous review, development, and evaluation of sustainability models.", "(2) Model identification and development using Explainable AI (XAI): Utilizes Shapley values to determine individual direct and indirect contributions of variables to model output, aiding in interpretation, monitoring, and optimization of hierarchical models.", "(3) Network analysis for key driver identification: Applies network science tools (e.g., degree centrality, closeness centrality, betweenness centrality) to analyze relationships between variables, identify key nodes, and select intervention points that significantly influence model output, especially when combined with Shapley values as edge weights.", "(4) Continuous model validation and maintenance: Emphasizes the necessity for continuous model development and maintenance through data-driven ML techniques (e.g., retraining linear regression models) to ensure quality performance and adaptation to spatial and temporal changes.", "(5) Parameter optimization: Demonstrates improving model accuracy (e.g., for municipal water use equation) through parameter optimization within the MLOps framework.", "(6) Model deployment: Mentions deploying models first to a small subset for behavior evaluation prior to extensive implementation, and using transition trajectories and digital twin simulations.", "(7) Monitoring and maintenance: Continuous monitoring, improvement, and validation processes to assure quality performance and identify corrective actions, adapting models to environmental changes.", "(8) Data quality assessment and preprocessing: Includes defining objectives, collecting and verifying data quality, data cleaning, feature engineering, scaling, normalization, and imputation as part of the data understanding and preparation phases.", "(9) Model interpretation and visualization: Uses Shapley-based network visualizations to translate information for decision-makers, showing direct and indirect contributions of variables and identifying policy intervention points."]
gaps_identified: ["The literature on utilizing network and data-driven tools for SDG assessment has been growing, but their integration and contribution throughout the entire model lifecycle remains an area of underexplored research.", "Traditional sustainability models are often static and not designed for continuous improvement, validation, and maintenance in changing environments.", "Standard MLOps primarily focuses on retraining the same model, but there is a need for methodologies that enable the creation of new models by incorporating new variables and insights to address structural flaws.", "Black-box machine learning models, while potentially accurate, lack interpretability, making it hard to explain why predictions are made, which is crucial for decision-making in sustainability.", "The effectiveness of individual policy intervention points can vary depending on the current value of other inputs, highlighting a gap in continuous policy monitoring tools.", "Shapley values may not be interpretable if data is incorrect, models act on incorrect assumptions, have inappropriate biases, or use an incorrect set of input variables, necessitating robust validation checks.", "The method for identifying potential intervention points cannot propose exact policies, nor can it substitute human decision-makers.", "Current models often do not account for the trade-offs and negative influences that a change in one variable in a sub-model might have on other interconnected models or global systems (e.g., food supply)."]
key_findings: The paper proposes a novel MLOps lifecycle framework integrated with network science and explainable AI (Shapley values) for managing sustainability models, particularly for Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). It demonstrates that continuous model development and maintenance through data-driven techniques are crucial for adapting to changes and improving accuracy, as shown by retraining a linear regression model for SDG6.4.2. The application to the Hungarian water model reveals that industrial water withdrawal and the service sector's gross value added are significant drivers for water use efficiency and water stress, suggesting these as effective policy intervention points. The study highlights the importance of validated model structures and the use of Shapley-weighted network analysis to identify influential variables and critical paths for informed decision-making.
method: The research proposes a methodological framework combining MLOps, CRISP-ML(Q), network science, and Explainable AI (Shapley values). It includes a case study applying these methods to a sub-model of the Hungarian water model for SDG assessment. The methodology involves: (1) defining the MLOps/CRISP-ML lifecycle stages, (2) integrating data science (EDA, correlation, causality, feature engineering) and network science (knowledge graphs, centrality measures) tools into each stage, (3) using Shapley values to quantify direct and indirect variable contributions to model outputs, (4) employing network analysis (degree, closeness, betweenness centrality) to identify key drivers and intervention points, and (5) validating model accuracy against observed data and other ML models (linear regression, k-NN).
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Design, Model Development, Operations (encompassing business and data understanding, data preparation, modeling, evaluation, deployment, monitoring, and maintenance)
future_research: Future development plans should prioritize advancements in model evaluation by refining methodologies for handling data, and continuously innovate MLOps to proactively identify and rectify structural flaws in models through retraining or creating new ones with updated variables and insights. There is a need to evaluate policy-making in a system-of-systems setting to account for trade-offs between interconnected models and global impacts.

title: Review in Adoption of DevOps, AIOps, DataOps, GitOps, MLOps in IT MLEs in Germany

title: Responsible AI Pattern Catalogue: A Collection of Best Practices for AI Governance and Engineering
journal: ACM Computing Surveys
reason: This paper provides a comprehensive catalogue of patterns for operationalizing Responsible AI across governance, process, and product layers, directly addressing the deployment, operation, and maintenance of AI systems in production with a focus on ethical considerations.
evidence_level: MLR synthesis of academic and grey literature
deployment_context: General AI systems, with specific patterns applicable to various industries (e.g., autonomous vehicles, healthcare, financial services). Focus on ethical deployment and governance across the entire lifecycle.
operational_constraints: (1) AI systems involve a higher degree of uncertainty and more ethical risk due to their dynamic, autonomous, opaque decision-making, and historical-data-dependent behaviors. (2) Ethical issues can arise at any step of the development lifecycle, cutting across many AI and non-AI components of systems beyond AI algorithms and models. (3) AI ethics principles are generally abstract and domain-agnostic, making them challenging for practitioners to operationalize without concrete guidance. (4) Many regulations are in development across jurisdictions, causing interoperability challenges for organizations. (5) Ethical risk assessment is often a one-off, algorithm-level activity with subjective judgment, insufficient for highly uncertain and continual learning AI systems. (6) The cost of managing bills of materials for all components can be high, especially as AI systems evolve frequently. (7) Ethical metrics are hard to identify for data collection in ethical black boxes, and decisions on what data to record and where to store it are challenging.
mlops_practices: (1) Multi-level governance patterns: Establishing industry-level (e.g., RAI regulation, regulatory sandbox, building code, RAI standard, RAI maturity model, RAI certification, trust mark, independent oversight), organization-level (e.g., leadership commitment for RAI, ethics committee, code of ethics for RAI, ethical risk assessment, standardized reporting, role-level accountability contract, RAI software bill of materials, ethics training), and team-level (e.g., customized agile process, tight coupling of AI and non-AI development, diverse team, stakeholder engagement, continuous documentation using templates, failure mode and effects analysis (FMEA), fault tree analysis (FTA), verifiable claim for AI system artifacts) structures and processes. (2) Process patterns: Including AI suitability assessment, verifiable ethical requirements, data requirements throughout the entire lifecycle, ethical user stories (for requirements engineering); multi-level co-architecting, envisioning cards, design modeling for ethics, system-level ethical simulation, human-centered interface design for explainable AI (for design); RAI governance of APIs, RAI governance via APIs, ethical construction with reuse (for implementation); ethical acceptance testing, ethical assessment for test cases (for testing); continuous deployment for RAI, extensible, adaptive and dynamic ethical risk assessment, multi-level co-versioning (for operation). (3) Product patterns: Bill of materials registry, verifiable ethical credential, co-versioning registry, federated learner (for supply chain); AI mode switcher, multi-model decision maker, homogeneous redundancy (for system); continuous ethical validator, ethical sandbox, ethical knowledge base, ethical digital twin, incentive registry, ethical blackbox, global view auditor (for operation infrastructure).
gaps_identified: (1) Without further best practice guidance, practitioners are left with nothing much beyond truisms when trying to operationalize AI ethics principles. (2) Significant efforts have been placed at the algorithm-level rather than system-level, mainly focusing on a subset of mathematics-amenable ethical principles (e.g., fairness), neglecting system-level ethical issues. (3) Existing agile development methods largely neglect AI ethics principles. (4) The development of AI components is more experimental with limited methodological support compared to non-AI components, leading to a methodological gap between AI and non-AI development. (5) Some ethical principles/requirements may not be easily quantitatively validated (e.g., human-centered values), and current practice often involves developers prioritizing one principle over others rather than building balanced trade-offs. (6) Simulation models cannot represent all behaviors and ethical impacts of AI systems in the real world, limiting the accuracy of ethical risk prediction. (7) Ethical metrics are hard to identify for data collection in ethical black boxes, and designing incentive mechanisms for responsible AI is challenging due to difficulties in measuring ethical impact and conflicting stakeholder views.
key_findings: This paper presents a Responsible AI Pattern Catalogue, derived from a Multivocal Literature Review, to operationalize responsible AI from a system perspective. It classifies patterns into multi-level governance, trustworthy process, and responsible-AI-by-design product patterns, offering systematic and actionable guidance for stakeholders. The catalogue addresses the gap between abstract AI ethics principles and practical implementation, moving beyond algorithm-level solutions to system-level considerations. Key findings include the necessity of continuous ethical risk assessment, multi-level co-versioning for traceability, and the use of patterns like 'AI mode switcher' and 'ethical digital twin' to manage uncertainty and risk in AI systems.
method: Multivocal Literature Review (MLR) covering both academic and grey literature, followed by data extraction, synthesis, and analysis to identify and classify patterns.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Requirements Engineering, Design, Implementation, Testing, Operation, Governance (across all stages)
future_research: Building a Question Bank and a software tool for AI risk assessment using the pattern catalogue to recommend mitigation strategies. Validating the utility, usability, and effectiveness of the pattern catalogue and supporting tools in industrial projects.

title: From Policy to Pipeline: A Governance Framework for AI Development and Operations Pipelines
journal: IEEE Access
reason: The paper introduces a governance framework (GEAP) for AI development and operations pipelines, focusing on integrating regulatory compliance directly into MLOps. It details how policies are expressed as machine-interpretable rules, how evidence is captured and managed, and how compliance is rendered deterministically across multiple regulatory regimes. This directly addresses the deployment, operation, monitoring, and governance of AI models in production.
deployment_context: High-risk clinical context (intensive-care sepsis early-warning system), but framework is designed to be domain-neutral and applicable to finance, autonomous systems, and safety-critical automation.
operational_constraints: (1) Existing engineering practice for AI governance remains largely manual, retrospective, and decoupled from operational pipelines, leading to inconsistent provenance, limited reproducibility, and inadequate clause-level traceability. (2) Current MLOps practices strengthen operational hygiene but do not describe how checks should control promotion or how evidence should be structured for auditable and replayable decisions. (3) Transparency documentation (Model Cards, Datasheets) is typically static, retrospective, and decoupled from promotion control, not binding evidence to deterministic gate criteria. (4) Regulatory frameworks define what must be evidenced but not how obligations should be operationalized inside pipelines, how evidence should be structured, or how promotion decisions should be evaluated. (5) Enterprise governance platforms, while providing integrated support, often have proprietary internal implementations that do not expose policy evaluation engines, evidence contracts, or gate semantics, making it difficult to determine if they provide signed evidence records, deterministic replayability, or clause-to-artifact traceability. (6) Software supply chain security mechanisms (SLSA, in-toto) are designed for software rather than AI-specific assets like datasets, evaluation bundles, or robustness reports. (7) Post-deployment assurance work improves monitoring but does not link assurance signals to promotion control nor provide replayable decision records.
mlops_practices: (1) Governance as Code (GaC): Regulatory and organizational policies are expressed as machine-interpretable rules (e.g., Rego policies) that define required inputs, evaluation predicates, and decision semantics. (2) Five Promotion Gates: Governance is integrated into a unified SDLC–MLOps execution spine with gates at Data, Training, Validation, Release, and Operations stages, each enforcing promotion decisions. (3) Tamper-Evident Evidence Backbone (EB): Signed, content-addressed artifacts (e.g., dataset manifests, training configurations, evaluation bundles, monitoring snapshots) are emitted at each gate into an append-only storage substrate. (4) Per-Run Conformity Bundle (CB): All evidence artifacts required for a pipeline run are assembled into a signed, content-addressed manifest, forming an auditable, replayable justification for all gate decisions. (5) Clause-to-Artifact Traceability (C2AT): A deterministic layer maps policy clauses to evidence artifacts in the CB, enabling clause-level coverage rendering for multiple regulatory frameworks without manual crosswalks. (6) Quantitative Governance Metrics: Metrics such as Clause Coverage (CC), Adequacy Score (AS), Reuse Factor (RF), Update Responsiveness (UR), and Waiver Hygiene (WH) are introduced to measure completeness, efficiency, and stability of governance practices. (7) Risk-Tiered Controls: Governance strength (e.g., mandatory checks, reviewer independence, SLA tightness) is determined by the AI system's risk tier (R1, R2, R3 as per EU AI Act). (8) Structured Waiver Governance: Waivers for non-conformance are strictly governed with scope, owner, expiry, and compensating measures, recorded in the EB and tracked through C2AT. (9) Automated Evidence Validation: GaC predicates validate evidence quality by operating over machine-readable artifact structures, eliminating manual documentation review. (10) Delta-Based Review: Reviewers can focus on changes between Conformity Bundles (CBs) rather than re-reviewing entire artifacts, localizing audit effort.
gaps_identified: (1) Lack of a unified architecture that expresses governance as executable rules, binds those rules to evidence captured during pipeline execution, and renders clause coverage deterministically across multiple regulatory regimes. (2) Absence of enforceable gate semantics or specified evidence contracts required for promotion decisions in responsible AI guidance. (3) Existing documentation artifacts (Model Cards, Datasheets) are typically static, retrospective, and decoupled from promotion control, not binding evidence to deterministic gate criteria. (4) Regulatory frameworks define what must be evidenced but not how obligations should be operationalized inside pipelines, how evidence should be structured, or how promotion decisions should be evaluated. (5) Existing policy engines do not produce a signed evidence contract for each run, provide clause-level traceability to regulatory regimes, or enforce risk-tiered controls, waiver semantics, or expiry-based governance for high-risk AI systems. (6) Software supply chain security mechanisms are not designed for AI-specific assets like datasets, evaluation bundles, or robustness reports. (7) Post-deployment assurance work does not link assurance signals to promotion control nor provide replayable decision records. (8) Regulatory frameworks lack a mandated pre-design artifact like an Ethical Requirements Specification (ERS) at the Data Gate. (9) Frameworks lack a safe concession mechanism for conditional passes, leading to informal waivers. (10) Emphasis on monitoring, but rollback preparedness is often asserted without evidence of rehearsal. (11) Focus on runtime logs, not development governance, for structured gate decision logs.
key_findings: The GEAP framework operationalizes AI governance by integrating machine-interpretable policies and evidence capture directly into MLOps pipelines. A synthetic case study of an ICU sepsis early-warning system demonstrates GEAP's ability to standardize promotion control, detect policy violations (e.g., subgroup sensitivity shortfall leading to a blocked gate), and produce replayable, audit-ready compliance manifests. Comparative analysis shows GEAP achieves significantly higher clause coverage and adequacy (AS of 0.91) compared to standard MLOps (AS of 0.33) and DevSecOps-only (AS of 0.21) baselines, while also reducing reviewer burden through delta-based inspection and enabling multi-regime compliance from a single evidence set. This indicates that governance can operate as a deterministic, reproducible, and verifiable pipeline property, leading to more disciplined, transparent, and accountable AI deployment practices.
method: Framework development and synthetic case study. The framework (GEAP) is formalized with architectural components (gates, Evidence Backbone, Conformity Bundle, Clause-to-Artifact Traceability) and governance metrics. A detailed synthetic case study of an intensive-care sepsis early-warning system (R3 risk tier) is used to demonstrate GEAP's application, including a failed gate example and comparison against MLOps and DevSecOps baselines using a 42-clause rubric.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Data Governance, Model Training, Model Validation, Model Release/Deployment, Model Operations/Monitoring
future_research: (1) Develop standardized Conformity Bundle (CB) schemas, conformance suites, and interoperability protocols for cross-organizational auditability and tool compatibility. (2) Conduct reference deployments across cloud-native, hybrid, and air-gapped settings to clarify minimal infrastructure requirements and improve portability. (3) Incorporate automated policy-linting tools, adversarial-evaluation modules, and drift-governance patterns into GaC libraries. (4) Perform empirical validation through operational deployments, regulator-in-the-loop studies, and longitudinal analyses of waiver dynamics, drift response, and audit effort. (5) Investigate human-computer interaction with CBs, C2AT renderings, and signed evidence artifacts to inform human-centered improvements. (6) Extend GEAP toward multi-stakeholder and domain-specific governance, including federated CBs, cross-organizational attestation protocols, and libraries for high-risk domains like finance and autonomous systems. (7) Curate open repositories of reference GaC policies, de-identified CBs, and governance benchmarks.

title: Towards MLOps: A Case Study of ML Pipeline Platform
journal: Proceedings - 2020 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Computer Engineering, ICAICE 2020
reason: The paper describes the construction and evaluation of an MLOps platform using Kubeflow and CI/CD tools, focusing on the operational aspects of ML pipeline execution, resource consumption, and continuous training.
deployment_context: Generic AI Implementation (cloud-native, containerized ML platform on a single physical machine)
operational_constraints: (1) High GPU utilization (89.756% to 98.3%) across various models, indicating potential performance bottlenecks when training multiple models simultaneously. (2) Relatively low memory and framebuffer memory utilization (47.424% to 72.554% and 963.245 to 6795.425 MiB respectively) compared to GPU usage, posing a challenge for ML platform providers to improve their utilization when GPU becomes a bottleneck. (3) Time and resource consumption are more dependent on the design, construction, and implementation of the computational models themselves rather than simply the number of layers or parameters, making performance prediction and optimization complex.
mlops_practices: (1) End-to-end ML pipeline orchestration: Phases like data processing, data validation, feature extraction, model design, training, evaluation, application development, quality checks, deployment, and maintenance are orchestrated into multistep pipelines. (2) Continuous Training (CT): Models are continuously trained or retrained when specific events occur, such as new training data availability or model performance degradation. (3) CI/CD integration: Existing CI/CD tools (Drone) are used with a Git service (Gitea) to automate packaging and uploading Kubeflow pipelines, triggering model retraining upon code changes. (4) Containerization: ML workflow steps are wrapped as reusable components packaged as Docker images. (5) Version control for code and data: Gitea with Git LFS is used to version algorithm codes and training datasets, supporting model traceability. (6) Performance monitoring: GPU utilization, memory utilization, and framebuffer memory usage are recorded during Kubeflow pipeline executions to identify bottlenecks.
gaps_identified: (1) Current ML platforms face uncertainty regarding their performance, specifically how much computing resources they consume and the time required for model training. (2) There is a challenge for ML platform providers to improve memory and framebuffer memory utilization when GPU usage reaches a bottleneck, as GPU utilization is often high while memory utilization is comparatively low. (3) The relationship between model characteristics (layers, parameters) and resource consumption is not straightforward, indicating a need for better understanding or tools to predict performance based on model design.
key_findings: This study built and evaluated an MLOps platform combining Kubeflow with CI/CD tools on a physical machine. It found that the ML task pipeline accounts for over 98% of total time and resource consumption, with CI/CD pipeline executions completing in seconds. A key finding is that GPU utilization is consistently high (89.7-98.3%) across diverse deep learning models, suggesting a potential bottleneck for simultaneous training, while memory utilization is comparatively lower. The paper concludes that time and resource consumption are more influenced by the specific design and implementation of ML models than by simple metrics like layer count or parameter number.
method: Experimental evaluation. The authors built an ML platform using Kubeflow, Gitea, and Drone. They constructed and ran ML pipelines to train seven different deep learning neural networks (MobileNet, ShuffleNet, ResNet, VGG, DenseNet, GoogLeNet, Inception V3) on the CIFAR-100 dataset. Time and resource consumption (GPU utilization, memory utilization, framebuffer memory used) were recorded and analyzed for both CI/CD and Kubeflow pipeline stages.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Data Processing, Model Training, Model Evaluation, Model Deployment, Monitoring, Continuous Integration, Continuous Delivery, Continuous Training
future_research: Not explicitly stated, but implied by the identified gaps: research into improving memory and framebuffer memory utilization when GPU is a bottleneck, and developing better methods to predict and optimize resource consumption based on specific model designs.

title: Past, Present, Future: A Comprehensive Exploration of AI Use Cases in the UMBRELLA IoT Testbed
journal: 2024 IEEE International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Communications Workshops and other Affiliated Events, PerCom Workshops 2024
reason: The paper details the deployment and operation of various AI/ML models within the UMBRELLA IoT testbed, explicitly discussing MLOps needs and challenges in this real-world, edge-centric environment.
deployment_context: Industrial IoT (IIoT) / Smart City / Edge Computing (UMBRELLA IoT testbed with streetlights, robotics, building sensors)
operational_constraints: ["(1) Limited network quality and low-power resource-constrained IoT networks (e.g., Bluetooth or LoRaWAN) impact the ability to share model parameters for Federated Learning, creating a significant bottleneck for transmitting substantial data volumes.", "(2) Constraints on energy usage, communication bandwidth, and other network resources on IoT devices hinder their participation in model training.", "(3) The open-access nature of the UMBRELLA testbed, where end-users deploy containerized experiments, raises security concerns for system functionality, requiring real-time intrusion detection at the edge.", "(4) The need to reduce latency for data sharing and decision-making in collaborative robotics crowdsensing systems, especially for sensor data sharing, position/location sharing, and status updates.", "(5) Current MLOps capabilities in UMBRELLA are limited to Kubernetes orchestration (DevOps), lacking native ML-related automation for processing training data, model training/validation, and ML model lifecycle management, which poses challenges for trustworthy crowd-sensing systems."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) Containerized deployment: All experiments and applications are deployed as containers across devices, orchestrated through a cloud-native platform (Kubernetes clusters).", "(2) Centralized backend: A unified backend controls all applications, providing storage and visualization capabilities.", "(3) Edge computing for inference: ML models for streetlight monitoring (computer vision and classification) operate on UMBRELLA nodes (Raspberry Pi CM3+ with RPi Camera Module).", "(4) Federated Learning (FL) for distributed training: An FL system is implemented on UMBRELLA edge devices to minimize transmission of model parameters, reducing network bandwidth and energy consumption while maintaining data privacy.", "(5) Selective Updates (SU) FL method: Used to minimize communication volume in FL training by employing a sliding/adaptive observation window and threshold to gather local client model performance before updating the Parameter Server.", "(6) Semi-supervised FL for intrusion detection: A system is developed to detect malicious container activity at the edge, with FL training following a classical approach of independent client training and global model aggregation.", "(7) Data pipeline for security: Raw auditd logs are processed at the edge, extracting field names and values, followed by event-based feature engineering to transform logs into a structured dataset for anomaly detection."]
gaps_identified: ["There is a missing native MLOps platform specifically designed for UMBRELLA that can automate ML-related functions beyond basic Kubernetes orchestration. Current capabilities are limited in processing training data, conducting model training/validation, and ML model lifecycle management.", "Existing IoT testbeds generally exhibit limited capabilities for handling only specific tasks, with short-supported use cases and a notable absence of access to real-time, real-world data, especially in AI/ML-enabled platforms."]
key_findings: The UMBRELLA IoT testbed successfully deploys and validates various AI/ML use cases in real-world scenarios, including automated streetlight monitoring (achieving >90% accuracy for ML model), digital twin for building environmental sensing (autoencoder model with <1% error for temperature), large-scale Federated Learning (SU method shows substantial advantages in convergence speed and accuracy), and intrusion detection at the IoT edge (FL model reaches >98% accuracy). The paper highlights the critical need for a tailored MLOps platform to automate model pipelines and establish trust in such complex, distributed IoT/IIoT environments, as current DevOps-centric orchestration falls short for full ML lifecycle management.
method: Case study / Testbed implementation and evaluation
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Deployment, Monitoring, Retraining, Governance (security)
future_research: Developing an in-UMBRELLA MLOps platform to automate ML pipelines, including processing training data, model training/validation, and ML model lifecycle management. Exploring semantic communication, communication and control co-design, and automated planning for multi-robot crowdsensing systems to reduce latency and improve efficiency. Expanding AI use cases to smart city crowdsensing applications like air pollution monitoring and traffic control, leveraging edge computing for offloading cloud demands.

title: Tailoring MLOps Techniques for Industry 5.0 Needs
journal: 2023 19th International Conference on Network and Service Management, CNSM 2023
reason: The paper directly addresses tailoring MLOps practices for Industry 5.0, focusing on the unique challenges and requirements of deploying and managing ML models in industrial settings, including Digital Twins and Edge AI.
evidence_level: Conceptual/Survey with empirical input (AIMS5.0 project survey)
deployment_context: Industrial AI (Industry 5.0, Cyber-Physical System of Systems, Digital Twins, Edge AI, manufacturing, production, logistics, aerospace, health)

title: AI-Driven Model-Retraining Architecture to Sustain Operational Accuracy in Data-Drifting Environments
journal: IEEE Symposium on Wireless Technology and Applications, ISWTA
reason: The paper proposes and validates an AI-driven model-retraining architecture that integrates drift detection, agentic scheduling, and data governance to maintain operational accuracy in production ML systems, specifically demonstrating it in a smart-manufacturing predictive maintenance context. This directly addresses AI deployment and operations.
deployment_context: Smart-manufacturing predictive maintenance (CNC machines, sensor telemetry, maintenance logs, operational metadata). The architecture is designed to be domain-agnostic, with potential use cases in financial services (fraud detection), healthcare (clinical risk scoring), retail/e-commerce (demand forecasting), and smart cities/IoT (traffic/energy prediction).
operational_constraints: (1) Data drift: Statistical properties of incoming data diverge from training distribution, degrading predictive accuracy. (2) Data quality degradation: Drift affects accuracy, completeness, consistency, timeliness, and validity of data, challenging underlying data-quality landscape. (3) Cost-benefit trade-offs: Retraining frequency must be balanced against infrastructure costs (compute resources). (4) Regulatory compliance: Need to maintain data lineage, schema validation, and quality scoring for compliance and stakeholder trust, especially in safety-critical sectors.
mlops_practices: (1) Continuous drift detection: Hybrid strategy combining statistical hypothesis testing (Kolmogorov–Smirnov test) with online learning detectors (ADWIN) to quantify drift severity using Population Stability Index (PSI) and Kullback–Leibler divergence. (2) Agentic retraining orchestration: An agentic AI controller (implemented with a reinforcement-learning policy, DQN, tuned via Bayesian optimization) autonomously schedules retraining jobs, allocates compute resources, and coordinates data preparation when drift scores and Data Quality Index (DQI) trends exceed predefined thresholds. (3) Data ingestion and quality governance: A governed pipeline enforces data-quality checks across accuracy, completeness, consistency, timeliness, and validity, generating a composite DQI and supporting data lineage and versioning. (4) Model training and evaluation: New models are trained on high-quality data, evaluated with F1-score, AUC, RMSE, and fairness metrics, and promoted only if they achieve predefined accuracy gains and no fairness degradation. (5) Deployment and monitoring: Approved models are containerized and deployed via existing MLOps infrastructure (Kubernetes, TensorFlow Extended (TFX), Kubeflow), with continuous monitoring of inference latency, prediction drift, and post-deployment DQI. (6) Dynamic threshold configuration: Thresholds for retraining actions are configured dynamically based on historical variance and operational tolerance.
gaps_identified: (1) Lack of integrated automated retraining with rigorous data-governance: Contemporary MLOps and AIOps pipelines provide CI/CD but rarely include comprehensive data-governance processes or automated means to restore data quality once drift occurs. (2) Limited integration of data quality restoration with continuous ML model retraining: While automated scoring of data quality dimensions exists, its integration with continuous ML model retraining remains limited. (3) Absence of agentic AI for drift-aware MLOps: Few implementations of agentic AI specifically target orchestrating model-retraining decisions and managing complex data pipelines in a drift-aware manner. (4) Inability of current frameworks to assess and restore data quality when drift occurs: Existing MLOps/AIOps frameworks enable scheduled or event-driven model updates but typically treat data governance as a peripheral task.
key_findings: The proposed AI-driven model-retraining architecture effectively detects and remediates data drift, significantly improving predictive accuracy and data quality. In a smart-manufacturing predictive maintenance case study, the system elevated the F1-score from 0.62 to 0.93 (p < 0.01) and restored the composite Data Quality Index (DQI) from 0.65 to 0.87. Furthermore, the agentic orchestrator achieved operational efficiency, reducing GPU utilization by 31% and cloud-compute costs by 28% compared to fixed-schedule retraining, demonstrating the value of drift-aware, data-governed, agentic retraining for sustainable MLOps.
method: The study proposes an AI-driven model-retraining architecture and empirically validates it through a case study in a smart-manufacturing predictive maintenance environment. Controlled data drift was induced by shifting sensor distributions and adding noise. The system's performance was evaluated using predictive metrics (F1-score, AUC, RMSE) and data-quality metrics (DQI, missing-value ratios, schema-validation failures, latency). Statistical significance was tested using paired t-tests. A comparative baseline analysis against conventional time-based retraining was also conducted.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Deployment, Monitoring, Operations, Maintenance (specifically model retraining and data governance in production).
future_research: (1) Domain-Adaptive Data-Quality Indexing: Formulate and validate adaptive weighting schemes for DQI dimensions to accommodate domain-specific salience. (2) Hybridized Retraining Triggers: Design and evaluate retraining protocols integrating statistical drift diagnostics with human-in-the-loop feedback. (3) Scalable Deployment and Evaluation: Undertake comprehensive deployment assessments across heterogeneous cloud–edge infrastructures and multi-tenant operational environments. (4) Explainable Agentic Governance: Investigate explainable, agent-oriented policy mechanisms capable of generating auditable decision logs for compliance. (5) Performance Evaluation of Retraining Strategies: Develop standardized evaluation frameworks to quantify effectiveness, stability, and efficiency of various retraining strategies under different drift scenarios and governance constraints.

title: Managing Entanglement in AI-Enabled Safety-Critical Systems (AISCS)
journal: 2026 IEEE 16th Annual Computing and Communication Workshop and Conference, CCWC 2026
reason: The paper directly addresses the deployment and operational challenges of AI in safety-critical systems, focusing on 'entanglement' as a key architectural concern that impacts dependability. It proposes an architectural framework and MLOps principles for managing this entanglement throughout the AISCS lifecycle.
deployment_context: AI-enabled Safety-Critical Systems (AISCS) in domains such as transportation (autonomous vehicles), healthcare, manufacturing, electric power, and defense. Specifically, an autonomous robot in nuclear power stations is used as a case study.
operational_constraints: ["(1) Architectural entanglement: Hidden, cross-cutting, and context-dependent dependencies between AI and non-AI components that amplify uncertainty and risk, leading to cascading faults and potential system-level hazards.", "(2) Latency and timing constraints: Critical in safety-critical systems where delays or failures in one component can inhibit others.", "(3) Degraded performance outside nominal operating conditions: AI models may perform poorly when encountering inputs or requests outside their training distributions, ambiguous operational contexts, or missing decision rules.", "(4) Uncertainty and non-determinism: Inherent in AI systems, especially when dealing with unanticipated situations, making predictability difficult.", "(5) Model and data drift: Changes in data distributions or model behavior over time can introduce new dependencies and compromise safety.", "(6) Persistent difficulties in verification and validation (V&V): The complex interdependencies make it hard to ensure the system meets rigorous safety and regulatory standards.", "(7) Adaptability constraints: Many adaptive strategies (e.g., hierarchical reinforcement learning) decompose problems into interdependent sub-tasks, increasing inter-component dependency and entanglement, which limits overall system adaptability to evolving conditions without degradation."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) Model monitoring systems: Used for detecting bias and analyzing feature importance to understand and manage entanglement.", "(2) Explainable AI (XAI) techniques: LIME, SHAP, LOCO, and Partial Dependence Plot are used to clarify model behavior and identify dependencies.", "(3) Data governance practices: Involving data isolation, data anonymization, and data quality checks to manage data-related entanglement.", "(4) Federated learning: Utilized for distributed training and model aggregation to reduce centralized dependencies.", "(5) Modular architecture design: A design-time strategy to ensure separation, isolation, and decoupling of AISCS components.", "(6) Separation of concerns: A design-time strategy to reduce interdependencies.", "(7) Interface abstraction: A design-time strategy to manage component interactions.", "(8) Encapsulation and data isolation: Implementation strategies to prevent hidden dependencies.", "(9) Dynamic adaptation and fallback mechanisms: Runtime strategies to manage uncertainty and ensure system resilience.", "(10) Dependency visualization: An evaluation and testing strategy to identify and understand component relationships.", "(11) Architectural evaluation: Used to assess and mitigate the effects of entanglement.", "(12) Automated testing: To verify system behavior and detect issues arising from entanglement.", "(13) Serving-Microservice Architecture: A proposed architectural design that separates system parts into independent services, each with its own learning module, responding to stimuli from sensors, and incorporating a 'Safety App' for handling uncertainty and ensuring safe actions."]
gaps_identified: ["Current tools and frameworks lack a universally accepted taxonomy for entanglement in software systems, especially in AISCS, making it difficult to understand, define, and assess.", "Principled mitigation strategies for architectural entanglement are scarce, despite its centrality to dependable operation.", "Existing architectural designs often do not explicitly handle entanglement, leading to hidden, cross-cutting, or context-dependent dependencies that compromise safety.", "There is no established method to link entanglement measurement directly to concrete design and operational decisions to improve predictability and trustworthiness in AISCS."]
key_findings: The paper introduces a taxonomy of entanglement (structural, data, behavioral, temporal, epistemic) to characterize its facets in AI-enabled safety-critical systems (AISCS). It proposes a 'Serving-Microservice' architectural framework, rooted in MLOps principles, that provides mechanisms for monitoring, controlling, and mitigating entanglement throughout the AISCS lifecycle. The case study of an autonomous robot demonstrates how this architecture reduces interdependence between learning components, improving modularity, maintainability, and safety by ensuring independent service execution and a dedicated 'Safety App' for uncertainty handling.
method: The research employs a conceptual and architectural design approach. It first formalizes the concept of entanglement in AISCS and develops a taxonomy. This taxonomy is then used to evaluate common architectural patterns. Building on this, the paper proposes a novel 'Serving-Microservice' architectural framework, integrating MLOps principles and established software architectural patterns. A case study of an autonomous robot's motion is used to illustrate the problem of entanglement and demonstrate the application of the proposed architecture.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Design, Deployment, Operations, Monitoring, Maintenance, Governance
future_research: Future research needs to focus on ensuring that remaining entanglement in AISCS does not affect system performance. More studies are required to improve the 'Serving-Microservice' architectural design for AI-enabled safety-critical systems.

title: A Taxonomical Review of Machine Learning Paradigms in Medical Care
journal: 4th International Conference on Automation, Computing and Renewable Systems, ICACRS 2025 - Proceedings
reason: This paper is a review that explicitly discusses Machine Learning Operations (MLOps) in healthcare, focusing on deployment, monitoring, and governance challenges and solutions. It directly addresses the operational aspects of AI in a specific domain.
deployment_context: Medical Care / Healthcare Informatics (e.g., clinical decision support, medical imaging, physiological signal analysis, EHR)
operational_constraints: ["(1) Absence of interpretation, bias, and reproducibility in complex models: Clinicians insist on transparency to trust automated systems, which is complicated by deep learning models.", "(2) Privacy regulations (HIPAA, GDPR) limit data sharing: This impedes large-scale supervised learning across institutions.", "(3) Limited availability of curated labeled datasets: Hinders the practical uptake of advanced models like Transformers.", "(4) Lack of model transparency: A barrier to adoption for models like Transformers.", "(5) Domain adaptation issues across institutions: Models struggle to generalize across different hospitals due to data heterogeneity.", "(6) Ethical and legal constraints on AI decisions: Requires accountability, bias auditing, and testimony for regulatory approval.", "(7) Discrepancy between outstanding in-silico results and clinical implementation: The challenge of moving high-performance predictive models to clinically integrated, trustworthy, and regulation-adherent systems."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) Federated Learning: Allows multiple hospitals to train shared models without exchanging raw data, addressing privacy concerns (e.g., FedAvg, FedProx).", "(2) CI/CD pipelines for healthcare: Integrate data versioning, model monitoring, and rollback safety to enhance reliability of clinical deployment.", "(3) Model Governance Policy: General conditions for approval and clinical deployment of models.", "(4) Containerized Deployment: Facilitates the transition of models into hospital systems.", "(5) Explainable AI (XAI) techniques: Custom-built techniques like SHAP, LIME, and Grad-CAM are used to make black-box models more interpretable through attention maps, increasing clinician trust.", "(6) Continuous Monitoring (Model Meander): Ongoing observation to identify degradation in model accuracy due to patient population shifts or changes in clinical practice.", "(7) Feedback Loop (Clinician Feedback): Direct input from physicians and nurses to check model performance.", "(8) Continuous Training: Retraining models in case of low performance, looping back to data ingestion and training stages to maintain accuracy and reliability.", "(9) Secure Enclaves for Data Storage: Storing sensitive datasets in isolated and secure environments to preserve various regulations like GDPR.", "(10) De-identification: Safeguards installed to prevent the extraction of Protected Health Information (PHI)."]
gaps_identified: ["Standard ML approaches struggle with noisy or incomplete data prevalent in clinical settings.", "Traditional centralized data training models are incompatible with strict privacy regulations like GDPR and HIPAA, necessitating decentralized approaches.", "The gap between outstanding in-silico (research) results and practical clinical implementation due to issues of trustworthiness, operational viability, transparency, and bias.", "The need for standardized metrics for explainability and transparent reporting in AI research to build clinician trust and facilitate regulatory approval.", "Current tools often lack the ability to handle the heterogeneity of healthcare information (EHR, images, genomics, sensor data) with traditional analysis methods."]
key_findings: The paper identifies that while deep and transformer-based models offer high predictive accuracy (e.g., Transformers with 0.96 AUC), their clinical adoption is hampered by issues of transparency, ethics, and continuous monitoring. Federated learning and MLOps are crucial for bridging the gap between research and clinical implementation by addressing data privacy, bias, and operational viability. Integrating explainable AI, continuous validation, and adaptive learning pipelines are essential for ensuring reliability, equity, and compliance in healthcare AI systems.
method: Systematic Literature Review (SLR) of ML in healthcare from 2015-2024, searching PubMed, Scopus, Web of Science, IEEE Xplore, and ACM Digital Library. Papers were categorized into five paradigms: symbolic, sub-symbolic (neural networks), hybrid, federated learning, and transformer-based methods. Quality and risk-of-bias assessments prioritized multi-institutional validation, clear performance measures, and explicit transparency/ethical governance policies.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Data Collection and Preparation, Feature Extraction and Training, Trustworthiness Assurance, Deployment and Management, Improvement Strategy (Continuous Improvement)
future_research: The paper implicitly suggests future research should focus on further developing and integrating Federated Learning and MLOps to overcome privacy, ethical governance, and practical implementation challenges in healthcare. Continued work on explainable AI frameworks and standardized metrics for transparency and bias detection is also highlighted as crucial for regulatory consent and clinician acceptance.

title: FedEdge-Learn: a Semi-Supervised Federated Learning Framework for Industry 5.0
journal: Proceedings - IEEE Symposium on Computers and Communications
reason: The paper introduces FedEdge-Learn, a federated learning framework for Industry 5.0, specifically addressing challenges in deploying ML models in industrial environments such as data privacy, limited labeled data, and distributed data. It discusses operational constraints and proposes MLOps practices for these settings.
evidence_level: Empirical Evaluation (Public and Real-world Industrial Datasets)
deployment_context: Industrial 5.0 (manufacturing, predictive maintenance, quality control for gearboxes and ice cream machines), Edge Computing, Distributed IoT environments.
operational_constraints: ["(1) Limited access to labeled data: Industrial environments often have vast amounts of unlabeled data, and manual labeling is generally not feasible.", "(2) Stringent privacy requirements: Industrial data often contains sensitive information that must be protected to comply with regulatory standards (e.g., GDPR) and safeguard proprietary technologies, preventing centralized data aggregation.", "(3) Decentralized nature of industrial data: Data is collected across numerous interconnected edge devices in different locations, making centralized collection impractical due to bandwidth limitations and privacy concerns.", "(4) Management complexity of distributed applications: Federated Learning (FL) has not been widely adopted in Industry 5.0 due to the inherent complexity of managing distributed systems.", "(5) Lack of high-quality, balanced labeled data: FL algorithms typically assume clients have high-quality, balanced labeled data, which is usually not feasible in manufacturing environments.", "(6) Latency requirements: Edge intelligence is needed to provide near real-time insights without the delay incurred by sending data to centralized cloud servers.", "(7) Cold-start problem for new machines: New machines often lack sufficient operational history to train robust local models, requiring a global model to accelerate deployment.", "(8) Communication overhead: Large-scale IoT deployments with many devices can face challenges with limited bandwidth and connectivity, making efficient communication crucial.", "(9) Class imbalance: Industrial datasets often have a minority class (e.g., broken gearboxes) that is underrepresented, leading to poor model performance if not addressed.", "(10) Difficulty in matching clusters with real-world classes: Unsupervised clustering algorithms with a fixed number of clusters can fail if collected data does not correspond to the right number of clusters, especially when a machine only collects examples of one class for a long time."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) Federated Learning (FL) for distributed training: Enables training ML models across multiple edge devices without sharing raw data, addressing privacy and decentralized data challenges.", "(2) Semi-supervised learning with global data sharing: The central server shares a small, curated set of high-quality labeled examples (synthetically generated or externally annotated) with each client during initialization or early training. This helps local models align clusters with real-world classes and addresses the cold-start problem and class imbalance.", "(3) Edge intelligence: Data processing and ML model inference/training occur directly on edge devices to ensure near real-time insights, reduce data exposure, and allow for customized models.", "(4) Aggregation of local centroids: In the case of unsupervised clustering (K-means), edge devices compute local centroids, which are then sent to a cloud-based central server for aggregation (averaging) and sent back to clients for further iterations, reducing communication overhead.", "(5) Fast bootstrapping for new machines: Leveraging a globally trained model to accelerate the deployment of ML applications on new machines that lack sufficient local data.", "(6) Use of open-source FL frameworks: Prototype developed using Flower (flower.ai) for developing and deploying federated learning systems.", "(7) Performance evaluation with homogeneity and completeness scores: Metrics used to evaluate clustering quality against known labels, ensuring clusters are pure and complete.", "(8) Iterative centroid refinement: K-means clustering evolves through iterative exchange of centroids between clients and server, converging once updates stabilize.", "(9) Simulation of real-world data distribution: FL clients can drop minority class examples to simulate extremely uneven data distribution and incomplete information common in industrial scenarios."]
gaps_identified: ["FL has not been widely adopted in Industry 5.0 settings due to inherent management complexity and the lack of an effective turnkey solution.", "Existing FL algorithms typically assume clients have high-quality, balanced labeled data, which is usually not feasible in manufacturing environments.", "There is a clear need for a turnkey solution to enable and manage FL in Industry 5.0.", "FL still lacks sufficient experimentation in real-world use cases.", "There is a shortage of easily applicable frameworks that can be readily deployed in diverse environments or that incorporate unsupervised FL and enable data sharing from the server to the clients to improve model accuracy.", "A possible shortcoming of unsupervised FL with unlabeled data is the difficulty in matching clusters with real-world classes (e.g., normal behavior/anomaly), especially when clients only see a subset of classes or only one class for extended periods.", "Clustering algorithms with a fixed number of clusters fail if the collected data do not correspond to the right number of clusters, which is particularly relevant in Industry 5.0 where a machine may collect only examples belonging to one class for a long time."]
key_findings: The FedEdge-Learn framework effectively enables semi-supervised federated learning for Industry 5.0, addressing challenges like data privacy, limited labeled data, and decentralized data. The K-means algorithm, when implemented in FL settings with global data sharing, consistently demonstrated comparable performance to its centralized counterpart, even with imbalanced or single-class client datasets. A minimal quantity of labeled data (e.g., ten examples per class) shared from the server is sufficient to mitigate class imbalance and restore K-means accuracy, demonstrating low communication overhead. This approach allows for rapid deployment and scalability, making the trained clustering model suitable as a binary classifier in production with stable performance, low computational requirements, and fast inference times.
method: The paper introduces FedEdge-Learn, a novel semi-supervised Federated Learning framework. It uses a K-means clustering algorithm enhanced by globally shared data. The framework was prototyped using Flower and scikit-learn. Evaluation was conducted using both the public CIFAR-10 dataset (with varying clients and data distribution) and a real-world industrial dataset from Bonfiglioli (containing data from broken and functional gearboxes, simulating class imbalance). Performance was measured using homogeneity and completeness scores, comparing centralized, local, FL, and FL with global data sharing configurations.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Deployment, Operations, Monitoring, Maintenance (specifically addressing model training and adaptation in production environments, data privacy, and cold-start problems for new machines).
future_research: Future work includes experimenting with datasets from different real-world use cases, exploring the integration of advanced anomaly detection models in the FL process to address the challenge of interpreting discovered clusters (especially for anomaly categorization), and refining communication efficiency between the server and clients.

title: On the impact of black-box deployment strategies for edge AI on latency and model performance
journal: Empirical Software Engineering
reason: The paper empirically evaluates various black-box deployment strategies for Edge AI, focusing on their impact on latency and model performance across Mobile, Edge, and Cloud tiers. It directly addresses the deployment, operation, and optimization of ML/AI models in production environments, aligning with the SLR's scope.
evidence_level: Empirical evaluation in a simulated environment
deployment_context: Edge AI environment with interconnected Mobile, Edge, and Cloud tiers, simulating resource-constrained (Mobile, Edge) and resource-abundant (Cloud) hardware, and varying network bandwidths (1 Mbps to 200 Mbps). Focus on Computer Vision models (ResNet, ResNext, FCN, DUC).
operational_constraints: ["(1) Latency and privacy challenges due to data transmission across Wide Area Networks (WAN) for cloud-based deployments.", "(2) Higher latency in mobile-based deployments due to limited computational resources (CPU/RAM).", "(3) Increased computational latency on Edge devices compared to Cloud, and higher data privacy threat compared to Mobile.", "(4) Costly operations for Quantized models in resource-constrained Mobile tiers (e.g., for ResNet and ResNext subjects), leading to higher CPU/Memory utilization than Identity models.", "(5) Inability of the CUDA execution provider in ONNX Runtime to fully support CUDA kernels for quantized graphical nodes during Cloud deployment, leading to CPU fallback and slower inference.", "(6) Impact of intermediate data size during transmission across networks, especially in Edge-Cloud and Mobile-Cloud partitioned strategies with limited Cloud network bandwidth (1 Mbps).", "(7) Architectural complexity of models (e.g., DUC with 355 graph nodes) contributes to higher inference latency across all tiers.", "(8) Cardinality-based architecture of models like ResNext leads to higher memory usage and occasional latency spikes on Edge devices due to increased intermediate activations and parameter storage."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) Model Partitioning: Splitting DNN models into sub-models (head and tail) for distributed inference across Mobile-Edge, Edge-Cloud, or Mobile-Cloud tiers to enhance data privacy, increase computation efficiency, reduce memory requirements, and improve scalability. The paper uses an equal-size (MB) partitioning approach.", "(2) Model Quantization: Reducing the precision of model calculations (e.g., from 32-bit floating point to 8-bit integer) to decrease computational cost and memory footprint. The study uses static Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) with Intel Neural Compressor and ONNX Runtime (CPU) backend.", "(3) Early Exiting: Modifying pre-trained models to allow early predictions by terminating execution at intermediate stages (Early Exits/Classifiers/Sub-branches) to trade accuracy for speed. This is achieved by manually short-circuiting identically structured sub-graphs in ONNX computational graphs.", "(4) Hybrid Operators: Combining multiple optimization techniques, such as Quantized Early Exit (QE) and Quantized Early Exit Partitioned (QEP), to achieve synergistic benefits in latency and accuracy.", "(5) Containerization (Docker): Using Docker containers to simulate and isolate Mobile, Edge, and Cloud tiers, providing a lightweight, consistent, and reproducible environment for inference experiments.", "(6) Network Emulation (Linux Traffic Control): Configuring Linux Traffic Control utility within Docker containers to simulate varying network bandwidths (1 Mbps to 200 Mbps) between tiers.", "(7) ONNX Runtime: Utilizing ONNX (Open Neural Network Exchange) as a standard format for interoperable AI models and ONNX Runtime as a high-performance inference engine for deployment across various devices and hardware platforms (CPUs, GPUs via CUDA Execution Provider).", "(8) Empirical Performance Evaluation: Systematically measuring end-to-end (round-trip) inference latency and accuracy (Top-1/Top-5 for classification, mIoU for segmentation) across different deployment strategies and environmental conditions."]
gaps_identified: ["Standard MLOps practices for black-box model deployment often rely on trial-and-error to find optimal combinations of deployment operators and tiers, lacking systematic empirical data to guide decisions.", "Existing surveys classify Edge AI model inference techniques but often treat operators in isolation, overlooking potential synergies and practical integration in real-world scenarios.", "Current tools and frameworks lack automated solutions for dynamic optimal partitioning points across diverse model architectures, requiring manual analysis and modification of computational graphs.", "GPU-based Cloud environments face challenges with full CUDA kernel support for quantized graphical nodes, leading to CPU fallback and suboptimal performance for quantized models.", "Previous studies on model partitioning and early exiting often consider lightweight models or single image inputs, limiting generalizability to complex, high-resolution models and varying input data sizes in real-world Edge AI environments.", "Automated tools for black-box transformations like Early Exit and Partitioning across all types of models do not exist, requiring significant manual effort and domain expertise."]
key_findings: The study empirically compares black-box Edge AI deployment strategies, revealing that the Edge tier generally offers significantly faster inference latency for various operators (Identity, Quantized, Early Exit, Quantized Early Exit) due to its computational resources and proximity, especially for models with large input data. Quantization provides faster inference on Edge with minimal accuracy loss (<0.4%), making it preferable over Early Exit which incurs medium accuracy drops (2.53% to 11.56%). Mobile-Edge partitioning consistently outperforms other multi-tier strategies and monolithic Mobile deployments, particularly for complex models, but its effectiveness is highly dependent on intermediate data size and network bandwidth. Cloud deployment is optimal for small input models even at low bandwidths (≤10 Mbps), while larger models require ≥50 Mbps for performance convergence. The findings provide a foundational dataset for MLOps engineers to make informed deployment decisions and could inspire future automated recommendation systems for Edge AI.
method: The study employs a nested factorial design with empirical inference experiments. It uses four state-of-the-art Computer Vision models (ResNet, ResNext, FCN, DUC) as subjects. Experiments are conducted in a Docker-containerized environment simulating Mobile, Edge, and Cloud tiers with emulated hardware specifications (CPU, RAM, GPU) and network bandwidths (1-200 Mbps via Linux Traffic Control). Inference latency (end-to-end round-trip time) and accuracy (Top-1/Top-5, mIoU) are measured for various black-box deployment strategies: Identity, Quantization, Early Exit, Partitioning, and their hybrid combinations (Quantized Early Exit, Quantized Early Exit Partitioned). Statistical analysis includes Shapiro-Wilk tests for normality, Kruskal-Wallis tests for group comparisons, and Conover post-hoc tests with Cliff's Delta effect size for pairwise comparisons.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Deployment, Operations, Monitoring, Maintenance, Governance (implicitly through trade-off analysis)
future_research: Future research should focus on optimizing Quantization in GPU-based Cloud environments by modifying model structures or developing custom CUDA kernels. The testbed can be extended with tools like cgroups, perf, and powerstat to collect system-level metrics (CPU/memory usage, network throughput, energy consumption) for multi-objective evaluation, including carbon footprint. title: Implementing MLOps practices for effective machine learning model deployment: A meta synthesis
journal: CEUR Workshop Proceedings
reason: This paper is a meta-synthesis of systematic reviews on MLOps practices, tools, and challenges for effective ML model deployment. It directly addresses the operational aspects of ML/AI models in production.
deployment_context: General ML model deployment in production environments, with mentions of cloud, edge, and various industries (finance, healthcare, manufacturing, retail, IoT).
operational_constraints: (1) Complexity of managing the machine learning model lifecycle, including versioning, tracking, and reproducibility of models and data. (3) Challenges related to monitoring and maintaining models in a production environment, including detecting data drift and model performance degradation. (4) Challenges related to integrating software development with the machine learning pipeline. (5) Challenges related to ensuring data security and privacy when deploying machine learning models. (6) The quality, availability, preparation, labeling, and integration of data from various sources is a significant challenge that requires a lot of time and resources. (7) The problem of interpreting and explaining the results of model operation to end-users and business stakeholders. (8) The gap between software engineering and machine learning skills – data scientists often do not understand the requirements of certain production environments, and software developers do not have sufficient machine learning skills. (9) The effective distribution, parallelization, and orchestration of data and ML tasks. (10) The diversity of computing infrastructure.

(5) Data collection and processing: collecting, cleaning, transforming, and enriching data for training models. (6) Model development and training: selecting algorithms, developing model architecture, training and validating models. (7) Model evaluation and testing: evaluating model performance on test data, conducting reliability, security, and compliance tests. (8) Model deployment: packaging models with required dependencies, deploying to target environments using container technologies (Docker), cloud platforms (AWS, Google Cloud, Azure), or as web services (REST API). key_findings: This meta-synthesis defines MLOps as an approach for managing, automating, and operationalizing ML model development, deployment, and maintenance, based on software engineering and DevOps principles. The study highlights common frameworks (e.g., MLflow, Kubeflow, cloud platforms, containerization) and tools, emphasizing automation, experiment tracking, versioning, and monitoring. Key challenges include lifecycle management, scalability, performance, data drift, and the skill gap between data scientists and software developers. The paper also points out open issues such as the need for MLOps standards, interpretability, and improved data management.
method: Meta-synthesis of systematic literature reviews and a review of MLOps products and providers, following Chrastina's methodology. It involved defining the research subject, identifying relevant sources, close examination for commonalities and differences, determining relationships between works, reciprocal translation of results, synthesis, and expression of the synthesis.
