title: From Concept to Implementation: The Data-Centric Development Process for AI in Industry
journal: Proceedings - 2023 10th IEEE Swiss Conference on Data Science, SDS 2023
reason: The paper proposes a data-centric AI development process and MLOps pipeline specifically tailored for industrial applications, addressing challenges faced by SMEs in deploying AI.
deployment_context: Industrial AI, specifically in machining and manufacturing, targeting Small and Medium-sized Enterprises (SMEs).
operational_constraints: (1) Small datasets: SMEs often suffer from limited data availability, unlike large consumer internet companies. (2) Data drift: The characteristics of data can change over time in industrial settings, requiring continuous adaptation. (3) Sparse ML knowledge: SMEs often lack in-house expertise in machine learning, hindering AI adoption. (4) Costly annotations: Manual labeling of data is expensive, especially for scarce industrial data. (5) Lack of integrated tools: Existing DCAI frameworks are often highly customized or poorly maintained, hindering adoption in diverse industrial use cases. (6) Limited general solutions for error analysis: The tooling landscape for error analysis, especially for identifying specific samples degrading model performance, is still scarce and often use-case specific.
mlops_practices: (1) Iterative collaboration between domain experts and ML engineers: A tight, iterative loop is established where domain experts provide data/labels, triggering automatic model building, and ML engineers analyze errors, requesting further input from domain experts. (2) Automated model building pipeline: New data/labels trigger an automatic pipeline for training and evaluation. (3) Data labeling tools: Tools like Label Studio are used by domain experts for efficient and user-friendly annotation of time series data, reducing reliance on ML engineers. (4) Data version control: DVC (Data Version Control) is used on top of Git to track changes in data, ensuring a consistent data structure over time. (5) Model lifecycle management: MLflow is employed to track model information for every run, independent of Git commits. (6) Error analysis tools: Tools like Microsoft's Error Analysis are used by ML engineers to identify specific data samples that degrade model performance, guiding data improvement efforts. (7) Communication and orchestration tools: Data warehouses (e.g., MySQL) facilitate the transfer of labeled data and error analysis requests between roles, while orchestration tools (e.g., Airflow) integrate the entire process and automate triggers for new model builds upon data arrival.
gaps_identified: (1) Lack of practical, transferable DCAI development processes: Existing DCAI overviews and frameworks lack practical utility and concrete implementation guidance for practitioners. (2) Insufficient integration of human-in-the-loop: Many DCAI approaches either fully automate or focus solely on human integration for labeling, without a tightly coupled, iterative feedback loop between domain experts and ML engineers. (3) Scarcity of general error analysis tools: The tooling landscape for error analysis, particularly for identifying specific data samples that degrade model performance, is still underdeveloped and often highly specific to certain use cases (e.g., CV, NLP), lacking general solutions for industrial applications. (4) Need for automated data augmentation: Synthesizing data holds enormous prospects but is challenging and not yet fully integrated into current DCAI pipelines. (5) Lack of integrated MLOps packages: The underlying MLOps pipeline for DCAI is not yet available as a fully integrated package, requiring custom assembly of tools.
key_findings: The paper introduces a data-centric AI development process and a supporting MLOps pipeline designed for industrial applications, particularly for SMEs. A core finding is the necessity of tight, iterative collaboration between domain experts and ML engineers, contrasting with traditional model-centric approaches. The proposed process leverages open-source tools for data labeling (e.g., Label Studio), data/model versioning (DVC, MLflow), and error analysis (e.g., Microsoft Error Analysis) to enhance data quality and facilitate AI adoption in environments with small datasets and sparse ML knowledge. The authors identify a gap in general error analysis tooling and the need for integrated MLOps packages for DCAI.
method: The paper's contribution is based on a systematic literature review of existing DCAI concepts and implementations. It then proposes a novel, transferable template for a data-centric development process, derived from an AI application in the machining industry. The method involves defining roles, process steps, and providing an overview of suitable open-source tooling to enable practical implementation.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Data Preparation, Model Training, Model Evaluation, Monitoring (implicitly through error analysis and continuous feedback loops), Deployment (implied by 'successfully deploy the provided process template').
future_research: (1) Integration of automated data augmentation techniques to synthesize data. (2) Wrapping up the MLOps pipeline as an integrated package for easier adoption. (3) Comparative studies of different pipeline configurations to assess their efficacy in facilitating AI system development in practice. (4) Development of more general and robust error analysis tools, especially for novel problems and use cases.

title: AI Assessment in Practice: Implementing a Certification Scheme for AI Trustworthiness
journal: OpenAccess Series in Informatics
reason: This paper describes a practical certification scheme for AI trustworthiness, focusing on implementation details, tools, and methodologies for assessing AI systems throughout their lifecycle. It explicitly discusses MLOps practices for continuous compliance and efficient lifecycle management, making it highly relevant to AI deployment and operations.
evidence_level: Empirical (tested on two real-world computer vision use cases)
deployment_context: Generic AI Implementation (certification scheme applicable across various AI systems, including high-risk medical applications and vehicle detection)
operational_constraints: (1) Lack of practical guidelines and methodologies for achieving and assessing AI trustworthiness, despite extensive ethical guidelines. (2) Difficulty in aggregating diverse AI risks (bias, transparency, security vulnerabilities, ethical considerations) into a holistic assessment. (3) Significant need for interdisciplinarity, combining inputs from law, ethics, and computer science, which is challenging to operationalize at scale. (4) Absence of established technical methods and metrics for certain trustworthiness aspects like fairness and human oversight, making automation and clear implementation difficult. (5) Subjective nature and difficulty in quantifying decision-making processes of foundational models (e.g., LLMs) pose challenges for standardized trustworthiness evaluation.
mlops_practices: (1) Requirements Management: Jira is used as a requirement management tool to organize, document, and trace objectives and associated Means of Compliance (MOCs), ensuring meticulous documentation, traceability, and periodic auditing. (2) Version Control: Git (GitHub) and MLflow are used to version models, code, and parameters, documenting all changes. Oxen is used for data versioning. (3) Experiment Tracking: MLflow provides tooling for tracking experiments. (4) CI/CD Pipelines: GitHub Actions and Apache Airflow are used for workflow scheduling and monitoring, facilitating automated testing and deployment processes. (5) Automated Testing and Evaluation: A full software pipeline is implemented to perform technical assessments of AI models, executing tests based on methods from toolboxes like Captum, Alibi, AIX360, ART, and UQ360. (6) Continuous Monitoring: Post-deployment, continuous monitoring of AIS ensures ongoing compliance and reliable performance in real-world conditions. (7) Data Engineering Pipelines: Pipelines are run to prepare data for training and evaluation and perform data-related trustworthiness evaluations.
gaps_identified: (1) Regulatory requirements and technical standards do not provide clear instructions on which specific methods and metrics can be used to assess properties for trustworthy AI systems. (2) Existing algorithmic toolboxes for AI trustworthiness (e.g., explainability, robustness) have been developed largely disconnected from regulatory and certification frameworks, making their suitability for compliance assessment unclear. (3) The absence of established technical methods and metrics for certain trustworthiness aspects, particularly human oversight and fairness, makes their assessment and automation challenging. (4) Current frameworks often address individual AI risks in isolation, lacking widely accepted methods for aggregating these risks into a holistic assessment. (5) The subjective nature and difficulty in quantifying the decision-making processes of foundational models (e.g., LLMs) pose significant challenges for evaluating and validating their trustworthiness within a standardized framework.
key_findings: The paper presents a comprehensive AI certification scheme developed through academic-industry collaboration, addressing the gap between ethical guidelines and practical application. It integrates MLOps principles and tools like Jira for continuous compliance and efficient lifecycle management, and leverages 95 technical methods for assessing trustworthiness aspects such as reliability, transparency, safety, and security. The scheme was empirically tested on two high-risk real-world computer vision use cases (medical applications and vehicle detection), demonstrating its effectiveness and adaptability. A key finding is that while automation is straightforward for reliability and transparency, aspects like human oversight and fairness present intricate challenges due to the absence of established technical methods and metrics.
method: The method involves a collaborative academic-industry project to develop a certification scheme. This includes an extensive review and integration of 38 key documents from various standards and regulatory bodies, evaluation and selection of requirements management tools (Jira), identification and empirical testing of 95 technical methods for AI trustworthiness assessment, and implementation of an MLOps system for automated workflow and continuous compliance. The scheme was validated through two real-world computer vision use cases.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Project Setup and Requirements Engineering, Data Engineering, Model Development, Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment (CI/CD), Monitoring and Maintenance, Verification and Validation, Operation.
future_research: (1) Further development of the certification scheme to include additional key aspects such as data and data governance, fairness, and data privacy. (2) Development of new requirements, Means of Compliance (MOCs), and methods specifically tailored to foundational models (e.g., LLMs) to address their context-dependent and unpredictable behavior and the challenges in quantifying their decision-making processes. (3) Research into establishing reliable metrics and technical methods for assessing human oversight and fairness in AI systems, which are currently challenging to automate effectively.

title: AutoPilot AI: Architecting Self-Healing ML Systems with Reinforcement Feedback Loops
journal: 2025 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Information Technology, Electronics and Intelligent Communication Systems, ICITEICS 2025
reason: The paper proposes an architectural framework for self-healing ML systems using reinforcement learning feedback loops, directly addressing the operational aspects of deploying and maintaining ML models in production environments.
evidence_level: Experimental findings on real-life data and benchmark datasets
deployment_context: Cloud-native, scalable architecture compatible with existing MLOps stacks (Kubernetes, MLflow, Kubeflow, Airflow). Applicable to mission-critical industries like healthcare diagnostics, autonomous vehicles, financial fraud, and cybersecurity. Also compatible with edge devices for industrial IoT and autonomous vehicles.
operational_constraints: (1) Maintaining model accuracy, stability, and operability over time in non-stationary environments with dynamic data (concept drift). (2) Classical MLOps pipelines are highly dependent on human interaction for monitoring, retraining, and redeploying models, leading to excessive loading and risk of operational failure. (3) Delayed consequences of actions in ML systems (e.g., retraining can last minutes to hours, effects noticeable only after deployment). (4) Decision-making under uncertainty in production environments, requiring prevention of overconfident judgments.
mlops_practices: (1) Real-time monitoring of deployed ML models: Accumulating metrics on prediction performance, latency, data distribution statistics, and system resource usage. (2) Automated anomaly and drift detection: Using statistical and ML-based methods (e.g., PSI, Jensen Shannon divergence, Page Hinkley test) to detect covariate drift, concept drift, and performance deterioration. (3) Reinforcement learning (RL) agent for policy selection: An RL agent (Policy Engine) observes system state, learns optimal policies, and triggers corrective actions (retrain, fine-tune, rollback, reallocate resources, do-nothing). (4) Automated actuation: An Actuator component implements chosen corrective actions without human involvement, communicating with CI/CD pipelines for retraining, deployment updates, or rollbacks with minimal downtime. (5) Explainability modules (XAI): Integration of LIME and SHAP for local interpretability and surrogate models for global explanations, providing human-readable justifications for agent decisions. (6) Logging and auditing: Comprehensive logging of all actions and decisions to an ELK stack for traceability and compliance. (7) Scalable and cloud-native deployment: Microservices implemented as containers in Kubernetes clusters, allowing horizontal scaling and distributed training. (8) Continuous learning: An experience replay buffer records past states, actions, rewards, and outcomes, periodically used to retrain the RL agent. (9) Online and batch correction modes: Online mode for small degradations (dynamic threshold tuning, inference-time adjustments) and batch mode for severe performance loss (offline retraining, staging, testing, controlled re-deployments like canary or blue-green). (10) Validation and rollback mechanisms: Post-recovery monitoring to confirm system recovery, with negative rewards and rollback initiation if performance doesn't improve. (11) Security features: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC), TLS encryption, token authentication, and API gateways.
gaps_identified: (1) Existing automatic retraining and scheduling methods are often non-real-time and lack a feedback loop between model performance and system behavior. (2) Current model monitoring and feedback loop solutions (e.g., Evidently AI, Arise) offer observability but cannot autonomically actuate corrective actions. (3) Concept drift detection algorithms (ADWIN, DDM, Page-Hinkley) are not typically implemented in closed-loop systems. (4) A significant gap exists in systems providing a self-healing, fully automated, reinforcement-driven architecture for ML operations.
key_findings: AutoPilot AI, a self-healing ML system architecture leveraging reinforcement feedback loops, significantly improves operational resilience. Experimental results show it retains over 94% accuracy, reduces Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR) by over 40% (to 49 minutes), decreases false alarm rates by 50% (to 11%), and achieves a 47% cost-efficiency improvement compared to static retraining. The system also maintains high service availability (99.5%) through intelligent recovery actions, demonstrating its capacity for autonomous adaptation and recovery in dynamic environments.
method: The paper proposes an architectural framework and evaluates it through experimental studies. The system is implemented using open-source tools on a Kubernetes-based MLOps cluster. Experiments were conducted on three benchmark datasets with known drift properties (Electricity Usage, Air Quality forecasting, Credit Card Fraud Detection) and compared against periodic retraining and heuristic-driven recovery baselines. Ablation studies were performed to assess the contribution of individual components.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Model Monitoring, Model Retraining, Model Deployment, Model Governance, Model Maintenance, Model Operations
future_research: (1) Broaden the architecture to federated self-healing systems for decentralized settings and privacy-preserving model adaptation. (2) Incorporate Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) for complex systems with interdependent models. (3) Develop Trust-Eager/Risk-Conscious Policies with reward shaping and bounded RL for high-stakes applications. (4) Optimize explainability through co-learning causal relationships and neuro-symbolic approaches. (5) Develop lightweight versions of AutoPilot AI for deployment on resource-constrained edge devices.

title: VISLIX: An XAI Framework for Validating Vision Models with Slice Discovery and Analysis
journal: Computer Graphics Forum
reason: This paper introduces VISLIX, an XAI framework for validating computer vision models by identifying and explaining data slices where models underperform. It focuses on deployment and validation aspects, discussing how to identify, understand, and address model failures before deployment, which aligns with the scope of AI deployment and operations.
deployment_context: Autonomous driving, surveillance (safety-critical domains)
operational_constraints: (1) Existing slice discovery methods often rely on expert-defined image metadata or visual concepts, which are labor-intensive to acquire and miss complex scenarios (e.g., 'car occluded by a tree'). (2) Global image embeddings used by some slice discovery methods are not well-suited for object detectors, as they overlook detection locality and cannot distinguish multiple detections in the same image. (3) Interpreting data slices is a labor-intensive and mentally demanding process that heavily relies on the expert’s domain knowledge and is prone to bias or errors. (4) Current slice discovery methods are meant to run only once and cannot interact with users, limiting hypothesis testing and iterative refinement. (5) Model hallucinations in LLMs/VLMs can cause ungrounded explanations, such as confusing occlusion with misclassification or misinterpreting tiny detections. (6) The fixed context region (2x detection window) for context-aware embeddings may capture insufficient context, especially for small detections, leading to inconsistent slices.
mlops_practices: (1) Model validation: VISLIX provides a framework for rigorous evaluation of ML models before deployment, especially in safety-critical domains. (2) Data slicing: Automatically identifies data subgroups where models underperform to mitigate performance issues. (3) Explainable AI (XAI): Leverages LLMs and VLMs to generate natural language explanations for data slices, aiding in understanding model failure modes. (4) Human-in-the-loop validation: Enables experts to interactively explore slices, inspect and refine slice details, and test hypothetical scenarios through visual manipulation and natural language queries. (5) Performance monitoring: Computes precision and recall for each data slice to assess its significance and identify those with lower values. (6) Model refinement/retraining: Insights gained from VISLIX can be leveraged to enhance model robustness through fine-tuning, by retrieving similar images from training data and retraining the model with reweighting techniques for worst-group optimization.
gaps_identified: (1) Standard data slicing methods for vision models often require additional image metadata or visual concepts, which are hard to acquire and fall short in tasks like object detection. (2) Existing data slicing methods lack a human-in-the-loop solution that allows experts to form and test hypotheses interactively. (3) Prior methods for interpreting data slices often provide high-level descriptions, overlooking nuanced errors in detection. (4) Current interactive systems for data slice analysis primarily focus on presenting pre-generated slices, lacking robust interactive discovery and hypothesis testing capabilities. (5) General-purpose predefined questions for VLM/LLM explanations may not generalize well across all scenarios (e.g., weather questions for indoor settings). (6) Existing cropping strategies for context-aware embeddings may not capture sufficient context for small detections, leading to inconsistent slices.
key_findings: VISLIX is an XAI framework that leverages VLMs and LLMs to validate computer vision models in a human-in-the-loop manner. It automatically discovers and explains data slices, particularly in object detectors, and empowers experts to efficiently explore slices and interactively test hypothetical slices through a visual analytics system. An expert study and three use cases demonstrate its effectiveness in providing comprehensive insights for validating object detection models. The framework is model-agnostic and can benefit from ongoing advancements in foundation models, showing improved mAP values (e.g., 3% higher for person detection) after fine-tuning based on VISLIX-identified slices.
method: The paper proposes a novel visual analytics framework (VISLIX) that employs state-of-the-art foundation models (LLMs and VLMs) for human-in-the-loop slice discovery and analysis in computer vision models, specifically object detection. The method involves context-aware embedding generation, dimensionality reduction (UMAP), clustering (HDBSCAN) for slice finding, and a two-step process (individual explanations, then summarization) for slice explanation using LLaVA and GPT. An interactive visual analytics system with linked views (slice list, slice plot, slice detail) supports exploration and hypothesis testing. The framework was evaluated through an expert study with six ML practitioners and three use cases on car, person, dog, and chair detectors.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Model Validation, Model Monitoring, Model Retraining/Refinement
future_research: (1) Improving the slice discovery method by developing a cropping strategy that prioritizes detection while maintaining sufficient context, especially for small detections. (2) Enabling user control of UMAP and HDBSCAN parameters for slice discovery, while balancing complexity and computational cost. (3) Enhancing the quality of slice explanations by allowing user-modifiable prompts, using golden explanations as few-shot examples, and incorporating TPs as contrastive examples. (4) Allowing experts to tailor core questions for VLM/LLM explanations to specific applications. (5) Improving the visual analytics system by displaying ground truth for incorrect detections to identify mislabeled data. (6) Expanding the search space beyond the validation set (e.g., via image retrieval) to improve hypothesis testing and enable model refinements with additional training data. (7) Extending VISLIX to other vision tasks like semantic segmentation, requiring careful consideration for defining context and intersection regions for irregularly shaped segments.

title: Multi-metric Approach for Decomposition of Microservice-Based Data Science Workflows
journal: Lecture Notes in Computer Science (including subseries Lecture Notes in Artificial Intelligence and Lecture Notes in Bioinformatics)
reason: The paper focuses on a multi-metric approach for decomposing data science workflows into microservices, explicitly addressing MLOps implications for training and deploying ML models in production. It discusses architectural decisions, runtime evaluation, and cost considerations for microservice-based AI systems.
deployment_context: Cloud environments (EC2, ECS Fargate) for data science workflows, specifically an automated startup assessment system within an automotive manufacturer. Focus on microservice architectures for ML model training and inference.
operational_constraints: (1) High volume and velocity characteristics of data in data science workflows impact architectural decisions, requiring efficient data exchange between microservices. (2) Long-running processing tasks, such as data preparation and ML model training, need to be accommodated within the microservice architecture. (3) The quality of cohesion metrics for microservice decomposition is strongly dependent on the choice of function and parameter names, requiring standardization. (4) Trade-off decisions are necessary between competing metrics like workflow execution times, service activation times (cost proxy), and domain metrics (scaling requirements). (5) Current approaches lack recommendations for weights of metrics and workloads, making architectural choices dependent on manual preferences.
mlops_practices: (1) Microservice decomposition: A multi-metric approach combining design (cohesion) and runtime metrics (workflow execution time, service activation time, domain metric) to identify optimal microservice architectures for data science workflows. (2) Automated deployment: Software architecture designed to deploy microservice architectures concurrently in cloud environments (e.g., AWS CloudFormation, TOSCA) using Docker images on ECS Fargate instances. (3) Workflow scheduling: Implemented using tools like Apache Airflow or Jmeter for load testing, supporting orchestration or choreography patterns. (4) Monitoring and evaluation: Runtime evaluation metrics (Average Change of Workflow Execution Times, Change of Total Service Activation Time, Domain Metric) are used to assess deployed architectures under different workload conditions (volume, velocity). (5) Cost optimization: Service activation time is used as a proxy for the cost of an architecture in cloud environments. (6) Scaling requirement identification: The Domain Metric is used to identify bottlenecks and scaling potentials for microservices.
gaps_identified: (1) Lack of support for software architects in designing and identifying microservices specifically for data science workflows, which differ from transaction-oriented applications due to high volume and velocity. (2) Current literature either assumes microservices are already designed or ends identification after the design phase, lacking approaches that combine design and runtime metrics for data science workflows. (3) Existing approaches for microservice identification lack consideration of cohesion metrics in workflow-based systems. (4) Lack of clear recommendations for weighting different metrics (e.g., cost, performance, scalability) and workload probabilities when making architectural trade-off decisions for data science microservices.
key_findings: The paper presents a multi-metric approach for decomposing data science workflows into microservices, combining design (cohesion) and runtime metrics (workflow execution time, service activation time, domain metric). It demonstrates that different microservice architectures perform optimally across various metrics, necessitating trade-off decisions. The evaluation using a real-world startup assessment use case from an automotive manufacturer shows that the approach can support architects in selecting suitable microservice architectures by considering workload conditions and metric preferences. The work contributes an abstract process model and a software architecture for concurrent deployment and automated analysis in cloud environments, with implications for MLOps in training and deploying ML models.
method: Design Science Research (implied by proposing an approach and architecture), Empirical Evaluation (case study with real-world use case and experiments)
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Design, Deployment, Monitoring, Operations, Governance (cost implications)
future_research: (1) Conduct more experiments with a wider range of workload configurations. (2) Develop recommendations for the weights of metrics and workloads, moving beyond manual preferences. (3) Investigate the influence of function and parameter naming on cohesion metric quality. (4) Develop simulations to describe and test runtime behavior of microservice architectures during the design phase, reducing the need for full implementation.

title: MLOps in the Metaverse: Human-Centric Continuous Integration
journal: IEEE Journal on Selected Areas in Communications
reason: The paper directly addresses MLOps challenges in the metaverse, focusing on continuous integration and fine-tuning of deployed models with human-generated data. It discusses operational constraints like sporadic data generation and privacy, and proposes a specific MLOps practice (SPIN) to handle these.
deployment_context: Metaverse (human-centric, decentralized, edge devices like VR/AR headsets, M1 Max CPU for client fine-tuning)
operational_constraints: ["(1) Sporadic and unpredictable data generation: Labeled data samples from human users in the metaverse are produced sporadically and unpredictably over time, unlike conventional FL where client data is assumed to be readily available in each communication round.", "(2) Small, incremental local datasets: Local data samples in human-centric metaverse environments are generated sporadically in small increments, not in large batches (hundreds of thousands), which can make conventional FL ineffective.", "(3) Data privacy concerns: The metaverse involves sensitive personal information, making it crucial to guarantee the privacy of user-generated data in a decentralized environment.", "(4) Computational resource limitations on client devices: Client devices (e.g., headsets) in the metaverse are computationally much less powerful than servers (e.g., without ready GPU access), meaning local model fine-tuning takes considerable time.", "(5) Model staleness due to slow client training and frequent pull requests: When clients take a long time to fine-tune models, and pull requests arrive frequently, clients may receive models that are significantly older than the one into which their update is aggregated, leading to poor performance or convergence failures.", "(6) Heterogeneity in training capabilities and data distribution: Users have significant variation in training speed and data distribution, which can impact convergence and fairness in model contribution.", "(7) Potential for low-quality or malicious data: The assumption that new data samples always lead to better models is not always true, especially with malicious users intentionally feeding low-quality data into the system."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) Human-centric Continuous Integration (CI): Adopts CI principles where data samples generated by human interaction are utilized as quickly as possible for fine-tuning deployed models, rather than waiting for fixed communication rounds.", "(2) Client-initiated Pull Requests: Clients proactively request the globally deployed model from the server whenever a sufficient amount of new data becomes available, rather than waiting for server selection.", "(3) Minimum Data Accumulation Threshold: Clients are required to accumulate a minimum number of data samples (e.g., 64 for YOLOv8 object detection) before sending an initial pull request to mitigate convergence failures due to very small local datasets.", "(4) Server-side Aggregation with Timeout (π): The server aggregates model updates when a minimum number of updates (K) is received OR a timeout (π) expires, to mitigate model staleness and ensure timely aggregation, especially with heterogeneous client training speeds.", "(5) Federated Averaging (FedAvg) for Aggregation: The server uses FedAvg to aggregate model updates from clients.", "(6) Asynchronous Communication: SPIN maintains asynchrony and concurrency of pull updates, allowing clients to send updates whenever data is generated, and the server responds immediately with the current global model.", "(7) Pre-trained Model Fine-tuning: Focuses on fine-tuning already deployed and pre-trained models (e.g., YOLOv8) rather than training from scratch, suitable for resource-constrained edge devices."]
gaps_identified: ["Conventional Federated Learning (FL) paradigms do not consider the sporadic and unpredictable timing of data sample production from human users, assuming data is readily available in each communication round.", "Conventional FL literature has not studied cases where private data from human users arrives over time sporadically and in small increments, which is typical in human-centric metaverse environments.", "Existing FL mechanisms (e.g., AFL) do not fully consider sporadic event arrivals when client data becomes available, how such arrival processes affect convergence, or the specific task of fine-tuning deployed models.", "Existing FL mechanisms do not consider potential conflicts between pull requests from some clients and push updates from others to the server, or how such conflicts should be best resolved in a human-centric, asynchronous setting.", "Standard FL approaches do not effectively handle the problem of model staleness when client devices are slow and pull requests are frequent, leading to clients training on significantly outdated models.", "Current FL aggregation algorithms do not inherently account for the dynamic and variable count of updates eligible for aggregation when user participation is sporadic and uncontrolled."]
key_findings: The paper proposes SPIN, a novel MLOps mechanism for fine-tuning pre-trained models in the metaverse using human-generated, sporadically arriving data. SPIN integrates continuous integration principles with federated learning, employing client-initiated pull requests and a server-side aggregation with a timeout (π) to mitigate model staleness. Experimental results across image classification (MNIST, EMNIST, CIFAR-10) and object detection (YOLOv8 on COCO128) tasks demonstrate that SPIN significantly outperforms state-of-the-art asynchronous FL mechanisms like FedBuff and PORT in convergence speed, achieving up to 4.11 times faster convergence. The findings highlight the effectiveness and robustness of SPIN in specialized metaverse scenarios with resource-constrained edge devices and sporadic data.
method: The method involves proposing a novel algorithm (SPIN) based on continuous integration and federated learning principles. It includes a mathematically rigorous convergence analysis for non-convex settings with dynamic user arrival rates (Poisson distribution). The algorithm is implemented in the PLATO FL research framework, and its performance is evaluated through extensive experiments using benchmark datasets (MNIST, EMNIST, CIFAR-10, COCO128) and models (LeNet-5, ResNet-18, YOLOv8) on both GPU servers and M1 Max CPU devices. Comparisons are made against state-of-the-art asynchronous FL mechanisms (FedBuff, PORT).
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Deployment, Monitoring, Maintenance (specifically, continuous fine-tuning and retraining)
future_research: Future work includes studying how malicious users can be excluded from the fine-tuning process to ensure high-quality data samples contribute to model improvement. The authors also plan to explore new research challenges where models need to be fine-tuned using human interaction, and local training is no longer feasible due to resource constraints, extending beyond metaverse-specific scenarios.

title: Designing a cloud-native MLOps pipeline using Databricks: A case study in industrial practice
journal: Future Generation Computer Systems
reason: The paper presents an engineering case study on designing and evolving a cloud-native MLOps pipeline in an industrial setting, explicitly discussing deployment, operational challenges, and collaboration practices for ML systems.
evidence_level: Empirical evidence from real deployment contexts
deployment_context: Industrial setting (Fastems, a manufacturing company) using Databricks platform for DataOps and ModelOps, and Azure DevOps for CI/CD and governance. Focus on IoT and sensor data from the shop floor.
operational_constraints: ["(1) Security and data-privacy constraints: Operationalized through workspace isolation, role-based access control, and secrets management integrated into deployment automation.", "(2) Cost constraints: Focused on having data ingestion be more batch-oriented compared to more costly real-time data ingestion.", "(3) Organizational governance structure: Deployment is intentionally integrated into an existing organizational governance structure (Azure DevOps) rather than fully automated within the MLOps stack, reflecting a deliberate risk management strategy.", "(4) Legacy notebooks without test coverage: Automated unit tests were omitted for ModelOps notebooks due to time constraints and existing legacy code, requiring alternative validation mechanisms.", "(5) Time constraints: Prevented the implementation of automated unit testing for ModelOps notebooks.", "(6) Need for interactive data exploration and debugging: Notebook chaining was preferred for DataOps over asset bundles to allow inspection of intermediate data states and incremental validation during an active exploration phase."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) Platform-centric approach: Using Databricks for automation, reproducibility, and scalable operation, integrating versioned data management, experiment tracking, model registry, CI/CD for ML, and environment isolation.", "(2) Workspace isolation: Multiple isolated Databricks workspaces (dev, qa, prod, sandbox) are used for different stages of the ML lifecycle to support controlled promotion, isolation, and governance.", "(3) Shared Hive metastore: All workspaces share a common Hive metastore to ensure consistency in data definitions and simplify data governance across environments.", "(4) Lakeflow Jobs for orchestration: Native Databricks service used for managing and automating data engineering, machine learning, and data analytics tasks, ensuring repeatable training runs and centralized scheduling.", "(5) Asset bundles for packaging: Used to package all necessary resources (jobs, tasks, notebooks, libraries, clusters) into a single deployable unit for consistent execution across environments.", "(6) Medallion model: Layered data architecture (bronze, silver, gold) built on Delta Lake for incremental refinement of raw sensor data.", "(7) MLflow for model lifecycle management: Used for experiment tracking, model training, registration, and stage-based promotion (Staging, Production) to enable formal tracking, review, and governance.", "(8) Git-backed notebooks and version control: All source code, including Databricks notebooks, libraries, and bundle descriptors, is version-controlled in an Azure DevOps Git repository, adopting a trunk-based workflow.", "(9) Databricks Repos: Linked to Azure DevOps to allow developers to raise pull requests directly from the Databricks UI.", "(10) Azure DevOps CI/CD pipelines: Manages source control, build pipelines, and deployment flows, integrating with Databricks CLI tools for reproducible deployments.", "(11) Hybrid deployment strategy: Automation stops at model promotion within Databricks, and deployment is manually managed through the organization's existing Azure DevOps governance framework.", "(12) Compute cost controls: Configured directly in Databricks through cluster policies to ensure training and inference workloads remain within budget constraints.", "(13) Data validation tests: Performed during the DataOps phase to identify structural issues, serving as an alternative to automated unit tests for ModelOps notebooks.", "(14) Reusable notebook components: Progressively extracted and parameterized to support different configurations and pipeline stages, later consolidated into a project template."]
gaps_identified: ["The gap between idealized MLOps automation and its actual realization under industrial constraints is precisely what vendor documentation and conceptual frameworks do not address.", "Existing studies primarily focus on either (i) identifying and categorizing tools within the MLOps ecosystem or (ii) assessing maturity progression across organizations, but do not provide a comprehensive architectural implementation of how MLOps practices are operationalized within a particular industrial platform stack."]
key_findings: The paper presents a platform-grounded reference architecture for industrial MLOps adoption, derived from an engineering case study. It details how established MLOps principles were concretely instantiated and adapted under real organizational and security constraints, including deliberate deviations from vendor guidance. Key findings include the importance of artifact-driven coordination, where shared data layers serve as concrete reference points for discussion, and the value of treating data and code as reusable building blocks. The study highlights a dimension-asymmetric maturity progression, where orchestration and lifecycle management are fully realized within the platform, but deployment is intentionally integrated into an existing Azure DevOps governance structure.
method: Engineering case study following Runeson and Höst, involving active participation of researchers as contributors and observers. Data collected from personal meeting notes, direct access to notebooks/configurations, internal presentations, and first-hand observation over 18 months. Triangulation across development artifacts, CI/CD records, and collaborative design episodes.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: End-to-end MLOps lifecycle, covering DataOps (ingestion, transformation, validation), ModelOps (experiment tracking, training, registration, promotion), and DevOps (CI/CD, infrastructure provisioning, deployment).
future_research: ["Extending automated unit tests to legacy notebooks.", "Implementing model drift monitoring.", "Developing automated retraining pipelines."]

title: Green MLOps to Green GenOps: An Empirical Study of Energy Consumption in Discriminative and Generative AI Operations †
journal: Information (Switzerland)
reason: The paper empirically investigates energy consumption in MLOps and GenOps pipelines for both discriminative and generative AI models, focusing on operational aspects like inference, training, and hardware utilization. It provides practical guidelines for sustainable ML operations.
evidence_level: Empirical measurements on real-world hardware configurations.
deployment_context: MLOps and GenOps pipelines, including real-time, high-frequency interactions for LLMs (e.g., chatbot platforms) and image classification tasks for discriminative AI. Deployment on various hardware configurations (consumer-grade to high-performance enterprise servers with multiple GPUs).
operational_constraints: ["(1) Model architecture uniqueness limits cross-model generalizations for energy efficiency, meaning a model with similar characteristics to an energy-efficient one may not exhibit comparable efficiency.", "(2) Training and inference durations are not directly correlated, making cross-phase or cross-hardware energy estimations unreliable.", "(3) Hardware power profiles are not strictly linear; manufacturers often push device limits for marginal performance gains, leading to inefficiencies.", "(4) Larger Generative AI models (e.g., 70B parameters) incur significantly higher energy costs per request, especially at lower Requests Per Second (RPS) rates where resource utilization is less efficient.", "(5) The latency of LLM inference is likely to increase as the server approaches saturation capacity, creating a trade-off with energy efficiency gains from higher utilization.", "(6) Access to hardware energy consumption measurements is often required for accurate energy profiling, and when unavailable, predictive models based on computational efficiency metrics, model hyperparameters, and hardware characteristics must be built.", "(7) The substantial cost and limited accessibility of training large generative models make it impractical for many practitioners, leading to a focus on inference with pre-trained models."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) Energy profiling: Software-based power measurements using NVML (for GPUs) and RAPL (for CPUs/DRAM) are employed to monitor energy consumption during training and inference, enabling replication across diverse configurations.", "(2) Model architecture selection: Prioritizing models that are energy-efficient during inference (e.g., ResNet or VGG for discriminative AI) is recommended for real-world applications, as inference accounts for a significant portion of energy consumption.", "(3) Batch size tuning: Optimizing batch sizes to minimize power consumption is identified, with an optimal batch size existing beyond which further increases yield no additional improvements.", "(4) Hardware provisioning: Orchestrating model deployment to ensure hardware operates close to its saturation point (without exceeding it) leads to the best energy-performance results for both discriminative and generative AI.", "(5) Power capping optimization: Techniques like power capping can mitigate inefficiencies caused by non-linear hardware power profiles and significantly reduce energy consumption.", "(6) Short-lived profiling: Using short-lived experiments (e.g., training for one epoch or inferring for a small number of samples) as a reliable predictor of energy consumption for larger-scale scenarios due to linear scaling of time and total energy.", "(7) Model size selection for LLMs: Choosing appropriately sized LLMs based on anticipated Requests Per Second (RPS) and computational requirements to achieve substantial energy savings, with smaller models (1-3B parameters) offering a good balance for moderate request rates.", "(8) Fine-tuning smaller models: Fine-tuning smaller models to achieve accuracy levels comparable to larger models is a viable strategy to reduce energy and resource consumption for generative AI.", "(9) Prompt caching: Leveraging pre-computed tokens from matching prompt prefixes to reduce redundant computation and overall energy usage in GenOps pipelines.", "(10) Dynamic model selection and adaptive inference: Integrating energy profiling into MLOps/GenOps frameworks to enable dynamic model selection, power capping, or adaptive inference based on operational requirements."]
gaps_identified: ["Current literature lacks a systematic evaluation of energy-saving methods for ML, especially empirical studies on real-world hardware.", "Existing works on energy consumption often focus on smaller-scale investigations, impacting the accuracy of a given model, without exploring trade-offs across various configurations and parameters without compromising model accuracy.", "There is a lack of investigations reporting energy consumption across the different phases of GenOps, particularly for inference.", "None of the existing works focused on how model characteristics and number of requests impact the energy consumption of an MLOps or GenOps pipeline.", "Current studies do not account for all possible application-specific optimizations, such as quantization-aware training or adaptive model scaling at runtime.", "An investigation targeting the energy consumption of different model layer types (e.g., convolutional, activation, pooling) is needed to provide more practical guidelines to ML practitioners."]
key_findings: This study empirically demonstrates that optimizing model architecture, hyperparameters, and hardware provisioning can yield significant energy savings for discriminative AI without compromising performance, often surpassing marginal accuracy improvements. For generative AI, balancing model size and reasoning with request-handling capability is crucial for energy efficiency, as larger models do not necessarily consume more energy when utilization is low. The research highlights that energy consumption dynamics vary significantly across training, inference, and hardware configurations, emphasizing the need for tailored strategies within each ML pipeline stage, and provides practical guidelines for designing green and sustainable ML operations.
method: The study employs an empirical investigation using software-based power measurements (NVML for GPUs, RAPL for CPUs/DRAM) to profile energy consumption. Experiments were conducted on various hardware configurations (HC-1 to HC-4) for both discriminative AI (image classification with CIFAR-10 dataset and various CNN architectures) and generative AI (LLM inference with Meta Llama models and Chatbot Arena dataset). Correlation analyses (Pearson r and Spearman ρ) were performed to identify relationships between energy consumption and various model/operational metrics.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Data Processing, Experimentation, Training/Evaluation, Inference, Monitoring, Deployment, Fine-tuning, Prompt Design and Management.
future_research: Future research should explore adaptive strategies for energy management, real-time deployment considerations, and broader hardware-software co-design approaches to further improve sustainability in ML pipelines. This includes considering other hardware architectures (e.g., edge devices, ARM-based), a larger set of hardware configurations, and investigating application-specific optimizations like quantization-aware training or adaptive model scaling at runtime. Additionally, an investigation into the energy consumption of individual model layer types (e.g., convolutional, activation, pooling) is suggested to provide more granular guidance for energy-efficient model building.

title: MLOps for Advanced Metering Infrastructure Applications
journal: IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies Conference Europe
reason: The paper details the application of MLOps principles and practices for deploying and maintaining AI models in the Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI) domain, specifically for appliance detection and energy usage disaggregation. It discusses operational aspects like data curation, model monitoring, retraining, and deployment in a real-world industrial context.
deployment_context: Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI) for energy utilities (appliance detection, energy usage disaggregation, electric heating detection).
operational_constraints: (1) Real-world ML problems involve multiple unstructured data sources and various data formats that are frequently updated, requiring robust data curation pipelines and version control. (2) Model performance can degrade over time due to model drift, necessitating continuous monitoring and retraining. (3) Appliance-level sub-metering data (ground truth labels for disaggregation) is rarely available, requiring internal creation or collection from third parties. (4) Data sources vary widely in structure (databases with different schemas, file archives with different formats and access patterns) and evolve over time, making data preparation nontrivial. (5) Model retraining introduces the risk of data leakage if splits are simply re-randomized, preventing fair model comparison. (6) Model-specific transformations (e.g., different context windows, seasonal training) are required, which do not fit into generic feature set curation.
mlops_practices: (1) Data Curation Pipelines: Well-defined pipelines and version control track code changes and data updates, ensuring reproducibility by recording curation date and transformations. (2) Feature Set Curation Jobs: Isolated jobs transform source data into a standard feature format, recording schema and calendar versions, and isolating performance bottlenecks. (3) Split Curation Jobs: Combine feature sets, create training/validation splits, and employ stratified sampling for minority group representation. (4) History-Preserving Splits: Maintain assignment of existing data while randomizing only newly added samples during retraining to mitigate data leakage and enable systematic tracking of prediction changes. (5) Preprocessing Pipelines: Model-specific transformations are packaged with the model to ensure flexibility and reusability of feature sets. (6) Model Evaluation and Selection: Multiple models are trained and evaluated, with metrics stored in MLFlow, and subject matter expert (SME) discussions guide model selection. (7) Model Catalog: Used to register and package scoring code, model weights, and preprocessing pipelines, with models declaring required features in metadata. (8) Model Deployment Server: Hosts models by spinning up compute resources, loading packaged conda environments and scoring code, and exposing HTTPS endpoints for predictions. (9) Continuous Model Monitoring: Tracks data distribution and model performance post-deployment to detect model drift. (10) Automated Retraining: Triggered by significant drift or scheduled at regular intervals, leveraging existing jobs and pipelines with minimal configuration updates. (11) Cloud-based MLOps Platform (OCI Data Science): Leverages jobs for self-contained environments, scheduling, resource monitoring, logging, provenance, and isolation, with automated pipelines via DAGs.
gaps_identified: The paper implicitly highlights that standard ML project methodologies like CRISP-DM do not fully address the operational complexities of continuous deployment, monitoring, and maintenance in industrial settings. Specifically, it points out that many businesses do not realize the need for continuous model monitoring after deployment to address model drift, and that academic research often relies on fixed benchmark datasets, which contrasts with the dynamic, unstructured, and frequently updated data in real-world industrial applications.
key_findings: The paper demonstrates the importance of MLOps in industrial ML applications, specifically within Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI). It presents a robust MLOps framework that automates data curation, model training, evaluation, deployment, and monitoring, enabling efficient combat against model drift. A case study on electric heating detection shows that retraining models with fresher data using their MLOps architecture effectively recovers and improves performance degraded by drift. The authors conclude that MLOps provides a reproducible, scalable, and time-efficient framework, allowing data scientists to focus on higher-value tasks.
method: Case Study / Experience Report. The authors describe their MLOps approach and then demonstrate its application with a real-world case study on electric heating detection using real customer AMI data from six different regions. They compare the performance of a model trained on 2020 data with a model retrained on 2023 data to illustrate model drift and the effectiveness of their retraining process.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Data Preparation, Model Training, Model Evaluation, Model Deployment, Model Monitoring, Model Retraining, Governance (version control, reproducibility).
future_research: (1) Continued importance and adoption of MLOps in various energy applications (load forecasting, electric vehicle detection, appliance disaggregation). (2) Further development and adoption of more sophisticated MLOps processes driven by recent advances in agentic AI.

title: A TinyML Soft-Sensor for the Internet of Intelligent Vehicles
journal: 2022 IEEE International Workshop on Metrology for Automotive, MetroAutomotive 2022 - Proceedings
reason: The paper details the development and deployment of a TinyML model (soft-sensor) onto an OBD-II automotive scanner for CO2 emission estimation, explicitly discussing the MLOps workflow, quantization techniques, and performance on embedded hardware. This directly addresses AI deployment and operations in a specific industrial context.
deployment_context: Automotive (OBD-II scanner, Macchina A0 microcontroller) for real-time CO2 emission estimation (soft-sensor).
operational_constraints: ["(1) Limited computational power and memory on ultra-low power embedded devices (e.g., Macchina A0 with ESP32-WROVER-E module) necessitate model optimization techniques like quantization.", "(2) Real-time inference requirements for critical automotive applications (e.g., ADAS) demand low latency, which can be challenging on resource-constrained edge devices.", "(3) Data acquisition from vehicles relies on the OBD-II system, which may not natively expose all desired variables (e.g., CO2 emission), requiring derivation from other available parameters (e.g., MAF, AFR, Density).", "(4) The need for on-device inference to ensure responsiveness and privacy, while avoiding energy costs and latency associated with wireless communication to cloud platforms, especially in regions with low bandwidth."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) Data Collection: Real-world data fetched from several vehicles via OBD-II automotive scanners using the Torque OBDPro app, collecting intake pressure, intake temperature, speed, and mass airflow (MAF) every 1 second.", "(2) Data Processing: CO2 emission is derived from MAF and other parameters (AFR, Density) when not natively available. Min-max normalization is applied to variables to prevent dominant influence.", "(3) Model Design: Multilayer Perceptron Neural Network with varying hidden layers (1-16), neurons (1-80), and activation functions (relu or sigmoid) to investigate impact on memory, performance, and inference time.", "(4) Model Training: 70% of the dataset used for training with Adamax optimizer, learning rate 0.001, batch size 20, 100 epochs, MSE loss function, and MAPE evaluation metric. 0.2% for validation.", "(5) Model Evaluation & Optimization: Two quantization methods (OPTIMIZE FOR SIZE and EXPERIMENTAL SPARSITY from TensorFlow Lite) are adopted to reduce model size, latency, and loss functions, or leverage weight sparsitivity.", "(6) Model Conversion: TensorFlow model converted to TensorFlow Lite and then to TensorFlow Lite Micro object, encapsulating scalar-tensors into a 2D array and wrapping into a 32-bit float list, finally converted to a C++ file.", "(7) Model Deployment: The C++ model artifact is transmitted from a serial port and deployed to the Macchina A0 microcontroller, which uses an ESP32-WROVER-E module and a Data Link Connection (DLC) interface to communicate with the vehicle ECU.", "(8) Inference Making: An OBD-II Emulator generates vehicle data, which is fed to the deployed model on the target hardware via a DLC port. Inference results are monitored and collected via a serial port connection to a PC Desktop."]
gaps_identified: ["Standard ML deployment often assumes sufficient computational resources and continuous connectivity, which is not viable for ultra-low power embedded devices in vehicles.", "Traditional cloud-based ML inference introduces latency and privacy concerns, and incurs energy costs for wireless communication, which is problematic for critical automotive systems and areas with low bandwidth.", "Existing methods for vehicle diagnostics often require an internet connection to transfer data for off-board processing, limiting their use in remote or low-connectivity regions."]
key_findings: This study successfully developed and deployed a TinyML soft-sensor for CO2 emission estimation on an OBD-II automotive scanner. Key findings include that quantization methods significantly reduce model size (average 92% reduction), with sparsity quantization being more efficient for increasing network complexity. The relu activation function consistently outperformed sigmoid, reducing inference time by 81.95% and MAPE by 50.5% on average, and was less sensitive to network complexity. The work demonstrates the feasibility of on-device ML inference for automotive applications, addressing resource constraints and connectivity challenges.
method: Experimental study with a custom TinyML workflow. Real-world data collected from vehicles, models trained and optimized using TensorFlow/TensorFlow Lite/Micro, and deployed on a Macchina A0 microcontroller. Performance evaluated based on memory usage, inference time, and MAPE.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Data Collection, Data Processing, Model Design, Model Training, Model Evaluation & Optimization, Model Conversion, Model Deployment, Inference Making (all stages of the MLOps workflow are explicitly covered).
future_research: Exploration of more complex network scenarios using convolutions and recurrent architectures.

title: MLOps: A Taxonomy and a Methodology
journal: IEEE Access
reason: This paper provides a comprehensive taxonomy and methodology for MLOps, detailing the stages of ML model deployment, operation, and maintenance in production environments. It explicitly discusses challenges and practices relevant to industrializing ML solutions.
deployment_context: General MLOps, with a specific medical use case (Oravizio) and mentions of industrial production, biomedicine, finance, cyber-security, and manufacturing as beneficiaries.
operational_constraints: (1) Data are constantly changing, implying ML models need retraining or pipeline rebuilding to tackle feature drift. (2) Manual model development and operations slow down the industrialization of ML methodologies. (3) The most time-consuming step in releasing an ML solution into production is Operations. (4) ML models often fail to generalize outside the training data distribution. (5) Lack of explainability and transparency of 'black-box' models and decisions hinders trust and adoption in industrial production. (6) Different formats of data pose issues during pre-processing to create a standard for later analysis (e.g., Oravizio case). (7) ML projects have created new challenges not present in traditional software development, such as tracking input data, data versions, and tuning parameters to keep production deployment up-to-date. (8) ML model performance tends to decay after model deployment due to changes in the input data stream over time (performance drift). (9) The increasing use of Deep Learning models leads to immense growth in computation and energy required, contributing to climate change.
mlops_practices: (1) Continuous Integration (CI): Automated software building and testing, including testing and validating data, data schemes, and models. Involves source code management, pushing/pulling changes to trigger builds, checking latest code and data versions, running unit tests, building/running ML models, testing and validation, packaging models, and pushing container images to a registry. (2) Continuous Delivery (CD): Ensures an application is always in a production-ready state, enabling seamless roll-out of new models with low risk using safe deployment techniques like A/B tests. Involves deploying trained models to staging environments, archiving models in a registry, and automatic activation based on schedules or production environment responses. (3) Continuous Training (CT): Automatically retraining and serving models, triggered by periodic schedules, performance degradation, or data changes. Involves strategies for how much data to use (fixed/dynamic window, representative subsamples) and what to retrain (continual vs. transfer learning, offline vs. online learning). (4) Continuous Monitoring (CM): Managing risks of in-production models by checking for performance drift, detecting outliers, and alerting operators when model accuracy drops. Monitors both technical metrics (e.g., precision, recall) and business KPIs (e.g., time-to-market, infrastructure cost, profitability). (5) Explainable AI (XAI): Promoting explainable decision-making to build user trust and improve transparency by identifying factors contributing to predictions. (6) Data Acquisition: Collecting and preparing data, including extraction, exploratory analysis, cleaning, splitting into training/validation/test sets, and feature engineering. Addresses insufficient data through data augmentation or transfer learning. (7) ML Methodology: Iterative process of selecting and testing ML algorithms, features, and hyperparameters, often starting with simpler models (bottom-up approach). (8) ML Training and Testing: Iterative process of training and optimizing models, evaluating with holdout, cross-validation, or bootstrap validation, and tracking experiments for reproducibility. (9) Sustainability/Carbon Footprint Calculation: Considering the energy and carbon footprint of DL models throughout the MLOps lifecycle (training, testing, CI, CD, CT) and adopting a bottom-up approach to model complexity to mitigate environmental impact.
gaps_identified: (1) The current literature on MLOps is still mostly disconnected and sporadic, lacking a clear and standardized methodology. (2) There is no standardized and common pipeline to follow for an end-to-end MLOps project. (3) In many ML projects, model development and operations are carried out manually, without implementing MLOps, which slows down industrialization. (4) Most ML projects focus solely on developing accurate ML models, neglecting the integration and monitoring of the ML solution in the industrial environment. (5) Researchers often define MLOps issues without involving business partners, leading to misalignment between research and the transition from research to production and model maintenance. (6) There are no other use cases available in the literature that have a clear pipeline of MLOps where the process from problem understanding to model deployment and continuous training, delivery, and monitoring is clearly explained.
key_findings: This paper proposes a taxonomy for clustering MLOps research and a ten-step methodology to standardize the release of ML applications in industry. It emphasizes that MLOps, drawing from ML, DevOps, and data engineering, is crucial for industrializing ML solutions by integrating automation, integration, and monitoring across the entire ML system lifecycle. The methodology highlights the importance of continuous practices (CI, CD, CT, CM), explainability, and sustainability, addressing the current disconnected and sporadic nature of MLOps literature. The authors stress that following well-defined guidelines is the only way to allow traceability and reproducibility of results in an Open Science context.
method: Literature review (2015-2022) to propose a taxonomy and a conceptual methodology for MLOps. The methodology is based on ten steps: business problem understanding, data acquisition, ML methodology, ML training & testing, continuous integration, continuous delivery, continuous training, continuous monitoring, explainability, and sustainability. A case study (Oravizio) is presented to illustrate a workflow.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: End-to-end MLOps lifecycle, covering: Business Problem Understanding, Data Acquisition, ML Methodology, ML Training & Testing, Continuous Integration, Continuous Delivery, Continuous Training, Continuous Monitoring, Explainability, and Sustainability.
future_research: Apply the proposed methodology to specific use cases such as biomedical imaging and finance. Conduct experimental work to test the defined pipeline. Address the remaining challenge of applying the ML methodology to an end-to-end use case, demonstrating the consequences of omitting certain phases. Further research into specific areas like biomedicine, finance, cyber-security, and manufacturing to benefit from MLOps adoption.
