title: From Ops to Experience: AIOps-Enabled MLOps with Identity-Aware and Customer-Informed Pipelines
journal: 2025 3rd World Conference on Communication and Computing, WCONF 2025
reason: The paper proposes an MLOps framework that integrates AIOps, IAM, and customer feedback loops for deploying and managing ML systems in production, specifically in SaaS-based multi-tenant environments. It discusses operational challenges and proposes solutions for reliability, security, and user alignment.
deployment_context: SaaS-based multi-tenant environments, industrial IoT (predictive maintenance scenario)
operational_constraints: (1) Lack of intelligent automation in traditional MLOps leads to operational inefficiencies. (2) Absence of robust identity-awareness results in security vulnerabilities and access misconfigurations. (3) Inadequate mechanisms for incorporating real-time customer feedback reduces model relevance over time and misaligns user experiences. (4) Observability is often treated as an afterthought, hindering rapid diagnosis and mitigation of incidents. (5) Fragmentation and siloed implementations of MLOps, AIOps, IAM, and customer feedback lead to isolated subsystems. (6) IAM solutions are tailored to IT systems but fail to capture the specific needs of ML artifacts, such as model version access or dataset lineage protection. (7) Customer feedback is often relegated to post hoc analysis rather than being embedded into model lifecycle processes or retraining criteria. (8) Managing multi-tenancy, resource isolation, and compliance-driven access control in SaaS environments.
mlops_practices: (1) AIOps Engine for intelligent monitoring, anomaly detection (using metric deviation δ(t)=|M_t −μ_t |>κ·σ_t), and self-healing capabilities, triggering auto-recovery or retraining. (2) IAM Layer for policy-based access governance, enforcing secure and compliant model lifecycle management through integrated IAM policies (P(u_j ,a_i )= allow, if r(u_j )∈R(a_i )). (3) Customer-Informed Layer embedding feedback loops from users to influence model retraining, deployment strategies, and prioritization, classifying user input using a signal weighting function (λ= Σ w_i ·I[φ(f_i )=critical]). (4) Unified Observability through a telemetry bus that uses log vectors for trace correlation (sim(L_t1 ,L_t2 )= (L_t1 ·L_t2 )/(||L_t1 ||·||L_t2 ||)) and tags each metric and log stream with a tenant UUID for cross-tenant isolation. (5) SaaS Integration with tenant-level access governed by a policy schema (Π(r,C_τ )=permit ⇐⇒ r |=C_τ) enforcing API scopes, temporal constraints, and quota enforcement. (6) Intelligent Policy-Aware Pipeline Orchestration algorithm (Algorithm 1) integrating AIOps, IAM, and feedback loops for secure and adaptive ML pipeline execution. (7) OAuth 2.0-compliant authorization gateway with token introspection and refresh mechanisms for access enforcement. (8) Tamper-evident tenant-specific audit trails for compliance with standards like SOC 2, HIPAA, and GDPR.
gaps_identified: (1) Traditional MLOps lacks robust integration with intelligent automation (AIOps), fine-grained security (IAM), and mechanisms for incorporating real-time customer feedback. (2) Existing MLOps implementations emphasize pipeline automation and CI/CD but lack robust integration with AIOps, IAM, and customer-centric engineering. (3) Most IAM solutions are tailored to IT systems but fail to capture the specific needs of ML artifacts, such as model version access or dataset lineage protection. (4) Current literature largely overlooks how customer-facing signals can be operationalized in tandem with AIOps and IAM to improve ML system responsiveness and contextual relevance. (5) Few studies address observability across both infrastructure and ML model behaviors, limiting holistic root cause analysis and adaptive response capabilities. (6) Customer feedback is often relegated to post hoc analysis rather than being embedded into model lifecycle processes or retraining criteria.
key_findings: The proposed MLOps framework, integrating AIOps, IAM, and customer-informed feedback loops, significantly enhances the reliability, security, and user alignment of ML systems in production. A simulation-based case study in predictive maintenance demonstrated over 50% improvement in Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR), measurable security gains through access anomaly detection, and increased responsiveness to end-user needs, leading to higher user satisfaction. The study highlights that each component—AIOps for intelligent automation, IAM for access governance, and customer engineering for feedback—contributes uniquely to operational robustness, establishing a strong foundation for operationalizing ML systems with both technical rigor and user-centric accountability.
method: The paper proposes a novel architectural framework and evaluates it using a simulation-based case study in a predictive maintenance scenario. The experiment involved four progressive phases: (i) Baseline MLOps, (ii) MLOps + AIOps, (iii) MLOps + AIOps + IAM, and (iv) Full-stack integration with customer-informed feedback loops. Quantitative metrics such as MTTR, Downtime Reduction, Access Anomalies Detected, Retraining Frequency, and User Satisfaction Index were tracked.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Deployment, Monitoring, Operations, Maintenance, Governance, Iteration/Retraining
future_research: (1) Integrating reinforcement learning agents into the AIOps layer for adaptive policy tuning based on evolving telemetry patterns. (2) Incorporating federated IAM policies to enhance multi-tenant support in cross-organizational deployments. (3) Exploring real-time NLP models to classify and prioritize user feedback with higher semantic precision. (4) Evaluating the framework on diverse workloads, such as large language models (LLMs) or edge ML systems, to provide broader generalizability and scalability insights.

title: Preliminary Findings on Establishing a Privately Governed Data Ecosystem for MLOps Data Sharing
journal: Proceedings - 2025 IEEE 45th International Conference on Distributed Computing Systems Workshops, ICDCSW 2025
reason: This paper directly addresses the establishment and governance of a data ecosystem for MLOps data sharing, focusing on inter-organizational collaboration and the technical and governance challenges involved in deploying and operating ML models in a shared data environment.
deployment_context: Inter-organizational data ecosystem (WARA-Ops) for MLOps data sharing, involving industry and academia, focused on data-driven operational research.
operational_constraints: ["(1) Data sensitivity and privacy: Different organizations have varying levels of data sensitivity and internal regulations, requiring careful handling to preserve privacy and integrity during sharing. This includes concerns about how data is handled between tenants and protected from platform personnel.", "(2) Legal and corporate trust: Establishing trust between different organizations and even with the platform orchestrator's personnel is a significant challenge, requiring transparent mechanisms and potentially auditability.", "(3) Data ownership: Legal challenges related to data ownership arise when sharing data between businesses (B2B) and businesses-to-customers (B2C).", "(4) Performance impact on embedded products: Data collection from products and services can have performance implications on embedded systems.", "(5) Lack of established 'match-making' channels: Participants may lose interest if they cannot find the right collaborators for their data and problems, highlighting a need for mechanisms to connect data owners with researchers.", "(6) Cost of data collection and preprocessing: High costs associated with collecting, cleaning, and labeling data necessitate data sharing and reuse, but this introduces its own set of challenges.", "(7) Traceability to training data: Federated learning, while a technical solution for distributed data, makes traceability to training data difficult, which is an upcoming requirement for many applications.", "(8) Lack of adequate tool support and infrastructure: Current tools and infrastructure are often insufficient to facilitate inter-organizational data sharing effectively.", "(9) Need for specific governance mechanisms per data source: Organizations require the ability to set specific agreements or licenses for their data, implying that a one-size-fits-all governance model is insufficient."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) Data catalog with restricted access: The WARA-Ops platform provides a catalog of shared data where access is controlled.", "(2) Sandbox environment (Jupyter Notebook): Users are provided with a sandbox in the form of Jupyter Notebook for data exploration and experimentation.", "(3) Metadata presence: Emphasized as important for data quality and structure, even in early development stages, to facilitate data understanding and reuse.", "(4) Community building and networking: The platform aims to foster collaboration and 'match-making' between data owners and researchers to facilitate data sharing and problem-solving.", "(5) Auditability: Mentioned as a potential mechanism to help with legal and corporate trust, suggesting a need for logging and tracking data access and usage.", "(6) Flexible governance handling: The platform needs to support setting specific governance rules and licenses for different data sources to satisfy various organizational requirements.", "(7) Scalable data storage and processing: The underlying infrastructure is designed for scalable storage, annotation, processing, and visualization of large operational datasets.", "(8) Support for collaborative work: The platform aims to provide support for users to interact and work on common challenges."]
gaps_identified: ["Knowledge on governance for inter-organizational data sharing is still lacking.", "Availability of adequate tool support and infrastructure to facilitate data sharing is insufficient.", "Current tools and frameworks do not sufficiently address the need for transparent and auditable data handling mechanisms to build trust between organizations and platform providers.", "Existing solutions struggle with providing flexible governance mechanisms that allow for specific agreements and licenses for different data sources within a shared ecosystem.", "There is a gap in 'match-making' channels to effectively connect data owners with researchers for collaboration, leading to disengagement if not addressed.", "Federated learning, while addressing distributed data, makes traceability to training data difficult, which is a growing requirement."]
key_findings: This study investigates the establishment and governance of the WARA-Ops data ecosystem for MLOps data sharing through a case study involving interviews and observational studies. Key findings highlight that incentives for participation revolve around improving technological maturity and community building, with business gains being secondary. Governance is heavily influenced by trust, especially in early stages, but there's a strong need for transparent, auditable, and flexible legal frameworks to manage data sensitivity and ownership. Technically, users prioritize simple yet powerful tools, including compute resources (GPUs), ease of use, metadata, and ML-specific functionalities like LLM APIs and MLOps for model/data control. The research provides preliminary advice for practitioners on tackling governance obstacles and for researchers on addressing legal alignment and collaboration in Open Data Ecosystems.
method: Qualitative Case Study (Semi-structured interviews, observational study of technical infrastructure, validation survey at a seminar)
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Data Management, Governance, Deployment, Monitoring (implicitly through data sharing for training/testing)
future_research: ["Interview further actors within the WARA-Ops ecosystem and conduct more interview rounds with platform developers and orchestrators.", "Compare WARA-Ops against other data ecosystems of different maturity with a similar focus on industry-academia data sharing and research collaboration.", "Design a model that describes a set of requirements across different domains to satisfy Open Data Ecosystem participants' needs.", "Perform a multiple case study exploring purely the technological side of Open Data Ecosystems, assessing a variety of tools and techniques.", "Address the problem of generalizing and defining a set of governance requirements for an efficient Open Data Ecosystem by exploring more ecosystem cases of different sizes and maturity."]

title: From XAI to MLOps: Explainable concept drift detection with the partial dependence profiles-based approach
journal: Future Generation Computer Systems
reason: This paper proposes a novel concept drift detection method (PDD) that leverages explainable AI (Partial Dependence Profiles) to monitor and understand model degradation in production environments. It directly addresses model monitoring and adaptation, which are core MLOps concerns.
evidence_level: Experimental results on synthetic and real-world datasets (SEA, Hyperplane, NOAA, Ozone, Elec2, Friedman) comparing PDD with six other drift detection methods (HDDM-A, HDDM-W, KSWIN, PH, DDM, EDDM).
deployment_context: Generic AI Implementation (standard software-centric ML deployment contexts)
operational_constraints: (1) Traditional drift detection methods often rely on metrics such as accuracy or marginal variable distributions, which may fail to capture subtle but important conceptual changes, especially when concept drift occurs without performance and distribution metrics changes. (2) Current data drift detection methods often rely solely on performance and distribution metrics, which are insufficient to understand the underlying cause of changes in the model. (3) High computational cost for methods like permutation-based variable importance or SHAP, making them impractical for detecting changes in the relationship between response and explanatory variables in streaming data. (4) Infeasible use for multi-class classification for PDD, as calculating derivatives can be challenging for categorical variables with a small number of levels. (5) Increased computational cost for PDD when the number of explanatory variables increases, as it may be necessary to detect concept drift based on other explanatory variables beyond just the most important one. (6) PDD can be more affected by small changes in sparse vectors because its three metrics are averaged across all selected observation points, potentially leading to disproportionate PDI values.
mlops_practices: (1) Continuous model monitoring: The proposed PDD method, along with other compared methods, continuously monitors data streams for concept drift. (2) Adaptive retraining: When concept drift is detected by PDD, the new data batch is added to the training set, and the model is retrained to adapt to the new concept. (3) Explainable drift detection: PDD leverages Partial Dependence Profiles (PDPs) to not only detect concept drift but also provide interpretable insights into which variables drive the drift and how their relationship with the target variable evolves. (4) Variable importance tracking: The method identifies and tracks the most important variable using permutation importance, which is then used for PDP calculation and drift detection.
gaps_identified: (1) Current data drift detection methods are insufficient to understand the underlying cause of changes in the model, as they rely solely on performance and distribution metrics. (2) Existing XAI tools often face challenges in some data drifts or rely on high computational cost methods (e.g., permutation-based variable importance or SHAP) that are not always practical for detecting changes in the relationship between response and explanatory variables in streaming data. (3) The inability of some XAI tools (like SHAP) to distinguish between covariate and concept drift due to inappropriate use. (4) Lack of studies focusing on the causes and explainability of concept drift detection methods. (5) Traditional statistical tests for concept drift operate solely based on data distribution and may lead to false alarms. (6) Traditional methods fail to reflect changes in the model’s information utilization when concept drift occurs without performance and distribution metrics changes. (7) Existing software and technologies for data drift detection are complex to implement and quite limited, often not providing specific instructions for model monitoring.
key_findings: The Profile Drift Detection (PDD) method effectively balances sensitivity and stability in concept drift detection while maintaining high predictive performance across various synthetic and real-world datasets. Unlike black-box methods, PDD provides interpretable insights into the variables driving the drift and how their relationships with the target variable evolve, enabling a deeper understanding of model degradation. While PDD generally detected fewer but more consistent drifts compared to highly sensitive methods like KSWIN and EDDM (which risk more false positives), its performance can be sensitive to batch size and model overfitting, particularly in complex models like Random Forests. A crucial finding is the consistent identification of key variables influencing model predictions and drift detection across datasets, highlighting the importance of focused variable analysis in dynamic environments.
method: The paper proposes Profile Drift Detection (PDD), a novel concept drift detection method based on Explainable AI (XAI) techniques, specifically Partial Dependence Profiles (PDPs). PDD quantifies changes in PDPs using three complementary metrics: Partial Dependence Index (PDI), L2 distance, and L2Der (L2 distance of derivatives). These metrics are calculated from PDPs of train/test sets and compared against new incoming data batches. If all three metrics exceed predefined thresholds, concept drift is detected, triggering model retraining. The method was evaluated through six experiments on synthetic (SEA, Hyperplane, Friedman) and real-world (NOAA, Ozone, Elec2) datasets, comparing PDD's performance (accuracy, number of drifts) against six traditional drift detection methods (HDDM-A, HDDM-W, KSWIN, PH, DDM, EDDM) using Logistic Regression, Decision Tree, and Random Forest models.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Monitoring, Retraining, Governance (Explainability)
future_research: (1) Reducing the computational cost of PDD by integrating techniques like 'compress then explain' for large datasets. (2) Using PDD to monitor how the relationship between the response variable and explanatory variable changes when concept drift is detected, especially when model training costs are lower than PDP generation costs. (3) Applying PDD in predictive churn and data privacy applications to make changes explainable after retraining an ML model.

title: InsectNet: Real-time identification of insects using an end-to-end machine learning pipeline
journal: PNAS Nexus
reason: The paper describes the development and deployment of InsectNet, an end-to-end ML pipeline for real-time insect identification, explicitly discussing MLOps practices for model training, inference, and democratization, including continuous monitoring and updates.
deployment_context: Agricultural pest management, biodiversity monitoring, citizen science platforms, museum collections, biosecurity applications (ports of entry, geographic borders). The system is deployed as a publicly available web application accessible via computer or mobile device.
operational_constraints: ["(1) Need to handle intraspecies dissimilarity and interspecies similarity, life-cycle stages, camouflage, diverse imaging conditions, and variability in insect orientation in real-world conditions.", "(2) Scarcity of labeled data for specific insect species or regions, making it expensive and time-consuming to generate expert-verified datasets for fine-tuning.", "(3) Data imbalance challenges, where some insect species have significantly fewer images (e.g., 38 images) compared to others (e.g., 136,000 images), complicating deep learning model training.", "(4) Noisy or inaccurate labels in large-scale biological datasets (like iNaturalist 'research grade' identifications) that are not always expert-vetted.", "(5) The need for high trustworthiness in predictions, especially in critical applications like biosecurity, where misclassifications can have significant ecological and economic consequences.", "(6) Challenges in distinguishing visually similar species with subtle morphological differences and taxonomic ambiguities (e.g., certain damselflies, butterflies, bumblebees) from photographs alone.", "(7) Inconsistent name assignments in public databases further complicate classification and high-confidence predictions."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) End-to-end ML pipeline: InsectNet is designed as a three-component pipeline covering training, inference, and democratization, leveraging MLOps for seamless and efficient deployment.", "(2) Global-to-local model strategy: A global model is initially trained using a large dataset (citizen science data, self-supervised learning) and then fine-tuned with smaller, expert-verified regional datasets to create local insect identification models.", "(3) Self-supervised pretraining (SSL): Used to learn robust low-dimensional feature spaces from unlabeled data, making the model resilient to noisy labels and reducing the need for extensive labeled datasets during initial training.", "(4) Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection: An energy-based metric is wrapped around the classifier to flag images that deviate significantly from the training data distribution, preventing predictions on low-resolution, blurred, or confusing images.", "(5) Conformal prediction: Used to produce prediction sets (rather than a single species category) with rigorously guaranteed confidence (e.g., ≥97.5%), providing a graceful way for human intervention when the model is uncertain.", "(6) Continuous monitoring and maintenance: Practices are integrated to ensure the web app's reliability and performance, creating a dynamic system that adapts to emerging challenges and evolving insect classifications.", "(7) Periodic model updates: The model's backbone is periodically updated to accommodate additional insect species and taxonomic changes.", "(8) Data wrangling workflow tool (iNatSD): Developed to allow users to intuitively download customizable datasets of high-quality images in an ML-analysis-ready format, streamlining data preparation.", "(9) Democratized access: The classifier, trained model weights, and MLOps workflows are made publicly available through a web-based portal (https://insectapp.las.iastate.edu) to enable community adoption.", "(10) Uncertainty quantification (UQ): Incorporated alongside OOD detection to flag potentially unreliable classifications, allowing for further expert validation when necessary."]
gaps_identified: ["Current image-based insect identification tools have a narrow scope of datasets, covering only a few insect species, habitats, and regions.", "Existing methods struggle with unbalanced datasets, unidentified insect species within geographic areas, and challenging scenarios like overlapping insects, morphologically similar species, and intraspecies variations.", "Many current applications lack extensive documentation of their accuracy or require payment for access to certain features, hindering widespread adoption and trust.", "The inherent difficulty in achieving high classification accuracy for species with overlapping morphological traits, even with advanced models, highlights a need for more nuanced data or methods.", "Inconsistent name assignments in public databases complicate classification and high-confidence predictions, indicating a need for better data standardization or handling of such inconsistencies."]
key_findings: InsectNet, an end-to-end ML pipeline, achieves over 96% classification accuracy for 2,526 agriculturally and ecologically relevant insect species by leveraging a global-to-local fine-tuning strategy with self-supervised pretraining on large, noisy datasets. The system incorporates out-of-distribution detection and conformal prediction to enhance trustworthiness and provide uncertainty quantification, allowing for human intervention in critical scenarios. This approach significantly outperforms models trained from scratch, especially in data-scarce regional contexts, demonstrating the efficiency of leveraging global models for specialized tasks and democratizing access through a web-based application and open MLOps workflows.
method: The study employs a system development and empirical evaluation methodology. It involves developing a deep learning model (InsectNet) using self-supervised learning (SSL) for pretraining on large, noisy datasets (iNaturalist, Instagram hashtags), followed by fine-tuning on smaller, expert-verified datasets. The model's performance is quantitatively evaluated across various challenging scenarios (e.g., low sample size, intraspecies dissimilarity, interspecies similarity, camouflage, sexual dimorphism, varying orientation, multiple insects in frame) using metrics like classification accuracy and mean per-class accuracy (MPCA). The system also integrates out-of-distribution detection and conformal prediction for robustness and trustworthiness. The developed system is deployed as a public web application.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Data Collection & Preparation, Model Training & Evaluation, Model Deployment, Model Monitoring & Maintenance, Data Feedback Loop, Model Governance
future_research: ["Implement an ensemble approach to enhance confidence scoring, along with a qualitative confidence scale that leverages existing OOD and UQ to improve transparency and user trust.", "Collect more images for underrepresented or misclassified species, especially those capturing finer details that distinguish visually similar species, to improve model accuracy.", "Extend the work to insect counting rather than just classification, with applications in crop production and plant breeding.", "Better integrate these technologies in integrated pest management (IPM) and climate-smart pest management (CSPM)."]

title: Agile MLOps: Bridging the Gap Between Agility and Machine Learning Operations
journal: IFIP Advances in Information and Communication Technology
reason: The paper discusses the integration of Agile methodologies with MLOps to improve the deployment, monitoring, and management of ML models in production, using a case study to illustrate the concepts.
deployment_context: Generic AI Implementation (Web App for Steak Fat Analysis)
operational_constraints: (1) Traditional software development methodologies struggle with the iterative, nonlinear nature of ML workflows, leading to inefficiencies and slow delivery cycles. (2) Ensuring ML models can handle growing data volumes while maintaining performance requires structured processes and automation. (3) ML projects often face shifting requirements due to changing datasets or evolving business goals.
mlops_practices: (1) Iterative Model Development: Teams use sprints to train and validate models on subsets of data, refining them incrementally and deploying minimum viable models (MVMs) for early feedback. (2) CI/CD Pipelines: Automation of unit tests for model performance, data validation checks, and deployment workflows to allow rapid iteration and rollback. (3) Cross-Functional Collaboration: Agile ceremonies like sprint planning and retrospectives facilitate teamwork among data scientists, engineers, and business stakeholders. (4) Continuous Monitoring: Monitoring model performance to identify when retraining or updating is necessary. (5) MLOps Maturity Model: Organizations adopt a maturity model, starting with manual processes and gradually integrating CI/CD, continuous testing, and automated monitoring. (6) Tooling: Use of JIRA for Scrum activities, Slack for real-time communication, GitHub for version control, MLflow for experiment tracking and model versioning, and Google Colab for model development.
gaps_identified: (1) Many organizations adopt fragmented strategies for Agile-MLOps integration, lacking a unified, lifecycle-spanning framework. (2) A unified framework is needed that links Agile iteration with operational ML tasks.
key_findings: This paper demonstrates the synergy between Agile methodologies (specifically Scrum) and MLOps in managing ML models in production. The case study of the Steak Fat Analysis Web App illustrates how Scrum sprints enable continuous progress, from prototype development to advancing automation, including CI/CD pipelines and performance monitoring. The integration of Agile principles into MLOps maturity models fosters innovation, flexibility, and operational efficiency, supported by tools like JIRA, Slack, and MLflow. This approach allows organizations to optimize ML workflows and respond effectively to evolving business needs.
method: Case Study (Steak Fat Analysis Web App) and Literature Review
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Full ML Lifecycle (Development, Deployment, Monitoring, Retraining, Governance)
future_research: The paper implicitly suggests future work in further developing and validating unified, lifecycle-spanning frameworks for Agile-MLOps integration, particularly in achieving higher levels of automation (CI/CD/CT -Monitoring-Alerting) as outlined in the maturity progression.

title: Smart manufacturing: MLOps-enabled event-driven architecture for enhanced control in steel production
journal: Journal of Systems and Software
reason: The paper details an MLOps-enabled event-driven architecture for deploying and operating DRL models to control steel production, explicitly discussing operational constraints, MLOps practices, and gaps in applying standard solutions to heavy industry.
deployment_context: Industrial AI (Steel Production, Induction Furnace Control)
operational_constraints: ["(1) Stringent security requirements: Traditional off-the-shelf cloud solutions are not feasible due to security concerns in industrial settings.", "(2) High latency requirements: Temperature power adjustments need to be applied quickly due to rod movement and potential overheating, necessitating an edge-computing approach to minimize control latency.", "(3) Integration with legacy systems: Existing steel production infrastructure often relies on legacy systems (e.g., Siemens PLCs, OPC-UA servers), presenting considerable technical challenges for integrating modern EDAs and ML solutions.", "(4) Complex, non-linear dynamics: Traditional control systems struggle with the complex, non-linear dynamics of industrial heating processes, requiring advanced ML/DRL algorithms.", "(5) Noisy industrial environments: Robust learning frameworks are needed to operate reliably in noisy industrial environments.", "(6) Narrow forging temperature window: Maintaining steel within a very narrow temperature window is critical; under- or overheating leads to poor-quality products and scrap, especially during warmholding or plant restarts.", "(7) Data volume and variety: The sheer volume and variety of data generated in modern steel plants require sophisticated data processing and analysis pipelines.", "(8) 24/7 production environment: Ensuring the reliability and performance of data pipelines and control systems in a continuous 24/7 production environment is a significant challenge.", "(9) Air-gapped/limited connectivity: The need for edge deployment implies limited or no continuous cloud connectivity for model registries or updates during production shifts, requiring local model management.", "(10) Manual operator reliance: Some main processes, like heating steel bars, still depend on manual adjustment by experienced operators, leading to inconsistencies and challenges during non-standard modes (e.g., warmholding)."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) MLOps-driven architecture: A structured framework is used to manage the entire lifecycle of the control software, including ML solutions, ensuring seamless management of ML models throughout their lifecycle.", "(2) Edge-compute platform: A micro-service edge-compute platform is deployed to ingest real-time sensor data and host the digital twin and DRL agent, minimizing control latency and addressing security concerns.", "(3) Event-Driven Microservices Architecture (EDMA): Utilizes microservices with small, fast-executing tasks and a publisher/subscriber model (via Apache Kafka) for real-time data processing and control, ensuring low latency.", "(4) Digital Twin (DT) integration: A DT serves as a virtual model of the physical furnace, providing a platform for implementing and training DRL algorithms and synchronizing with the physical furnace for real-time control.", "(5) Real-time data ingestion: Sensor data from PLCs are collected via an OPC-UA server and pushed to a Kafka message broker for real-time processing by the EDMA.", "(6) Model wrapping for deployment: Exported DRL models are wrapped with layers that translate common input to model-specific input and output to common output, unifying the interface for deployment regardless of inner model details.", "(7) Unified model interface: A standardized interface for DRL models (23 input features including 18 temperatures and 5 power values, 20 output features for probability distributions of power changes) is used to facilitate deployment and management.", "(8) Model normalization and feature engineering: The inner logic of deployed models includes removing unnecessary input features, normalizing input features to [0, 1], and interpolating virtual sensor temperatures.", "(9) Hot-swapping of algorithms: The microservice API allows for dynamic selection and uploading of new algorithm versions to Algorithm storage without requiring system rebooting.", "(10) Dedicated storage services: Redis (for cache, power control config, forge sensors storage), Apache Cassandra (for telemetry storage), and file system (for algorithm storage) are used to meet specific data storage and access requirements.", "(11) Performance monitoring: Processing times for microservices (Telemetry Parser, Power Control, Data Manager) are continuously monitored against expected thresholds to ensure real-time control and system stability.", "(12) Hyperparameter search and optimization: Comprehensive hyperparameter searches are conducted for DRL algorithms (DQN, PPO) to identify optimal configurations for different scenarios and reward functions.", "(13) Reproducibility via random seeds: Random seeds are used for pseudo-random number generation in libraries to ensure reproducibility of training jobs.", "(14) Model maintenance and management updates: Crucial MLOps aspects are addressed, including how models are loaded, updated, and managed with multiple versions, ensuring the virtual representation remains synchronized with the physical furnace."]
gaps_identified: ["Standard, commercialized cloud solutions are challenging for industrial control applications due to security concerns and high latency requirements.", "Comprehensive studies showcasing results and lessons learned from implementing customized MLOps solutions in heavy industries like steel production are notably lacking.", "Adapting MLOps practices, typically used in software development, to the unique requirements of steel manufacturing processes is a significant challenge.", "Integrating Event-Driven Architectures (EDAs) with existing steel production infrastructure, which often relies on legacy systems, presents considerable technical challenges.", "Combining MLOps and EDA in steel production control systems introduces new complexities in data management, model deployment, and system maintenance.", "Direct quantification of waste reduction in industrial settings like steel production is difficult due to production priorities limiting experimental control groups, operational constraints restricting prolonged data collection, resource limitations affecting sensor deployment, and proprietary concerns.", "The application of DRL in industrial settings is still evolving, with challenges in formulating appropriate data-driven algorithms for high-dimensional, non-linear dynamics, developing robust learning frameworks for noisy environments, and integrating advanced algorithms with existing control systems.", "Making DRL model decisions more interpretable to human operators through explainable AI techniques is an area requiring future research to enhance trust and facilitate wider adoption."]
key_findings: The paper presents an MLOps-enabled event-driven architecture for DRL-based control in steel production, demonstrating significant potential for waste reduction and quality improvement. The system, built on a micro-service edge-compute platform and a digital twin, effectively manages real-time sensor data and optimizes induction furnace heating. Empirical evaluation shows that microservices operate within strict timeframes (e.g., Telemetry Parser at 4.622ms vs. 5ms max, Power Control at 4.055ms vs. 1000ms max), with network latency being a primary factor for Data Manager's higher processing time (9.922ms vs. 1000ms max). The DRL models, particularly PPO with a symmetric reward function, achieved high scores in maintaining desired temperature profiles, indicating improved efficiency and reduced material waste in both normal production and warmholding scenarios.

title: Streamlining the Operation of AI Systems: Examining MLOps Maturity at an Automotive Firm
journal: Proceedings of the Annual Hawaii International Conference on System Sciences
reason: The paper presents a case study on MLOps maturity in an automotive firm, directly addressing the deployment, operation, and maintenance of ML/AI models in a production environment. It discusses specific challenges and practices related to MLOps.

operational_constraints: ["(1) Current industry standards for quality assurance do not recommend specific measures for ML systems, requiring significant upfront effort to collect and develop verification criteria and high-quality test datasets.", "(2) Setting up integration tests for ML systems is challenging, especially when integrating with physical hardware (e.g., camera hardware in the loop for image data).", "(3) Difficulty in configuring the sensitivity of monitoring to detect relevant variations in time without triggering a flood of false alarms.", "(4) Lack of appropriate MLOps tools on the market that fit domain-specific requirements for monitoring, leading to in-house development (e.g., for data drift detection in manufacturing image data).", "(5) Obtaining ground-truth feedback on the actual real-world performance of the ML system is challenging, especially when unique, accurate sensor measurements are not available, relying instead on manual user feedback via service desks.", "(6) Streamlining the labeling of new data for retraining is difficult, particularly when labeling is non-trivial, requires domain knowledge, and involves determining which data to label and the extent of business involvement.", "(7) External development and operation of ML systems (ML-P2) can limit seamless access to traceability and reproducibility information for the internal organization.", "(8) High inherent complexity of the underlying ML model (e.g., deep learning on unstructured image data) makes automating data preparation and annotations more challenging and complicates debugging and error tracing due to model opacity.", "(9) High environmental complexity, especially in early project phases, makes it difficult to stabilize ML systems in dynamic production environments, as novel environmental parameters often require model or system modifications.", "(10) Idiosyncrasy of the use case decreases the likelihood of finding suitable off-the-shelf MLOps solutions, research, or best practices, forcing custom development (e.g., for data drift detection or ML system verification in quality assurance)."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) Automated ML training and testing pipelines are implemented, with some projects (ML-P3) achieving fully automated deployment.", "(2) Manual deployment of ML models is used in projects (ML-P1, ML-P2) during stabilization phases where frequent adjustments are needed.", "(3) Regular collection of measures for ML system performance (e.g., logging prediction confidence) is performed, with manual checks by ML engineers.", "(4) In-house solutions are developed for advanced monitoring, such as data drift detection in manufacturing image data (ML-P1), due to a lack of suitable commercial tools.", "(5) Established standard processes are used to integrate business experts for streamlining new data preparation and labeling for retraining (ML-P1).", "(6) Exploration of active learning is considered to proactively select new training data that maximizes learning benefits (ML-P1).", "(7) Tools for versioning ML-specific artifacts and experiment tracking are applied to ensure traceability and reproducibility of ML systems.", "(8) A group-wide computer vision platform is used to achieve synergies by sharing trained ML models and other ML artifacts across projects."]
gaps_identified: ["Current industry standards for quality assurance lack specific measures for ML systems, creating a gap in standardized verification processes.", "The market for MLOps tools is still evolving and heterogeneous, leading to a lack of appropriate off-the-shelf solutions for domain-specific monitoring requirements (e.g., data drift detection in manufacturing image data).", "There is a gap in automated, 'intelligent monitoring' solutions that can automatically raise alarms based on critical deviations without triggering false alarms, requiring manual checks.", "A lack of common standards for MLOps tools and processes hinders systematic exchange of ML artifacts (models, features, data) between projects, limiting synergy leverage.", "Existing MLOps tools and practices struggle with the inherent complexity of deep learning models, particularly in debugging and error tracing due to their opaque nature.", "MLOps frameworks do not adequately address the challenges of deploying ML systems to multiple locations with varying environmental, data, and predictive conditions."]
key_findings: The study identifies several factors influencing MLOps maturity in ML projects at an automotive firm, ranging from laborious manual deployments to fully automated ML pipelines. Key facilitating factors include organizational MLOps experience, appropriateness of existing MLOps tools, and business criticality of the use case. Inhibiting factors include the ML model’s inherent complexity, insufficient quality of new data, environmental complexity, and the idiosyncrasy of the use case. The research highlights that MLOps maturity typically grows over time and that human users remain vital in maintaining ML systems despite increasing automation.
method: Qualitative Case Study (Yin, 2018) with 17 semi-structured interviews across three ML projects at an automotive firm, supplemented by biweekly project meetings. Data analysis involved initial coding, organizing findings using MLOps principles (continuous training/integration/deployment, monitoring/feedback, traceability/reproducibility), assessing maturity levels, and identifying influencing factors.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Deployment, Operations, Monitoring, Maintenance, Governance (Traceability/Reproducibility)
future_research: ["Further explore project-inherent factors as contingencies in ML projects.", "Investigate the role of human users in MLOps.", "Examine the process of how MLOps matures within projects and organizations."]

title: Seamless Transition From Machine Learning on the Cloud to Industrial Edge Devices With Thinger.io
journal: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
reason: The paper presents an MLOps workflow for deploying ML models to industrial edge devices, addressing the challenges of transitioning from cloud training to edge deployment in Industry 4.0 environments. It discusses operational constraints, specific MLOps practices, and identifies gaps in existing solutions.
deployment_context: Industrial IoT (Anomaly Detection on Temperature/Humidity Sensors), Edge Computing, Hybrid Cloud-Edge
operational_constraints: ["(1) Edge devices are limited in bandwidth, storage, and processing power, making it impractical to perform complex ML training directly on them.", "(2) Running ML processes exclusively on the cloud can lead to long latencies in industrial IoT environments.", "(3) Large amounts of data must be transmitted to the cloud for analysis, incurring bandwidth costs and potential bottlenecks.", "(4) Strict security policies in industrial settings may not allow exposing sensitive information to the cloud.", "(5) Connections between local devices and the cloud can be unreliable in industrial environments.", "(6) Deploying ML solutions on the edge is not trivial due to few available tools for the cyber-physicality of IoT systems.", "(7) Manual transfer of trained ML models to IT Ops teams for deployment is inefficient and does not scale, especially for multiple edge devices or frequent updates.", "(8) Creating variations of the same model or new models often involves manual re-running of pipelines or copying/pasting code, which is inefficient.", "(9) Changing data source configurations or common pipeline parts requires manual updates across related scripts, which is time-consuming and error-prone.", "(10) Lack of active performance monitoring once a model is deployed on edge devices makes it difficult to detect performance degradation or deviations.", "(11) Existing MLOps platforms (TFX, ModelOps, Kubeflow) are often limited to a single ML framework, require cloud execution, or complex environments (e.g., Kubernetes), leading to high setup costs and limited on-premise applicability.", "(12) Edge devices in industrial settings typically use ARM64 architecture with Linux, requiring ML models to be compiled for the specific target architecture."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) Automated ML Training Pipeline: ML scripts are split into reusable, composable, and independent components (data extraction, preparation, training, evaluation, serving, monitoring).", "(2) Containerization for ML Pipeline Steps: Each ML pipeline step is executed within a Docker container, allowing definition of specific ML frameworks, dependencies, and library versions (e.g., r-base:4.2.1 image for R scripts).", "(3) Hybrid Cloud-Edge Architecture: Cloud is used for computationally expensive ML training and validation, while edge devices are used for executing the generated models.", "(4) Automated ML Deployment Pipeline: A pipeline is defined to automatically pack, publish, and deploy trained models to edge devices or other cloud services, triggered by events like successful model validation.", "(5) ML Service Image: The final artifact of the deployment pipeline is a complete Docker image (ML Service Image) containing the base ML framework, trained model, default execution parameters, and prediction service scripts, stored in an image registry.", "(6) Multi-architecture Image Building: Docker BuildX is used to simultaneously build images for multiple target architectures (e.g., linux/amd64 for cloud, linux/arm64 for edge devices).", "(7) Edge Device Agent for Dynamic Updates: A Thinger.io agent running on the edge device is responsible for initializing and dynamically updating ML environments by pulling new ML Service Images from the registry and binding device resources to the exposed ML service.", "(8) Low-Code Pipeline Editor: An ML workspace provides a graphical user interface to design and execute ML pipelines using drag-and-drop predefined nodes for data extraction, step execution, image building, deployment, and notifications.", "(9) REST API for Model Serving: Trained models are exposed as prediction services via REST APIs, allowing consumption by other applications or services on both cloud and edge.", "(10) Time-Series Buckets: IoT device data is stored in time-series databases, enabling ML scripts to query data by date, apply aggregations, and filter by sensor ID or asset location.", "(11) File Storages: ML scripts and intermediary artifacts are stored in native file storages managed by the platform.", "(12) Metadata Stores: ML scripts can obtain running parameters and configurations from generic metadata stores, accessible via REST API or environment variables.", "(13) Event-Driven Triggers: An event manager can trigger ML pipelines based on changes in scripts, performance degradation, or manual triggers.", "(14) Integrated Monitoring: Thinger.io's monitoring capabilities are used to ingest ML model performance data, create dashboards, and generate alerts or events based on custom conditions, enabling automatic ML retraining triggers."]
gaps_identified: ["There is a large difference between designing and training an ML model, potentially in the cloud, to create ML services that can be deployed and consumed on the edge.", "Deploying ML solutions on the edge is not trivial because there are few tools available for facing the cyber-physicality of IoT systems in industrial environments.", "Current MLOps platforms (TFX, ModelOps, Kubeflow, Apache Airflow, Amazon SageMaker) suffer from multiple constraints: they are limited to a single ML framework; they require a cloud to be executed and so cannot be executed on-premise servers; and they require complex environments for its execution (i.e., Kubernetes). Thus, setup costs are high, or solutions are not sufficiently open and lack good community support rather than technical support, which limits their applicability in scientific and academic environments.", "Those existing MLOps approaches are not focused on cloud-edge collaboration and thus do not provide a complete solution including automatic edge deployment.", "The deployment of ML models to edge devices is typically performed manually, and little effort has been made to scale and automate this process."]
key_findings: This article proposes an MLOps workflow within the Thinger.io IoT platform to streamline the transition from ML model training in the cloud to automated deployment on industrial edge devices. The workflow integrates automated training and deployment pipelines using Docker containers for modularity and portability across heterogeneous edge architectures (e.g., ARM64). Performance tests show that the proposed workflow adds minimal overhead (all steps under a minute for the anomaly detection use case) and achieves high throughput for edge-deployed ML services (207.45 requests/second), demonstrating a reliable, reusable, and productive environment for Industrial AI.
method: The paper proposes a novel MLOps workflow and framework. It then demonstrates its application through a real-world use case: an anomaly detection service for temperature and humidity sensors using PCA. The performance of the workflow steps (training, building, pushing, notifying, pulling, running) and the deployed ML service throughput are empirically evaluated on a cloud server and a Raspberry Pi-based industrial IoT edge device.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Data Ingestion, Data Preprocessing, Model Training, Model Evaluation, Model Deployment, Model Monitoring, Continuous Integration (CI), Continuous Delivery (CD), Continuous Training (CT)
future_research: Future work includes applying this approach to other paradigms like fog computing, integrating decentralized ML methods (federated learning), adding support for building ML models for more heterogeneous devices (GPUs, TPUs, microcontrollers), and defining a monitoring pipeline to evaluate ML model performance and establish retraining criteria.

title: Towards secure digital media: A drift-aware MLOps framework for adaptive stego content sterilization
journal: Future Generation Computer Systems
reason: The paper proposes a drift-aware MLOps framework for adaptive stego content sterilization, explicitly discussing continuous monitoring, autonomous retraining, and stable long-term deployment of ML models in production, directly addressing AI deployment and operations.
deployment_context: Cloud-scale and adversarial multimedia environments for stego content sterilization. The system is deployed within a Kubernetes environment using TensorFlow Serving.
operational_constraints: ["(1) Evolving embedding strategies: Steganography algorithms continuously change, making static detection models ineffective over time.", "(2) Non-stationary data distributions: The characteristics of digital media and embedded content shift, leading to concept drift.", "(3) Adversarial environments: Attackers actively try to bypass detection, requiring adaptive models.", "(4) Need for low-latency inference: Real-time stego content sterilization requires fast model predictions.", "(5) Catastrophic forgetting: Frequent retraining on new data can degrade performance on previously learned steganography patterns.", "(6) Resource constraints: Scaling inference throughput in Kubernetes environments can lead to saturation due to shared GPU and system resources.", "(7) Trade-off between removal and preservation: Aggressive payload removal risks perceptual degradation of clean images, especially at very low payload conditions where embedded signals are statistically indistinguishable from natural image variations."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) Data Management: Collects, annotates, tags, and versions clean and stego-embedded image datasets from heterogeneous sources, stored in cloud object storage.", "(2) Pipeline Orchestration: Aggregates data ingestion, validation, and retraining workflows, structured to preserve residual responses across spatial scales for selective retraining.", "(3) Model Tracking and Versioning: Logs each training iteration with configuration, residual suppression behavior, and evaluation metrics using MLflow, retaining model versions for rollback and analysis.", "(4) Model Registry: Centralized management of successive MSRFS-Net versions with metadata on training data, architectural updates, and validation outcomes.", "(5) Model Serving and Deployment: Supports low-latency inference, scalable execution, and seamless model replacement using TensorFlow Serving in a Kubernetes environment.", "(6) Drift Monitoring and Triggering: Detects performance degradation using a Combined Drift Score (CDS) that integrates metric drift (perceptual quality, stego removal effectiveness) and statistical drift (feature distribution changes) to trigger autonomous retraining.", "(7) Automated Retraining: Triggered when CDS exceeds a predefined threshold for a set number of consecutive monitoring windows, using a controlled data replay strategy to mitigate catastrophic forgetting.", "(8) Temporal Smoothing: Exponential moving averages are applied to drift signals to reduce sensitivity to transient fluctuations and short-term noise.", "(9) Adaptive Binning: Feature-wise adaptive binning is used for statistical drift computation to capture non-linear shifts in residual energy and edge-related feature densities.", "(10) Temporally Weighted Distributions: Applied to emphasize recent observations in statistical drift computation."]
gaps_identified: ["Existing drift monitoring often remains largely observational, with limited integration into autonomous retraining policies, which is critical in adversarial and long-running multimedia security pipelines.", "Improving model architecture alone is insufficient to maintain reliable stego content sterilization in adversarial environments; it requires a systematic framework for continuous data ingestion, performance monitoring, and adaptive retraining.", "Many existing MLOps pipelines remain only partially automated, with drift monitoring often treated as an observational component rather than an actionable trigger for adaptation."]
key_findings: The paper proposes a drift-aware MLOps framework for adaptive stego content sterilization, integrating a multi-scale residual feature sterilization network (MSRFS-Net) with continuous monitoring and autonomous retraining. The Combined Drift Score (CDS), which unifies performance-based metric drift and statistical distributional shift, effectively triggers retraining, leading to sustained sterilization performance. Experimental results show that MLOps-driven adaptation stabilizes performance under drift, yielding up to 10%–15% improvements in perceptual quality and 20%–25% gains in stego removal efficacy across diverse datasets and steganographic algorithms. This transforms stego content sterilization from static deployment into a resilient, continuously evolving framework suitable for cloud-scale and adversarial multimedia environments.
method: The methodology involves designing an MLOps-aware multi-scale residual feature sterilization network (MSRFS-Net) and integrating it into a drift-aware MLOps framework. This framework includes data management, pipeline orchestration, model tracking, registry, deployment, performance monitoring, and automated retraining. Drift detection is based on a Combined Drift Score (CDS) that incorporates metric drift (PSNR, SSIM, NIQE, RR) and statistical drift (Jensen–Shannon Divergence with adaptive binning and temporal weighting). Experiments are conducted on BOSSbase, BOWS2, CelebA, and MSCOCO datasets with evolving steganography algorithms (HUGO, J-UNIWARD, GMAS, ROAST) to simulate concept drift. Ablation studies are performed on MSRFS-Net architecture and drift triggering strategies. System performance and scalability are analyzed in a Kubernetes environment.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Data Management, Model Training, Model Deployment, Model Monitoring, Model Retraining, Model Governance
future_research: ["Extend the framework to high-resolution video streams and multimodal content, where temporal and cross-modal drift introduce additional complexity.", "Incorporate advanced continual learning strategies beyond simple data replay to further mitigate catastrophic forgetting during frequent updates.", "Integrate explainability mechanisms for scale-specific drift signals to improve transparency in security-sensitive deployments.", "Integrate autonomous or agent-driven orchestration within MLOps pipelines for more resilient multimedia security systems."]

title: Decentralized Machine Learning Governance: Overview, Opportunities, and Challenges
journal: IEEE Access
reason: This paper provides a comprehensive overview of decentralized ML governance, discussing its components, opportunities, and challenges. It directly addresses the operational and governance aspects of ML systems in a decentralized context, which is relevant to understanding different implementation paradigms.
deployment_context: Generic AI Implementation (focus on decentralized governance, not specific industrial applications)
operational_constraints: (1) Centralized governance faces a massive problem due to the growing ML research, leading to lack of transparency and vulnerability to security risks as the central authority has complete access to all data. (2) Scalability issues in centralized ML where the central node can only handle a limited number of clients at a time. (3) Decentralized ML faces privacy and security risks, such as sensitive data leakage to untrusted resource contributors during model training and potential for security risks from malicious participants. (4) Performance limitations in decentralized ML compared to cloud and data center-based ML. (5) Difficulties in audit and compliance guarantee in decentralized ML. (6) DAO governance challenges include defining roles, responsibilities, and incentives for stakeholders, managing large communities, and monitoring needs, which are resource and labor-intensive. (7) Centralization or plutocracy in DAOs due to delegation of governance authority to small committees or domination by participants with more resources. (8) Low community engagement in DAOs, with many stakeholders abstaining or ceding power. (9) Voter fatigue, manipulation of voting processes, and voter bribery in blockchain-based governance. (10) Long delays for off-chain voting decisions to be reflected on-chain in hybrid governance models. (11) Security vulnerabilities in decentralized governance software (smart contracts, off-chain code) that malicious actors can exploit. (12) Interoperability and integration challenges between various decentralized ML governance components (identity, access control, rights management, verifiable ML, value chain, finance, risk management) and with traditional cloud-based infrastructures. (13) Privacy challenges in decentralized ML governance, including identity management, ownership, access control, voting, and audibility, especially balancing strong privacy with accountability. (14) Legal and regulatory challenges for DAOs, such as uncertainty of legal status, making participants legally liable, and compliance with data privacy regulations like GDPR and CCPA.
mlops_practices: (1) ML value chain management: Proposed to be managed by decentralized governance, moving from a pipeline process to value chain management, with collaboration facilitated by smart contracts and DAOs to track value flow and incentivize co-creation. (2) Decentralized identity management: Utilizing Self-Sovereign Identities (SSI), Decentralized Identifiers (DIDs), and Soul-Bound Tokens (SBTs) to establish accountability, manage access to ML artifacts and resources, and participate in governance. (3) Decentralized ownership and rights management: Managing ownership, usage rights, development rights, and modification rights for ML assets (data, models, code) through decentralized mechanisms, potentially with community-owned keys for decryption of encrypted assets stored in decentralized storage. (4) Decentralized risk management: Incorporating reputation-based, staking-based, and insurance-based approaches to manage security, privacy, societal, and financial risks, with a focus on decentralized insurance for ML services. (5) Decentralized decision-making process: Implementing community-led decision-making for ML processes (e.g., expanding datasets, adopting model designs, licensing models) using DAO characteristics, smart contracts, and various voting strategies (weighted, delegate, ranked-choice). (6) Decentralized ML finance: Establishing DAOs (donation, consortium, crowd-funding, revenue-sharing) to fund ML projects and services, addressing the rising cost of training large ML models.
gaps_identified: (1) Existing ML governance is narrowly defined and mostly centralized, failing to fully exploit the potential of decentralization, particularly from a governance perspective. (2) MLOps, as an extension of DevOps, primarily revolves around infrastructure-level tools and processes, with support for ML governance still in early stages. (3) In-depth research is needed for decentralized ML value chain governance before it can be fully embraced in practice, requiring cross-disciplinary collaboration. (4) Challenges remain in integrating DIDs/verifiable credentials with ML governance to achieve security and privacy-protection guarantees, supporting interoperability between decentralized and centralized identities, and understanding the meaning of soul-bound tokens for ML systems. (5) How to ensure data integrity, confidentiality, and rights protection when a community or DAO owns ML assets, and how to manage licensing when the issuer is a decentralized virtual organization. (6) How to audit ML artifacts owned by a community and resolve disputes over ownership or rights between virtual organizations. (7) Existing homomorphic ML approaches suffer from low performance and are mainly suited for inference, not training, for confidentiality protection. (8) Lack of vendor-agnostic standards in Trusted Execution Environment (TEE) implementation, low performance compared to non-TEE ML, and vulnerabilities in TEE-based approaches. (9) Rigorous risk modeling and assessment of ML system risks based on solid theoretical foundations are needed for the success of decentralized ML insurance. (10) Modeling and pricing financial and economic risks involving ML systems are relatively new research topics. (11) Whether decentralized ML governance can eventually lead to better decisions than centralized governance remains an open hypothesis. (12) Open research questions exist regarding privacy-preserving voting, defense against attacks and manipulation of decision-making, mitigation of governance risks, and fairness in the governance process. (13) Optimizing returns from investment to a portfolio of ML projects, measuring social impacts of ML services for social goods, and quantifying ROI when ML governance improves social goods are open questions in ML finance. (14) Most courts must implement legal changes to accept blockchain evidence, creating legal uncertainty for DAOs.
key_findings: This paper proposes a new framework for decentralized ML governance, expanding its scope beyond security and privacy to include value chain management, ML finance, and community management. It identifies that while traditional MLOps is centralized and infrastructure-focused, decentralized governance leverages blockchain, DAOs, and smart contracts to enable community-driven decision-making, shared ownership of ML assets, and novel risk/finance models. The work highlights significant opportunities in areas like transparent value co-creation and robust identity management, but also outlines critical challenges including DAO governance limitations, security vulnerabilities in smart contracts, interoperability issues, privacy concerns, and legal/regulatory uncertainties. The paper serves as a roadmap for future research to address these gaps and unlock the full potential of decentralized ML governance.
method: Literature Review/Conceptual Framework
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Governance, Operations, Deployment, Monitoring, Maintenance
future_research: (1) Conduct in-depth examinations and case studies of specific use cases across different contexts to understand applicability and identify implementation challenges. (2) Focus on practical implementations, including DAO tooling, governance contract design patterns, and demonstrations using case studies. (3) Iteratively fine-tune governance mechanisms. (4) Address ethical and regulatory considerations. (5) Explore interoperability and standardization, including a model-driven extension to decentralized ML governance. (6) Leverage insights from decentralized governance research and bridge knowledge gaps to strengthen the connection between ML governance and DAO research.

title: Perspectives On Machine Learning Inference Serving in Real-World Settings
journal: Software - Practice and Experience
reason: This paper directly addresses the deployment, operation, and maintenance of ML/AI models in production across various industries, including manufacturing and IoT, providing empirical insights into MLOps practices and architectural trade-offs.
evidence_level: Empirical (semi-structured interviews with practitioners across eight companies)
deployment_context: Cloud (public, private, on-premises), Edge, IoT, Embedded (NodeJS library). Sectors include advertising, gaming, healthcare, financial services, software platforms, and manufacturing.
operational_constraints: ["(1) High-frequency model updates (daily) in advertising/marketing campaigns (Cases 1, 7) create a trade-off between meticulous versioning/automation and rapid deployment to avoid model staleness.", "(2) Large number of models (hundreds daily in Case 1) makes identifying faulty models challenging with manual quality assurance.", "(3) Real-time streaming applications (video/speech in Cases 2, 3) impose strict low-latency constraints, requiring high-performance communication protocols (gRPC, WebSockets) and optimized inference pipelines.", "(4) Regulatory requirements in financial services (Case 4) impose explainability requirements on algorithms and models, necessitating increased traceability and robust monitoring.", "(5) Handling many unseen PDF layouts during inference in OCR systems (Case 5) requires robust models and potentially human-in-the-loop validation.", "(6) IoT/Edge deployments (Cases 6, 8) are constrained by hardware heterogeneity, limited computational resources, memory footprint, and network connectivity (e.g., air-gapped edge box in Case 8).", "(7) Offline edge deployment (Case 8) requires packaging the entire solution (container, code, model binaries, configurations) into a single zip file for transfer, as the edge box is not connected to the internet.", "(8) Integration with legacy components (Case 1) can lead to less flexibility in model scaling, monitoring, and orchestration when models are embedded as libraries.", "(9) Lack of ground truth data in production (Cases 3, 5) makes direct model performance monitoring and drift detection challenging, leading to reliance on proxy metrics.", "(10) Over-decomposition of components into excessive microservices can introduce additional communication overhead, increasing inference latency and maintenance complexity."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) Model Versioning and Storage: Manual versioning (direct annotation or tool-based with DVC/Git) for model binaries or configuration files (Cases 1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 8). Derived versioning from S3 bucket's versioning feature (Cases 3, 7).", "(2) Quality Assurance: Local manual tests and validation (Cases 1, 2, 7). Human-in-the-loop validation for model accuracy (Case 7). Automated CI/CD pipelines with unit, integration, and end-to-end tests (Cases 3, 4, 5, 8).", "(3) Model Packaging: Docker containers for packaging model artifacts and inference pipeline components (Cases 2, 3, 4, 5, 8). Non-containerized packaging (NodeJS library, EC2 instances with Python scripts) for rapid deployment (Cases 1, 7). Standardized formats (PMML, ONNX) for IoT platforms (Case 6). Zip files for offline edge deployment (Case 8).", "(4) Inference Serving Patterns: Model-as-a-Service (independent API endpoint) using microservices (Cases 3, 5, 8). Model-as-a-Dependency (model imported as library) for low latency and simpler integration (Cases 1, 2, 7). Hybrid serving (batch + online) to cater to diverse business needs (Cases 4, 6).", "(5) Communication Protocols: gRPC and WebSockets for real-time, low-latency streaming inference (Cases 2, 3). REST APIs for non-real-time online inference (Cases 1, 4, 5, 6). MQTT protocol for communication between containers in offline edge deployments (Case 8).", "(6) Inference Pipeline Design: Monolithic pipelines (entire pipeline in single container) for simplified deployment and reduced latency (Case 2). Decoupled microservices for independent scaling and updates (Cases 3, 5). Configuration-based pipelines for IoT platforms (Case 6).", "(7) Monitoring: End-to-end service availability and latency monitoring (all cases). Infrastructure resource monitoring (CPU/GPU, memory, container health) using tools like Splunk, CloudWatch, Grafana, Prometheus (Cases 1, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8). Model accuracy monitoring with F1 scores, precision, recall when ground truth is available (Cases 2, 7). Proxy metrics (edit counts, test coverage, prediction distribution) for drift detection when ground truth is unavailable (Cases 3, 4, 5). Automated scaling frameworks (Kubernetes, AWS Lambda, AWS Fargate) coupled with monitoring for capacity control (Cases 2, 3, 4, 7).", "(8) Model Retirement: Archiving retired models in offline servers (Case 5), but generally a lack of formal policies."]
gaps_identified: ["Lack of integrated tools and standard operational proxies for drift detection when ground truth is not accessible, leading to reliance on reactive measures or less reliable proxies.", "Absence of dedicated versioning principles for ML models that capture the co-evolution of data, code, and parameters throughout the model’s lifecycle.", "Limited empirical evaluation of ML serving patterns in a quantitative manner (latency, costs, operability under various traffic conditions, failure conditions).", "IoT platforms often lack in-built software quality management functionalities, expecting QA workflows to be managed using external tools and processes, leading to fragmented delivery workflows.", "The general lack of formal model retirement policies across multiple cases underscores a gap in the full lifecycle management of ML models.", "Asymmetry in MLOps maturity: organizations often prioritize immediate system reliability (packaging, infrastructure monitoring) before developing advanced approaches for ML lineage (sophisticated model versioning, proactive model monitoring)."]
key_findings: This multi-case study empirically investigates real-world ML deployment workflows and inference architectures across eight diverse companies. It identifies five core deployment themes: model versioning and storage, quality assurance, monitoring, model packaging, and inference serving. The study reveals a maturity gradient from manual, ad-hoc practices to automated CI/CD pipelines, highlighting architectural trade-offs between tightly coupled vs. loosely coupled model deployment patterns. Key findings include the rarity of proactive model drift monitoring and the use of hybrid inference serving patterns to balance latency and scalability requirements, demonstrating that MLOps adoption involves context-specific adaptations and trade-offs rather than uniform adherence to prescribed frameworks.
method: Exploratory multi-case study research design. Data collected through semi-structured interviews with practitioners (1-2 engineers per company) across eight ML systems in various sectors (advertising, finance, healthcare, manufacturing, software platforms). Thematic analysis applied to extract recurring patterns across the deployment lifecycle. Cross-case analysis conducted to synthesize attributes and identify contextual influences.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Model Deployment, Inference Serving, Monitoring, Model Versioning and Storage, Quality Assurance, Model Packaging.
future_research: Research on methods to validate and calibrate operational proxies for drift detection when ground truth is not accessible. Development of dedicated versioning principles for ML models that capture the co-evolution of data, code, and parameters. Empirical evaluation of ML serving patterns in a quantitative manner (latency, costs, operability under simulated/realistic traffic conditions, failure conditions).

title: Data Chameleon: A Self-adaptive Synthetic Data Management System
journal: Lecture Notes in Computer Science
reason: The paper introduces Data Chameleon, a self-adaptive MLOps system for managing synthetic data generators, directly addressing the deployment, operation, and maintenance of AI models (specifically generative models) in production, with a focus on adaptability to changing requirements and data contexts.
deployment_context: Generic AI Implementation (data sharing, e-commerce, fraud detection, personalized marketing, inventory management, trend analysis)
operational_constraints: ["(1) Manual establishment and management of different synthetic data generators with diverging properties is error-prone, time-consuming, lacks flexibility, and is costly and impractical.", "(2) Different use cases of the same problem require different synthetic datasets, as utility, fidelity, and privacy requirements may vary, necessitating adaptation in synthetic data production.", "(3) Attributes and authorized original records involved in synthetic data generation evolve over time due to new use cases or data subject requests (withdrawal/update), requiring the synthetic data system to adapt.", "(4) Concept drifts in the original data (changes in underlying distribution and relationships) can render old data generators obsolete, necessitating continuous monitoring and updates.", "(5) It is mathematically impossible to simultaneously optimize privacy, utility, and fidelity in synthetic data generation, leading to inherent trade-offs that must be managed adaptively based on context."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) Self-adaptive system architecture (MAPE-k model): Data Chameleon uses a Monitor, Analyze, Plan, Execute, and Knowledge (MAPE-k) loop to continuously monitor context changes (concept drifts, new requests, data changes) and trigger adaptation actions.", "(2) Dynamic generator selection and sampling: The system adaptively samples from the most appropriate synthetic data generator(s) at run-time based on the data request's privacy and utility requirements, or merges data from multiple generators.", "(3) Longer-term generator evaluation and adaptation: Data Chameleon monitors and evaluates the overall suitability of available generators over time to detect evolutions in data demand or concept drifts, autonomously deciding to re-train existing generators or instantiate additional ones.", "(4) Automated generator creation/configuration: For new use cases or drifts, the system can automatically try to find the right configuration for a new generator based on given privacy/utility requirements, or allow manual configuration.", "(5) Data change adaptation (retraining/unlearning): In response to original data changes (e.g., 'right to be forgotten'), generative models are retrained or incrementally trained, potentially using machine unlearning techniques to remove sensitive entries without full retraining.", "(6) Concept drift handling strategies: Employs different strategies for various drift types: archiving/discarding all generators for sudden/permanent drift, adding new generators and updating cached data for recurrent drift, and manual intervention for gradual changes."]
