mlops_practices: ["(1) Structured Inference Optimization Approach: A systematic method for optimizing DNN inference performance on legacy systems, involving identifying constraints, Kepner-Tregoe (KT) decision analysis, design space exploration, and comparison with baseline.", "(2) DNN Architecture Selection: Choosing architectures (e.g., MobileNetV1, MobileNetV2) based on parameters, operations, and inference time trade-offs.", "(3) DNN Model Optimization: Applying techniques like weight clustering (grouping similar weights to reduce memory footprint) and weight pruning (removing low-impact neurons) using TensorFlow model optimization toolkit.", "(4) Post-Training Quantization: Reducing precision of weights and biases (e.g., float32 to float16 or uint8/int8) to reduce network size and computation time, using TensorFlow model optimization toolkit or Intel OpenVINO toolkit.", "(5) Target Deployment Platform Selection: Experimenting with different hardware platforms like CPU (Intel i7), VPU (Intel Neural Compute Stick 1 - NCS1), and TPU (Google Coral USB accelerator), considering their architectural advantages and trade-offs between speed and accuracy.", "(6) Inference Engine Selection: Utilizing specific inference engines such as TensorFlow Lite, OpenCV, OpenVINO, and Edge TPU runtime, which are tailored to different hardware platforms and model formats.", "(7) Baseline Experimentation: Establishing a reference inference time and Mean Absolute Error (MAE) using a standard TensorFlow model on a CPU with OpenCV for comparison against optimized approaches.", "(8) Performance Monitoring: Measuring average inference time (including image transfer, inference, and output return) and MAE to evaluate optimization effectiveness."]
gaps_identified: ["Traditional MLOps pipelines do not cater directly to legacy systems, requiring customized inference pipelines for DNN deployment.", "Standard inference optimization approaches result in poor inference performance on brownfield industrial architectures with existing legacy hardware and software.", "Weight clustering and weight pruning, while reducing model size, do not significantly improve inference speed because the number of operations remains the same.", "Quantization (e.g., float32 to float16) alone may not yield significant inference time improvements; further quantization to uint8 or int8 is needed to reduce computation time.", "VPU platforms like NCS1 can introduce significant overhead due to frequent re-connection and data transfer times, making them unsuitable for high-throughput applications without addressing these communication bottlenecks.", "TPU accelerators with USB interfaces (e.g., Google Coral USB accelerator) can suffer from increased inference time due to data transfer delays over the USB connection, suggesting a need for PCIe interfaces."]

method: The paper employs a structured approach combining literature review, Kepner-Tregoe (KT) decision analysis, and empirical design space exploration. It identifies constraints of a legacy industrial system (semiconductor die bonder), filters feasible optimization techniques using a weighted criteria matrix, and then experimentally evaluates the performance (inference time and MAE) of selected DNN architectures, model optimizations (pruning, clustering, quantization), target platforms (CPU, VPU, TPU), and inference engines. future_research: Future work involves studying the impact of other aspects in the MLOps pipeline for inference optimization. Specifically, investigating alternative clustering and pruning algorithms to improve inference speed, exploring further quantization to uint8/int8, experimenting with Neural Compute Stick 2 (NCS2) for VPU platforms, and using Coral accelerators with PCIe interfaces to reduce data transfer delays.

title: Model Accuracy-Oriented Data Sets Visualization for Understanding Temporal Changes in Data
journal: Proceedings of the International Conference on Information Visualisation
reason: This paper proposes a visualization algorithm specifically designed to help data scientists understand temporal changes in data and model performance within MLOps frameworks, directly addressing operational aspects of AI.
deployment_context: Generic AI Implementation (MLOps frameworks)
operational_constraints: (1) Difficulty in intuitively understanding temporal changes in large, ever-increasing, and drifting data sets at a glance, which is crucial for determining optimal model update/switch timing and training data selection. (2) Existing visualization tools in MLOps platforms (e.g., Vertex AI, SageMaker, Azure ML) typically offer graphs specific to particular metrics (drift detection, performance monitoring) but require domain expertise and are not well-suited for quickly grasping data changes.
mlops_practices: (1) Data drift monitoring: The proposed visualization algorithm helps identify trends, periodicity, and anomalies in data sets over time. (2) Model performance monitoring: The visualization simultaneously displays the predictive performance of the model through contour plots, showing which data sets the model is effective for and which can be used for enhancement. (3) Informed decision-making for model updates/retraining: By understanding data changes and model efficacy, data scientists can make more informed decisions on when to update/switch models or select training data. (4) Model reuse strategy: The visualization can identify periodic data changes, suggesting that storing and reusing old models when similar data patterns reappear can lead to efficient ML operations (e.g., Powersupply dataset case study).
gaps_identified: (1) Current dimensionality reduction methods (PCA, t-SNE, UMAP) are too detailed for understanding temporal changes in large data sets, as they visualize individual samples rather than changes across data sets. (2) Existing MLOps platform visualization tools are metric-specific and lack the intuitive, at-a-glance overview needed for comprehensive understanding of data changes and model performance.
key_findings: The paper proposes a visualization algorithm that maps multiple time-ordered data sets and a prediction model onto a single plot, using accuracy vectors derived from models trained on each dataset to quantify data distribution similarity. This visualization allows data scientists to intuitively comprehend temporal changes (trends, periodicity, anomalies) and the predictive performance of models simultaneously. Case studies with six real-world open datasets demonstrate that the visualization provides valuable insights into data changes, facilitating more informed decision-making in MLOps, such as identifying stable data distributions, periodic changes, or consistent data evolution patterns.
method: The method involves an algorithm with two sub-procedures: EMBEDDINGDATA and EMBEDDINGMODEL. EMBEDDINGDATA trains 'description models' on each time-partitioned dataset, computes accuracy vectors (accuracies of each description model on all datasets), and then uses Isomap for dimensionality reduction to embed these accuracy vectors into a 2D space. EMBEDDINGMODEL trains an accuracy predictor (a unimodal regression model) to map the 2D embedding space to the prediction model's accuracy. The final visualization combines scatter plots of the embedded datasets with directed arrows showing temporal flow and contour plots representing model accuracy.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Monitoring, Operations, Maintenance
future_research: The paper does not explicitly state future research directions, but the work implies potential for extending the visualization to other ML tasks (e.g., regression, though mentioned as applicable), exploring different embedding techniques, or integrating with real-time MLOps dashboards.

title: Deploying AI Models in Resource-Constrained Environments

mlops_practices: ["(1) Integrated DevOps/MLOps pipeline: Orchestrated by GitHub Actions, integrating MLflow with custom automation scripts, connecting software development (CI/CD) with machine learning lifecycle management.", "(2) Change Detection: A shell script inspects Git diffs to identify changes in training data, configuration files, or model code, labeling the cause (Data, Script, or Both) to decide if retraining is necessary, preventing unnecessary computation.", "(3) Training and Tracking: When retraining is required, the pipeline executes training jobs, logging metrics (accuracy, validation loss), parameters, and artifacts to MLflow for immediate comparison against prior runs.", "(4) Commit Linkage: Each commit is appended to a structured history (CSV) recording commit ID, author, branch, MLflow run ID, metrics, and model version, creating a permanent mapping from repository activity to model outcomes.", "(5) Reporting and Visualization: Automated scripts generate human-readable Markdown summaries and SVG charts, plotting validation accuracy trends, showing medians/moving averages, and highlighting significant shifts (regressions or improvements) in red, with links back to GitHub commits.", "(6) Model Delivery: The pipeline integrates with the MLflow Model Registry, dynamically fetching the correct version of the trained model and bundling it into the Android APK, ensuring the deployed application always contains the intended model artifact without manual intervention.", "(7) Lifecycle Management: Outdated runs and unused model versions are pruned automatically unless explicitly tagged or promoted to protected stages (e.g., Production) to maintain a clean, auditable environment.", "(8) Performance Report (Trace+Summarize): A dedicated reporting step generates transparent summaries and links commits to outcomes, providing developers with a direct link from commits to results and immediate visibility of critical changes."]

key_findings: The paper presents an automated DevOps/MLOps pipeline that links every Git commit to its corresponding training run, metrics, and deployed model, ensuring commit-to-performance traceability. method: The proposed pipeline is orchestrated by GitHub Actions and integrates MLflow with custom automation scripts. It follows a two-lane workflow (DevOps and MLOps) with cross-lane interactions. The approach is validated on a real-world mobile application use case.

title: An Artificial Intelligence Approach to Intrusion Detection in Smart Home IoT Environments
journal: Communications in Computer and Information Science

deployment_context: Smart Home IoT environments, specifically on a Raspberry Pi 3 Model B+ acting as a gateway, with a cloud backend for model evolution.
operational_constraints: ["(1) Limited computational resources on IoT hardware (e.g., Raspberry Pi) prevent the deployment of complex deep learning models directly at the edge.", "(2) Network latency makes cloud-centric architectures unsuitable for mitigating time-sensitive internal threats like lateral movement in smart homes.", "(3) The 'set-it-and-forget-it' nature of smart home devices means users lack the technical capabilities and expectations for expert configuration, continuous updates, and active alert tasks required by conventional NIDS.", "(4) Model staleness is a critical challenge as new threats emerge, requiring continuous adaptation without manual intervention.", "(5) Class imbalance in network security datasets (benign instances vastly outnumber attack samples) can lead to model bias if not addressed during training.", "(6) High-volume DoS/DDoS traffic cannot be fully transmitted to the cloud layer for analysis, requiring edge-level mitigation to prevent congestion and preserve bandwidth.", "(7) The need for strict consistency between training and deployment environments to guarantee operational integrity and predictive accuracy of the ML model on edge devices.", "(8) Framework lock-in and portability issues when deploying models trained in one framework to diverse hardware platforms with different inference engines."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) Hybrid Edge-Cloud Architecture: A lightweight LightGBM model is deployed at the edge (Raspberry Pi) for real-time binary classification, while a more powerful CNN-LSTM model in the cloud handles incremental learning and knowledge distillation.", "(2) Automated Model Evolution/Retraining Loop: When an anomaly is detected at the edge, the anomalous traffic data is escalated to the cloud. The cloud model performs incremental learning and knowledge distillation to train a new, more robust edge model.", "(3) Model Distillation: The cloud-based 'teacher' CNN-LSTM model distills its knowledge into an optimized, lightweight 'student' LightGBM model suitable for edge deployment, enabling the student to absorb complex decision boundaries with lower computational overhead.", "(4) Containerization (Docker): The updated edge model, along with its runtime environment and dependencies, is automatically containerized into a Docker image for consistent and reproducible deployment.", "(5) Automated Model Deployment/Redeployment: Edge gateways periodically check a registry for new Docker images. When available, the device automatically pulls the latest version and replaces the existing container in a controlled manner, ensuring uninterrupted operation.", "(6) Real-time Monitoring and Alerting: The edge device publishes immediate security alerts via MQTT when an attack is detected, ensuring rapid user notification.", "(7) Feature Engineering and Selection: NFStream is used for reproducible flow extraction and feature engineering, followed by a feature selection process (using three methods including LightGBM's embedded feature importance) to identify a compact set of 17 informative features.", "(8) Data Preprocessing: One-Hot Encoding for categorical features, SMOTE for addressing class imbalance, and correlation analysis for feature space refinement are applied.", "(9) Model Conversion (ONNX): Trained models are converted to the ONNX format to overcome framework lock-in and enhance portability across diverse hardware platforms and inference engines.", "(10) Expert-Guided Refinement: Anomalies flagged by the edge are queued in the cloud for expert analysis and labeling (true positives/false positives) before triggering retraining, combining autonomous distillation with expert refinement.", "(11) Edge-level Mitigation for Volumetric Attacks: DoS/DDoS attacks are mitigated directly at the edge using packet-level filtering to block traffic from identified sources, preventing upstream congestion and preserving bandwidth for cloud analysis."]
gaps_identified: ["Existing hybrid IDS frameworks often lack a concrete, automated mechanism for the continuous evolution, optimization, and deployment of the edge model, transforming conceptual models into practical, self-adaptive defense systems.", "Conventional NIDS are fundamentally unsuitable for smart home contexts due to their dependency on prior threat knowledge (ineffective against zero-day attacks) and the requirement for expert configuration and continuous updates, which are beyond the average user's capabilities.", "Cloud-centric architectures are undermined by network latency, making them unsuitable for mitigating time-sensitive internal threats.", "Most existing edge-centric models rely on shallow or static, rule-based systems, which are severely limited by IoT hardware and vulnerable to sophisticated attacks.", "The challenge of ensuring strict consistency between training and deployment environments for ML models on resource-constrained edge devices.", "The need to overcome framework lock-in and enhance model portability across diverse hardware platforms and inference engines in IoT deployments."]
key_findings: This paper proposes a novel self-adaptive hybrid Edge-Cloud intrusion detection system for smart home IoT environments, addressing the conflict between real-time detection and deep analytical power. The system deploys a lightweight LightGBM model on a Raspberry Pi at the edge for immediate binary classification (average detection delay of 8.4 ms) and escalates anomalies to a cloud-based CNN-LSTM model for incremental learning and knowledge distillation. The updated edge model is then automatically containerized and redeployed, establishing a continuous learning loop. title: SliceTeller: A Data Slice-Driven Approach for Machine Learning Model Validation
journal: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
reason: The paper introduces SliceTeller, a tool for ML model validation and iteration focused on identifying and mitigating performance issues in specific data slices. It directly addresses model evaluation, debugging, and improvement in MLOps cycles, including real-world use cases in autonomous driving and fire detection.
deployment_context: Generic AI Implementation
operational_constraints: ["(1) Manual data slicing is time-consuming, involving manually creating rules, running evaluation scripts on subsets, and comparing results across various data subsets.", "(2) ML experts cannot explore all possible data subsets to identify relevant failure cases due to an exponentially large search space created by numerous interpretable meta-data (e.g., weather, temperature).", "(3) Retraining models by prioritizing critical slices is time-consuming, especially for neural network architectures, making it infeasible to exhaustively apply optimization on all combinations of data slices.", "(4) It is unclear how optimizing for specific slices will trade-off performance on other slices and whether the result will still meet product requirements.", "(5) Keeping track of multiple models trained across different data slices requires significant effort from experts.", "(6) Drawing experimentation conclusions and identifying slice trade-offs requires switching between development tools multiple times.", "(7) GroupDRO (Distributionally Robust Optimization) requires prior knowledge of group information in training data, which is not readily available for most real-world ML problems.", "(8) GroupDRO training is time-consuming, making it infeasible to exhaustively apply it on all combinations of data slices to determine ideal slices for optimization.", "(9) On-board processors in critical applications (e.g., autonomous driving) have limited compute power, restricting the complexity of deployable models (e.g., favoring XGBoost over deep neural networks).", "(10) Ensuring customer-defined quality on hand-crafted critical slices is still a manual guarantee needed even with automated tools.", "(11) The need for interpretable metadata to slice models often requires intensive and expensive manual annotation."]
mlops_practices: ["**Automated Data Slice Finding:** Uses DivExplorer (a Frequent Pattern Mining-based algorithm) to automatically identify problematic data slices where model performance significantly differs from the overall metric.", "**Slice Summarization:** Employs a redundancy pruning approach to reduce the number of data slices presented to the user, focusing on more general slices first.", "**Slice-Based Model Evaluation:** Computes and displays model-agnostic (e.g., Support, Accuracy) and model-specific metrics for each identified data slice.", "**Slice-Based Data Understanding (Slice Detail View):** Provides visualizations like Slice Distribution View (for tabular data) to show metadata distribution shifts across slices and MatrixScape View (for image data) to contextualize images with metadata for root cause analysis.", "**Slice Prioritization (What-If Analysis):** Utilizes the 'SliceBoosting' algorithm to quickly estimate the performance impact and trade-offs of optimizing the model for particular data slices without full retraining.", "**Model Optimization (Importance Weighting):** Modifies the loss function during retraining by assigning heavier weights to training samples belonging to selected problematic slices.", "**Model Optimization (Group Distributionally Robust Optimization - GroupDRO):** Prioritizes worst-performing slices during the training process by optimizing for the worst-case loss among groups, rather than the average loss.", "**Slice-Based Model Comparison:** Allows side-by-side comparison of multiple model versions' performance at the individual data slice level, facilitating identification of trade-offs.", "**Iterative Model Improvement Workflow:** Supports a cycle of identifying problematic slices, understanding failures, estimating optimization effects, retraining, and comparing new model versions.", "**Transparency and Communication:** Aims to help engineers identify and convey model limitations and capabilities to customers, aiding in informed decision-making for model release."]
gaps_identified: ["Existing slice-oriented model validation solutions mainly focus on search efficiency and scalability, largely ignoring how to understand and interpret the impact of these data slices.", "It is a non-trivial task to customize and select data slices of interest based on domain-specific requirements.", "Current approaches for model optimization (e.g., GroupDRO) require group information of training data to be known beforehand, which is often unavailable for real-world ML problems.", "GroupDRO training is time-consuming, making it infeasible to exhaustively apply it on all combinations of data slices to determine ideal slices for optimization.", "Most existing visualization works focus on the data exploration stage and rarely provide optimization solutions to close the entire MLOps loop.", "Current model comparison methods are usually done at an aggregated level (e.g., a single metric value), lacking detailed slice-level insights."]
key_findings: SliceTeller is a novel Visual Analytics (VA) tool that enables data slice-driven validation, debugging, comparison, and improvement of ML models. It automates problematic slice discovery, provides visualizations for understanding failure cases, and introduces SliceBoosting for efficient estimation of optimization trade-offs. Through three use cases, including two real-world industrial applications, the system demonstrated its effectiveness in identifying biases, improving model performance on critical slices (e.g., increasing accuracy by over 0.2 for some slices in object height classification), and uncovering data quality issues (e.g., mislabeled data in fire detection), thereby significantly speeding up model iteration in MLOps cycles.
method: Mixed Method (Tool Development, Algorithm, Case Study, Expert Interviews)
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Model Validation, Model Evaluation, Model Debugging, Model Retraining/Iteration, Model Monitoring (implicitly, for identifying problematic slices in validation data)
future_research: ["Investigate automatic methods to generate interpretable metadata for data slicing, potentially using self-supervised learning to identify visual concepts.", "Evaluate the SliceBoosting algorithm's effect estimation with additional model optimization techniques and more public datasets.", "Extend SliceTeller to support regression models by adapting the slice finding algorithm to measure prediction correctness and defect identification.", "Incorporate problem-specific metrics for slice evaluation, as requested by domain experts.", "Integrate explanations for model predictions (e.g., importance maps) into the SliceDetailView.", "Allow users to manually add and track critical data slices for which 100% accuracy is required."]

title: Demystifying mlops and presenting a recipe for the selection of open-source tools
journal: Applied Sciences (Switzerland)
reason: This paper provides a comprehensive overview of MLOps, including workflow stages, roles, and a detailed comparison of open-source tools. It also presents an example workflow for object detection, directly addressing the deployment and operational aspects of ML models.
deployment_context: Generic AI Implementation (software-centric ML deployment, object detection with images)
operational_constraints: (1) ML is a metrics-driven process, requiring manual tracking of multiple experiments, which increases overall complexity and is error-prone. (2) Data are not versioned, making reproducibility difficult as results depend on both code and evolving data. (3) Lack of model versioning or a model registry makes it hard to reproduce past models. (4) Code reviews and unit/integration tests are often missing in ML development, which is typically limited to individual development environments. (5) The end product of manual ML is a single model, not a whole pipeline, hindering collaboration and sharing of artifacts. (6) Lack of CI/CD in traditional ML workflows, as operations and development are distinct. (7) Deployed models cannot be retrained automatically, leading to model staleness or concept drift and infrequent releases. (8) Changing one parameter can impact the entire pipeline and may lack versioning or contradict existing configurations. (9) High dimensionality of image data, making interpretation and understanding complex. (10) Image datasets are generally enormous, requiring extra storage resources. (11) Training data-driven models for image processing is time-consuming and requires extensive computational resources. (12) Quality assessment of images is challenging due to factors like poor lighting or camera hardware.
mlops_practices: (1) Continuous Integration (CI) and Continuous Delivery (CD) are adopted from DevOps to automate code integration, testing, and deployment. (2) Continuous Training (CT) is integrated to automatically retrain and deploy models when performance degrades due to concept or data drift. (3) Automated data validation is performed using metrics like SSIM for image quality assessment, removing low-quality images from datasets. (4) Experimentation tracking is implemented using tools like MLflow Tracking to log metrics, artifacts (models, plots), and parameters for each run, enabling comparison and reproducibility. (5) Data versioning is achieved using tools like DVC to track datasets and their evolution, linking them to specific model versions. (6) Model versioning and registration are managed through tools like MLflow Registry to store models, their versions, and metadata, facilitating governance and faster deployment. (7) Hyperparameter optimization is automated using libraries like Hyperopt to find best-performing model configurations. (8) Model packaging is done using tools like MLflow Models to save trained models as reproducible Python functions. (9) System monitoring (latency, throughput, disk utilization, CPU/GPU usage, API calls) is crucial for counteracting deployment errors and ensuring system health. (10) Input data monitoring (data schema, consistency, quality, drift, outliers, training/serving skew) is performed to detect changes in data characteristics that could impact model performance. (11) Model performance monitoring (output distribution shift, task-specific metrics like AUC, precision, recall, feature importance, concept drift, bias/fairness, numerical stability) is used to prevent decreasing model effectiveness and trigger retraining.
gaps_identified: (1) No single open-source tool currently provides a fully automated MLOps workflow, requiring combinations of tools with overlapping features and potential redundancy. (2) Specific tasks, such as data validation for images, are more difficult to automate than others. (3) Lack of a dashboard in some data versioning tools (e.g., DVC) for visualizing experiments, leading to preference for other tools (e.g., MLflow) for this task. (4) Some tools have high competency requirements (e.g., Kubernetes for Polyaxon, Kubeflow, Flyte, Pachyderm, Cortex, SeldonCore), making them difficult to configure and potentially unnecessary for smaller teams or less frequent model updates. (5) Integration costs can be high for platforms like TFX, making them less ideal for small projects with infrequent model updates. (6) Some tools (e.g., H2O) are more like analytics platforms and do not offer the freedom to integrate state-of-the-art custom models, limiting flexibility. (7) Lack of native support for scheduling pipelines in some tools (e.g., ZenML). (8) Difficulty in integrating general workflow management platforms (e.g., Airflow) into ML projects already under development. (9) Commercial platforms for MLOps can lead to high costs for multiple iterations and lack 100% transparency, hindering flexible and evolving workflows. (10) The need for an orchestrator and network layer for fully automating MLOps workflows, which is often missing in simpler setups. (11) The absence of continuous monitoring and continual learning for models in simpler workflows, leading to manual rollbacks and lack of automated updates.
key_findings: The paper systematically demystifies MLOps by defining its principles, involved roles, and workflow stages. It provides a comprehensive comparison of 26 open-source MLOps tools against a set of 14 requirements, highlighting their strengths and weaknesses. A key finding is that no single open-source tool can achieve a fully automated MLOps workflow, necessitating a combination of tools. The paper also presents a practical, partially automated MLOps workflow for object detection using a combination of Git, DVC, GitHub Actions, MLflow, and Hyperopt, demonstrating how to integrate these tools for continuous training and experiment tracking in a data-centric approach.
method: Literature Review, Tool Comparison, Case Study (Exemplar Workflow)
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Data Management, ML Preparation, ML Training, Model Management, Model Deployment, Operations and Monitoring
future_research: Investigate the potentials of AutoML capabilities for MLOps workflows, focusing on deep integration and orchestration, infrastructural restrictions, and requirements. Explore novel offerings for interconnection capabilities between tools and more holistic metric support. Conduct expert interviews regarding experiences with interconnecting and integrating various tools for complete MLOps pipelines. Pursue the integration of emerging data quality assurance concepts into MLOps tools and workflows.

journal: 2025 17th Seminar on Power Electronics and Control, SEPOC 2025

mlops_practices: (1) Model Deployment: YOLOv11 models are deployed within a ROS 2 framework, utilizing a modular architecture with distinct nodes (Camera Driver, YOLOv11 Node, Debug Node) for different functionalities. (5) Performance Evaluation: Metrics like Confusion Matrix, Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and mAP (mAP@50, mAP@50-95) are used to assess model performance during training and validation.

key_findings: This work successfully integrates YOLOv11 with ROS 2 for real-time object detection and human pose classification in robotic applications. method: The method involves a system architecture design with ROS 2 nodes, selection and training of YOLOv11 models on specialized datasets (Bottles and Cups Detection, Jump Fall Combined) using Google Colab GPUs, and evaluation using standard computer vision metrics (Precision, Recall, mAP, Confusion Matrix). title: Engineering MLOps Pipelines With Data Quality: A Case Study on Tabular Datasets in Kaggle
journal: Journal of Software: Evolution and Process
reason: This paper directly addresses MLOps by proposing and evaluating an automated approach for data quality checks in ML pipelines, focusing on the operationalization and maintenance aspects of ML models in production.
deployment_context: Generic AI Implementation (Kaggle datasets, pre-production phase of ML pipelines)
operational_constraints: (1) Lack of awareness and adoption of data quality checks by practitioners leads to biases, errors, and inefficiencies during model construction and operationalization. (2) Inability to automatically derive domain-specific knowledge (e.g., label classes) prevents automated assessment of metrics like class imbalance and feature-label correlation. (3) The timing and process of data integrity checks are highly dependent on user-specific workflows and unique MLOps pipeline configurations, making a one-size-fits-all automated approach challenging. (4) Open-source datasets often suffer from missing or incomplete data due to inadequate collection protocols or human error. (5) Open-source datasets frequently include noisy data (mislabeled samples, incorrect values, duplicates) which can degrade model training. (6) Datasets can be outdated, not reflecting current environmental states or populations, leading to models that fail on current data distributions. (7) Sampling bias in datasets, curated under specific conditions, can lead to models that do not generalize well to underrepresented populations or use cases. (8) Cultural and demographic biases in datasets can result in reduced accuracy for certain groups and exacerbate societal inequities. (9) Intentional manipulations and adversarial biases in competitive ML contexts can affect the integrity of downstream tasks. (10) Unclear licensing terms for some open-source datasets restrict their use in commercial or sensitive applications. (11) Datasets containing PII or sensitive attributes may not adhere to data protection regulations, even when anonymized. (12) Domain mismatch means datasets designed for one domain do not transfer well to another. (13) Many open-source datasets are relatively small and fail to capture the scale of data encountered in real-world applications, leading to overfitting or poor generalization in production.
mlops_practices: (1) Automated data quality checks: The study proposes and evaluates an automated approach for architecting ML pipelines using semi-automated data quality checks, specifically focusing on tabular data. (2) Data quality tool selection: A systematic process for identifying and selecting data quality tools (e.g., Deepchecks) based on features, maintenance status, and community support. (3) Metric-driven quality assessment: Categorization and application of data quality metrics (data duplicates, feature-feature correlation, missing values, mixed data types, mixed nulls, outlier sample detection, single value in column, special characters, string length out of bounds, string mismatch) to assess dataset quality. (4) Pre-production data validation: Emphasis on assessing and ensuring data quality during pre-production phases to prevent errors and biases from entering ML models. (5) Data de-duplication: Enforcing data de-duplication by checking duplicity in a key-resolved fashion and applying precedence rules for categorical features (FAIL > WARN > PASS). (6) Handling pre-divided datasets: Analyzing all possible sets associated with datasets, including train and test sets, and external CSV files, to ensure comprehensive quality checks. (7) Automated script for reproducibility: Development of an automated script to download, extract, run quality checks, and delete datasets to ensure reproducibility and manage large data volumes. (8) Prioritizing robust documentation and metadata: Recommendation for detailed documentation and metadata for datasets, including feature descriptions, anticipated data types, and potential data quality issues. (9) Encouraging collaboration and knowledge sharing: Fostering collective learning and shared experiences related to data quality among practitioners.
gaps_identified: (1) A significant number of practitioners do not currently use data quality checks or measurements as gateways for model construction and operationalization. (2) There is a lack of empirical studies investigating how practitioners navigate data quality issues in applied settings, such as ML pipelines. (3) Existing data quality tools often require users to define specific thresholds and manually configure conditions, introducing complexity that falls outside the scope of fully automated quality assessment. (4) Current automated data quality assessment methods cannot handle metrics requiring domain knowledge, such as class imbalance and feature-label correlation, because label classes vary in naming and positioning across datasets. (5) There is a need for automated methods to detect 'MLOps smells' beyond current data quality checks. (6) Kaggle's ranking criteria (Hotness, Most votes, Usability) are not always aligned with data quality and integrity, leading to highly-ranked datasets with significant quality issues. (7) The most effective time to run data integrity checks (before model training, after raw data preprocessing) is highly dependent on user-specific workflows and unique MLOps pipeline configurations, making a generalized automated solution difficult. (8) Current comparative analysis of data quality tools is relatively simple and does not establish statistically significant correlations between ranking categories and specific types of data quality issues.
key_findings: The study empirically evaluates data quality tools and applies Deepchecks to analyze tabular datasets from Kaggle, revealing that even highly-rated datasets frequently contain significant data integrity issues such as feature correlation, duplicates, and single-value columns. These issues, often overlooked by practitioners, can introduce errors and biases into ML models, compromising their reliability. The research highlights a misalignment between Kaggle's popularity rankings and actual data quality, underscoring the critical need for rigorous, automated data quality checks in pre-production ML pipelines. The paper proposes design principles for practitioners, emphasizing comprehensive data quality checks, standardized labeling, robust documentation, and knowledge sharing to enhance model reliability and generalizability.
method: The methodology involved four phases: (1) Data collection and mining from Kaggle using API and web scraping. (2) Data quality tools analysis, identifying and selecting Deepchecks. (3) Quality metric analysis, categorizing metrics into data structure, feature analysis, and string quality. (4) Empirical study, executing automated quality checks on top-ranked Kaggle datasets and generating descriptive statistics. The study used Python packages like Pandas, Matplotlib, and Seaborn for analysis and visualization, and involved expert MLOps practitioners for synthesizing lessons learned.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Data Preparation, Model Training (pre-production phase), Monitoring (data quality aspect)
future_research: (1) Develop automated methods to detect MLOps smells, including metrics like Class Imbalance and Feature-Label Correlation, which currently require domain knowledge. (2) Conduct more rigorous experiments to investigate statistically significant correlations between Kaggle's ranking categories (Hotness, Most votes, Usability) and specific types of data quality issues (data structure, feature analysis, string quality). (3) Replicate the study using diverse data sources beyond Kaggle to enhance external validity and generalizability. (4) Extend the study to computer vision tasks, focusing on their unique challenges and evaluation metrics. (5) Examine Kaggle's updated ranking criteria to assess whether newer repositories demonstrate improved statistical performance. (6) Analyze notebooks associated with Kaggle repositories to investigate if highly-voted notebooks effectively address initial data quality issues or if problems persist.

title: AutoDrift: A Forecast-Aware Concept Drift Detection and Retraining Pipeline in MLOps with CMAPSS
journal: Proceedings of the IEEE International Conference on Big Data Computing Service and Applications, BigDataService
reason: The paper presents AutoDrift, a forecast-aware concept drift detection and retraining pipeline specifically designed for MLOps in time series forecasting and predictive maintenance systems. It discusses deployment, monitoring, and retraining strategies in detail.
deployment_context: Industrial applications in aviation, IoT, large-scale telemetry monitoring, manufacturing plants (PLCs), oil and gas rigs, healthcare (ICU metrics). Specifically uses NASA CMAPSS dataset for engine degradation prediction.
operational_constraints: (1) Traditional monitoring pipelines rely on threshold-based anomaly detection or static retraining intervals, which are reactive, not scalable, and do not generalize well to non-stationary environments. (2) Static retraining policies tend to either overfit (due to frequent retraining) or underperform (due to stale models). (3) Existing metric tracking tools like Prometheus and Grafana lack integration with adaptive retraining logic driven by statistical signals. (4) Most existing drift detection solutions focus on offline detection or require manual intervention for retraining. (5) Drift detection is sensitive to window size and statistical thresholds; misconfiguration can delay detection or cause frequent retraining. (6) Latency introduced by orchestration tools like Airflow can vary across cloud platforms and impact overall pipeline responsiveness.
mlops_practices: (1) Forecast-aware concept drift detection: Integrates Facebook Prophet's confidence intervals with statistical drift detectors (Kolmogorov–Smirnov test, ADWIN) to trigger retraining only when prediction volatility or drift exceeds critical thresholds. (2) Automated retraining pipeline: Apache Airflow orchestrates data ingestion, forecasting, drift detection, conditional retraining, and MLflow logging. (3) Model versioning and tracking: MLflow is used to log model artifacts, performance metrics, drift statistics, and metadata (experiment ID, cycle ID, retraining reason), and to version retrained models in a model registry (Staging/Production). (4) Conditional retraining logic: Retraining is triggered only when the p-value from drift detection is significant (<0.05), prediction error (e.g., MAE) exceeds a moving average threshold over N cycles, AND time since last retraining is greater than N=100 cycles, with a buffer threshold (δ=0.03) to reduce false positives. (5) Cloud-agnostic deployment: The framework is designed to be compatible with Kubernetes, Docker, AWS, and on-premises environments. (6) CI/CD automation: Supports integration into CI/CD systems for seamless deployment and updates. (7) Role-based access control (RBAC): Supports RBAC for model retraining and registry permissions for enterprise deployment. (8) Audit logs and model lineage tracking: MLflow tracking server provides audit logs and model lineage tracking for reproducibility and compliance.
gaps_identified: (1) A critical missing piece in MLOps practices is the incorporation of forecast-aware, drift-triggered retraining mechanisms. (2) Few existing platforms provide an integrated approach that unifies forecasting, drift detection, and retraining in an automated and modular pipeline. (3) Existing commercial MLOps platforms (Google Vertex AI, AWS SageMaker Pipelines, IBM Watson) typically require custom hooks to implement drift-aware retraining logic and often rely on batch evaluations or periodic retraining rather than true forecast-based adaptation. (4) Frameworks like River (formerly Creme) support online learning with drift detectors but are less suited for batch-trained models like Prophet. (5) Prophet does not offer built-in drift detection or automated adaptation, which limits its long-term robustness.
key_findings: AutoDrift, a forecast-aware concept drift detection and retraining pipeline, significantly improves model efficiency and reliability in MLOps. operational_constraints: ["(1) 'Generalization illusion': High laboratory accuracy (e.g., >95% on random-split datasets) fails to translate to novel threats in operational environments, overestimating performance by approximately 12.3%.", "(2) 'Collaboration deficit': Privacy concerns, regulatory restrictions, and competitive considerations prevent organizations from effectively sharing raw threat intelligence, making centralized data aggregation untenable.", "(3) 'Operational fragility' against concept drift: Models rapidly degrade as malware behaviors evolve, and existing systems either lack drift detection or require manual intervention for updates, creating dangerous windows of vulnerability.", "(4) Real-time performance: Graph/sequence models often sacrifice real-time performance, exhibiting average latency >450ms, which is too slow for critical security applications.", "(5) Enterprise scalability: Current federated learning implementations are limited to ~1,000 clients, whereas real-world enterprise requirements exceed 10,000 clients/events per second.", "(6) Infrastructure overhead: The microservice-based deployment requires significant infrastructure, potentially hindering adoption in resource-constrained settings.", "(7) Data collection bias: VM-based data collection may not fully capture behaviors on physical machines, affecting generalizability to bare-metal environments.", "(8) Privacy-accuracy tradeoff: Federated learning incurs a performance cost; the framework achieves 75.1% accuracy compared to 93.7% in centralized training, a gap that cannot be entirely closed without weakening privacy guarantees."]

key_findings: The paper identifies critical limitations in current behavioral malware detection systems, including the 'generalization illusion' (12.3% overestimation of performance), privacy constraints preventing collaborative threat intelligence, and operational fragility against concept drift. quantification: Probabilistic forecasting using quantile regression, ensemble dispersion, EMOS calibration, and conformal prediction, with metrics like CRPS, coverage, and PIT.", "(6) Forecast explainability: Multi-stage interpretation process including SHAP attributions, grouping factors by ontological classes, and natural language explanations.", "(7) Drift monitoring: Detecting changes in input data distributions, variations in model error statistics, degradation of forecasting quality, and semantic inconsistencies, generating drift alerts.", "(8) MLOps automation: Providing automation for model training, CI/CD pipelines, service monitoring, container management, logging, availability control, and operational alerting.", "(9) Model lifecycle management: Managing model storage in a Model Registry, recording versions, metrics, and parameters, verifying model integrity, implementing validation gates (decision gates), and supporting safe deployment strategies (e.g., shadow deployment, rollback mechanisms).", "(10) Model governance: Formalized checkpoints (decision gates) to determine model readiness for subsequent lifecycle stages, with emphasis on regulatory compliance and model traceability, including technical consistency, documentation, reproducibility, and regulatory compliance checks."]
gaps_identified: ["(1) Absence of unified semantic control and interpretable data models: Most architectures lack a semantic backbone defining domain entities, attributes, units, and relationships, leading to duplicated concepts, integration difficulties, and limited extensibility.", "(2) Explainability remains shallow and model-centric: XAI methods are predominantly post-hoc tools (SHAP/LIME) and rarely transformed into domain-relevant, human-readable explanations, with no fully traceable explanation chain from raw data to forecast.", "(3) Underdeveloped monitoring of model degradation and data drift: Drift detection is often treated as an experimental artifact rather than an industrial risk, with limited continuous monitoring pipelines and managed drift control processes, leading to silent model degradation.", "(4) Lack of automated adaptation and lifecycle management of models: Even with drift detection, models are rarely updated via structured adaptation strategies, lacking integrated systems for version control, safe retraining, validation thresholds, integrity checks, and rollback mechanisms.", "(5) Insufficient robustness to incomplete, noisy, and semantically inconsistent data: Many studies rely on 'clean' experimental datasets, ignoring real-world sensor failures, missing SCADA data, corrupted meteorological files, or inconsistent temporal indices.", "(6) Absence of an end-to-end trustworthy architecture integrating all trust attributes: Research efforts are fragmented across individual directions (accuracy, explainability, UQ, drift, feature selection, MLOps) without integrating them into a single, verifiable, semantically consistent, and operationally applicable architecture."]
key_findings: The systematic review of 59 articles on wind and photovoltaic power forecasting reveals that while individual trust attributes (XAI, UQ, drift monitoring, robustness, MLOps, governance) are increasingly incorporated, they remain fragmented at the architectural level, limiting applicability in real operational environments. Six structural gaps are identified, including the absence of semantic data models, shallow model-centric explainability, underdeveloped drift monitoring, lack of automated model lifecycle management, insufficient robustness to real-world data defects, and the absence of integrated end-to-end architectures. Based on these findings, a high-level reference architecture is proposed, formalizing trustworthiness as a system-level property by integrating semantic, technological, and functional trust layers within a unified data and model lifecycle, aiming for reproducible, interpretable, and operationally reliable forecasting.
method: Systematic Literature Review (SLR) following PRISMA 2020 guidelines, followed by narrative and architectural synthesis to propose a conceptual architecture.

title: Exploring Trade-Offs in MLOps Adoption

(3) Choosing between simple, explainable models and advanced, high-accuracy models, where stakeholders often prioritize feature explanations over raw prediction accuracy, especially in early adoption stages or for operational efficiency (Company A, E, G). (4) Balancing the level of data processing (raw vs. processed) with transparency, particularly in sensitive use cases like banking and healthcare, where anonymization for privacy can reduce transparency and traceability (Company A, B, C, G). (5) Deciding between fully automated MLOps frameworks and semi-automated frameworks with human supervision, where complex ecosystems of models or sensitive use cases (e.g., financial crime prevention) necessitate human oversight due to time, cost, and ethical considerations (Company E, B). (6) Lack of in-house organizational knowledge for monitoring, debugging, and quick response for deployed ML models, hindering immediate deployment even when models are ready (Company G).
mlops_practices: (1) Feasibility testing: Company E uses this to assess the viability of a business case before committing resources. (2) Data classification and precaution: Company G collaborates with security teams to classify data sensitivity and establish precautions for data privacy. (3) Event tracking and anonymization: Company G implements event tracking systems in game clients/servers, streaming events to GCP, splitting, aggregating, and filtering for PII, saving both raw and anonymized versions to balance transparency and privacy. (4) Iterative model development: Data scientists at Company E start with simple models to assess performance and quickly iterate to identify improvements. (5) Agreement between monitoring and development teams: Company G emphasizes this for effective delivery of the final product. (6) Production readiness assessments: Suggested as a mitigation to reduce risks in immediate deployment by investigating a company's readiness to deploy and operate models.
gaps_identified: The paper highlights that companies lack guidance to make informed decisions regarding MLOps adoption trade-offs. Specifically, there's a gap in balancing: (1) progressive ML development with trust, (2) pursuing a business case versus terminating it, (3) cloud versus edge computing, (4) simple versus advanced models, (5) level of processing versus transparency, (6) fully automated versus semi-automated frameworks, and (7) operationalizing models versus organizational preparedness. Current MLOps literature is scarce and dispersed, offering limited guidance on how to adopt these practices effectively, especially concerning the strategic management of these trade-offs.
key_findings: This multi-case study involving nine practitioners from seven companies identifies key trade-offs companies face when adopting MLOps, categorizing them into Business, Architecture, Process, and Organization concerns of the BAPO model. The research reveals that companies struggle to balance competing choices such as progressive ML development versus trust, cloud versus edge computing, and fully automated versus human-supervised frameworks. The findings offer suggestions to mitigate these trade-offs, including employing ethical considerations, conducting feasibility studies, adopting hybrid architectural approaches, using ensemble models, ensuring clear documentation and data governance, building feedback loops, and evaluating production readiness. The study contributes by providing a structured understanding of MLOps adoption challenges and practical recommendations for practitioners.
method: Multi-case study with semi-structured interviews involving nine practitioners from seven companies across diverse domains (statistics, banking, healthcare, pharmaceutical, vehicle manufacturing, gaming, mining). Data analysis involved identifying trade-offs from interview transcripts and notes, followed by categorization using the BAPO model and observer triangulation.

title: Model Drift Detection and Automated Retraining in Production ML System

key_findings: The DRIFT-ACT framework, combining real-time statistical drift detection with automated, parameter-efficient retraining (LoRA), significantly improves model robustness and reduces manual maintenance. It achieved over 90% drift detection accuracy and 65% retraining efficiency across diverse datasets (finance, IoT, e-commerce, healthcare). Performance metrics include Detection Accuracy, Retraining Efficiency, Post-Drift Accuracy, Adaptability Score, and Explainability Score. title: A Dataflow-Oriented Approach for Machine-Learning-Powered Internet of Things Applications
journal: Electronics (Switzerland)
reason: The paper proposes Zenoh-Flow, a dataflow programming framework for deploying and managing ML pipelines in decentralized IoT environments, directly addressing operational aspects of ML in production.
deployment_context: IoT / Edge / Cloud-to-Things Continuum (Smart Green Homes project with 13 houses, each with 5 sensors)
operational_constraints: ["(1) Resource constraints on IoT devices (limited processing power, memory, battery life) make traditional host-centric communication protocols like MQTT less suitable.", "(2) Network limitations in IoT environments, including potential for intermittent connectivity and high latency, complicate data transfer and real-time processing.", "(3) Privacy and security concerns in decentralized IoT deployments require data to be decoupled from specific hosts and locations.", "(4) Heterogeneity and sheer volume of data sources in IoT environments make data collection and transformation complex.", "(5) Non-periodic sensor data publishing (data only published when values change beyond a threshold) requires mitigation strategies to create periodic data streams for ML models.", "(6) The need for continuous retraining and redeployment of ML models due to seasonal changes or environmental pattern shifts (e.g., vacations affecting indoor presence prediction) requires robust and automated pipeline management.", "(7) Traditional MLOps practices, often based on a single pipeline running on an isolated machine, fail to address specific challenges of data transfer and parallelization across various pipeline stages in a distributed Cloud-to-Things continuum.", "(8) Data Flow Programming (DFP) models often lack guidance on handling real-time processing, deadlines, computation periodicity, progress tracking, and geo-distributed deployment of operators, placing additional responsibilities on developers."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) Dataflow programming (DFP) pattern: Applications are represented as directed graphs of components (operators) with data flowing between them, generalizing pipes-and-filters for distributed pipelines.", "(2) Declarative application definition: Zenoh-Flow allows defining application graphs declaratively, specifying operators, interconnections, and requirements (e.g., deadlines, hardware access), simplifying development and deployment across the Cloud-to-Things spectrum.", "(3) Distributed registry for operators and applications: Zenoh-Flow stores application descriptors and operators in a distributed registry, enabling automatic deployment and allocation across the Cloud-to-Things continuum.", "(4) Location-transparent communication: Zenoh-Flow establishes communication between operators in a location-transparent manner, allowing components to be deployed anywhere without application or configuration changes.", "(5) Data-centric transport layer (Zenoh protocol): Utilizes Zenoh for peer-to-peer communication, optimizing data transfer, enabling secure communication, and enhancing interoperability across distributed systems, reducing communication hops and avoiding single points of failure.", "(6) Real-time features: Incorporates deadlines, time-stamping (Hybrid Logical Clock with unique IDs for total ordering), and progress tracking for real-time IoT applications.", "(7) Internal state for operators: Zenoh-Flow operators can maintain an internal state, enabling more complex applications requiring historical information and supporting determinism.", "(8) Automated model retraining and redeployment: The pipeline includes a batcher to collect samples, multiple parallel trainers for different ML algorithms, and a comparator to evaluate models. When a model's performance surpasses the current best, it is published to an evaluator for inference, triggering continuous redeployment.", "(9) Containerization with Docker: Each component of the ML pipeline is run as an isolated Docker container, managed via docker-compose for reproducibility and interconnectivity.", "(10) Data preprocessing and synchronization at the gateway: Sensor data are synced and transformed into a periodic stream at the gateway, filling missing values to mitigate non-periodic sensor publishing."]
gaps_identified: ["MLOps best practices, while offering a comprehensive approach for continuous training and deployment, fail to address the specific challenges of data transfer and parallelization across various pipeline stages in decentralized, resource-constrained IoT environments.", "Existing Data Flow Programming (DFP) models lack guidance on handling real-time processing, deadlines, computation periodicity, progress tracking, and geo-distributed deployment of operators, leading to increased development complexity and maintenance efforts.", "Traditional host-centric communication protocols (like MQTT with a central broker) introduce additional communication hops, higher latency, and a single point of failure, which are unsuitable for the decentralized, low-latency requirements of next-generation IoT applications."]
key_findings: Zenoh-Flow, a dataflow programming framework, simplifies the implementation and management of end-to-end ML pipelines in decentralized Cloud-to-Things IoT environments. The framework demonstrated significant improvements in overall performance and network usage (40% less network overhead, lower RTT) compared to an MQTT-based implementation in a real-world smart home scenario, without degrading model accuracy. It achieves this by abstracting communication aspects, enabling declarative application definition, and leveraging a data-centric transport layer (Zenoh protocol) for peer-to-peer communication. This approach reduces development time and costs by shrinking the codebase by 32% and facilitating easier re-definition and scalability of ML pipelines.
method: The paper proposes Zenoh-Flow, a novel framework, and validates its feasibility by implementing a real-world ML pipeline scenario (predicting user presence in smart homes) using both Zenoh-Flow and MQTT. The implementations were compared based on model accuracy (Mathews Correlation Coefficient), network overhead, and Round-Trip-Time (RTT) in a Dockerized testbed environment. The code and dataset are publicly available.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Deployment, Operations, Monitoring, Maintenance
future_research: Future work includes improving the pipeline by creating dedicated trainers and evaluators for each house to enhance model results, and applying Zenoh-Flow to different tasks such as distributed learning, using it as a tool to move model weights for training across various machines.

key_findings: This paper presents an MLOps framework and a four-stage maturity model (Automated Data Collection, Automated Model Deployment, Semi-automated Model Monitoring, Fully-automated Model Monitoring) derived from a systematic and grey literature review. title: Industrial MLOps: a systematic review of architectures and implementation challenges
journal: Production and Manufacturing Research
reason: This systematic literature review directly addresses MLOps architectures and implementation challenges in industrial and manufacturing contexts, aligning perfectly with the research questions.
evidence_level: Empirical (review of studies with real-world deployment or implementation readiness)
deployment_context: Manufacturing and maintenance environments (e.g., additive manufacturing, digital twin technologies, vision-based inspection systems, industrial vacuum pump manufacturing, Industry 5.0)
operational_constraints: ["(1) Heterogeneity in the OT Layer: Diverse industrial communication protocols (e.g., OPC UA, MODBUS, BLE), devices, and data formats create integration barriers for building automated MLOps pipelines.", "(2) Data Scarcity and Quality: Limited availability of high-quality, labeled ground-truth data, especially in manufacturing processes with infrequent operations, restricts effective model training and testing, increasing overfitting risk.", "(3) Model Performance Degradation: Changes in production processes or manual adjustments can impact model performance over time (model drift, data drift), requiring frequent updates and continuous monitoring.", "(4) Scalability for Data Volume and Heterogeneous ML Frameworks: Automated ML systems need to scale quickly to handle sudden surges in data volume and manage different software environments flexibly, particularly when integrating new, diverse hardware.", "(5) Resource Constraints of Edge Devices: Edge devices in industrial IoT settings operate with minimal processing capabilities, restricted memory, and limited network bandwidth, making it difficult to run complex ML models or use standard MLOps tools.", "(6) Interpretability and Explainability: Difficulty in understanding and explaining how AI models make decisions is a fundamental barrier to building trust, especially in manufacturing where prediction errors have serious consequences.", "(7) Non-Technical Challenges: High infrastructure costs, unclear data science role definitions, cultural conflicts between data science and software teams, and a shortage of required skills hinder MLOps adoption."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) End-to-End ML Lifecycle Management: Architectures manage the entire process from data collection and preprocessing to model deployment and continuous monitoring, often with high levels of automation in stages like data preprocessing, feature extraction, model training, and validation.", "(2) Integration with DevOps Practices: Core DevOps principles like continuous integration (CI), continuous delivery (CD), continuous training, and version control are adopted to streamline the ML lifecycle.", "(3) Version Control: Employed for tracking changes, smooth collaboration, reversibility, accountability, security/backup, and scalability across models, data, and code (e.g., using artifact repositories, Model Package Group Store (MPGS), Pipeline Package Group Store (PPGS), Git, Docker registries).", "(4) Continuous Monitoring and Maintenance: Includes initial incoming data monitoring, service logs tracking, pipeline monitoring, infrastructure monitoring, manufacturing process monitoring, and model performance monitoring (e.g., using drift detection algorithms, analytics tools, automated retraining pipelines, MLflow, Kube-Flow, Kubernetes, Promethus).", "(5) Edge Computing and IoT Integration: ML models are deployed directly on resource-constrained IoT edge devices for real-time data processing and decision-making, often in hybrid edge-cloud architectures (e.g., using Thinger.io, Siemens Industrial Edge, DEEPMON microservices).", "(6) Digital Twin and Cyber-Physical System (CPS) Integration: Digital twins are incorporated into MLOps architectures to enable real-time prediction, optimization, monitoring, and control of physical assets, facilitating data exchange and virtual simulations (e.g., using Twin Digital Twin Architecture (TDTA)).", "(7) Microservices and Containerization: Architectures utilize containerization (e.g., Docker, Kubernetes) to encapsulate ML models and their dependencies, and microservices for modular deployment, enabling independent development, deployment, and scaling of functionalities, especially at the edge."]
gaps_identified: ["Fully automated MLOps frameworks remain underdeveloped, often requiring manual intervention and oversight at various stages like initial model development setup, compliance, QA configuration, and infrastructure setup.", "Lack of accurate metrics to detect malfunctions or trigger model retraining, with monitoring often conducted at a 'very basic level' without comprehensive explainability tools or automated retraining triggers.", "Significant gaps exist between conceptual MLOps frameworks and actual industrial practice, particularly regarding continuous training and monitoring for model health.", "Uncertainty about root causes of performance issues (model degradation versus underlying data problems) complicates operationalizing continuous training.", "Limited capabilities of edge AI platforms compared to cloud platforms, especially for 'far edge devices' which are more resource-constrained and cannot use standard MLOps tools.", "Need for more research to explore optimal strategies to balance automation with human oversight in MLOps, especially for complex ML systems requiring domain expertise and careful validation.", "Lack of standardized definitions and a common understanding across the MLOps field, particularly for manufacturing-specific implementations."]
key_findings: The study identifies seven key implementation challenges for Industrial MLOps, including heterogeneity in OT, data quality/availability, operational issues like model drift, scalability, edge device constraints, interpretability, and non-technical factors. It proposes architectural recommendations such as modular design, containerized microservices, and human-in-the-loop approaches to address these challenges. A significant finding is that fully automated MLOps frameworks are underdeveloped, and a balance between automation and human oversight is often necessary for quality control and safety in industrial settings. The research emphasizes the critical role of edge computing, digital twins, and robust monitoring systems in successful industrial AI deployment.
method: Systematic Literature Review (SLR) following PRISMA guidelines, combined with thematic analysis of 12 selected articles. The SLR protocol focused on manufacturing-specific MLOps implementations with detailed architectural specifications and evidence of real-world deployment or clear implementation readiness. Thematic analysis involved generating initial codes for architectures and challenges, assembling them into themes, and validating them through consultation and comparison against the dataset. A recommendation mapping process connected identified challenges to suitable MLOps architectural themes.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: End-to-end MLOps lifecycle, including data collection, preprocessing, model training, deployment, monitoring, and maintenance.
future_research: Empirically investigate the implementation and construction of MLOps architectures, focusing on critical features and encountered challenges. Develop standardized architectures and best practices for MLOps in manufacturing. Improve MLOps adoption across different manufacturing sectors. Investigate efficient scaling of MLOps architectures for large-scale manufacturing operations. Explore optimal strategies to balance automation with human oversight in MLOps.

title: Using FactoryML for Deployment of Machine Learning Models in Industrial Production
journal: IEEE International Conference on Emerging Technologies and Factory Automation, ETFA
reason: The paper introduces FactoryML, a framework specifically designed for deploying and integrating ML models in manufacturing environments, addressing unique operational challenges like air-gapped networks and PLC integration.
deployment_context: Industrial production, manufacturing factory environments, shopfloor, edge devices, air-gapped networks, PLC-controlled machinery.
operational_constraints: ["(1) Low-latency response times are required for manufacturing processes like object detection for human-robot collaboration and regression for CNC cutting.", "(2) Restricted network access to model serving infrastructure after model deployment due to safety, security, and production concerns, potentially requiring air-gapped deployments.", "(3) Pre-existing PLC infrastructure for monitoring, which new solutions should integrate with to minimize complexity and heterogeneity.", "(4) Mission-critical data often cannot leave premises due to quantity or business security concerns, making cloud-based MLOps unsuitable.", "(5) Manufacturing equipment, including ML models, should not be updated during production due to throughput criticality.", "(6) Safety and security requirements, governed by standards like IEC 62443, may restrict both internet and local network access to automation equipment.", "(7) Costly process of obtaining labeled data makes ad hoc retraining and modeling infeasible in factory settings.", "(8) Incompatibility between existing theoretical MLOps frameworks and the reality on the factory shopfloor, where requirements like human-in-the-loop for retraining/redeployment are often not met.", "(9) The possibly disruptive and destructive nature of obtaining labeled data in the factory environment is not accounted for by standard MLOps."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) Model consolidation: Trained models, input/output shapes, and dependencies are combined into a stand-alone artifact with a known model signature, utilizing MLflow.", "(2) Model packaging: Consolidated models are packaged into Docker containers alongside an inference server (MLServer) and compressed into a single file for physical transfer.", "(3) Offline edge deployment: Packaged models can be physically transferred to a deployment machine and deployed directly using FactoryML, supporting air-gapped environments.", "(4) Remote network deployment: Models can be deployed remotely from a development machine to a networked deployment machine using FactoryML CLI.", "(5) PLC integration via OPC UA: An 'OPC-UA Link' container is deployed on the deployment machine to facilitate communication between the ML model and the PLC, using an OPC UA server hosted by the PLC.", "(6) Automated OPC UA configuration generation: FactoryML generates an OPC-UA server configuration file based on the model signature, simplifying integration.", "(7) PLC-based monitoring integration: The 'OPC-UA Link' polls the inference server's metric endpoint and writes monitoring metrics to specified output tags on the OPC-UA server, leveraging existing PLC/SCADA infrastructure for monitoring.", "(8) Model tagging: Added to FactoryML to address business stakeholder concerns about knowing which model is currently deployed and running.", "(9) Safety-net implementation: Configured thresholds in the PLC to prevent specific actions without meeting conditions, and set maximum/minimum drying times, to mitigate risks from sensor drift or model degradation.", "(10) Scheduled labeled data collection: Added to the manufacturing equipment's maintenance schedule to address the infeasibility of ad hoc retraining due to costly data acquisition."]
gaps_identified: ["Standard cloud-based MLOps frameworks are unsuitable for factory environments due to low-latency requirements, restricted network access (air-gapped deployments), and data residency concerns.", "Existing MLOps frameworks lack easy integration with pre-existing PLC infrastructure, which is the backbone of factory IT.", "Current MLOps frameworks do not adequately address the need for packaging and deployment of models without cloud/internet/network access.", "Theoretical MLOps frameworks do not account for the reality of factory shopfloors, where requirements like human-in-the-loop for retraining/redeployment are often not met.", "The disruptive and destructive nature of obtaining labeled data in factory environments is not accounted for by existing MLOps frameworks."]
key_findings: The FactoryML framework simplifies the deployment and integration of ML models in manufacturing factory environments by providing factory-native solutions. It facilitates packaging models into a portable format and enables communication with deployed models via Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs), addressing critical operational constraints like air-gapped networks and low-latency requirements. A qualitative comparison shows FactoryML's unique support for airtight deployment and PLC integration, making it the most suitable MLOps framework for factory settings, though it ideally combines with other frameworks for model training and experimentation. The case study on industrial drying optimization demonstrated FactoryML's ability to integrate with existing factory infrastructure and address stakeholder concerns regarding trust and sensor drift through features like model tagging and PLC-based safety nets.
method: Framework development, qualitative comparison against existing MLOps frameworks, and a case study demonstrating implementation in a Danish manufacturing company.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Deployment, Operations, Monitoring, Integration
future_research: Future work will explore a quantitative evaluation of FactoryML and the incurred overhead in processing when utilizing the framework. Additionally, opportunities to expand the functionality of FactoryML to also enable experimentation support and ML pipelines will be explored.
