title: CloudInfraBot: Reinforcement Learning-Driven Infrastructure Automation for Azure-Based MLOps Environments
journal: 2025 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Green Industrial Electronics and Sustainable Technologies, GIEST 2025
reason: The paper directly addresses the operational challenges of deploying and managing AI/ML models in cloud environments, specifically focusing on infrastructure automation for MLOps pipelines. It proposes a novel RL-driven framework to optimize resource provisioning, which is a core operational aspect of MLOps.
deployment_context: Cloud-based MLOps environments (Azure-specific, but generalizable principles for cloud ML). Focus on CI/CD pipelines for model training, validation, and deployment.
operational_constraints: ["(1) Dynamic workload variability: ML workloads (training, inference) have highly variable and bursty resource demands that traditional static rules or threshold-based autoscalers fail to capture, leading to inefficiencies.", "(2) Resource constraints and cost-performance trade-offs: Optimizing for multiple objectives like latency, cost, utilization, and reliability simultaneously is challenging with static provisioning.", "(3) Disjointed separation between pipeline logic and infrastructure logic: Standard MLOps platforms (e.g., Azure ML Pipelines) support scheduling but rely on external, often static, autoscaling tools, creating inefficiencies when resource needs vary unpredictably across pipeline stages.", "(4) Limited integration of intelligent agents: Most RL-based infrastructure management systems are for general-purpose cloud workloads or academic simulations, lacking deep integration into real-world MLOps workflows.", "(5) Oversimplified heuristics: Existing autoscaling solutions often use basic metrics like CPU utilization thresholds, which fail to capture multidimensional ML workload patterns such as GPU saturation, data transfer delays, and job queue depth.", "(6) Lack of dynamic policy learning: Current systems require predefined policies that may not suit the variability of machine learning pipelines and do not learn from runtime feedback."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) Reinforcement Learning (RL)-driven infrastructure automation: CloudInfraBot uses an RL agent to dynamically allocate compute and storage resources during CI/CD execution, learning optimal policies based on long-term rewards.", "(2) Multi-objective reward function optimization: The RL agent optimizes for latency, cost, utilization, and reliability, allowing for real-time trade-offs.", "(3) Native integration with Azure MLOps ecosystem: The agent integrates with Azure DevOps (for CI/CD triggers), Azure Machine Learning Pipelines (for ML task execution), and Azure Resource Manager APIs (for infrastructure control).", "(4) Continuous learning from historical deployment outcomes: The system continuously learns from past resource utilization metrics, SLA adherence, and deployment outcomes to improve future provisioning decisions.", "(5) Real-time performance feedback loop: The Azure ML Orchestrator sends real-time performance feedback (e.g., training time, failed job logs) to the RL agent for state estimation and policy refinement.", "(6) Pipeline-stage synchronized provisioning: A Pipeline Integrator component synchronizes provisioning decisions with active pipeline stages, ensuring resources are ready before execution and enabling rollback mechanisms.", "(7) State space design for MLOps context: The RL agent's state space includes resource metrics (CPU/GPU usage, memory, disk I/O), pipeline context (training, validation, deployment), job queue length, and provisioning status.", "(8) Action space for granular resource control: Actions include adding/removing nodes, selecting VM types (e.g., GPU vs. CPU), and choosing pricing models (e.g., Spot instances)."]
gaps_identified: ["No existing mainstream MLOps framework integrates reinforcement learning for infrastructure orchestration natively.", "Most RL approaches in cloud research focus on horizontal scaling alone, ignoring VM class selection, GPU requirements, or pricing model decisions (e.g., Spot vs. Reserved), which are essential in ML workloads.", "Existing frameworks do not address the unique challenges of infrastructure control in Azure-based MLOps environments, particularly the need for a unified system that learns from historical deployment outcomes, responds to real-time metrics, and integrates with existing MLOps pipelines."]
key_findings: CloudInfraBot, a novel reinforcement learning-driven infrastructure automation framework, significantly improves MLOps pipeline efficiency in Azure environments. Experimental evaluations across real-world training and deployment workflows demonstrate that CloudInfraBot reduces pipeline latency by up to 31%, lowers compute cost by 33%, and minimizes SLA violations compared to baseline autoscaling techniques. This work establishes a foundation for intelligent infrastructure-as-code systems capable of self-adapting to the complexities of cloud-native AI operations by embedding an RL agent directly into the infrastructure provisioning cycle of Azure ML workflows.
method: The paper proposes CloudInfraBot, an RL-driven infrastructure automation framework. It models infrastructure management as a Markov Decision Process (MDP) and uses a policy gradient-based agent (REINFORCE algorithm) to learn optimal provisioning strategies. The system integrates with Azure DevOps, Azure ML Pipelines, and Azure Resource Manager APIs. Evaluation is conducted through experimental runs on real-world and synthetic MLOps workloads (training, inference, retraining) on Azure, comparing CloudInfraBot against static autoscalers, Kubernetes HPA, and a simulated deep RL autoscaler.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Deployment, Operations, Monitoring, Governance (specifically infrastructure provisioning and management during CI/CD execution for training, validation, and deployment stages).
future_research: ["Cross-Cloud Orchestration: Integrating support for AWS and Google Cloud to enable cloud-agnostic policy learning and provisioning.", "Hierarchical and Multi-Agent RL: Employing advanced RL architectures for more granular control across layered pipeline components (e.g., per-stage resource agents).", "Incorporating Model-Aware Metrics: Using domain-specific performance indicators such as model accuracy drift or data skew to fine-tune reward signals.", "Security and Compliance Awareness: Embedding security posture and compliance metrics (e.g., data locality, encryption standards) into infrastructure decision-making.", "Human-in-the-Loop Controls: Introducing explainable action suggestions and feedback loops from ML engineers and DevOps teams to improve trust and observability."]

operational_constraints: ["(1) High maintenance and support costs for Lambda architecture due to two separate pipelines (batch and streaming).", "(2) Data reconciliation difficulties in Lambda architecture due to different computation engines and storage formats for streaming and batch processing.", "(3) Batch processing latency in Lambda architecture can be a serious issue for real-time monitoring.", "(4) Kappa architecture requires passing all analytical data for processing through the streaming data bus, leading to noticeable delays, inefficient resource use, and increased complexity in administering the streaming bus under high data throughput.", "(5) Balancing low latency with cost efficiency when operating many near-real-time tables using micro-batching (Spark Structured Streaming).", "(6) Data representativeness remains a risk, as EHR, syndromic, and environmental signals each have biases that can lead to drift or spurious correlations.", "(7) Micro-batch streaming model may not meet the lowest-latency requirements for certain facility-level metrics, potentially requiring selective use of true event streaming or vendor systems."]

title: Towards MLCertOps in Aviation
journal: AIAA/IEEE Digital Avionics Systems Conference - Proceedings

deployment_context: Aviation (UAV landing platform identification) - safety-critical, regulated environment.
operational_constraints: ["(1) Changes in model behavior (due to new training data, retraining, or input distribution shifts) typically trigger re-certification, which is time-consuming and expensive, making automatic retraining infeasible in safety-critical aviation.", "(2) Performance degradation due to data drift or embedded biases in training data poses additional safety risks in aviation.", "(3) Traditional certification processes are not well-suited to ML due to its black-box nature, limited explainability, and evolving behavior.", "(4) The need for continuous verification and safety assurance mandated by regulatory bodies (e.g., EASA) goes beyond the current scope of standard MLOps, requiring explicit safety assessments and post-development verification under well-defined criteria.", "(5) Ensuring proactive architecture design to support monitoring (EXP-06) is a pre-deployment requirement that standard MLOps does not explicitly address.", "(6) Defining suitable monitoring metrics for performance degradation and re-certification triggers remains challenging in aviation contexts (e.g., using image brightness as a proxy for performance degradation)."]

title: Cloud-Native LLMOps Meets DataOps: A Unified Framework for High-Volume Analytical Systems
journal: 2025 International Conference on Computing Technologies and Data Communication, ICCTDC 2025
reason: The paper proposes a unified framework for Cloud-Native LLMOps and DataOps, directly addressing the deployment, operation, and maintenance of LLMs in high-volume analytical systems. It discusses specific MLOps practices, operational challenges, and performance metrics.
deployment_context: Cloud-native environment for high-volume analytical systems, specifically enterprise document intelligence (summarization, entity extraction, semantic tagging of invoices, contracts, insurance forms).
operational_constraints: (1) Conventional MLOps pipelines are insufficient for high-volume, generative LLM workloads due to their scale, complexity, and compute requirements. (2) Operational silos frequently occur when LLMOps and DataOps are not integrated in a cloud-native environment, prolonging deployment and creating fragmented monitoring systems. (3) LLM-specific challenges include prompt length variability (exceeding token limits), cold start latency for first model calls, and data skew requiring weighted sampling for retraining batches. (4) Managing LLMs at scale requires careful GPU allocation, model quantization strategies, and cache management. (5) Timely prompt adaptation, while useful, may result in unintended prompt injection vulnerability unless sanitized. (6) Secure and explainable pipelines are an open problem, especially in regulated settings like healthcare or finance.
mlops_practices: (1) Containerized microservices: Architecture units are released as containerized microservices, supporting auto-scaling via Kubernetes HPA, fault tolerance using retries, circuit breakers, and pod health checks, and portability across cloud vendors. (2) Event-driven data pipelines: Inference jobs and retraining pipelines can be started by data triggers or model alerts, powered by Knative Event or Apache Kafka for decoupled and reactive system behavior. (3) Continuous model observability: Centralized observability stack monitors data quality (data drift, latency, record loss) and LLM output (prompt length, response tokens, hallucination scores, user feedback) using Prometheus, Grafana, and OpenTelemetry. (4) Semantic metadata layers: Consumed data is augmented with semantic metadata (entity recognition, document type, contextual tags) to enable subsequent filtering, timely personalization, and dynamic prompt adaptation using cosine similarity. (5) Policy-based automation: IAM and RBAC policies for access control, data encryption at rest and in transit, and audit logging for pipeline actions. (6) Lineage-aware monitoring: DataOps layer manages data ingestion, transformation, validation, and lineage tracking using Apache Airflow and dbt. (7) Continuous feedback loop model retraining and assessment: LLM outputs are stored and used to trigger data corrections or model retraining when anomalies are observed, based on a quality score combining user ratings and model confidence. (8) GitOps-based CI/CD: Git is used to version-control infrastructure and pipeline configurations, with deployments via Argo CD or Flux CD. (9) Model Registry: MLflow tracks all LLM versions, artifacts, and metadata for rollback and reproducibility. (10) Canary Deployments: New LLM releases are deployed to a limited user base or traffic before full production. (11) Optimized inference tools: Hugging Face Transformers, DeepSpeed-MII, and ONNX Runtime are used for LLM serving.
gaps_identified: (1) Conventional MLOps pipelines are not sufficient for the scale, complexity, and compute requirements of high-volume, generative LLM workloads. (2) Existing MLOps tools (MLflow, TFX, Kubeflow) lack first-class support for the compute-intensive, latency-critical needs of LLMs. (3) DataOps tools (Apache Airflow, dbt, Great Expectations) are generally not tied to model execution workflows. (4) LLMOps solutions (Hugging Face Inference Endpoints, NVIDIA NeMo) often have poor data integration across the data-model boundary and limited observability. (5) Few frameworks treat LLM operation pipelines and data pipelines as equivalent and run them on the same architecture. (6) Current proposals for co-designing AI and data workflows (Feature Stores, metadata catalogs) are often optimized for supervised learning and do not scale to generative LLM systems with prompt dynamics, context windows, and inference caching. (7) Secure and explainable pipelines for LLMs remain an open problem, especially in regulated settings.
key_findings: The paper presents a unified Cloud-Native LLMOps and DataOps framework for high-volume analytical systems, demonstrating significant performance improvements over traditional siloed pipelines. A case study in enterprise document intelligence showed a 40% reduction in inference latency (from 4.5 to 2.7 seconds) and an 89% increase in throughput (from 18 to 34 documents/minute). The framework also improved token entropy (3.01 to 3.27) and prompt adaptation scores (0.61 to 0.78) through semantic tagging and dynamic prompt selection, leading to higher feedback quality scores (3.2 to 4.1). These results confirm that integrating LLMOps and DataOps in a cloud-native architecture enhances efficiency, responsiveness, and output quality for real-time AI workloads.
method: Architectural design and experimental evaluation. The authors propose a unified framework and then implement it in a hybrid cloud environment using a specific technology stack (Kubernetes, AWS EKS, Airflow, dbt, Great Expectations, Hugging Face Transformers, DeepSpeed-MII, MLflow, Prometheus, Grafana, GitHub Actions, ArgoCD). A case study on enterprise document intelligence (3,000 documents) was conducted to benchmark performance against traditional, siloed pipelines using metrics like inference latency, throughput, token entropy, prompt adaptation score, and feedback quality score. An ablation study and load stress testing were also performed.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Deployment, Operations, Monitoring, Maintenance, Governance
future_research: (1) Incorporating federated learning for privacy-preserving LLM deployments in distributed settings. (2) Scaling the framework to include LLM routing strategies for selecting optimal models per request. (3) Integration of self-healing and auto-prompting modules for continuous model behavior correction and evolution. (4) Research into zero-trust security frameworks and regulatory compliance solutions for high-risk sectors. (5) Extending the framework with semantic caches and vector databases to accelerate repeated prompt queries.

title: Drift Lens: Real-time unsupervised Concept Drift detection by evaluating per-label embedding distributions
journal: IEEE International Conference on Data Mining Workshops, ICDMW
reason: This paper proposes a real-time unsupervised concept drift detection method, DRIFTLENS, specifically designed for deep learning models in production. It addresses the operational challenge of maintaining model performance in dynamic environments where data distributions change over time, a core MLOps concern.
deployment_context: Generic AI Implementation (text classification, deep learning models in production)
operational_constraints: ["(1) Production systems often produce unlabeled data, and data labeling is costly and time-consuming, limiting the applicability of supervised drift detection methods.", "(2) Drift detection should be performed in real-time to be effective; otherwise, the model may react too late.", "(3) State-of-the-art drift detection solutions show limited effectiveness and efficiency, especially for unstructured data like text, due to sparse and complex input representations.", "(4) Existing unsupervised techniques often lack accuracy or are too complex and slow to be effective, particularly when analyzing raw inputs with sparse representations."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) Real-time unsupervised concept drift detection: DRIFTLENS continuously monitors incoming data in fixed-size windows to detect shifts in data distribution without requiring ground truth labels.", "(2) Embedding-based drift detection: The method exploits the inner representations (embeddings) of deep learning models, which are easier to model and process than raw inputs, to increase accuracy and speed.", "(3) Per-label drift characterization: DRIFTLENS estimates probability distributions and computes Fre´che´t Inception Distance (FID) scores separately for each predicted label, allowing identification of which labels are most impacted by drift.", "(4) Baseline data modeling: An offline phase establishes a reference distribution (baseline) from historical data, against which new data distributions are compared.", "(5) Dimensionality reduction (PCA): Principal Component Analysis is applied to embedding vectors to reduce dimensionality, making distribution modeling and distance computation more efficient.", "(6) Threshold-based alerting: Drift is detected when the FID score for a label exceeds a dynamically estimated threshold, calculated using k-fold cross-validation on the baseline data.", "(7) Continuous monitoring and visualization: The system provides a real-time drift monitor showing FID scores over time for each label, with markers indicating detected drift."]
gaps_identified: ["Current drift detection methods for deep learning models often require ground truth labels, which are expensive and time-consuming to obtain in real production environments.", "Existing unsupervised drift detection techniques are often too slow or inaccurate for real-time application, especially for complex, unstructured data like text.", "State-of-the-art methods do not provide detailed per-label drift characterization, making it difficult for users to understand which parts of the model's learned concepts are most affected by drift."]
key_findings: DRIFTLENS, a real-time unsupervised embedding-based per-label drift detection framework, demonstrates promising results in text topic classification. It achieves high accuracy (e.g., 0.93-1.00 for gradual drift) and significantly faster execution times (less than 1 second per window for typical sizes) compared to state-of-the-art methods like TorchDrift and AlibiDetect, which can be hundreds of times slower. A key contribution is its ability to characterize drift on a per-label basis, providing actionable insights into which concepts are degrading.
method: The paper proposes DRIFTLENS, a novel real-time unsupervised per-label concept drift detection methodology. It involves an offline phase to model baseline per-label embedding distributions using PCA and estimate thresholds, and an online phase to extract embeddings from new data, model their per-label distributions, compute Fre´che´t Inception Distance (FID) scores against the baseline, and detect drift if scores exceed thresholds. Experimental validation was performed on a fine-tuned BERT model for text topic classification using the Ag News dataset, simulating various drift patterns and comparing against two state-of-the-art techniques (TorchDrift and AlibiDetect).
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Monitoring, Maintenance
future_research: ["(1) Execute further experiments with new NLP models and classification tasks.", "(2) Tailor the proposed approach to new data types such as images, videos, and audios with different deep learning models.", "(3) Extend the methodology to different types of drift (not only the introduction of a new concept).", "(4) Enhance the proposed method to deal with drift adaptation correctly."]

title: Streamlining ML Training in Kubernetes: An MLOps Architecture with Kubeflow
journal: IOT 2025 - Proceedings of the 15th International Conference on the Internet of Things 2025
reason: The paper proposes an MLOps architecture for ML training and deployment on GPU-enabled Kubernetes clusters at the Edge, using Kubeflow and GitHub Actions. It discusses the operational aspects of setting up and running ML pipelines in this specific environment.
deployment_context: Edge Computing / GPU-enabled Kubernetes clusters
operational_constraints: (1) Kubernetes and Kubeflow lack native GPU access, requiring the NVIDIA device plugin for efficient model training. (2) Enabling GPU access within individual Kubernetes namespaces is challenging due to the relative novelty of Kubeflow and lack of detailed explanations/practical examples. (3) Resource-intensive tasks on the same machine during training can lead to overload, requiring careful resource management. (4) Building complex Docker images (e.g., EPOS) can exceed free-tier time limits on CI/CD platforms like GitHub Actions. (5) The architecture does not support a private Docker registry, requiring pushing images to public or external registries.
mlops_practices: (1) Automated training pipelines: Kubeflow Pipelines DSL is used to define and orchestrate ML workflows in temporary containers. (2) Containerization: Docker images are used to create self-contained environments with all model dependencies, ensuring consistency, portability, and reproducibility. (3) CI/CD with GitHub Actions: Automates testing, version control, and deployment by triggering pipeline YAML file generation and submission to Kubeflow upon code pushes. (4) GPU resource management: NVIDIA device plugin and Kyverno are used to enable and manage GPU access within Kubernetes namespaces. (5) Dataset management: Datasets are stored in a shared Kubernetes volume (Dataset Registry) and mounted to containers for training. (6) Model versioning and storage: Trained models are stored in a MinIO volume or a shared Kubernetes volume, and model versions are indicated in Kubeflow Pipelines. (7) Pipeline versioning: GitHub Actions trigger pipeline execution based on committed pipeline definitions. (8) Isolated environments: Kubernetes namespaces are employed to organize and isolate components of different ML applications.
gaps_identified: (1) Lack of native GPU access in Kubernetes and Kubeflow, necessitating external plugins like NVIDIA device plugin. (2) Insufficient documentation and practical examples for enabling GPU access within individual Kubernetes namespaces using Kubeflow. (3) Absence of integrated monitoring capabilities in the proposed architecture, which is crucial for real-time monitoring of containers and host systems on edge nodes. (4) Lack of a local Docker registry to speed up image retrieval and reduce energy consumption. (5) Limited options for local CI/CD (e.g., GitLab CI/CD or Jenkins on edge devices) to automate image creation and deployment locally.
key_findings: The paper successfully demonstrates an MLOps architecture integrating Kubeflow and GitHub Actions for streamlined ML training on GPU-enabled Kubernetes clusters at the Edge. It addresses the challenge of GPU access in containerized environments using Kyverno and the NVIDIA plugin, and highlights the benefits of automation for consistency and efficiency. The case study with the EPOS model and IndustryShapes dataset validates the architecture's practical application, while also identifying several areas for future improvement, particularly in monitoring and local registry integration.
method: Architectural design and implementation, followed by a practical application/case study using the EPOS model and IndustryShapes dataset to evaluate the proposed architecture.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: ML Training, Model Deployment, Infrastructure Management, CI/CD
future_research: (1) Integrating monitoring capabilities, potentially using EdgeCloudMon, for real-time monitoring of containers and host systems on edge nodes. (2) Implementing a local Docker registry to speed up image retrieval and reduce energy consumption. (3) Exploring local CI/CD solutions like GitLab CI/CD or Jenkins running on edge devices to automate image creation and deployment locally. (4) Integrating StreamK3s to automate the deployment of trained models directly on edge devices with real-time streaming support.

Management: Recording every execution of an ML system to capture artifacts, enable comparisons, guarantee reproducibility, and track model versions across multiple edge devices.", "(11) Hybrid MLOps Workflows: Distributing tasks between cloud and edge environments to leverage advantages of both, often using frameworks like Kubeflow and Kubernetes for workflow management and deployment.", "(12) operational_constraints: (1) Data drift (covariate and concept drift) in multivariate time series data from sensors can significantly degrade anomaly detection model performance, leading to false positives or undetected anomalies. (3) The challenge of interpreting detected anomalies and drift to identify root causes for maintenance teams. mlops_practices: (1) Real-time data collection and statistical analysis: Continuously gathers and evaluates sensor data from smart manufacturing infrastructure, computing critical statistical indicators using NumPy and Pandas. (2) Sliding window approach: Segments data for detecting transient anomalies and persistent drifts over extended periods. (3) Unsupervised deep learning-based drift detection: Uses a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) to detect data drift in real time by comparing reconstruction error and Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence between current and reference data distributions. (4) Drift interpretation using SHAP values: Analyzes constituent features to offer actionable insights to operational personnel, identifying underlying causes like variations in sensor readings. (5) Automated model retraining: Activates in response to identified drifts, incorporating recent data via TensorFlow and Keras to adapt the model to shifting data distributions without disrupting ongoing operations. gaps_identified: (1) Traditional analytical methods struggle with the complexity and high dimensionality of multivariate time series data for anomaly detection. (2) Supervised drift detection techniques are constrained by the availability of labeled data, limiting their applicability in real-world MLOps scenarios. (3) Existing systems may lack robust mechanisms for real-time monitoring and automatic model retraining to maintain performance under data drift. (4) The need for improved concept drift detection to adapt to evolving sensor relationships. (6) Scalability challenges for MLOps frameworks when dealing with increasing numbers of sensors and larger datasets.
key_findings: The proposed MLOps-driven framework effectively integrates a VAE-based anomaly detection system with SHAP-based interpretability for real-time data drift detection in multivariate time series. Experimental evaluation on real-world datasets from smart factories demonstrated that the approach improved the F1 score by up to 0.18, maintaining robust model performance under data drift conditions. The system successfully identified both sudden anomalies and gradual data drift, providing actionable insights for predictive maintenance and ensuring reliability and operational efficiency in dynamic industrial environments.
method: The research proposes a real-time data drift detection mechanism using an unsupervised deep learning approach. It involves continuous data collection and statistical analysis with a sliding window, online drift detection using a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) to monitor reconstruction error and Kullback-Leibler divergence, and drift interpretation using SHAP values. The system includes an automated model retraining capability triggered by detected drifts. The methodology was evaluated through a case study on a smart factory predictive maintenance system using real-world sensor data.

title: HarmonE: A Self-adaptive Approach to Architecting Sustainable MLOps
journal: Lecture Notes in Computer Science
reason: The paper directly addresses the operational challenges of deploying and maintaining ML models in production, specifically focusing on sustainability (energy consumption, accuracy, cost) and proposing a self-adaptive MLOps architecture to manage these trade-offs. operational_constraints: ["(1) Dynamic and non-stationary nature of traffic patterns (time-of-day, accidents, seasonal variations) requires frequent model updates to retain accuracy.", "(2) Frequent retraining of ML models is computationally intensive, leading to increased energy consumption and associated operational costs, posing a long-term environmental and economic sustainability challenge.", "(3) Traditional MLOps practices often overlook environmental and economic impacts, focusing primarily on technical robustness.", "(4) Existing self-adaptation approaches typically rely on instantaneous reactions, leading to aggressive adaptations that can increase operational overhead.", "(5) Balancing multiple sustainability dimensions (technical, environmental, economic, social) simultaneously during runtime operations is complex."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) Self-adaptive MLOps pipeline using MAPE-K loop: Continuously monitors metrics (prediction accuracy, energy consumption, data distribution shifts) and triggers adaptation strategies based on predefined sustainability goals and thresholds.", "(2) Model Retraining: Triggered by the Managing System in response to runtime uncertainties like data/model drift or sustained performance degradation, with new models and training data versioned.", "(3) Data and Model Versioning: Stores trained models with their corresponding training datasets in a Versioned Model Repository (VMR) to enable reuse when similar traffic conditions reoccur, avoiding redundant retraining.", "(4) Model Switching: Dynamic selection among a predefined spectrum of pre-trained models (e.g., LR, SVM, LSTM) based on runtime observations and adaptation boundaries for metrics like energy consumption and inference accuracy.", "(5) Performance Monitoring: Periodically aggregates runtime metrics such as prediction accuracy (R2 score), normalized energy usage (Ēi), and data distribution shifts (KL Divergence) to assess system health.", "(6) Uncertainty Detection: Compares monitored metrics (e.g., EMA of performance score, normalized energy, drift metric) against design-time defined adaptation boundaries and dynamic thresholds to identify when adaptation is necessary.", "(7) Strategy Evaluation: Selects appropriate adaptation strategies (model switch, retraining, model compression, quantization) from a Tactics Repository based on the current runtime context and historical performance metrics.", "(8) Explicit Sustainability Goals: System architects define measurable sustainability goals (e.g., reducing energy consumption, improving accuracy, minimizing costs) and adaptation thresholds at design time, captured in a Decision Map (DM)."]

key_findings: HarmonE, a self-adaptive MLOps architectural approach, effectively balances predictive performance and energy consumption in ML deployments. In a Digital Twin of an Intelligent Transportation System, HarmonE reduced energy consumption by 54.5% compared to LSTM+PRT while retaining 95% of its predictive accuracy. The system achieves this by dynamically adjusting configurations through model switching, selective retraining, and versioned model reuse, triggering adaptations only when predefined sustainability boundaries are reached, thus outperforming periodic retraining methods in resource efficiency and long-term operational overhead. The decision-making process itself is highly efficient, consuming only 1.3660% of total system energy.
method: The paper proposes an architectural approach (HarmonE) integrating self-adaptive capabilities into MLOps pipelines using the MAPE-K loop. It defines a Managed System (training and inference subsystems), a Decision Map for sustainability goals, and a Managing System (Knowledge Base, Monitor, Analyzer, Planner, Executor). The approach was validated through experiments on a Digital Twin of an Intelligent Transportation System for traffic flow prediction, comparing HarmonE against eight baselines (fixed models, models with periodic retraining, and switching-only approaches) using real-world traffic data with induced drift. Metrics included energy consumption, predictive accuracy (R2, MSE), and inference time.

future_research: Future work will focus on validating HarmonE in more diverse and complex real-world environments, including domains such as computer vision and natural language processing. title: From Prototype to Production: A Checklist for Production-Ready AI Focused on Data Quality, Monitoring, and Human Oversight
journal: Conference Proceedings - IEEE SOUTHEASTCON
reason: The paper provides a comprehensive checklist for deploying and maintaining AI systems in production, focusing on data quality, monitoring, and human oversight. It directly addresses operational aspects of MLOps.
deployment_context: Generic AI implementation (software-centric ML deployment contexts), with examples from fraud detection and healthcare.
operational_constraints: (1) ML system behavior relies heavily on data and cannot be fully specified in advance, making exhaustive testing difficult and introducing new failure modes as the environment changes. (2) Obtaining ground truth labels for model performance monitoring often has a delay, requiring the use of proxy metrics. (3) Humans can become complacent or overwhelmed when overseeing AI if the system is too autonomous or complex, leading to automation bias or algorithmic aversion. (4) Expecting humans to catch rare errors in a split-second with no context is a recipe for failure in human oversight.
mlops_practices: (1) Defining and enforcing data schemas with constraints (e.g., feature types, ranges, distributions) and automatically validating them during data ingestion, before model training, and during production inference. (2) Continuously monitoring input data for drift and missing values using metrics like feature means, frequencies, and out-of-range values, with careful threshold tuning for alerts. (3) Versioning datasets, features, and labels, keeping immutable snapshots for each model release, and documenting data provenance and preprocessing steps. (4) Monitoring model performance using business-relevant KPIs (e.g., precision/recall, AUC) on rolling windows, and tracking prediction confidence and calibration. (5) Implementing explainability and traceability features (e.g., input data, model output, confidence, rationale, feature importance) to support human investigation and oversight. (6) Defining safe rollback or disable switches and retraining triggers, and staging canary or shadow deployments before full rollout. (7) Establishing escalation thresholds and human-in-the-loop interventions for high-risk decisions or anomalies, with clear authority and ability for humans to override AI decisions. (8) Training and supporting human operators to understand AI capabilities and limitations, and designing workflows to keep them engaged and vigilant.
gaps_identified: The paper implicitly highlights gaps in standard ML development by emphasizing the need for extensive operational practices beyond model architecture and offline accuracy. Specifically, it notes that 'many AI projects face failure since the data is messy, since AI is not monitored properly, or possibly end-users are not involved enough,' indicating that current development practices often overlook these critical production aspects. It also points out that 'ensuring meaningful oversight is not trivial; humans can become complacent or overwhelmed if the AI is too autonomous or complex,' suggesting a gap in designing effective human-AI interaction for oversight.
key_findings: The paper presents a checklist for production-ready AI systems, structured around three pillars: data quality, continuous monitoring, and human oversight. It argues that high accuracy on a test dataset is insufficient for production, emphasizing the need for reliable data, continuous performance monitoring, and human intervention for exceptions and ethical nuances. The work contributes a set of best practices and guidelines, supported by case studies and references, to bridge the gap from prototype to production, ultimately fostering robust and trustworthy AI systems.
method: Literature review and synthesis of best practices to compile a checklist.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Deployment, Operations, Monitoring, Maintenance, Governance
future_research: Automating more checklist items, such as automated bias detectors, advanced drift detection algorithms, and better human-AI interfaces for oversight.

title: MLPacker: A Unified Software Tool for Packaging and Deploying Atomic and Distributed Analytic Pipelines
journal: 2022 7th International Conference on Smart and Sustainable Technologies, SpliTech 2022
reason: The paper introduces MLPacker, a tool for packaging and deploying ML models and analytic pipelines in production, addressing challenges in MLOps such as distributed deployments, streaming, and decoupling from the training phase.
deployment_context: Edge-fog-cloud continuum, distributed systems, REST API, streaming data processing, local and remote deployments, containerized environments (Docker).
operational_constraints: (1) Variety of computing environments (Cloud, distributed, edge, hybrid) complicates deployment. (2) Different programming languages, frameworks, or libraries for ML models make automation of deployments a hurdle. (3) Each ML library often has its own recommended deployment method, hindering unified automation. (4) Traditional MLOps tools often focus on specific ML algorithms or frameworks (e.g., Torchserve, TFX). (5) Lack of support for distributed analytic pipelines in many existing tools. (6) Limited support for packaging in streaming mode; most tools only offer REST API. (7) Many MLOps libraries (e.g., MLFlow) require integration into the training phase, forcing data scientists to handle deployment tasks.
mlops_practices: (1) Model/Pipeline Packaging: MLPacker packages ML models and analytic pipelines into reusable software artifacts, supporting both REST API and streaming modes. (2) Distributed Deployment: Enables deploying different stages of an analytic pipeline on distinct machines (edge, fog, cloud layers) using publish-subscribe technologies for inter-machine communication. (3) Atomic Deployment: Supports deploying an entire pipeline on a single machine. (4) Decoupled Deployment: The tool is independent of the training phase, allowing data scientists to focus on model development without integrating packaging/deployment libraries. (5) Containerization: Supports deploying models/pipelines within Docker containers for portability and isolation, or natively on the OS. (6) Infrastructure as Code (IaC) for Deployment: Uses Ansible roles to install Docker, pull images, and execute containers on target machines for remote deployments. (7) Configuration-driven Deployment: Utilizes an extension of PADL (Analytical Pipeline Definition and Deployment Language) to define analytic pipelines, meta-information, connector properties, and infrastructure for deployment.
gaps_identified: (1) No existing tool simultaneously supports deployment in API mode, deployment in streaming mode, consideration for both atomic and distributed analytic pipelines, and independence from the training phase. (2) A significant gap exists in research for a unified approach to handle these four features concurrently. (3) None of the analyzed tools provide support for packaging a pipeline in streaming mode.
key_findings: MLPacker is a novel tool addressing key MLOps challenges by providing a unified solution for packaging and deploying atomic and distributed analytic pipelines. It uniquely supports both REST API and streaming deployment modes, and crucially, decouples the deployment process from the model training phase. The tool's architecture, leveraging components like RestMe, StreamMe, Bundler, and Deploying service, enables flexible deployments across local/remote and containerized/native environments, as demonstrated through three distinct case studies involving an LSTM Autoencoder for anomaly detection.
method: The paper describes the design and implementation of a software tool (MLPacker). It includes a review of related work to identify gaps, details the architecture and components of MLPacker, explains its input configuration (using PADL extension), and outlines the operational workflow. The tool's capabilities are validated through a scenario with three distinct case studies demonstrating different deployment configurations (API mode, atomic stream pipeline, distributed stream pipeline).
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Packaging, Deployment, Serving, Monitoring (future work)
future_research: (1) Support for ML training pipelines. (2) Creation of a wider methodology integrating missing MLOps phases such as monitoring and retraining. (3) Automatic adaptation of models for edge deployment using techniques like weight pruning and quantization. (4) Inclusion of labels in PADL to target computing layers (e.g., Kubernetes) instead of specific machines. (5) Support for replicas of pipeline steps and horizontally distributed pipelines. (6) Extension to support other programming languages, frameworks, connectors, and API mode pipeline deployments. (7) Integration of environment variables to avoid exposing passwords in PADL.

operational_constraints: ["(1) OT network air-gap: Traditional IoT protocols like LoRa or SigFox (0G) are insufficient for civil engineering SHM if data are not preprocessed by the sensor hardware through edge computing before being sent, requiring 4G/5G for high sampling transmission (500Hz).", "(2) Limited free vibration signal duration: The short span of the pilot bridge resulted in free vibration signals lasting less than 8 seconds, requiring precise capture and high sampling rates.", "(3) Temperature sensitivity of sensors: Sensors were sensitive to temperature, causing offsets in the signal and generating false triggering due to RMS threshold exceedance, leading to invalid signal capture.", "(4) Noise interference: Under certain climate circumstances, noise was induced, causing false triggering issues similar to temperature sensitivity.", "(5) High computational cost for cloud-only processing: Storing and processing the entire high-frequency vibration dataset solely in the cloud would be prohibitively expensive due to large data volumes.", "(6) Power availability: Measurement equipment with high measurement and transmission rates cannot withstand many hours without power, necessitating solar panels and accumulators in remote locations.", "(7) Uncertainty in modal identification: Working with short free vibration signals and low-cost MEMS sensors leads to higher uncertainty in modal identification compared to high-sensitivity, high-price accelerometers."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) Hybrid Edge-Cloud Architecture: On-premises middleware (Mosquitto, Node-RED, InfluxDB) handles real-time data ingestion, preprocessing (filtering, noise reduction, FFT analysis), and local storage. Cloud platform (Microsoft Azure, Databricks, MLflow) provides scalable data lake storage, batch processing, AutoML for model training, and MLOps for model management and deployment.", "(2) Trigger-based Data Capture: Data capture is activated only during train crossing events (initial pulse signal) to conserve bandwidth and battery life.", "(3) Real-time FFT Analysis at Edge: On-premises middleware performs Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) analysis on free vibration segments to identify prominent vibrational frequencies in real-time.", "(4) Unsupervised Learning for Baseline: K-means clustering is used on empirical vibration data (three principal frequency peaks) to group similar vibration patterns into 'normal operating condition' clusters, establishing baseline behavior profiles.", "(5) Automated Machine Learning (AutoML): Databricks AutoML is leveraged to automatically train and evaluate different ML algorithms (e.g., LightGBM, decision trees, random forests) and tune hyperparameters for classifying vibration patterns.", "(6) MLflow for Model Management: MLflow is used to track, version, stage, and deploy machine learning models into production in a serverless, scalable way, managing the model lifecycle.", "(7) Continuous Learning and Retraining: Historical sensor data stored in a cloud data lake is regularly used to retrain and update the machine learning model, enhancing accuracy and adapting to gradual changes (seasonality, aging).", "(8) Remote Calibration and Offset Adjustment: Regular remote calibration of MQTT sensors is performed when notable temperature changes are forecast to correct for temperature-induced signal offsets.", "(9) Digital Twin Visualization: A BIM model of the bridge integrates real-time sensor key_findings: This pilot study successfully demonstrated a digital twin solution for railway bridge SHM using low-cost wireless accelerometers and a hybrid edge-cloud architecture. The system achieved ≥99.9% classification accuracy for detecting abnormal vibration patterns, significantly reduced installation complexity by over 50% (from 16h to 3h), and estimated a 75% reduction in maintenance complexity compared to traditional wired systems. The hybrid architecture enabled substantial cost savings (e.g., 90% reduction in cloud storage costs for processed data) by performing real-time processing at the edge and leveraging cloud for scalable batch analytics and ML model management, proving a viable and scalable model for widespread SHM deployment.
method: Case study with a real-world pilot implementation on an in-service railway bridge over two years. The methodology involved sensor deployment, data acquisition, hybrid edge-cloud data processing (FFT analysis at edge, k-means clustering in cloud), unsupervised learning to define normal vibration patterns, synthetic damage data generation (10% loss in steel modulus of elasticity), supervised learning (LightGBM classifier via AutoML), and continuous learning with MLflow for model management and retraining. Performance was evaluated based on classification accuracy, cost-effectiveness, and ease of deployment.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Data Ingestion, Data Preprocessing, Model Training, Model Evaluation, Model Deployment, Model Monitoring, Model Retraining/Continuous Learning
future_research: Extend to broader production deployments, improve digital twin model fidelity using advanced physics simulations and expanded sensor data, better quantify damage extent, localization, and progression. Explore applications for monitoring other infrastructure (e.g., wind towers). Shift more analytics onto intelligent sensors with energy harvesting and edge computing (embedded ML chips, e-peas MPPT, Voltaic Systems solar panels, Li-ion supercapacitors, Edge Impulse for edge processing) to reduce data throughput burden on middleware and cloud.

title: Consolidating TinyML Lifecycle With Large Language Models: Reality, Illusion, or Opportunity?
journal: IEEE Internet of Things Magazine
reason: The paper directly addresses the challenges and automation of the TinyML lifecycle, including deployment, optimization, and maintenance on resource-constrained devices, using LLMs. It provides a framework and empirical evaluation of operational aspects.
deployment_context: TinyML on resource-constrained IoT devices (e.g., microcontrollers like Arduino Nano 33 BLE Sense) for computer vision tasks (fruit classification).
operational_constraints: ["(1) Kilobyte-scale memory, reduced computational unit sizes, low clock frequencies, and simplified architectural features (often supporting only integer operations) on TinyML target devices.", "(2) Need to balance energy efficiency, processing speed, and compact model size against potential reductions in accuracy due to limited resources for long autonomous operation on low-capacity batteries.", "(3) Significant diversity in computing units and instruction set architectures (hardware heterogeneity) creates substantial challenges for TinyML development, complicating scalability and compatibility.", "(4) Requirement for device-specific models due to differing user requirements, such as resource and energy constraints.", "(5) Manual intervention in TinyML lifecycle management is resource-intensive and often impractical, especially in large-scale IoT deployments with hundreds or thousands of heterogeneous edge devices.", "(6) Limited ability of the LLM-based system to self-correct, especially in multi-step processes where errors in early stages propagate and compound, leading to high failure rates and resource consumption in complex tasks like sketch generation.", "(7) Monetary costs associated with recursive LLM API calls can accumulate significantly in large-scale IoT deployments, despite appearing minimal at a per-run level.", "(8) High variance in execution time for error-prone stages introduces unpredictability, posing challenges for time-sensitive IoT applications."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) LLM-powered automation for data processing: GPT-4o assists in automating preprocessing steps like data cleaning, normalization, and augmentation.", "(2) LLM-powered automation for model conversion and quantization: The framework generates scripts and configurations to convert models into formats suitable for resource-constrained devices (e.g., TensorFlow Lite).", "(3) LLM-powered automation for deployment sketch generation: The LLM generates C++ code tailored to specific hardware and application requirements for deploying models on devices like Arduino Nano 33 BLE.", "(4) Adaptive Prompt Engineering: Customized prompt templates are used for different TinyML lifecycle stages (e.g., model quantization, sketch generation) to guide the LLM with precise, context-specific instructions.", "(5) Error Handling and Iterative Refinement: A feedback-driven retry mechanism parses compilation or deployment errors, updates the prompt, and re-queries the LLM to regenerate code that better aligns with hardware constraints or resolves issues, without manual prompt rewriting.", "(6) Tool Integration and Orchestration: A 'Lifecycle Middleware' integrates the LLM with specialized embedded ML libraries (e.g., TFLite), device-specific compilers (e.g., arduino-cli), and lightweight runtimes, managing workflows and data inputs.", "(7) Human-in-the-loop Interventions: The framework is designed to reduce human involvement but acknowledges that certain stages may still benefit from expert oversight, though specific mechanisms for this are not detailed beyond 'targeted suggestions or alerts'.", "(8) LLM Tracing: Tools like LangSmith are used for monitoring, tracing, and storage management of LLM interactions to allow for debugging and optimization."]
gaps_identified: ["Current LLM technologies and the proposed framework have notable limitations in achieving full automation for TinyML lifecycle management, particularly in complex tasks.", "The system has a limited ability to self-correct, especially in multi-step processes where errors in early stages propagate and compound.", "Reliability issues exist in complex tasks such as sketch generation, leading to high failure rates and resource consumption variability.", "Constrained generalizability of LLM-generated code across highly heterogeneous hardware platforms is a challenge.", "LLMs struggle to reliably adapt to evolving application needs with minimal supervision.", "The high resource demands (computation and cost) of LLM API calls, especially for iterative error correction, pose a challenge for scalability and cost-effectiveness in large-scale IoT deployments."]
key_findings: The paper introduces a framework leveraging LLMs (GPT-4o) to automate key stages of the TinyML lifecycle, including data processing, model conversion/quantization, and deployment sketch generation. Empirical evaluation on a fruit classification task for an Arduino Nano 33 BLE board shows that LLMs can significantly improve development efficiency for tasks like model quantization (100% success, 6.09s avg. time) and data processing (90% success, 47.76s avg. time). However, complex tasks like sketch generation remain a bottleneck, exhibiting a low success rate (36.7%) and high resource consumption (60.55s avg. time, 13,321 avg. tokens), highlighting challenges in error resilience and generalizability across heterogeneous hardware. The work demonstrates the opportunity for AI-assisted embedded system development while underscoring the need for further refinement in prompt engineering and error handling.
method: The authors developed a 'Lifecycle Middleware' framework that integrates OpenAI's GPT-4o with TinyML tools. They conducted an empirical evaluation through a case study involving a deep neural network-based fruit classification application on an Arduino Nano 33 BLE board. Thirty full runs were performed for data processing, 8-bit model quantization/conversion, and sketch generation. Metrics collected included task completion success rate, execution time, and token consumption, with a maximum of five iterations for error resolution. The framework's implementation spans approximately 1,578 lines of Python code.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Data Processing, Model Optimization (Quantization & Compression), Model Conversion, Deployment (Sketch Generation), Monitoring (LLM Tracing for interactions).
future_research: Future research should focus on enhancing error resilience through LLM-based multi-agent systems (combining local and cloud LLMs) or lightweight embedded LLMs as local proxies, and adaptive prompt templates. Streamlining resource-intensive processes like sketch generation can be achieved by improving prompt engineering, workflow design, exploring domain-specific languages (DSLs), and hybrid approaches combining LLM-generated drafts with traditional compilers. Adaptive task allocation for cost efficiency, routing simpler tasks to smaller, cost-effective models and complex queries to larger models, is also a promising direction, potentially extending to specialized LLMs for dynamic updates and functionality shifts.

title: Data-driven predictive maintenance for two-stroke marine diesel engines using machine learning and MLOps
journal: Journal of Ocean Engineering and Science

deployment_context: Industrial AI (Predictive Maintenance for two-stroke marine diesel engines on container vessels)
operational_constraints: ["(1) Scarcity of labeled fault data and highly imbalanced operational datasets (healthy conditions dominate) in maritime sector, making direct classification challenging.", "(2) Difficulty of generalizing models to different vessels and operating conditions, even with the same engine type, due to variations in operational profiles and data quality.", "(3) Integration of multiple heterogeneous sensors often leads to interoperability issues and fragmented data streams.", "(4) Real-time onboard deployment is constrained by cybersecurity and computational limitations.", "(5) Practical data issues such as missing values, sensor noise, outliers, and intentional operational interventions by crew complicate model reliability.", "(6) Lack of standardized criteria for splitting datasets into healthy and faulty periods, relying on experience and trial-and-error, which can affect prediction accuracy.", "(7) Subjectivity in selecting moving average widths and threshold values for pattern monitoring due to lack of established research, leading to variations in fault detection and potential discrepancies from standard SPC practices (e.g., 1σ vs. 2σ/3σ thresholds).", "(8) Intentional manipulation of operational variables (e.g., power, Pmax) by onboard crew to avoid unnecessary alarms, which distorts true relationships and undermines model performance if used for training.", "(9) Data privacy, proprietary issues, and heterogeneous data formats across different shipping companies and vessel types complicate data sharing and model training for enhanced generalization.", "(10) Many commercial MLOps solutions operate as 'black boxes,' limiting transparency and making model interpretation difficult for domain experts, and access to internal model details may be restricted."]

key_findings: This study validates the expected operational behavior modeling method for predictive maintenance of marine diesel engines using MLOps, demonstrating its effectiveness for scuffing incident prediction. The MLOps tool (Amazon SageMaker) significantly accelerates model training and testing, making data-driven approaches more accessible to maritime specialists and data scientists. While the method shows limited generalization ability across different vessels, a practical approach combining 3-day and 7-day Exponential Moving Averages (EMA) for early warning and confirmation, particularly with temperature-based targets, yields a 50% success rate in detecting incidents, highlighting its potential for alarm generation and maintenance scheduling in shipping practice.
method: The study employs a quantitative approach, conducting three case studies based on real operational data from two different vessels. It utilizes supervised machine learning algorithms (Simple Linear Regression, Decision Tree, Random Forest, XGBoost, Support Vector Regression) within an MLOps framework (Amazon SageMaker) to implement an 'expected operational behavior modeling' method. This method involves training models on healthy data, calculating residuals, and using moving averages (SMA, EMA) with thresholds (1σ) to detect anomalies. Sensitivity analyses are performed on moving average widths, target variables, and the impact of operational features. Model performance is evaluated using MSE and R² metrics, and qualitative comparisons are made between manual coding and MLOps workflows.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Data preparation, Model training, Model deployment, Model monitoring, Model governance (implied by MLOps framework for repeatable and scalable management)
future_research: Future research should focus on improving the generalization ability of expected behavior modeling with MLOps for engine fault detection, considering data quality, more refined data preprocessing, and enhanced hyperparameter tuning. Exploring alternative or complementary monitoring strategies like Statistical Process Control (SPC) techniques, developing customized MLOps tools for marine onboard systems, and integrating deep learning techniques (e.g., LSTM) to account for temporal dynamics are also suggested. Additionally, developing other high-level engine degradation indicators from alternative datasets (e.g., scavenge port inspection reports) and addressing data privacy and security challenges for broader data sharing are crucial.

title: MLOps in Practice: Requirements and a Reference Architecture from Industry
journal: Lecture Notes in Computer Science
reason: This paper proposes a reference architecture and 32 requirements for MLOps based on a systematic review of industrial gray literature and expert interviews. It directly addresses the deployment, operation, and maintenance of ML/AI models in production.
paper_type: Systematic Literature Review and Expert Validation
evidence_level: High (Systematic review of gray literature, expert interviews)
deployment_context: Generic MLOps environments across various industries (e-Commerce, FinTech, Marketing, Software) as derived from gray literature and expert interviews.
operational_constraints: (1) Preventing vendor lock-in can be problematic as most organizations select and adopt a specific cloud provider or data/ML platform and prioritize time-to-market over flexibility and portability. (2) Most training jobs do not need auto-scaling since their workload is stable and their resource usage is predictable. (3) Monitoring models/data and triggering corrective actions may not have real-time constraints in practice, as diagnosing an issue and finding a resolution often require human intervention. (4) Implementing self-healing for model issues may be problematic, as interventions are usually complex and ad-hoc. (5) Compliance requirements are primarily related to raw data, which may contain sensitive data, rather than model output. (6) Practitioners rarely test models for robustness to model attacks.
mlops_practices: (1) Infrastructure as Code (IaC) for automating the provisioning and management of development, staging, and production environments. (2) Modular and containerized pipeline components for reusability and ease of composition. (3) Exporting executable ML pipelines from notebooks into production-ready workflow definition files. (4) Recording and querying metadata of ML experiments (code/data versions, configurations, output assets, performance metrics) for reproducibility and troubleshooting. (5) Training scaling strategies including model check-pointing, distributed training, use of hardware accelerators (GPU/TPU), AutoML, and training with data set slices. (6) Validation tests for all ML assets (data, code, models) to prevent training-serving skew and assess quality. (7) Prioritization and scheduling of validation tests and training jobs (e.g., running long tests during off-hours, training on small datasets). (8) Packaging models, scoring scripts, and dependencies into container images for ease of deployment. (9) Supporting various model deployment strategies: shadow, canary, and blue/green. (10) Supporting various model serving patterns: model-as-service (web/API/messaging), precompute (batch predictions), and model-as-dependency (embedded or downloaded at runtime). (11) Continuous monitoring and alerting for issues in models (data/concept drift), data, pipelines, and infrastructure, with triggers for corrective actions. (12) Custom actionable dashboards for monitoring models, pipelines, and infrastructure. (13) Supporting different pipeline triggering models: metrics-driven, schedule-driven, event-driven, and ad-hoc manual. (14) Version control and lineage tracking for ML assets (models, features, pipelines, code). (15) ML asset storage and marketplace (model registry, feature store, pipeline store). (16) Access control, governance, and regulatory compliance for ML assets, including policy-as-code for enforcement. (17) Ensuring interpretability of models and providing tools to generate and visualize explanations. (18) General data platform services (ingestion, storage, discovery, labeling, cleaning, transformation) and ML platform services (feature engineering, model exploration, hyperparameter tuning, model validation). (19) Establishing standards and practices for MLOps processes to improve quality and interoperability. (20) Treating ML assets as first-class citizens in DevOps processes, integrating unit, integration, and other tests into CI/CD pipelines.
gaps_identified: The paper identifies a problem for organizations in selecting the most appropriate tools and design options for MLOps due to the rapid proliferation of tools and gray literature. It implies a gap in consolidated, technology-independent guidance for MLOps implementation, which its reference architecture aims to address.
key_findings: This paper proposes a reference architecture and 32 requirements for MLOps, derived from a systematic review of 58 industrial gray literature sources and validated/refined through semi-structured interviews with six MLOps experts. The findings highlight the need for flexible, portable, and scalable infrastructure, comprehensive lifecycle management for ML assets, and robust monitoring and governance mechanisms. The research provides a technology-independent baseline to guide organizations in making informed design and technology choices for their MLOps environments and identifies key research challenges for widespread ML model adoption.
method: Systematic Gray Literature Review (SGLR) of 58 articles, blogs, white papers, and slides, followed by expert-based validation and refinement through a survey and semi-structured interviews with six MLOps practitioners.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: End-to-end MLOps lifecycle, covering Infrastructure, Platform Services (Pipeline Development/Execution, Experimentation/Training/Testing, Model Deployment/Serving, Monitoring/Feedback Loops, ML Asset Life Cycle Management/Governance, General Platform Services), Pipelines Layer (Build, Release, Data, Feature Engineering, Experimentation, Training, Scoring/Serving, Monitoring), and cross-cutting Governance and Automation modules.
future_research: Conduct a similar study on the operationalization of large language models (LLMOps). Study the effectiveness of LLMs in extracting requirements and architectural components from gray and academic literature.

title: Tracking end-of-life stage of chemicals: A scalable data-centric and chemical-centric approach
journal: Resources, Conservation and Recycling
reason: The paper describes a data engineering pipeline for preparing data for ML models in the context of chemical end-of-life tracking, explicitly mentioning scalability for production-level MLOps and addressing data/concept drift.
deployment_context: Environmental monitoring and risk assessment for chemical end-of-life (EoL) management, specifically tracking chemical flows from industrial facilities to off-site EoL activities (recycling, disposal, treatment, energy recovery, sewerage).
operational_constraints: ["(1) Data and concept drift: The statistical distribution of input variables (e.g., amount of chemical transferred) or the relationship between input and output variables (ground truth) can change over time due to factors like environmental regulations, technology advancements, or production volumes, leading to decay in deployed model performance.", "(2) Data imbalance: Some EoL transfer classes (e.g., surface impoundment) are significantly underrepresented in the data compared to others (e.g., recycling, disposal), which can lead to misclassification (false positives/negatives) in data-driven models.", "(3) Data granularity and harmonization: Different Pollutant Release and Transfer Register (PRTR) systems across countries have varying reporting requirements, thresholds, and classification systems for chemicals, EoL activities, and industry sectors, making data harmonization challenging and potentially leading to bias if not addressed.", "(4) Data reliability: The quality and verification level of reported chemical transfer flows vary, with some data being documented estimates or undocumented, requiring a reliability scoring system to ensure robust model performance.", "(5) Data accessibility and structure: PRTR data sources can be structured, semi-structured, or unstructured, requiring a flexible data engineering pipeline to extract, transform, and load information into a machine-readable format for ML operations."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) Data ingestion pipeline: A 'data engineering pipeline framework' is used to extract, transform, and load comprehensive data from publicly-accessible, siloed PRTR systems across different countries and reporting years into a harmonized 'PRTR_transfers' database.", "(2) Data harmonization: Specific procedures are implemented to harmonize chemical substances (using CAS numbers, connecting to services like U.S. Substance Registry Service), EoL activities (investigating and comparing categorizations, sacrificing granularity for balance), and industry sectors (using ISIC divisions for cross-walking national classifications).", "(3) Data quality assessment: A reliability score (1-5) is assigned to each record based on guidelines for Life Cycle Inventory (LCI) data quality (similar to ecoinvent's pedigree matrix), allowing for filtering or weighting data based on its verification method.", "(4) Data storage and accessibility: Transformed data is stored in a free and open-source database management system ('PRTR_transfers') in a machine-readable structure, ensuring scalability and accessibility for real-world operations and Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA).", "(5) Continuous data supply for retraining: The framework is designed to supply labeled data at an 'annual rate' (due to PRTR reporting cycles) to ready-to-use models, enabling retraining to address data and concept drift and maintain model performance over time.", "(6) Data-centric paradigm: The approach emphasizes 'iteratively improving the data, and holding the model fixed' (Ng, 2021) to enhance model robustness in production."]
gaps_identified: ["Current data-driven models for EoL chemical flow analysis can suffer from performance decay due to data and concept drift if not continuously supplied with updated, labeled data.", "Existing frameworks often lack the ability to integrate cross-year and cross-country data, limiting the applicability domain of models and the ability to account for dynamic changes in environmental regulations or economic factors.", "Granularity in EoL transfer classes and industry sectors in some existing frameworks can lead to data imbalance, causing misclassification (false positives/negatives) in predictive models.", "The lack of standardized, harmonized data across different national regulatory databases makes it challenging to build globally applicable and robust data-driven models for chemical EoL management."]
key_findings: This work presents a scalable data-centric and chemical-centric data engineering framework for tracking end-of-life (EoL) chemical flow transfers, leveraging publicly-accessible Pollutant Release and Transfer Register (PRTR) systems. The framework creates a harmonized 'PRTR_transfers' database that can supply data at an annual rate, which is crucial for maintaining the performance of data-driven models in production by addressing data and concept drift. Exploratory data analysis reveals significant data imbalance and drift over time and across countries, highlighting the necessity of the proposed data ingestion and harmonization approach. The developed framework enhances the applicability domain of data-driven models for environmental risk assessment and life cycle inventory by integrating cross-year and cross-country data, and it can be connected to other data sources for richer context.
method: The methodology involves designing a data engineering pipeline to collect, manage, and convert raw data from various siloed PRTR systems. This includes defining data sources and selection criteria (e.g., English version, chemical-centric, granularity), harmonizing chemical substances (CAS numbers), EoL activities (balancing granularity for class balance), and industry sectors (using ISIC). The extracted data is transformed into a generic, tabular form, stored in an open-source database, and assigned a reliability score based on LCI data quality guidelines. Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) is performed to identify data imbalance, data drift, and concept drift.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Data Preparation, Model Monitoring (via data supply for retraining), Model Deployment (scalability for production-level MLOps)
future_research: Future research should focus on developing a multi-level and multi-scale modeling system that integrates this framework with previous approaches for analyzing pollution abatement unit technologies and understanding the chemical EoL management chain and recycling loop. This would involve creating scalable systems to rapidly screen potential chemical exposure scenarios while maintaining a data-centric paradigm. Additionally, the findings could inform changes in regulatory reporting requirements to incorporate needed data entries for LCI and CFA at the EoL stage.

title: AI-Driven Cloud Infrastructure Provisioning: Leveraging Machine Learning, Observability, and IaC for Autonomous Cloud Operations

operational_constraints: (1) Data quality issues: The quality of ML outcomes is closely tied to the completeness and accuracy of incoming telemetry metrics; gaps lead to suboptimal or misleading inferences. (3) Vendor lock-in challenges: While the framework aims for cloud-agnosticism, some adaptation is required for different cloud providers, and initial prototyping used AWS-native services. mlops_practices: (1) Data Collection: Telemetry metrics (CPU load, memory, disk I/O, network throughput) are continuously streamed into a time-series database or object storage for preprocessing. (2) Training and Validation: Forecasting models (LSTM) are trained on historical trends, and anomaly detection algorithms learn normal operating thresholds. (3) Inference and Decision: Predictions are generated in near real-time for scaling actions, resource allocation, or corrective recovery. (4) Model Deployment: Models are packaged as serverless endpoints or Docker containers and invoked on a schedule or upon alarm conditions. (5) Infrastructure as Code (IaC) for Deployment: Terraform is used to translate ML-driven decisions into infrastructure changes, defining and applying version-controlled IaC blueprints. (6) Policy-as-Code: Integration with Open Policy Agent (OPA) or Sentinel ensures compliance and security guardrails before Terraform execution. (7) Closed-Loop Automation: A continuous feedback loop where observability tools detect anomalies, trigger Lambda functions to invoke ML models, which return decisions, and Terraform executes the required actions, with updated metrics fed back for continuous learning. (8) Cost Governance (FinOps): Tagging cloud assets and linking usage data with FinOps platforms ensures scaling decisions remain within budget thresholds. (9) Model Monitoring and Retraining: CloudWatch tracks model drift metrics and triggers automated retraining workflows when drift thresholds are exceeded.
gaps_identified: (1) Model Interpretability: Deep learning models, especially DRL agents, often lack interpretability, hindering stakeholder trust and adoption without Explainable AI (XAI) techniques. (2) Cross-Platform Orchestration: Gaps remain in seamlessly orchestrating ML systems across diverse cloud platforms without significant integration overhead. (3) Data Quality and Completeness: The effectiveness of AI-driven provisioning is highly dependent on the quality and completeness of telemetry data, which can be a challenge to maintain. (4) Vendor Lock-in: While the framework aims for portability, some level of adaptation is still required when migrating between cloud providers, indicating a gap in truly seamless multi-cloud deployment.
key_findings: The study demonstrates that combining machine learning, observability tools (e.g., AWS CloudWatch), and Infrastructure as Code (Terraform) leads to measurable gains in cloud infrastructure operations. Key quantitative results include a 25% reduction in average API response time during peak loads, a 40% decrease in Mean Time To Repair (MTTR), and a 22% reduction in monthly cloud spend. The framework also accelerated ML model deployment by 3x and increased service uptime from 99.82% to 99.95%. This research validates that intelligent infrastructure paradigms are a critical strategic imperative for creating scalable, economically resilient, and self-optimizing cloud ecosystems.
method: The methodology involves a proposed AI-driven cloud infrastructure provisioning system combining data observability, machine learning intelligence (LSTM for forecasting, Isolation Forests for anomaly detection, DRL for policy learning), and declarative automation (Terraform) into a tightly integrated feedback loop.
