title: Multi Datasource LTV User Representation (MDLUR)
journal: Proceedings of the ACM SIGKDD International Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining
reason: The paper describes the development and deployment of an AI model (MDLUR) for predicting Lifetime Value (LTV) in a mobile gaming context. It explicitly mentions efficient deployment and online testing in a live production environment via MLOps, demonstrating its maintainability. This directly addresses the operational aspects of AI implementation.
deployment_context: Mobile gaming industry (Bagelcode's 'Club Vegas Slots'), predicting user Lifetime Value (LTV) for marketing and personalized experiences.
operational_constraints: (1) High data sparsity: The dataset for LTV prediction, especially for high LTV users, is highly sparse due to a large number of features (over 400 fields including rare in-app events) and a significant imbalance between payers and non-payers (1:30 ratio). (2) Skewed asymmetric long-tail distribution: LTV data exhibits a skewed, long-tail distribution, complicating accurate prediction of user purchases and amounts. (3) Need for early identification of valuable users: Marketing strategies require identifying valuable users early in their lifecycle (e.g., predicting Day 14 LTV from Day 7 data) to optimize acquisition efforts. (4) Continuous model updates: The production environment requires continuous model updates on a weekly basis to adapt to new incoming user data and maintain performance.
mlops_practices: (1) Offline inference testing: The model undergoes offline inference tests on real-world data to evaluate performance before live deployment. (2) In-house MLOps system: An in-house MLOps system is used for continuous model updates on a weekly basis to emulate a live production environment. (3) Time-series cross-validation: 10-fold time-series cross-validation is used during training to prevent target leakage and ensure generalizable results in a time-series environment. (4) Data sampling for imbalance: Under-sampling is applied to training and validation data (1:3 payer to non-payer ratio) to address class imbalance, while test data remains unsampled to reflect real-world conditions. (5) Hyperparameter tuning: Grid-search is conducted to identify optimal loss functions (MSLE for skewed LTV distributions) and hyperparameters (ADAM optimizer, learning rate, batch size, epochs) with callbacks (ReduceLROnPlateau, EarlyStopping) for generalization and efficient optimization. (6) Data preprocessing: Data cleansing, sanity checks, outlier filtration, and feature scaling are performed to maintain data integrity and enhance model quality.
gaps_identified: (1) Conventional approaches struggle with effectively utilizing various data sources or accurately capturing interactions in sparse datasets for LTV prediction. (2) Existing methods, primarily focused on e-commerce recommendation systems, do not fully utilize the diversity of game data sources and lack a comprehensive understanding of these. (3) The trade-off of employing large feature sets from multiple sources often results in the 'curse of dimensionality' due to data sparsity, which conventional models struggle to manage. (4) Class imbalance and payer distribution issues are not effectively handled by all conventional LTV prediction models, leading to poor performance.
key_findings: The Multi Datasource LTV User Representation (MDLUR) model significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in LTV prediction for mobile games, achieving an RMSE of 28.13 and R2 of 0.84 for Day 14 LTV prediction from 7 days of data. Its superior performance stems from a novel architecture that tailors distinct models (DNN, AE, UNET, Transformer) to different data sources (user information, portrait, behavior data) and integrates them via a two-level Skip-connected Autoencoder (SAE) and weighted sum. The model effectively manages data sparsity and skewed distributions through dimensionality reduction techniques and appropriate loss functions (MSLE), demonstrating maintainability and robust performance in a live production environment via MLOps.
method: The paper describes the development of a novel deep learning model (MDLUR) for LTV prediction. It involves extensive experimentation, including comparisons with multiple baseline models (RFM, XGBoost, WhalesDetector, ZILN, MSDMT, BST), evaluation using various regression (RMSE, R2) and classification (WAP, WAR, WAF1) metrics, and hyperparameter tuning via grid search. The methodology also includes offline inference testing on real-world data to validate performance in a production-like environment and the use of an in-house MLOps system for continuous model updates.

title: Practical Benchmark of Open-Source MLOps Platforms: Comparing MLflow, Metaflow and ZenML Across Model Type
journal: Proceedings - RoEduNet IEEE International Conference
reason: The paper directly compares MLOps frameworks (MLflow, Metaflow, ZenML) across different ML model types, focusing on their impact on deployment, operational overhead, reproducibility, and developer experience. This aligns with the core interest in understanding practical aspects of AI implementation.
deployment_context: Generic AI Implementation (software-centric ML deployment contexts)
operational_constraints: (1) Increased execution time: MLOps frameworks introduce runtime overhead, with Metaflow having the biggest impact (∼110.8% increase for Random Forest model) and ZenML moderate (∼19.6% for Random Forest). (2) Increased storage requirements: Full versioning and traceability lead to significant disk usage increases (e.g., MLflow: 220.4x, ZenML: 220x, Metaflow: 143.4x artifact expansion). (3) Developer overhead for integration: Integrating MLOps frameworks requires additional lines of code and potential restructuring (e.g., Metaflow required substantial restructuring due to its step-based pipeline abstraction).
mlops_practices: (1) Experiment Tracking: MLflow uses `mlflow.log_param()`, `mlflow.log_metric()`, and `mlflow.log_artifact()` to record experiment metadata, model versions, and evaluation metrics. (2) Pipeline Orchestration: Metaflow uses `FlowSpec` and `@step` decorators to define workflows as a series of logical units, supporting step reuse and pipeline resumption. (3) Modular Pipeline Architecture: ZenML employs `@step` and `@pipeline` decorators to create modular, production-ready pipelines with integrated connectors for orchestrators, artifact stores, and tracking backends. (4) Automatic Versioning: Metaflow automatically versions parameters, code, and artifacts, storing them in a structured format. (5) Artifact Management: ZenML and Metaflow manage artifact storage, parameter logging, and metadata tracking automatically, ensuring strict artifact lineage. (6) Reproducibility Strategy: All random seeds are fixed, data preprocessing is standardized, and intermediate caches are cleared between runs; each experiment is executed multiple times with statistical analysis of performance variability.
gaps_identified: The paper highlights a lack of systematic benchmarks in both academic and industry literature that evaluate MLOps frameworks' performance in comparable and controlled environments. Most existing research focuses on high-level overviews, taxonomies, or isolated case studies, leading to limited empirical data for direct comparison using consistent evaluation criteria.
key_findings: This study empirically evaluated MLflow, Metaflow, and ZenML across tabular classification, image recognition, and text summarization tasks. Key findings include that all platforms improved experiment traceability and reproducibility, significantly reducing variability across runs. Runtime overhead varied, with MLflow introducing the least and Metaflow the most, but these trade-offs are justified by operational benefits. All implementations achieved equivalent model performance, while artifact volume and storage requirements were highest for Metaflow and ZenML due to extensive versioning. Integration complexity also differed, with MLflow requiring minimal code changes and Metaflow demanding substantial restructuring.
method: Empirical benchmark study. The authors developed MLOps-enhanced versions of baseline code for three ML models (Random Forest, ResNet, BERT-based summarization) using MLflow, Metaflow, and ZenML. They measured execution time, number and size of artifacts, model performance (accuracy, F1-score, TPR, FPR, loss), and lines of code added for integration. Each configuration was run five times with fixed random seeds, and statistical analysis was performed.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Experiment Tracking, Model Packaging, Model Deployment, Monitoring, Reproducibility, Version Control, Pipeline Orchestration, Governance (Traceability/Auditability)
future_research: The paper aims to fill the gap in repeatable, task-based performance evaluations across real-world ML workflows, informing practitioners and researchers in the evolving MLOps landscape. It also suggests that the publicly available codebase can be used by other researchers to reproduce results and expand experiments by adding more tools or models to the benchmark.

title: MLOps Adoption in the Manufacturing Industry: A Case Study with Zeiss SMT
journal: Communications in Computer and Information Science
reason: This paper is a case study on MLOps adoption in a manufacturing company, directly addressing challenges and solutions for deploying and operating ML models in an industrial context.
deployment_context: Manufacturing industry (low-volume optics manufacturing at Zeiss SMT), with specific focus on on-premises and isolated shop floor environments.
operational_constraints: ["(1) Data confidentiality requirements prohibit the use of cloud-based platforms for certain datasets, forcing reliance on less capable on-premises tooling.", "(2) Isolated shop floor environments mean production machines are isolated from the network, making cloud or edge deployment infeasible and requiring local model execution.", "(3) Small sample sizes due to low production volume and long manufacturing times limit the data available for ML training, especially for rare events like defects.", "(4) Manual model deployment procedures are time-consuming and error-prone, involving copying files, adjusting settings, and verifying versions on individual machines, leading to potential divergence.", "(5) Existing system interfaces, particularly control software for production machines, may require synchronous execution of external processes for ML models, constraining response times and model complexity.", "(6) Technological heterogeneity across projects makes it difficult to transfer learnings or solutions and increases effort for engineers switching teams.", "(7) Lack of on-premises tooling for experimentation hinders rapid prototyping and feedback collection in production environments.", "(8) Reproducible packaging is challenging in isolated shop floor environments, as dependencies need to be installed and kept in sync across machines and development environments.", "(9) The existing system being a monolith, especially if not implemented in Python, complicates integration of ML components."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) Using cloud ML platforms (e.g., Azure ML) for model development and deployment when use cases are compatible with data confidentiality and network constraints.", "(2) Falling back on on-premises tooling (e.g., internal job cluster, computer vision platform) when cloud platforms are not feasible due to data confidentiality or isolated environments.", "(3) Employing the sidecar pattern for model deployment on isolated production machines, where the model is deployed locally next to the code using it.", "(4) Combining tools, such as training models in Azure ML and then deploying them as sidecars to production machines.", "(5) Standardizing ML projects through best practice guidelines and template technology stacks to promote reuse and knowledge sharing.", "(6) Designing for independent model updates to allow new model versions to be rolled out without deploying the entire software system.", "(7) Packaging models using Docker containers for complete environment reproducibility, including dependencies.", "(8) Using the Open Neural Network Exchange (ONNX) format as an alternative packaging format for deployment in environments where Docker containers cannot run (e.g., production machines).", "(9) Preferring RESTful endpoints for communication between ML models and conventional system parts to standardize interfaces.", "(10) Implementing transparent data access via a single service or library to retrieve, integrate, and clean data through a unified interface, abstracting underlying data sources.", "(11) Having ML engineers co-own ML-based products to increase familiarity with the codebase and ease integration.", "(12) Deploying multiple models in parallel and using interchangeable configuration files to choose between them at runtime, tailoring accuracy to context and demonstrating improvements.", "(13) Using a shared memory interface for communication between the conventional system and the ML model as an alternative to RESTful endpoints.", "(14) Suggesting regulations to streamline access to datasets for data scientists, balancing speed with data security."]
gaps_identified: ["Existing MLOps literature and tools primarily focus on cloud-based microservice systems, neglecting the unique challenges of embedded systems and isolated shop floor environments.", "Current on-premises MLOps tooling is lacking compared to cloud offerings, particularly for experimentation and rapid prototyping.", "Standard drift detection and continuous integration/deployment practices assume continuous connectivity, which fails in air-gapped operational technology (OT) environments.", "There is a lack of documented solutions or best practices in existing scientific or grey literature for deploying and operating ML models in isolated shop floor environments."]
key_findings: The study identifies 13 challenges and 13 solutions for MLOps adoption in low-volume optics manufacturing at Zeiss SMT, based on interviews with 12 practitioners. The primary challenge is data accessibility, particularly security requirements prohibiting cloud platform use, which exacerbates issues with on-premises tooling and isolated shop floor environments. A key finding is that the 'isolated shop floor' challenge is largely absent from existing MLOps literature, indicating a research gap at the intersection of ML-based systems and embedded systems. The paper highlights that while many challenges are not unique to ML, their impact is disproportionately higher due to the data-driven nature and higher update frequency of ML systems.
method: Interview-based case study with 12 participants in 10 interviews at Zeiss SMT. Data collection involved semi-structured interviews, followed by automatic and manual transcription, participant approval of transcripts, and thematic analysis using open coding.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Deployment, Operations, Monitoring, Governance (implicitly through data access and security)
future_research: Extend the on-premise ML platform at Zeiss SMT for sensitive use cases; investigate approaches to automate ML model deployment in isolated shop floor environments; incorporate identified challenges into a test bed for evaluating ML-based systems; conduct similar case studies in other manufacturing companies to compare challenges and solutions.

title: XAI-Compliance-by-Design: A Modular Framework for GDPR- and AI Act-Aligned Decision Transparency in High-Risk AI Systems
journal: Journal of Cybersecurity and Privacy
reason: The paper proposes a modular framework for integrating XAI, compliance-by-design, and MLOps practices to ensure regulatory alignment and auditability in high-risk AI systems. It explicitly discusses operational aspects of AI deployment, monitoring, and governance, making it highly relevant to the research questions.
evidence_level: Proof-of-concept implementation with synthetic data, architectural design, and comparative analysis of auditability.
deployment_context: High-risk AI systems in cybersecurity and privacy-critical domains (e.g., intrusion detection, fraud detection, clinical decision support, credit scoring). The framework is designed for regulated environments under GDPR and AI Act.
operational_constraints: ["(1) Fragmented approaches to XAI, governance, and regulatory analysis: XAI methods are often added ad-hoc, governance frameworks remain conceptual, and regulatory analyses lack explicit links to ML workflow artifacts.", "(2) Lack of unified operational frameworks: Current solutions often focus on model performance or high-level governance, lacking end-to-end architectures that map technical artifacts to regulatory requirements, provide systematic compliance logging, and tamper-evident evidence bundles.", "(3) Difficulty in translating process controls into technical artifacts: Best practices require translating process controls (e.g., automated risk assessments, decision logs, audit trails) into continuously updated and legally mapped artifacts, which is challenging in continuously evolving large-scale AI pipelines.", "(4) Limited operationalization of explainability metrics: Explainability metrics (fidelity, stability, comprehensibility) are rarely translated into measurable indicators of regulatory alignment, posing practical challenges for engineering and compliance teams.", "(5) Data sharing constraints: In many regulated deployments, sharing operational datasets is constrained by privacy, security, contractual, and confidentiality obligations, necessitating reproducible evidence protocols over publishing sensitive data.", "(6) Risk of over-collection of data/metadata: Audit-oriented pipelines risk over-collecting data 'because it might be useful later,' which conflicts with data minimization principles.", "(7) Conflict between auditability and confidentiality: Storing evidence for auditability can conflict with confidentiality if sensitive raw inputs are logged, requiring evidence-by-reference and redaction mechanisms."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) Dual-flow design: Couples an upstream technical pipeline (data, model, explanation, monitoring) with a downstream governance pipeline (policy, oversight, audit, decision-making) orchestrated by a Compliance-by-Design Engine (CDE).", "(2) MLflow-backed evidence tracking and model registry integration: Uses MLflow for experiment tracking (runs, parameters, metrics, tags), an artifact store for persistent, queryable evidence, and a model registry for controlled promotion and versioning of deployable models.", "(3) Compliance logging: Maintains a structured, append-only record of lifecycle events (data snapshot creation, model training, explanation generation, drift checks) to support full lineage reconstruction for a given RUN_ID.", "(4) Evidence bundles: Per-run collection of technical artifacts (models, metrics, explanations, drift reports, hashes) organized in a deterministic directory structure and indexed by a manifest for audit and conformity assessment.", "(5) Decision dossiers: Per-run decision record capturing deployment-relevant conclusions (approve, reject, escalate) with minimal justification and pointers to the corresponding evidence bundle.", "(6) Technical–regulatory correspondence matrix: A mapping between regulatory obligations and concrete controls/artifacts produced by the pipeline, maintained by the CDE and versioned.", "(7) Reusable artifact kit: Specifies a minimal evidence schema (filenames, fields, linkage rules) for drop-in integration into existing MLflow-backed pipelines to produce consistent, audit-ready outputs.", "(8) Explainability layer: Generates auditable global (SHAP, Permutation Feature Importance) and local (LIME) explanation artifacts, making explainer choice and configuration explicit and versioned per run.", "(9) Drift monitoring: Includes lightweight drift indicators (Kolmogorov–Smirnov for numerical, KL divergence for categorical) with calibrated thresholds and auditable mitigation playbooks (intensified monitoring, rollback, retraining).", "(10) Policy-as-code and evidence gates: The CDE implements governance expectations as machine-verifiable evidence gates (e.g., completeness, lineage integrity, tamper-evidence, monitoring, explanation availability, regulatory coverage) that determine deployment eligibility and record outcomes.", "(11) CI/CD integration: Extends CI pipelines with policy linting, unit tests for explainability, and compliance gates; deployment pipelines enforce policies preventing promotion of models lacking oversight mechanisms or post-deployment monitoring hooks.", "(12) Post-deployment observability and revalidation: Telemetry feeds drift detectors and governance dashboards; periodic revalidation routines reassess model performance and explanation properties."]
gaps_identified: ["Persistent gap between mainstream engineering practices and legal–regulatory expectations regarding interpretability, traceability, and auditability.", "Explanation mechanisms often remain ad-hoc, unversioned, and weakly connected to formal compliance processes, hindering systematic demonstration of conformity.", "Lack of a unified operational framework that connects technical metrics and artifacts (models, explanations, logs, drift indicators) to concrete regulatory requirements in an implementable and auditable way.", "Current solutions often focus primarily on model performance and ad-hoc explanations or on high-level governance principles that are not operationally implemented in machine learning pipelines.", "Limited guidance on systematically implementing compliance controls (automated risk assessments, decision logs, traceable audit trails) in continuously evolving large-scale AI pipelines.", "Most existing XAI frameworks do not yet provide standardized, audit-ready compliance metrics.", "Only a minority of frameworks offer machine-verifiable mechanisms for continuous, native MLOps compliance (automated lineage tracking, versioned decision logs, model cards, tamper-evident audit trails)."]
key_findings: The XAI-Compliance-by-Design framework successfully integrates XAI, compliance-by-design, and MLOps into a modular, dual-flow architecture. It systematically produces verifiable, audit-ready artifacts (compliance logs, explanation reports, drift monitoring outputs, evidence bundles, decision dossiers) indexed by a unique RUN_ID, demonstrating how technical artifacts can be explicitly mapped to regulatory objectives (GDPR, AI Act, ISO/IEC 42001). This approach shifts from model-centric to evidence-centric governance, ensuring that high-risk AI deployment decisions are grounded in a structured, reproducible body of evidence beyond mere model performance, thereby operationalizing accountability and auditability.
method: Design Science Research (DSR) combining a conceptual model (GDPR, AI Act, ISO/IEC 42001), construction and evaluation of verifiable artifacts, and an operational MLOps pipeline. The framework is instantiated and validated through an end-to-end, Python-based proof-of-concept using a synthetic, intrusion detection system (IDS)-inspired anomaly detection scenario with a Random Forest classifier, SHAP, LIME, and drift indicators. Evaluation focuses on auditability outcomes (evidence completeness, lineage linkage, integrity, regulatory coverage, audit reconstruction effort, gate enforcement) rather than domain-specific performance.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Data Handling and Preprocessing, Model Training and Validation, Explainability and Monitoring, Evidence Bundle Construction, Decision Dossier Construction, Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment (CI/CD), Post-deployment Observability and Revalidation.
future_research: Empirical validation in real high-risk AI systems (e.g., credit scoring, clinical decision support, fraud detection); stakeholder-centered evaluation (auditors, data protection officers); extension to heterogeneous model families and modalities (LLMs, computer vision); standardization of evidence bundles and decision dossiers; exploration of richer governance interfaces and dashboards for non-technical stakeholders.

title: Linking Team-level and Organization-level Governance in Machine Learning Operations through Explainable AI and Responsible AI Connector
journal: Proceedings - International Computer Software and Applications Conference
reason: The paper proposes a multi-level governance pattern for Responsible AI (RAI) systems within organizations, focusing on bridging the gap between team-level and organization-level governance using MLOps and Explainable AI (XAI). It discusses the operationalization of ethical principles in AI deployment.
deployment_context: Generic AI implementation (cloud-based image classification model)
operational_constraints: (1) Ethical principles are often abstract and may not provide clear direction for specific implementation by developers. (2) It is challenging to show the contribution of each layer (AI software supply chain, AI system, and operation infrastructure) in an AI ecosystem to responsible AI. (3) Algorithm-level solutions alone are insufficient to address all ethical concerns related to AI, as principles are complex and multifaceted. (4) The absence of XAI and RAI in MLOps methods can result in unintended consequences and decreased trust in the system. (5) Implementing XAI and RAI can introduce added overhead to the MLOps pipeline.
mlops_practices: (1) Multi-level governance patterns: Establishing governance across industry, organization, and team levels for responsible AI. (2) Process patterns: Establishing trustworthy development processes. (3) Product patterns: Integrating responsive design into AI systems. (4) MLOps workflow stages: Data Management, ML Preparation, ML Training, and Deployment phases are outlined with specific roles and tasks. (5) XAI and RAI Connector (XRc): A proposed phase inserted between ML pipeline and ML deployment to analyze changes before migration, incorporating organization-level governance. (6) Model Type Identification (XRc sub-phase): Using static (code/architecture examination) and dynamic (API/performance examination) methods to identify ML model types. (7) XAI techniques Implementation (XRc sub-phase): AI/MLOps engineers select XAI methods (model-agnostic, model-specific, post-hoc, integrative) based on audience (end-users, regulators) and ML algorithm characteristics. (8) Gathering and Filtering XAI methods (XRc sub-phase): Defining requirements, gathering appropriate XAI methods, filtering based on complexity, computational resources, and technical detail, and combining methods for comprehensive explanations. (9) XAI Representation and API (XRc sub-phase): Presenting XAI explanations via text, visualizations, or other data forms, and providing an API for integration, allowing interactive visualizations, user input, dynamic explanations, and feedback mechanisms. (10) Decision Process (XRc sub-phase): Incorporating XAI API into organizational-level decision-making to decide on ML algorithm deployment based on transparency and fairness.
gaps_identified: (1) Current AI ethics frameworks primarily focus on the algorithmic level rather than the systems level. (2) Existing system-level approaches mostly cover single-level governance patterns, lacking multi-level abstraction and linking of responsible AI layers. (3) Ethical guidelines for AI development are often too general and theoretical for practical application by developers. (4) There is no standard for implementing multi-level governance patterns for Responsible AI with Explainable AI in MLOps. (5) A lack of clear connection between team-level and organization-level governance patterns in MLOps.
key_findings: The paper proposes a multi-level governance pattern for Responsible AI (RAI) systems, bridging the gap between team-level and organization-level governance through an Explainable AI and Responsible AI Connector (XRc) within MLOps. It identifies that current AI ethics frameworks are often abstract and lack system-level, multi-level governance. The XRc, integrated into the MLOps pipeline, provides a structured approach for identifying model types, implementing XAI techniques, gathering and filtering explanations, and presenting them via an interactive API to facilitate informed organizational decisions on AI deployment. A conceptual case study of a cloud-based image classification model demonstrates the XRc's effectiveness in improving transparency and fairness for non-technical stakeholders.
method: Conceptual framework development, literature review, conceptual scenario/case study
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Deployment, Monitoring, Governance
future_research: Exploring new and innovative XAI techniques and methods to enhance the efficiency and effectiveness of the XRc phase in MLOps. Continued research into the relationship between team-level and organization-level governance patterns in MLOps to fully understand best practices and strategies for bridging the gap.

title: An End-to-End Data and Machine Learning Pipeline for Energy Forecasting: A Systematic Approach Integrating MLOps and Domain Expertise
journal: Information (Switzerland)
reason: The paper proposes an end-to-end MLOps framework specifically for energy forecasting, detailing deployment, monitoring, and maintenance aspects with a strong emphasis on domain expertise and regulatory compliance. It includes a case study demonstrating the framework's application in a real-world setting.
deployment_context: Energy forecasting for building electricity usage (office building at University of Southern Denmark). The framework is designed for critical infrastructure contexts and aims to be compatible with existing on-premise systems like SCADA through pragmatic measures (e.g., lightweight API connectors, hybrid cloud deployment).
operational_constraints: (1) Energy forecasting projects require systematic approaches that span project inception to model deployment while ensuring technical excellence, domain alignment, regulatory compliance, and reproducibility. (2) Energy forecasting models must account for complex temporal patterns, weather dependencies, and regulatory requirements that generic data science approaches may overlook. (3) Poor data quality or misaligned project goals can lead to advanced models failing due to fundamental flaws in input data or problem definition. (4) The separation of domain experts from data scientists can lead to models that technically optimize a metric but fail to align with real-world needs and constraints. (5) Growing concerns about AI accountability and regulations (e.g., GDPR, EU AI Act) necessitate rigorous documentation of data sources, modeling decisions, and model outcomes at each step of the pipeline. (6) Forecasting pipelines in utilities and building operations often need to coexist with established supervisory control and on-premises systems such as SCADA, which are not always designed for cloud-native MLOps.
mlops_practices: (1) Formalized Feedback Loops: Explicit decision gates (Gates 0-6) at each phase with predefined validation criteria, requiring multi-stakeholder review and explicit sign-off. Controlled loopback mechanisms enable documented returns to earlier phases. (2) Systematic Energy-Domain-Expert Integration: Mandatory validation checkpoints throughout all phases, formally embedding energy domain feedback for validating data patterns, feature-engineering decisions, and model behavior against energy system knowledge. (3) Enhanced Documentation and Traceability: Each phase produces versioned artifacts (datasets, analysis reports, model artifacts, evaluation reports) with recorded metadata, providing end-to-end provenance for regulatory compliance. (4) Modularity and Phase Independence: The process is structured into self-contained phases with well-defined inputs and outputs, mirroring MLOps pipeline orchestration tools (e.g., Kubeflow, Apache Airflow). (5) Automated Monitoring and Update: Activation of scheduled or event-triggered automated operations (e.g., daily data ingestion, forecast generation, output quality evaluation). (6) Alert Mechanisms: Automatic generation of logs and alerts when output quality is unacceptable (e.g., RMSE degradation exceeding 25% from baseline). (7) Hard Sample Archiving: Archiving anomalous input-output pairs as hard samples for future retraining. (8) Containerized Deployment: Launching a containerized forecasting service (e.g., generating seven daily rolling forecasts). (9) Time-aware Data Splitting: Using configurable ratios (70% training, 20% validation, 10% test) while maintaining strict temporal ordering to prevent data leakage. (10) Differentiated Scaling Strategies: MinMaxScaler fitted exclusively on training data for neural networks, while unscaled data used for tree-based models.
gaps_identified: (1) Existing methodologies (like CRISP-DM) lack explicit mechanisms for iterative feedback, formal decision checkpoints, and continuous energy-domain-expert involvement. (2) Traditional process models do not fully address the need for continuous iteration and feedback loops in modern ML pipelines where models are retrained and redeployed regularly. (3) Many ML pipelines implemented in pure technical terms neglect sustained involvement of energy domain experts after the initial requirements stage, leading to models that fail to align with real-world needs. (4) While tools exist to version data and models, few conceptual frameworks explicitly require that each step’s outputs be recorded and linked to inputs for comprehensive traceability and regulatory compliance. (5) Most MLOps tools focus on automation and reproducibility but do not inherently ensure that a domain expert has vetted the model’s behavior or that documentation is written in human-readable form. (6) Existing frameworks do not provide a unified methodology that formally integrates iterative decision gating, domain validation, and comprehensive traceability in a modular, implementable way, especially for energy forecasting.
key_findings: The paper proposes a modular, end-to-end framework for energy forecasting that integrates formal decision gates, embeds domain-expert validation, and produces fully traceable artifacts. A comparative analysis demonstrates its advantages in functional coverage, workflow logic, and governance over existing approaches like CRISP-DM and generic MLOps pipelines. A case study on short-term electricity forecasting for a 2560m2 office building validated the framework, achieving 24-h-ahead predictions with an RNN, reaching an RMSE of 1.04 kWh and an MAE of 0.78 kWh. The results confirm that the framework enhances forecast accuracy, reliability, and regulatory readiness in real-world energy applications by systematically integrating technical rigor, domain expertise, compliance management, and adaptive governance.
method: Framework development, comparative analysis, and case study.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: End-to-end (Project Foundation, Data Foundation, Data Understanding and Preprocessing, Feature Engineering, Model Development and Evaluation, Model Deployment and Implementation, Automated Monitoring and Update)
future_research: Developing implementations support tools (standardized gate checklists, pipeline orchestration templates), conducting empirical validation studies comparing framework adoption outcomes across delivery metrics, and extending the methodology to broader energy informatics applications through systematic case study analysis.

deployment_context: Environmental monitoring (air quality prediction) in Slovenia, specifically the Airly platform.
operational_constraints: (1) Traditional software QA methods, centered on static code testing and deterministic outcomes, fall short for predictive models due to their continuous evolution with new and potentially shifting data distributions. (2) Model performance can deteriorate silently due to unobserved data drift, concept drift, or changes in underlying relationships between inputs and outputs. (3) Feedback loops in intelligent systems, where model predictions are fed back as training data, can amplify even small data errors, causing gradual model performance degradation over time. (4) ML systems are prone to silent failures, influenced not just by the model but by dependencies across the data pipeline, pre-processing logic, and external libraries. (5) After conducting tests, the interpretation of results can be non-trivial since metric thresholds (accuracy, F1 score, etc.) are commonly used as proxies for correctness, and understanding why a metric dropped requires human insight and debugging, often beyond the scope of automated testing.
mlops_practices: (1) Automated data pipelines: Raw sensor and weather data are obtained, transformed, merged, and validated at multiple stages. (2) Schema-driven data validation: Using Great Expectations, data must satisfy predefined quality criteria (schema compliance, valid ranges, distributional properties) before passing to subsequent pipeline steps. (3) Data version control (DVC): Every dataset snapshot is tracked alongside the code to ensure experimental reproducibility and traceability. (4) Continuous data monitoring and drift detection: The pipeline automatically tracks the distribution of incoming data and model predictions using tools like Evidently, triggering alerts or mitigation steps when significant deviations (data drift, concept drift) are detected. (5) Automated model training pipeline: Triggered by changes in code or data, it sets up consistent Python environments (Poetry), retrieves versioned datasets (DVC), trains models (TensorFlow, scikit-learn), and logs metrics (MAE, MSE, EVS) to MLFlow. (6) Model registration and versioning: Trained models are converted to ONNX format and registered/versioned in MLFlow, allowing storage of metadata, tagging, and management of aliases ('production', 'champion'). (7) Unit and integration testing for models: Unit tests validate individual components (data transformation, model structure), while integration tests verify the full execution path from data loading to model registration. (8) Production monitoring pipeline: Executes daily to assess prediction performance (MAE, MSE, EVS) and data quality, storing results in MongoDB and MLFlow for long-term observability. (9) Automated promotion of models: If newer models demonstrate better performance, they can be promoted to production by reassigning aliases through the platform interface. (10) CI/CD with GitHub Workflows: Each pipeline stage is implemented as a separate job within the CI/CD framework, ensuring rigorous validation of modifications and automated orchestration of interdependent stages.
gaps_identified: (1) Traditional software quality assurance methods, centered predominantly around static code testing and deterministic outcomes, fall short in addressing the nuanced demands posed by predictive models. (2) Testing ML systems is not yet fully standardized in industry, and software teams often struggle to apply traditional testing to ML projects, necessitating new practices. (3) Interpreting why performance metrics drop in ML systems often requires human insight and debugging beyond automated testing, indicating a gap in fully automated root cause analysis.
key_findings: The paper emphasizes that effective quality assurance for predictive models in intelligent systems requires rigorous and automated data validation, robust drift detection mechanisms, and systematic testing methodologies tailored for ML. The case study of the Airly platform demonstrates the practical benefits of embedding these practices into automated MLOps pipelines, leading to improved reproducibility, reliability, and maintainability. Key findings support a life cycle-centric view of QA, where continuous monitoring and automated interventions, such as retraining or recalibration triggered by drift detection, are crucial for sustained predictive accuracy and mitigating the impact of data and concept drift.
method: Case Study (Airly platform for air quality prediction) combined with a literature review of quality assurance methodologies for predictive models.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Data Validation, Data Testing (Drift Detection), Model Testing, Model Training, Model Deployment, Model Monitoring, Continuous Integration/Continuous Delivery (CI/CD)
future_research: Future work aims to systematically evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed QA approach by simulating various data-related scenarios (shifts in distribution, noisy inputs, missing values) to assess system resilience. They also plan a comparative analysis of model performance under QA-enabled versus non-QA conditions to quantify the benefits of integrated QA mechanisms in terms of predictive accuracy, robustness, and reliability over time.

title: Composable Assurance for AI Alignment: A Framework for Propagating Formal Safety Properties Through MLOps
journal: Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence
reason: This paper introduces a framework for propagating formal safety properties through MLOps, directly addressing the operational challenges of assuring AI systems in production.
deployment_context: Generic AI Implementation (RAG chatbot)
operational_constraints: (1) Traditional safety assurance methods are manual and document-centric, making them incompatible with the agile, iterative nature of MLOps, which often involves weekly or daily model deployments. (2) Safety evidence from practices like red-teaming and robustness testing remains fragmented and lacks a formal mechanism for composition and propagation throughout the development lifecycle. (3) The dynamic and emergent risks unique to AI systems are not effectively managed by static, point-in-time assurance processes. (4) The heterogeneity of evidence artifacts from diverse Test & Evaluation tools makes a formal composition operator ill-defined, leading to information silos.
mlops_practices: (1) Formal Safety Assertion (FSA): A standardized, machine-readable 6-tuple data structure (Pid, D, M, v, E, C) that verifiably links safety properties to specific AI artifacts (data, models, tools). (2) Composition Calculus: A set of formal rules governing how FSAs are propagated (transformed through sequential processes), aggregated (combined from parallel components), and linked by dependencies (logical relationships) within the MLOps pipeline. (3) Automated Governance: Integration of the formal assurance framework into CI/CD workflows to enable automated safety governance, such as pre-deployment compliance checks and dynamic policy enforcement. (4) Incremental Verification: An architectural optimization where the system maintains a cached state of validated FSAs and only re-verifies affected sub-graphs when a component changes, reducing computational cost. (5) Monitoring Feedback Loop: Continuous evaluation of live system performance in production, generating real-time FSAs (e.g., for factual error rate) that trigger automated policy enforcement actions like alerts or rollbacks.
gaps_identified: (1) Traditional safety cases are static and manually built, failing to adapt to the dynamic, continuously evolving nature of MLOps. (2) Existing AI safety evaluations are often performed as disparate, siloed activities whose results are not formally structured for automated use or composition. (3) Current methods lack a formal mechanism to compose fragmented safety evidence or propagate guarantees through the development lifecycle. (4) There is no concrete engineering methodology to realize 'living safety cases' that evolve over time to manage uncertainty in learning-enabled systems. (5) Current frameworks struggle to handle subjective judgments and imperfect evidence in real-world AI safety, which resist simple metrics and certain evidence.
key_findings: The Composable Assurance Framework (CAF) addresses the critical assurance gap in AI systems by formalizing and automating safety assurance within MLOps. It introduces Formal Safety Assertions (FSAs) as machine-readable objects and a Composition Calculus for their propagation and aggregation, transforming the MLOps pipeline into an evidence-gathering engine. The framework enables automated safety governance through CI/CD safety gates and supports dynamic, 'living' safety cases, as demonstrated by a RAG chatbot case study. This engineering-driven approach provides a scalable and automated foundation for building verifiably safer AI systems.
method: Framework development and proof-of-concept case study (RAG chatbot)
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Deployment, Operations, Monitoring, Governance, Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment (CI/CD)
future_research: Evolving CAF into a hybrid system to handle subjective judgments (e.g., value alignment) through Expert-Signed Attestations (ESAs) as non-automatable gating nodes. Extending the FSA 'Value' field to support richer data structures like confidence intervals for uncertain evidence. Making the aggregation function configurable with risk-aligned conflict-resolution strategies (e.g., worst-case selection, weighted trust, escalation to human review).

title: Artificial intelligence-driven forecasting and shift optimization for pediatric emergency department crowding
journal: JAMIA Open
reason: The paper describes the development and simulated deployment of an AI-driven system for forecasting emergency department crowding and optimizing physician shifts, explicitly discussing MLOps architecture for continuous model updates and data drift handling.
deployment_context: Pediatric Emergency Department (PED) at a single academic medical center in Turkey. The MLOps architecture was simulated, not live.
operational_constraints: ["(1) Manual workflow management hinders full automation of ML projects from development to real-world application in healthcare.", "(2) Unexpected surges in patient demand (e.g., pandemics, natural disasters) induce concept drift, severely impacting real-time forecasting model performance if not continuously monitored and adapted.", "(3) Traditional static staffing models in hospitals (e.g., fixed 4 physicians per shift) do not account for fluctuations in patient volume, leading to inefficiencies and discrepancies between staffing and actual demand.", "(4) Generating predictions only on Sundays for the upcoming week provides limited time for practical staff schedule adjustments, affecting real-world practicality.", "(5) The complex nature of health data and the need for behavioral changes among healthcare providers pose significant challenges to widespread AI adoption and effective implementation.", "(6) Ethical, legal, and moral issues related to model validation and integration into clinical workflows must be addressed for AI adoption in healthcare."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) Automated MLOps data pipeline: Data from the hospital's EHR system is continuously and securely transferred to object storage weekly, converted into hourly data, and prepared for model training.", "(2) Continuous model retraining: Models are retrained weekly with a cumulative training series that incorporates data from the previous week to adapt to changing conditions and improve accuracy.", "(3) Automated model selection: In the MLOps simulation, the best-performing model (based on the highest R2 score) is automatically selected weekly to produce forecasts for the upcoming week.", "(4) Data drift monitoring: The Kolmogorov-Smirnov (KS) test is employed to detect concept drift by comparing empirical cumulative distribution functions across identically sized data windows, ensuring deviations in data patterns are identified and addressed.", "(5) Performance monitoring: Weekly R2 scores are tracked for all models during both development and deployment phases to assess predictive accuracy and identify underperforming models.", "(6) Integration with operational systems: Forecasting results are compiled and presented to the emergency department manager weekly for approval, allowing prompt adjustments to the staffing schedule based on predicted patient demand."]
gaps_identified: ["Current tools/frameworks for emergency department crowding prediction (e.g., EDWIN, ICMED, SEAL, NEDOCS) are often oriented towards identifying crowding post-occurrence or provide only short-term forecasts (4-5 hours) with diminishing performance over longer horizons, which is insufficient for proactive workload optimization.", "Many ML projects in healthcare fail to progress from development to real-world application due to manual workflow management, highlighting a gap in standardized, automated ML processes.", "Existing staffing models in many healthcare settings are static and do not account for dynamic fluctuations in patient volume, indicating a need for more adaptable, AI-driven optimization models.", "The single-center design of this study limits the generalizability of findings, pointing to a gap in multicenter validation and implementation in diverse settings with dynamic staffing practices.", "There is a need for forecasting models that provide longer lead times without compromising accuracy to allow for more practical staff schedule adjustments."]
key_findings: The study successfully developed and simulated an AI-driven system, SO-SAFED, integrating advanced deep learning models with an MLOps architecture to forecast Pediatric Emergency Department (PED) overcrowding and optimize physician shift schedules. This MLOps approach facilitated continuous model updates and demonstrated resilience against data drift, outperforming traditional forecasting methods with a median R2 score of 60% after model selection, compared to 12% without MLOps. Shift optimization based on these forecasts adjusted staffing in 69 out of 84 shifts, increasing physician allocation by up to 30.4% during peak hours and reducing the patient-to-physician ratio by an average of 4.32-4.40 patients during busy shifts, thereby improving workforce distribution without increasing staff numbers. The study highlights the potential of MLOps to bridge the gap between AI development and real-world clinical application by ensuring model adaptability and sustained accuracy.
method: Retrospective analysis of 352,843 PED admissions (Jan 2018-May 2023) to develop and compare 20 time-series forecasting models (classical and deep learning). An MLOps simulation (Jan 2023-May 2023) automated data updates, weekly model retraining, and model selection based on R2 scores. Concept drift was monitored using the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test. Integer linear programming was used for shift optimization based on forecasted patient volumes.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Data Ingestion, Data Preprocessing, Model Training, Model Evaluation, Model Selection, Model Deployment (simulated), Model Monitoring (data drift, performance), Retraining.
future_research: Expand datasets through multicenter collaborations, potentially using federated learning to preserve data privacy. Develop forecasting models that provide longer lead times without compromising accuracy. Implement the optimization model in real-world environments with variable staffing practices and conduct multicenter validation and real-world implementation studies to fully leverage the benefits of the system.

title: What about the data? A Mapping study on data engineering for ai systems
journal: Proceedings - 2024 IEEE/ACM 3rd International Conference on AI Engineering - Software Engineering for AI, CAIN 2024
reason: This paper is a mapping study focused on data engineering for AI systems, directly addressing the deployment, operation, and maintenance aspects of AI by exploring data infrastructure, MLOps practices, and architectural considerations. It identifies challenges and solutions related to data quality, pipelines, and architectures in AI contexts.
evidence_level: Meta-research (mapping study of 25 papers), with some papers including case studies, experiments, or demos.
deployment_context: Generic AI Implementation (covers both training and production pipelines, system-wide and enterprise-wide data architectures for AI systems across various domains, including industrial IoT and automotive, but not exclusively industrial)
operational_constraints: (1) Most organizations struggle with data infrastructure to apply AI models in practice, both at a project level (e.g., how to retrain pre-trained models with small datasets, create synthetic data, merge data sources, store/version data, automate pre-processing) and an organizational level (e.g., integrating data from different systems, protecting sensitive data, dealing with privacy, cleaning data, designing enterprise-wide data architectures). (2) Data quality carries elevated significance in high-stakes AI due to its heightened downstream impact, yet it is often undervalued. (3) Lack of standardized terminology within data engineering for AI leads to diverse aspects being considered without a common understanding. (4) Data engineering for AI often focuses narrowly on setting up a data pipeline for a single ML model, rather than system-wide or enterprise-wide data architecture.
mlops_practices: (1) Data validation systems: Designed to detect anomalies in data fed into machine learning pipelines, deployed as an integral part of end-to-end ML platforms (e.g., Google's TFX). (2) Continuous training platforms: Adapt to changes in incoming data using techniques like proactive training, online statistics computation, and dynamic materialization to reduce retraining and deployment costs. (3) Data processing frameworks: Open-source frameworks that allow users to generate data pipelines from configuration files via web or command-line interfaces. (4) Data quality toolkits: Used to check training data quality, including workflows for calling APIs and evaluating various data quality metrics. (5) Synthetic data augmentation frameworks: Scalable frameworks tailored for specific applications (e.g., aerial autonomy) to generate synthetic datasets. (6) Multi-layer architectural patterns: Separate business logic from inference engines and data processing, and user interfaces from data collection, and data lakes from databases to improve operational stability. (7) Enterprise data catalogs: Provide comprehensive metadata management across data lakes and other data sources to enable self-service use of data and address data democratization. (8) Decentralized edge-to-cloud architectures: Implement machine learning pipelines for erroneous data repair and detection of deviations in sensor data, particularly in industrial IoT contexts. (9) DataOps: An approach that accelerates the delivery of high-quality results by automation and orchestration of data lifecycle stages, with identified use cases and an evolution model. (10) Automated training data validation: Frameworks that consider multiple levels of data validation checks (feature, dataset, cross-dataset, data stream) and provide actionable feedback and mitigation strategies. (11) Metadata engineering: Used to create Data Interface Abstractions (DIAs) for models, supporting dual deployment of models and DIAs, and checkpoints between phases in federated learning.
gaps_identified: (1) Most current papers focus on engineering training or production pipelines for AI systems, but lack overall data architecture guidance for AI systems or the AI-driven enterprise. (2) DataOps is not receiving enough attention in research, despite its importance in automating and orchestrating data lifecycle stages across the enterprise. (3) More research is needed on Data-Oriented Architecture (DOA) as a paradigm for AI data engineering, beyond the limited existing literature. (4) There is a need for effective methods to engineer AI systems that leverage new enterprise data management concepts like data fabrics and data meshes. (5) More research is needed on combining data spaces with federated learning for data sharing between organizations. (6) Most papers focus on data engineering for training data, but there is a lack of background on how to engineer production data pipelines, validate and monitor production data, and set up enterprise-wide production data architectures. (7) The integration of data-driven AI engineering with knowledge-driven AI engineering (knowledge engineering) needs further exploration. (8) The AI engineering research community could benefit from integrating with the AI system engineering research community, as they might run into similar data-related challenges.
key_findings: This mapping study provides an overview of existing literature on data engineering for AI systems from an AI engineering perspective, identifying 25 relevant papers from 2019-2023. It finds that while many papers address data engineering for training or production pipelines, there is a significant gap in overall data architecture guidance for AI systems or the AI-driven enterprise. The study highlights DataOps as a crucial, yet under-researched, area for accelerating enterprise data lifecycles and calls for more frameworks, best practices, and open-source tools to support practitioners. It also points to the need for more research on production data pipelines, enterprise-wide data architectures, and the integration of data-driven and knowledge-driven AI engineering.
method: Mapping study following Kitchenham and Charters guidelines, including query string definition, source selection (Google Scholar, ScienceDirect, ACM DL, DBLP, Elicit), paper selection based on inclusion/exclusion criteria, citation snowballing, and systematic coding of full texts.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Data (Generation, Ingestion, Transformation, Serving), ML (Training, Deployment, Monitoring), Dev (Development, Testing), Ops (Deployment, Monitoring, Maintenance, Governance)
future_research: (1) Update the analysis to include grey literature and books, and learn from case studies what is missing in practice. (2) Develop a data engineering toolbox for AI engineers, including tooling for project-level data pipelines and enterprise-level data architectures. (3) Develop an integrated data engineering and AI engineering approach. (4) Conduct a mapping study on 'How to do data engineering for Big Data?' to identify solutions applicable to AI systems. (5) Investigate the body of knowledge and tools related to data quality and data smells in AI systems. (6) Further research on DataOps as a separate research field, focusing on approaches that accelerate the entire enterprise data lifecycle. (7) More research on Data-Oriented Architecture (DOA) to establish its utility in solving AI engineering challenges. (8) Research on how to effectively engineer AI systems using new enterprise data management concepts like data fabrics and data meshes. (9) More research on combining data spaces with federated learning. (10) More background on how to engineer production data pipelines, validate and monitor production data, and set up enterprise-wide production data architectures. (11) Research on how to combine data-driven AI engineering with knowledge-driven AI engineering. (12) Explore the integration of AI engineering with systems engineering for data-related challenges.

title: An empirical guide to MLOps adoption: Framework, maturity model and taxonomy
journal: Information and Software Technology
reason: The paper directly addresses MLOps adoption, frameworks, and maturity models, focusing on the operational aspects of ML/AI deployment in production environments across various companies. It provides a structured approach to adopting, assessing, and advancing MLOps, which is central to the SLR's scope.
deployment_context: Diverse industries including statistics, banking, healthcare, pharmaceuticals, vehicle manufacturing, gaming, mining, telecommunications, home appliance manufacturing, aviation, autonomous driving, energy, and pumps. Focus on general MLOps adoption rather than specific industrial AI constraints.
operational_constraints: (1) Lack of awareness of best practices in MLOps leads companies to adopt similar, often suboptimal, approaches. (2) Existing MLOps maturity models (Microsoft, Google, Amazon) advise a 'one-size-fits-all' approach, which needs adjustment for each company's specific context. (3) Existing maturity models are not applicable for companies that want to skip or prioritize a specific stage based on their maturity. (4) Existing maturity models assume that adopting companies have the required size, infrastructure, and budget to progress through the model. (5) Existing maturity models do not consider external constraints (e.g., regulations) that may affect a company's ability to adopt MLOps. (6) Difficulty in balancing the level of detail and simplicity when claiming adherence to a particular MLOps maturity stage, as different product areas within a company may be at different stages. (7) Disconnect with the sequential approach of maturing DataOps before MLOps, especially in companies with diverse and frequently changing data sources, where DataOps maturation may need to be parallel with MLOps. (8) Significant ignorance of constant development and post-deployment activities after deploying the model, particularly for task-solving rather than purely predictive models. (9) Difficulty in differentiating between 'well-defined' and 'manual' CI/CD pipelines due to the tool-agnostic nature of the MLOps framework, requiring more strict requirements. (10) Mismatch in roles when companies start to focus on automation, as existing roles may not be aligned with the new automated processes.
mlops_practices: (1) Standardized and automated processes for data management, governance, versioning, and feature stores (DataOps stage). (2) Standardized and automated processes for model development, including code versioning, code review, model metadata management, and reproducible experimentation setup. (3) Standardized and automated processes for model deployment, including frequent deployments and highly automated CI/CD pipelines. (4) Standardized and automated processes for model monitoring and retraining, including performance alerting mechanisms and automated retraining triggers. (5) Clearly defined and specialized roles within teams, with a focus on automation and continuous improvement. (6) Regular communication and collaboration with stakeholders and teams, including automated reports/dashboards for performance updates. (7) Use of internal MLOps platforms to streamline and automate the end-to-end ML lifecycle (Company H). (8) Training simulation models for deployment in test rigs (Company I). (9) Utilizing microcontrollers inside embedded products to obtain training data (Company J). (10) Providing embedded solutions (Company K). (11) Service solutions for storing operational data and enabling informed decisions (Company M). (12) Adoption of federated ML, synthetic data generation, and homographic encryption for sensitive data (Company C). (13) Adherence to GxP (good practice) for pharmaceutical data (Company D).
gaps_identified: (1) Existing MLOps maturity models are 'one-size-fits-all' and do not account for company-specific contexts, priorities, infrastructure, budget, or external constraints like regulations. (2) A lack of emphasis on model quality and assurance in current MLOps frameworks. (3) Insufficient differentiation between 'well-defined' and 'manual' CI/CD pipelines in tool-agnostic frameworks. (4) Current MLOps literature and pipelines significantly ignore constant development and post-deployment activities for models. (5) The sequential maturation of DataOps before MLOps is not always practical, especially with diverse and new data sources, suggesting a need for parallel maturation. (6) Infrastructure and tooling descriptions in MLOps frameworks are often non-congruent with maturity stages, implying that more tools automatically lead to higher maturity. (7) A need for more specific focus on the development of software components/workloads for automated re-training and integration into automated software delivery pipelines, rather than just model development. (8) The 'Kaizen' stage in maturity models lacks concrete, measurable targets for continuous improvement. (9) The absence of specific descriptions or requirements for roles and tools in MLOps frameworks makes them less suitable for external evaluations.
key_findings: The study provides a comprehensive MLOps framework, maturity model, and taxonomy, empirically validated across fourteen companies, to guide the adoption, assessment, and advancement of MLOps practices. Key findings indicate that while companies share similarities in basic data practices, significant differences exist in advanced MLOps areas like feature stores and automated deployments. The proposed framework addresses limitations of existing models by offering a flexible, five-stage progression (Ad hoc to Kaizen) that incorporates organizational aspects and promotes continuous improvement. The MLOps taxonomy, based on regulatory compliance and operational excellence, helps companies tailor MLOps practices to specific use cases, revealing that most companies are not yet in their desired MLOps adoption stage.
method: Multi-case study involving semi-structured interviews with practitioners from 14 companies (7 for initial study, 6 for workshop, 5 for validation interviews) and MLOps workshops. Data analysis involved open coding and triangulation of interview transcripts. The MLOps framework, maturity model, and taxonomy were developed based on empirical findings and refined through validation feedback categorized as confirmatory, constructive, and optimization.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: End-to-end ML lifecycle, covering Data (collection, management, governance, versioning, feature stores), Model (development, training, versioning, metadata, experimentation, quality, assurance), Deployment (promotion, integration, CI/CD, rollback), Operations & Infrastructure (monitoring, retraining, alerting, infrastructure, tooling), and Organization (roles, collaboration, communication, training).
future_research: (1) Explore various strategies for transferring MLOps knowledge across teams within companies. (2) Emphasize the significance of quantitative metrics (e.g., automation, deployment success rate, integration/delivery decisions, pipeline triggers) to provide measurable insights into MLOps effectiveness and efficiency. (3) Combine quantitative metrics evaluation with the qualitative MLOps framework for a comprehensive perspective. (4) Explore the potential of a Kubernetes-based MLOps framework in achieving the MLOps practices outlined in the Kaizen stage of the maturity model.

title: Towards Trustworthy Machine Learning in Production: An Overview of the Robustness in MLOps Approach
journal: ACM Computing Surveys
reason: This paper is a comprehensive survey of robustness in MLOps, directly addressing the deployment, operation, and maintenance of ML/AI models in production environments. It details specific operational constraints, MLOps practices, and identifies gaps in current approaches related to achieving robustness.
deployment_context: General ML/AI production environments, with specific examples from e-commerce, traffic prediction, and medical imaging.
operational_constraints: (1) Dynamic nature of post-deployment data: Input data distribution P(X) often differs from training data, leading to performance degradation. (2) Costly or unavailable ground-truth labels: Ground-truth labels for production data might be expensive or impossible to collect immediately, hindering performance monitoring. (3) Data corruption and inconsistencies: Real-life applications suffer from high rates of erroneous data from sensors, requiring cleaning and corrections. (4) Data scarcity: Some real-world applications, especially in medical and industrial sectors, generate few data points, hindering the development of learning solutions with good generalization. (5) Resource limitations: Performing data operations requires huge computing and storage resources, which may not always be available in real-time production systems. (6) Concept drift: Changes in the probability distribution P(y|X) representing the relationship between input data and target variables, caused by factors like machinery degradation or changing customer behavior. (7) Label noise: Corruption in historical data due to incomplete information, measurement errors, or incorrect labels, leading to poor deployment decisions. (8) Large hyperparameter search space: Finding optimal hyperparameter values is challenging due to the vast search space, especially for complex algorithms like neural networks.
mlops_practices: (1) Robust Continuous Validation: Testing ML solutions against various challenges (anomalies, drift, adversarial attacks) using curated validation splits containing potential production data issues (outliers, missing data, distribution shifts) and selecting multiple performance metrics (e.g., F-score for class imbalance, AUC-ROC) to capture robustness characteristics. (2) Robust Continuous Versioning: Implementing a centralized version control mechanism to record historical versions of ML artifacts (configurations, hyperparameters, data, algorithms) with consistent version numbers for reproducibility and traceability. (3) Robust Continuous Monitoring: Lifelong monitoring of post-production models to detect degradation in model performance and data quality (outliers, distributional shift, concept drift) by comparing current behavior against a pre-defined benchmark, even when ground-truth labels are unavailable. (4) Robust Continuous Update: Triggering update signals based on performance degradation or distributional shift thresholds to re-train, adapt, or replace ML models with newly arriving data, often through feedback loops. (5) Robust Data Cleaning: Handling missing and duplicate values (e.g., imputation, data fusion), performing anomaly detection (point, contextual, collective anomalies), and enforcing domain-based constraints to ensure high data quality. (6) Robustness to Distribution Shift: Employing systematic detection (statistical tests like Kolmogorov-Smirnov, chi-square) and adaptation mechanisms (importance weighting, uncertainty estimation) to cope with changes in input data distribution. (7) Robustness to Data Scarcity: Utilizing data enrichment techniques like data augmentation (generating synthetic data, GANs) or transfer learning to enhance applications with limited training data. (8) Robust Hyperparameter Optimization: Employing automated and robust hyperparameter optimization methods (e.g., Bayesian optimization, efficient sampling, pruning algorithms) to find configurations that produce consistent performance across validation and test splits. (9) Robustness to Concept Drift: Diagnosing and detecting concept drift (e.g., via performance degradation, input data deviation, hybrid monitoring) and activating adaptation strategies like model re-training with recent data or ensemble approaches. (10) Model Generalizability: Improving the ability of ML models to generalize to unseen distributions through techniques like expanding training datasets with adversarial examples, cross-validation, regularization, and transfer learning. (11) Robustness to Label Noise: Implementing noise-tolerant strategies in learning algorithms, such as model-based methods or probabilistic methods (Bayesian, clustering), to handle corrupted historical data.
gaps_identified: (1) Lack of comprehensive frameworks for holistic robustness: None of the existing frameworks address all aspects of building a robust ML system; they partially handle robustness (e.g., robustness to outliers, drift, or noise). (2) Insufficient integration of external datasets in versioning: Current version control mechanisms like K2E lack integration with external datasets, limiting versatility. (3) Limited profiling capabilities in monitoring: Some monitoring systems like Velox lack comprehensive profiling capabilities, hindering in-depth diagnosis. (4) Inefficient GPU utilization for inference: Continuous update approaches like ModelCI-e do not fully utilize allocated GPUs for inference tasks. (5) Lack of real-time capabilities in data quality verification: Automated data quality verification platforms need enhanced real-time capabilities. (6) Limited applicability of distribution shift detection: Some distribution shift detection methods are limited to specific ML models, data types (e.g., image/textual), or tasks (e.g., classification). (7) Absence of model versioning in data scarcity solutions: Systems like Overton, which augment data for scarcity, lack model versioning capabilities. (8) Limited concurrent resource access: Resource scheduling systems like SensiX++ may require improvements in concurrent resource access for edge devices. (9) Lack of support for external optimizers in hyperparameter tuning: Frameworks like Optuna lack interfaces to support deploying external optimizers. (10) Inability to handle unseen drifts: Some concept drift approaches cannot handle unseen drifts, leading to accuracy drops. (11) Limited generalizability of generalization approaches: Many generalization methods are limited to specific tasks or domains (e.g., medical imaging, supervised tasks). (12) High hyperparameter tuning for label noise: Label noise handling methods like AutoDO may require tuning a large number of hyperparameters. (13) Uninvestigated trade-offs between robustness aspects: The underlying effects and trade-offs between various robustness aspects in MLOps subtasks are not thoroughly investigated. (14) Inadequate quantification of overall system robustness: Current evaluation methods primarily focus on quantitative performance metrics, lacking comprehensive, aspect-specific methodologies for assessing resilience to adversarial attacks, interpretability, and generalization across diverse datasets. (15) Lack of unified holistic robust MLOps approaches: There is a lack of clarity on how existing partial robustness approaches collectively address the multitude of influencing factors.
key_findings: This paper provides a comprehensive overview of robustness in MLOps, formalizing specifications for robust ML systems in production as a key component of trustworthy AI. It categorizes robustness aspects into automation management, DataOps, and ModelOps, detailing specific practices and challenges within each. A key finding is that while many existing MLOps tools support continuous operations, there are significant gaps in their built-in features for advanced robustness aspects like data augmentation, concept drift detection, and generalizability. The paper emphasizes the need for a holistic approach to robustness, acknowledging the complex interplay and trade-offs between different robustness aspects, and calls for flexible MLOps architectures to adapt to dynamic real-world applications.
method: Literature Review/Survey
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Data Collection, Data Preparation, Model Training, Model Testing, Model Evaluation, Model Deployment, Continuous Monitoring, Continuous Update, Governance, Versioning, Resource Management
future_research: (1) Investigate trade-offs in robustness aspects: Examine the underlying effects and trade-offs between various robustness aspects of MLOps subtasks, as improving one aspect may deteriorate another. (2) Quantify overall robustness: Develop comprehensive and aspect-specific methodologies for evaluating robustness beyond traditional performance metrics, including resilience to adversarial attacks, interpretability, and generalization, potentially using explainable AI techniques. title: Improving Automotive Aftermarket Forecasting with MLOps

operational_constraints: ["(1) Managing thousands of Machine Learning (ML) models with complex data in highly complex environments requires a robust MLOps approach.", "(2) The inherent complexity of predicting each part's sales, especially for intermittent demand patterns which can experience significant spikes or extended periods without sales.", "(3) The need for a transparent, reliable, and reproducible ML system from beginning to end, with a well-defined protocol for all ML life cycle workflows.", "(4) The challenge of identifying underlying relationships between automotive parts (e.g., brake pads bought with brake rotors, spark plug sales affecting injector sales).", "(5) The computational cost and time required for training a large number of single-time series models (thousands of parts) compared to a single multi-time series model, even if individual single-time series models train faster.", "(6) The need to reduce costs associated with computational resources for training/deployments, which can be triggered unnecessarily without proper governance."]

key_findings: The study proposes a novel MLOps pipeline for automotive aftermarket parts demand prediction, implementing two deep learning forecasting strategies: single-time series and multi-time series. It finds that the multi-time series approach, particularly using the Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT) model, significantly outperforms single-time series models for both 6-month and 12-month horizons, especially for intermittent demand patterns. The MLOps approach, leveraging GitOps and ChatOps, enhanced efficiency, transparency, and reproducibility, minimizing errors and optimizing resource management in managing a large number of models.

The MLOps architecture leverages Kubeflow on Azure Kubernetes Service, with GitOps, ChatOps, Pulumi, and MLFlow for workflow orchestration, deployment, and governance. Model performance is evaluated using MAE, RMSE, and SMAPE.
