title: Monitoring morphometric drift in lifelong learning segmentation of the spinal cord
journal: Imaging Neuroscience
reason: The paper describes a lifelong learning framework for deploying and continuously updating an AI model for spinal cord segmentation, explicitly discussing MLOps practices like automated monitoring of model drift and retraining strategies.
deployment_context: Medical imaging (spinal cord segmentation) in a multi-site clinical research setting, with implications for clinical diagnostic and prognostic biomarker derivation.
operational_constraints: ["(1) Obtaining stable morphometric measurements is challenging due to varying levels of partial volume effects from different MRI contrasts and resolutions, leading to subtle shifts in the boundary between the cord and the cerebrospinal fluid.", "(2) The stability of morphometric measurements is inherently dependent on the version of the segmentation tool and may drift as newer versions are released, posing a challenge for longitudinal studies.", "(3) Lack of standardization in previous automatic spinal cord segmentation, with models often developed in isolation using different procedures for ground-truth masks, architectures, and training strategies.", "(4) Morphometric measures exhibit substantial inter-participant variability driven by factors such as age and sex, limiting sensitivity to subtle changes, and normalization techniques assume morphometrics are computed using the same method as the normative reference, which breaks down with model evolution.", "(5) Data gathered from multiple sites tend to be noisy due to various imaging artifacts, metallic hardware, and environmental noise, and noisy GT masks (due to inter-rater variability) can disrupt model training by making models unintentionally focus on outliers.", "(6) Human intervention for data curation (e.g., manual correction of segmentations) is undesirable in a post-deployment lifelong learning scenario, making it prone to errors and hindering full automation.", "(7) Stagnation of the training data distribution over time, where new data from different populations (pediatric, adult, geriatric) or acquisition parameters can make older training data unrepresentative, requiring methods to detect and manage distribution shifts.", "(8) The current strategy for monitoring and evaluating morphometric drift relies on a fixed set of contrasts in a 'frozen' test set of healthy participants, which may not fully capture true performance across diverse pathologies and contrasts."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) Lifelong Learning Framework: A continuous training strategy to update models over time, monitor performance drift, and manage model retraining, ensuring robustness to data distribution shifts.", "(2) Automated Drift Monitoring with GitHub Actions: An automatic workflow triggered by model releases on GitHub that downloads public datasets, runs morphometric analysis, generates plots quantifying drift between current and previous model versions, and updates GitHub releases with results.", "(3) Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment (CI/CD): The automated GitHub Actions workflow facilitates model development through CI/CD by performing steps automatically once a model is released.", "(4) Frozen Test Set: A publicly available, 'frozen' test set is used to ensure newer models are evaluated fairly against earlier versions, avoiding data privacy issues when running morphometric analysis on the cloud.", "(5) Data Curation and Quality Control (QC): New datasets are labeled with existing automatic models, and a visual QC tool (sct_qc) is used to identify failed segmentations requiring manual corrections and flag images with strong artifacts for exclusion, ensuring input data quality.", "(6) Model Deployment and Accessibility: The trained model is deployed as an official release on GitHub and integrated into the Spinal Cord Toolbox (SCT), allowing easy download and use via a simple command.", "(7) Normative Database Update Strategy: The most recent version of the segmentation model is used to re-segment all images in a normative database of healthy adults, and scaling factors are computed to allow comparison of morphometric measures across different segmentation models, ensuring backward compatibility."]
gaps_identified: ["Standardization is lacking in the development of automatic spinal cord segmentation pipelines, with no continuous learning pipeline existing to monitor or mitigate drift in segmentation performance over time.", "Existing models are unable to automatically utilize incoming data as it arrives in a fully automated lifelong learning scenario, necessitating periodic human checks to prevent degradation of model performance.", "The current evaluation methods for morphometric drift, relying on a fixed set of contrasts in a 'frozen' healthy participant test set, may not adequately capture true model performance across diverse pathologies and contrasts.", "Methods are needed for evaluating morphometric drift beyond CSA, potentially including other commonly used spinal cord morphometrics, on data from both healthy participants and those with pathologies.", "Mechanisms to address stagnation of training data distribution are needed, such as comparing histogram-based distribution shifts using KL divergence or detecting drifts in the feature space by extracting radiomic features, to ensure continued relevance of training and test sets for future models."]
key_findings: This study presents an automatic spinal cord segmentation model trained on a diverse, multi-site dataset (75 sites, 1,631 participants, 9 MRI contrasts, various pathologies), achieving an average Dice score of 0.95 ± 0.03 and outperforming previous versions and pathology-specific models, especially for lumbar spinal cord and severely compressed/lesioned images. A novel lifelong learning framework with an automatic GitHub Actions workflow is introduced to monitor morphometric drift, providing a quick feedback loop for model development. The framework successfully updated a normative database, showing a nearly constant scaling factor for morphometric measures between model versions, demonstrating its utility for continuous model improvement and maintaining data consistency.
method: The study developed a 3D U-Net based segmentation model using the nnUNet framework, trained on a large, heterogeneous dataset. A lifelong learning framework was implemented using GitHub Actions for automated continuous integration and deployment (CI/CD), including automated monitoring of morphometric drift (CSA variability) on a 'frozen' test set. An ablation study was performed to assess the impact of recursively generated ground truth masks. The framework was validated by updating a normative database of spinal cord morphometrics and computing scaling factors.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Deployment, Monitoring, Retraining, Governance
future_research: ["Future work could add better methods for evaluating morphometric drift (e.g., by computing other commonly used spinal cord morphometrics) on data from both healthy participants and from participants with spinal cord pathologies, potentially adapting GitHub Actions workflows to include evaluations of pathological cases.", "Develop methods to address stagnation of the training data distribution when developing models over time, such as comparing histogram-based distribution shifts using KL divergence or detecting drifts in the feature space by extracting radiomic features, to ensure the continued relevance of the training and test sets for evaluating future models.", "Investigate strategies for keeping only a subset of old data when training new models if the drift between data distributions is large."]

title: Challenges and Opportunities in Integrating LLMs into Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment (CI/CD) Pipelines

deployment_context: Generic AI Implementation (LLMs in a cloud-based CI/CD pipeline for text summarization)
operational_constraints: (1) LLMs are computationally expensive and resource-intensive, requiring specialized hardware and infrastructure to train and run. (2) LLMs are prone to errors and biases, compromising quality and reliability, and raising ethical/social concerns. (3) LLMs are dynamic and evolving, requiring constant monitoring and updating to reflect the latest data and feedback. (4) Adapting and customizing CI/CD pipelines for LLMs is challenging due to the diversity and complexity of natural language, sensitivity/variability of LLM outputs, and ethical/social implications. (5) Developing and implementing effective and reliable testing and evaluation methods for LLMs is difficult. (2) DataOps: Collecting, preprocessing, and managing data for LLM training/evaluation, ensuring quality, security, and privacy. (3) ModelOps: Developing, testing, and deploying LLMs, ensuring performance, scalability, and robustness. (4) Prompt Engineering: Crafting effective prompts to guide LLM behavior and elicit desired outputs, ensuring relevance, accuracy, and diversity. (5) Human Feedback Loop: Incorporating human feedback into LLM training and refinement, ensuring alignment, fairness, and safety. This includes user ratings, reviews, comments, and requests for retraining/refinement. (6) Responsible AI: Designing, developing, deploying, and using LLMs responsibly, ensuring accountability, transparency, and ethics. (7) CI/CD Pipeline Integration: Automated workflows for LLM delivery, including code development, testing, deployment, and monitoring stages. (8) Cloud-based Deployment: Using Azure Machine Learning service for creating, managing, and deploying ML models, and Azure DevOps for CI/CD pipelines. (9) Performance Monitoring: Measuring latency, throughput, and availability of the deployed LLM web service. (10) Usage Monitoring: Tracking requests, users, sessions, and distribution/frequency of input/output texts. (11) Continuous Retraining/Refinement: Retraining LLMs on new data from user feedback and refining prompts based on user suggestions, then deploying new versions via CI/CD.
gaps_identified: (1) MLOps alone is not sufficient to handle the specific characteristics and requirements of LLMs, such as the diversity and complexity of natural language, the sensitivity and variability of LLM outputs, and the ethical and social implications of LLM applications. (2) Existing CI/CD pipelines require adaptation and customization to suit the specific characteristics and requirements of LLMs. (3) There is a need for effective and reliable testing and evaluation methods specifically for LLMs, beyond standard software testing.
key_findings: The paper proposes an LLMOps framework to address the unique challenges of deploying and maintaining Large Language Models (LLMs) in production. Through a case study integrating a T5 model for text summarization into an Azure-based CI/CD pipeline, the authors demonstrate that the LLM's performance (ROUGE scores) and quality (user ratings) significantly improved after incorporating a human feedback loop. This highlights the critical role of continuous monitoring, feedback integration, and iterative refinement in LLM operationalization, showing that LLMOps can lead to faster, higher quality, and more manageable LLM services.
method: The paper describes a case study methodology. It involved: (1) Data collection and preprocessing using the CNN/Daily Mail dataset. (2) LLM development and testing using the T5 model, fine-tuned on the training set and evaluated with ROUGE metric. (3) LLM deployment and monitoring using Azure Machine Learning and Azure DevOps to create a CI/CD pipeline. (4) Evaluation of performance (ROUGE scores), usage (requests, users, sessions), and feedback (ratings, reviews, human feedback loop) of the deployed LLM. title: Development of a virtual metrology system for smart manufacturing: A case study of spandex fiber production

operational_constraints: ["(1) Time synchronization: In continuous production, process data are highly dependent and autocorrelated, requiring consideration of time lags between process data and product quality, and tracing back to relevant process information points due to variable flow times.", "(2) Recirculation process: The recycling of intermediate products (e.g., dope polymer for additional ripening) complicates data synchronization as it affects both quality and quantity of subsequent products and alters operating conditions.", "(3) Autocorrelated features: Quality indicators and process data in continuous processes are autocorrelated, meaning they depend on previous measurements, which can cause multicollinearity and undermine explanatory power in models like LASSO.", "(4) Metrology delay: Manual quality inspections (e.g., dope polymer viscosity) take significant time (e.g., 30 min for sampling, 4-16 hours between inspections), leading to belated recognition of abnormal situations and inability to take preventive actions in real-time.", "(5) Non-constant flow time: The flow time of intermediate products is not constant due to chemical reactions and ripening procedures, making it difficult to synchronize process data with target quality attributes.", "(6) Need for explainable models: For advanced process control and stakeholder comprehension (operators, data scientists, regulators), the working mechanism of the VM model needs to be comprehensible, favoring white-box models over black-box ones.", "(7) Model refresh strategy: Models built in a certain period lose predictive power on new data streams, necessitating a robust refresh strategy to determine recalibration frequency and optimal training data, especially in response to uncertain operational events or concept drifts.", "(8) Non-constant sampling interval for quality data: The irregular sampling period for quality measurements makes it impractical to use recent measurements directly as an explanatory variable in conventional time series models, as patterns would be distorted."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) Feature engineering for time synchronization: Retention times for containers and recirculation pipes are calculated using flowmeters, solid content, spinning rates, tank sizes, pressure indices, and maximum discharge rates to align process data with target quality attributes, addressing variable time lags in continuous processes.", "(2) Feature selection (FS) and discretization (FD): Methods like MDLP, ChiMerge, mRMR, and IWFS are used to select a subset of relevant features (e.g., 30 out of 113 candidates) to improve model efficiency, interpretability, and predictive performance, validated with domain expert knowledge.", "(3) White-box model preference: LASSO regression is chosen for model construction due to its explainability and ability to provide risk measures (confidence/prediction intervals), which is crucial for operator understanding and decision-making in industrial settings.", "(4) Prediction smoothing: A post-hoc adjustment procedure is devised to combine machine learning predictions with the most recent measurement values, using dynamic weights based on the age of the reference value (e.g., less than 8h, 8-16h, more than 16h) to account for autocorrelated target values and non-equidistant sampling.", "(5) Rolling-origin hold-out sample testing: Used for evaluating feature selection and model accuracy, simulating how a VM system would function in practice by accumulating data in a moving window (e.g., 15-day window, 5-day test period).", "(6) Automatic model refresh strategy: A default refresh period (e.g., 15 days) is established based on domain knowledge, with a contingency plan for supplementary refresh periods (e.g., 29 days) triggered when prediction error (MAPE) exceeds a threshold (e.g., 1%), ensuring continuous usability and reliable predictions.", "(7) User dashboard for visualization and simulation: The VM model is integrated into a quality management system with a dashboard that visualizes real-time virtual predictions alongside actual sampling results, and allows for simulation of virtual operating conditions to guide preventive actions.", "(8) Monitoring of process data changes: The deployed system provides detailed visualization of selected predictors to inform operators whether operating conditions are within steady states, aiding in model update strategies and potential model reconstruction."]
gaps_identified: ["Abundance of literature on VM applications but less attention to continuous production contexts, overlooking fundamental challenges compared to intermittent/batch processes.", "Most previous VM studies were conducted on well-defined intermittent production processes (e.g., semiconductor industry) where exploration for relevant and/or interdependent features is less of a concern.", "Few studies have adopted prediction models that incorporate algorithmic transparency (explainable models), as few VM models have been deployed in the real-world.", "Little attention has been paid to refresh strategies for VM models, which are essential for models developed in isolated experimental systems to behave as expected in continuous production.", "Existing real-world VM applications with refresh strategies often use predetermined periods without verifying predictive performance over that period.", "Conventional time series models are not readily applicable to non-constant sampling intervals for autocorrelated data, as patterns would be distorted by the time factor.", "Previous studies utilizing lagged features in VM models often assume a constant time lag, which is not applicable to processes with variable flow times.", "Conceptualized synchronization processes in prior work suffer from excessive computational complexity due to including all data points within a relevant period, making them unfeasible for real-time monitoring."]
key_findings: The paper successfully developed and deployed a Virtual Metrology (VM) system for spandex fiber production, demonstrating how practical challenges in continuous processes (time synchronization, recirculation, autocorrelated features) can be addressed using data analytics. method: The study employs a case study approach for developing and deploying a Virtual Metrology (VM) system. The methodology includes prediction smoothing for autocorrelated target values and an operational optimization strategy for model refreshing, all evaluated using rolling-origin hold-out sample tests and validated with domain expert knowledge.

title: Scalable Inference Architectures for Operationalizing AI/ML in Enterprise Systems

mlops_practices: ["(1) Serverless inference deployment: Models load on-demand, scaling out automatically when requests arrive and tearing down during inactivity, eliminating idle costs. This is the default mode, with an option for provisioned concurrency for steady high load.", "(2) Modular architecture with decoupled components: Model hosting is separated from business logic and client interaction. Pre-processing and post-processing logic are handled in separate modules (serverless functions for lightweight tasks, proxy microservices for heavier processing).", "(3) Event-driven, graph-based workflow (Atlas engine): Orchestrates multiple reasoning tasks concurrently using a publish-subscribe asynchronous pattern, allowing horizontal scaling of agents/functions.", "(4) Automated CI/CD pipeline (HAR Deployer): Extends existing model delivery pipelines to interface with the new inference backend. When a new model version is registered to the Model Store, the pipeline triggers an import API call to make it available for serving.", "(5) Declarative configuration: YAML files specify model ID and backend type (serverless or dedicated) for API endpoints, allowing easy switching between model versions or infrastructure without code changes.", "(6) Comprehensive monitoring: Integration with central monitoring services to track request rates, latencies, and errors for each model.", "(7) Cold-start mitigation strategies: Prediction Service includes logic for graceful handling of initial request timeouts (retries, queuing), pre-warming models, or using provisioned concurrency for critical services.", "(8) Modular and portable design: Model execution engine is abstracted (using DJL Serving) to plug in different frameworks (TensorRT, PyTorch) and optimizations (rolling batch inference) without changing the external API. Adherence to standard interfaces (REST APIs, containers) for cloud portability and hybrid deployment.", "(9) Hybrid deployment strategy: Combining serverless autoscaling for variable/bursty demand with traditional dedicated infrastructure (provisioned GPU clusters) for consistently high QPS or stringent low-latency requirements.", "(10) Automated tests and safeguards in deployment pipeline: Every model import runs integration tests in a staging environment to catch errors before traffic is switched over.", "(11) Integrated compliance checks: The architecture allows insertion of compliance checks and guardrails (e.g., Einstein Trust Layer for toxicity filtering, data masking) in the pre/post-processing layers."]
gaps_identified: ["Traditional static deployments and cloud offerings (like Amazon SageMaker's dedicated instances) incurred idle costs and struggled with sudden spikes, requiring over-provisioning of GPU instances for worst-case traffic.", "Complex model deployment pipelines and quota management in previous setups complicated the lifecycle of continuously updating AI models, leading to manual deployment steps and slow iteration cycles.", "Meeting real-time latency requirements for LLMs (billions of parameters) was non-trivial with fixed infrastructure.", "Achieving high resource utilization (GPU) was difficult with static deployments due to variable workloads.", "Ensuring cloud neutrality and avoiding vendor lock-in with proprietary cloud features was a challenge for enterprise-wide adoption."]
key_findings: Salesforce developed a modular, platform-agnostic inference architecture combining serverless execution, dynamic autoscaling, and MLOps pipelines to operationalize large AI/ML models in enterprise systems. title: Continuous Software Engineering Practices in AI/ML Development Past the Narrow Lens of MLOps: Adoption Challenges
journal: E-Informatica Software Engineering Journal
reason: The paper directly addresses challenges in adopting continuous software engineering practices, including MLOps, for ML system development in production contexts, focusing on organizational and communication issues.
deployment_context: Generic AI Implementation (ML systems developed for internal use or external customers, with varying degrees of operational involvement post-deployment)
operational_constraints: ["(1) ML experts often work in silos, detached from the rest of the project team and customers, hindering collaboration required for continuous practices.", "(2) SE methods used by the organization at large (e.g., Scrum, SAFe) often have little impact on the ML development processes, leading to a disconnect.", "(3) Automated testing in ML development remains a challenge due to perceived lack of suitable tooling for specific system contexts and the high technical know-how required for ML components, leading to manual testing by developers.", "(4) Lack of direct user and customer interaction makes it difficult for ML experts to understand if user expectations are fulfilled and to ensure continuous use.", "(5) The emphasis placed on operations varies greatly depending on project context; if the customer takes over entirely after release, there is little need for operational involvement from the development organization, limiting continuous monitoring and improvement.", "(6) New ideas or innovations are not automatically added to ongoing ML projects when an external customer is involved, as the customer sets the scope and any changes require their approval and funding.", "(7) Communication issues between ML experts and other stakeholders (developers, customers) arise from a lack of shared knowledge, lack of guiding frameworks, and unclear roles and responsibilities.", "(8) Resource estimation in ML development is difficult, leading to existing ML experts being stretched thin across multiple projects.", "(9) Customer indecisiveness and insufficient grasp of ML development realities lead to unpredictable changes and unreasonable demands."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) Sprint-based planning: Some organizations adopt agile approaches with sprints, but their impact on ML development is often limited to planning periods, not strict adherence to methodologies.", "(2) Manual testing: ML functions are often tested manually due to perceived lack of automated tools or the specialized knowledge required.", "(3) Automated testing via MLOps pipeline: Only one respondent mentioned utilizing an MLOps pipeline for automated testing.", "(4) Post-release monitoring (customer-led): Monitoring and problem detection are largely left to the customer after initial release, with ML experts providing help only during a short 'crisis period'.", "(5) Internal feedback loops for continuous improvement: For internal projects, feedback gathered after deployment is used to continuously improve the system."]
gaps_identified: ["Standard continuous SE practices (e.g., continuous compliance, continuous trust, continuous security) are often not present or actively adopted in ML development contexts, indicating a gap in their applicability or perceived relevance.", "Existing SE methods and frameworks (e.g., Scrum, SAFe) are not effectively integrated into ML development processes, leading to ML experts working in silos.", "Automated testing tools and practices for ML components are perceived as lacking or difficult to implement, especially for 'exotic' ML functionalities.", "Mechanisms for direct and continuous interaction between ML experts and end-users/customers are often absent, hindering feedback loops for continuous use and improvement.", "Current project contractual models for externally commissioned ML projects do not adequately support continuous operations, improvement, and innovation from the development organization's perspective.", "There is a lack of shared knowledge and mutual understanding between ML experts and software developers/stakeholders, leading to communication issues that impede interdisciplinary collaboration."]
key_findings: The study identifies that the adoption of continuous software engineering practices in ML development faces significant challenges primarily due to communication issues and ML experts working in silos. Key findings include the absence of continuous compliance, trust, and security practices, the limited impact of organizational SE methods on ML development, and difficulties with automated testing. Furthermore, a lack of direct user/customer interaction and project contexts where customers take over operations after deployment hinder continuous use, improvement, and innovation. These challenges suggest that the ML project lifecycle often deviates from a continuous cycle towards a more rigid, step-by-step model.
method: Multiple case study with qualitative, thematic, semi-structured interviews (n=8 ML experts from different organizations). Data analyzed using deductive coding based on Fitzgerald and Stol's 13 continuous* practices.

key_findings: The CAPTURE framework, a seven-phase stakeholder-centered iterative MLOps lifecycle, addresses critical gaps in existing ML lifecycle frameworks by integrating stakeholder-centered requirements engineering with MLOps practices. Validated through a five-year longitudinal case study of a psychomotor skill learning system and expert interviews, CAPTURE supports governance of iterative development and strategic evolution through explicit decision gates. Key findings include the necessity of an intermediate stakeholder-alignment layer, the value of participatory modeling, and the importance of treating infrastructure as a governed lifecycle entity. By connecting technical MLOps with human-centered design, CAPTURE reduces the risk of sensor-based AI systems becoming ungoverned, non-compliant, or misaligned with user needs over time.
method: The CAPTURE framework was synthesized from four established standards (ISO/IEC 22989, ISO 9241-210, CRISP-ML(Q), SE4ML). Validation involved conceptual cognitive walkthroughs using hypothetical scenarios, a longitudinal five-year case study of a psychomotor skill learning system, and semi-structured interviews with ten domain experts.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Full lifecycle (Inception, Design, Development, Verification & Validation, Deployment, Operation & Monitoring, Re-evaluation, Retirement)
future_research: Future work includes developing formal artifact models for automated consistency checking, extending adoption checklists and gate templates into domain-specific instantiations, and creating tool implementations for CAPTURE phases and decision gate automation. Research directions also involve exploring AI-assisted lifecycle governance, formal model-based verification of ML lifecycle decisions, and extended case studies across higher-criticality sensor-based systems (medical diagnostics, autonomous vehicles, Industry 5.0, smart cities) to validate scalability and inform threshold calibration. Comparative studies between CAPTURE-guided and traditional ML development across diverse domains are also needed.

title: Bridging the Gap Between MLOps and RLOps: An Industry 4.0 Case Study on Architectural Design Decisions in Practice
journal: Proceedings - 2025 IEEE 22nd International Conference on Software Architecture, ICSA 2025
reason: The paper presents a case study on deploying Reinforcement Learning in an Industry 4.0 Cyber-Physical Production System, explicitly discussing MLOps and RLOps architectural design decisions and their applicability and adaptation in a production environment.
deployment_context: Industry 4.0 Cyber-Physical Production Systems (CPPS) for manufacturing automation, specifically Reinforcement Learning (RL) agents in digital twins.
operational_constraints: (1) RL models learn by interacting with dynamic environments, necessitating continual adaptation and real-time decision-making, unlike ML models trained on static datasets. (2) Traditional ML data processing (e.g., data pipelines, ETL pipelines, data splitting into training/test sets) is not suited for RL, which relies on environmental data and rewards. (3) Model packaging as typically done in MLOps is not applied, as RL models are loaded directly from a model repository into an agent via an orchestrator component. (4) AutoML was not applied in the case study, suggesting that highly specialized models or specific RL challenges might not be fully addressed by generic AutoML solutions.
mlops_practices: (1) MLOps as a holistic approach is adopted, though adapted for RL. (2) A data processing component (environment interpreter) is used to provide environment data to agents, supporting automatic data processing. (3) A specialized feature store is used for persistence and access to feature data, with data flowing from the environment interpreter to the feature store via a trigger for offline RL and imitation learning. (4) Data ingestion is batched, triggered by changes in model performance or the environment (e.g., factory layout changes). (5) Model performance degradation and environment changes trigger training pipelines or model serving components. (6) A model building pipeline and model builder component are used for model training, alongside development tools for manual experimentation. (7) Data checkpoints (modified for offline RL) are used during model building. (8) CI/CD pipeline automation is used for model serving component deployment and containerization of Docker images. (9) Data versioning is applied, with source code in a code repository, environment data in a feature store, model metadata in an ML metadata store, and models in a model registry. (10) The system supports 'n' versions of models in production for staged releases and model version rollback using a model registry. (11) Specialized models with multi-agent coordination via shared rewards are used for Model Architecture. (12) Centralized training and decentralized execution multi-agent RL is employed, where models are centrally trained and multiple decentralized digital twin agents are executed. (13) Checkpoints are used to facilitate resumable training. (14) Transfer learning is utilized by fetching pre-trained models and retraining them to reduce overall training time. (15) Versatile distribution frameworks with logically centralized control are used for RL Distribution Strategy. (16) RL hyperparameter tuning is applied during the agent's initial training phase.
gaps_identified: (1) The scarcity of scientific literature on RLOps creates a gap in understanding how to successfully integrate RL into the MLOps paradigm. (2) MLOps practices need adaptation to accommodate the distinct demands of RL, leading to a modified form of MLOps for RL (RLOps). (3) ML-specific training data tasks (extraction, transformation, preparation, validation, hyperparameter tuning) are not applicable to RL, requiring different data processing approaches. (4) Standard MLOps practices for data splitting (e.g., into training and test data) do not apply to RL projects that do not use datasets for supervised/unsupervised learning. (5) Model packaging, common in MLOps, is not directly applicable when RL models are loaded directly from a repository into an agent via an orchestrator. (6) Some ML decision options, like feature engineering and Pipeline/Orchestrator Triggers on new training data, appear incompatible with RL at first glance but require modification to be suitable.
key_findings: The study found that 21 out of 22 MLOps and RL Architectural Design Decisions (ADDs) and 37 out of 86 decision options were applicable and used in a real-world Industry 4.0 Cyber-Physical Production System (CPPS) utilizing Reinforcement Learning (RL). A key insight is that many existing MLOps practices are directly transferable or adaptable to RL-based industrial systems, indicating a high level of cross-compatibility. The paper categorizes ADD decision options into four groups based on their RL compatibility: inapplicable, unused, those with RL equivalents, and directly applied, providing a framework for practitioners to navigate RLOps implementation. This work bridges the knowledge gap between MLOps and RLOps, offering practical guidance for integrating RL into Industry 4.0 CPPS architectures.
method: Exploratory, qualitative industry case study on a Cyber-Physical Production System (CPPS). Content analysis of 15 CPPS artifacts (source code, CI/CD pipeline definitions, architectural schematics, UML diagrams, presentations) was performed to identify the use of 22 known Architectural Design Decisions (ADDs) and 86 associated decision options. Relevant parts of the system were formally modeled using CodeableModels and visualized in UML.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Data Processing, Model Training, Model Deployment, Model Monitoring, Model Governance (Versioning, Rollback)
future_research: Future research includes exploring RLOps ADDs across various industries, conducting longitudinal studies on RLOps ADD evolution, and performing quantitative impact analyses. These studies could reveal sector-specific practices, new ADDs, track the maturation of RLOps practices, and provide empirical evidence of their effectiveness.

title: A Modular Framework for Business-Specific Benchmarking of Large Language Models
journal: 2025 13th International Scientific Conference on Computer Science, COMSCI 2025 - Proceedings
reason: The paper proposes a framework for business-specific benchmarking of LLMs, integrating evaluation into the MLOps lifecycle and discussing operational aspects like continuous integration and automated scoring.
deployment_context: Generic AI Implementation (LLMs in enterprise, e.g., financial advisory chatbot)
operational_constraints: (1) Standardized, holistic evaluation frameworks (e.g., HELM) lack domain-specific fidelity required for nuanced business applications. (2) Building fully specialized benchmarks from scratch is resource-intensive, often resulting in ad-hoc, unscalable, and non-reproducible testing scripts. (3) Traditional human-in-the-loop evaluation for LLMs is costly and time-consuming, creating a bottleneck for continuous improvement. (4) Single-metric optimization in LLM evaluation can lead to models that perform well on generic tasks but fail on critical business-specific risks like regulatory compliance or brand voice alignment. (5) Ensuring the reliability and mitigating bias of automated LLM-as-a-Judge systems requires specific protocols like anonymization, judge selection, structured prompting, ensembling, and self-consistency.
mlops_practices: (1) Continuous Integration and Evolution: The benchmark suite is integrated into a CI/CD pipeline, triggering automated tests on model, fine-tuning data, or prompt modifications. Performance degradation below a threshold acts as a gate to prevent flawed model deployment. (2) Automated LLM-as-a-Judge (LJ) Pipeline: A robust, automated pipeline for qualitative and rubric-based scoring, designed to maximize reliability and mitigate single-model bias through anonymization, judge selection (stronger/different-family model), structured JSON prompting, ensembling (two different judge models, multiple samples), and self-consistency (verdict only accepted if judges agree). (3) Multi-Layered Metric Selection: Uses a suite of metrics across Task Performance, Efficiency, Robustness/Safety, and Business Outcome categories, aggregated via a weighted scoring model to produce a composite 'Launch Score'. (4) Version-Controlled Test Suite: Data corpus is version-controlled and documented using DVC (distributed version control) to ensure reproducibility. (5) Human Audit Loop: Outputs flagged with low confidence or judge disagreement are automatically routed for human review. (6) Asset and Task Registry: Provides version-controlled storage for all evaluation artifacts (model references, test data, prompt templates, metric configurations). (7) Metrics and Results Store: Persistent storage for all evaluation outputs, including raw model responses, computed metric scores, and execution metadata. (8) Dashboard and Alerting: Visualization and notification layer that presents evaluation results and triggers alerts based on predefined rule violations.
gaps_identified: (1) A gap remains for a cohesive, business-oriented framework that integrates disparate elements of LLM evaluation (holistic benchmarks, domain-specific datasets, evaluation harnesses, runtime guardrails). (2) Current integrated frameworks do not guide organizations through the entire pre-deployment evaluation lifecycle, from translating business KPIs into hybrid test suites to implementing and maintaining the evaluation within a scalable architecture. (3) Existing academic benchmarks do not measure performance against specific business KPIs (e.g., customer satisfaction, operational cost, issue resolution rate) or adequately test for business-specific risks (e.g., regulatory compliance, brand voice, domain-specific adversarial attacks).
key_findings: The Business-Specific Benchmarking Framework (BSBF) provides a structured methodology and reference architecture for creating and maintaining LLM evaluations aligned with business KPIs and risk profiles. The empirical case study, comparing three LLMs for a financial advisory chatbot, demonstrated that the automated LLM-as-a-Judge approach identified the fine-tuned specialist model (Model C) as the most suitable and safest, despite a standard benchmark likely favoring a generalist model (Model A). This framework significantly reduces the cost and time associated with evaluation while providing a more accurate, risk-aware assessment of a model's fitness-for-purpose, enabling data-driven decision-making and responsible AI adoption.
method: Framework development, Case Study (experimental evaluation of three LLMs against a business-specific benchmark)
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Evaluation, Monitoring, Deployment (gating)
future_research: The paper implicitly suggests future work in further optimizing LJ reliability, exploring different ensemble strategies, and integrating the framework with broader enterprise governance systems.

title: Real-World Deployment of a ML Pipeline for Pressure Wounds Prediction

key_findings: The paper successfully deployed a fully automated AI clinical decision support system (CDSS) for pressure wound prediction in a hospital setting, leveraging routinely collected EHR data. title: Blockchain-Based Provenance for Artificial Intelligence Lifecycle Traceability
journal: Proceedings of the IEEE International Conference on Industrial Technology
reason: The paper proposes a blockchain-based system for ensuring traceability and enforcing constraints throughout the AI lifecycle, directly addressing the deployment, operation, and governance of AI models in production.
deployment_context: Microgrid energy consumption forecasting (real-world setting, La Trobe Energy AI Platform (LEAP))
operational_constraints: (1) Existing blockchain-based provenance systems are passive, recording lineage after the fact but offering no mechanism to prevent non-compliant artifacts from progressing through the lifecycle. (2) Computational cost: Approaches that attempt on-chain policy evaluation or verifiable computation incur excessive overhead, making them impractical for AI lifecycles where models are retrained frequently and evaluated against multiple criteria. (3) Volume of data generated by high-frequency training and inference loops strains on-chain storage. (4) Traditional MLOps pipelines allow practitioners to bypass failing tests by modifying pipeline configuration or submit altered artifacts without detection if lineage tracking relies on mutable logs.
mlops_practices: (1) Merkle-DAG for AI Lifecycle Provenance: Captures snapshots of the AI lifecycle state at each stage, including datasets, features, models, metrics, evaluation records, and human interactions (reviews, approvals, compliance attestations) as a single tamper-evident structure. Artifacts are content-addressed nodes, with child nodes cryptographically committing to their parents. (2) Hybrid Architecture: Combines off-chain constraint evaluation with on-chain enforcement. (3) Off-chain Constraint Evaluation: Open Policy Agent (OPA) evaluates the current provenance state against predefined constraints (e.g., RMSE thresholds, fairness metrics, human oversight requirements) off-chain, producing a signed attestation if requirements are satisfied. (4) On-chain Enforcement: A smart contract verifies the OPA attestation and enforces lifecycle transitions, anchoring the new Merkle root only if the cryptographic proof is valid. (5) Storage Separation: Artifact content (raw data, model weights, reports) is stored in IPFS as content-addressed JSON-LD objects, while the blockchain records only the 32-byte Merkle root and its linkage to the previous root, reducing on-chain storage costs. (6) Finite State Machine (FSM) for Lifecycle: The smart contract implements the AI lifecycle as an FSM, where transitions between states (Development, Validated, Approved) are gated by constraint evaluation. (7) Trusted Oracle (OPA): The OPA engine is deployed as a managed service within a consortium's trust boundary and signs transition payloads, with the smart contract verifying this signature using the corresponding public key.
gaps_identified: (1) Existing blockchain provenance systems are passive and do not actively prevent non-compliant artifacts from progressing. (2) On-chain policy evaluation is computationally expensive and impractical for frequently retrained AI models. (3) Current verifiable computation (e.g., ZK-ML) implementations cannot yet support the throughput required for continuous integration pipelines. (4) Traditional MLOps tools rely on administrative controls for traceability, auditability, and accountability, which can be bypassed.
key_findings: The proposed system, combining a Merkle-DAG for provenance, off-chain OPA for constraint evaluation, and on-chain smart contracts for enforcement, effectively addresses AI lifecycle traceability. Empirical validation on an energy forecasting task demonstrated that the architecture successfully blocks non-compliant artifacts (e.g., models with invalid performance metrics) and tampered provenance from progressing through the lifecycle. This approach replaces administrative trust with cryptographic proof, ensuring auditability, traceability, and accountability by preventing dominant failure modes in AI pipelines.
method: The paper proposes a novel architecture and empirically validates it through a proof-of-concept implementation. It integrates a Python provenance agent for Merkle-DAG construction, an OPA sidecar service for constraint evaluation, and a Solidity smart contract deployed on a local testnet. Experiments simulate three scenarios (valid metrics, invalid metrics, tampered root) and a human oversight scenario to test the system's ability to enforce constraints and prevent non-compliant or tampered artifacts from progressing.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Data Management, Model Development, Model Evaluation, Model Deployment, Model Monitoring, Governance, Traceability, Auditability
future_research: Future work includes evaluating performance in production blockchain environments and integrating Zero-Knowledge Machine Learning (ZK-ML) techniques to enable cryptographic proof of constraint evaluation, thereby eliminating the need to trust the OPA oracle.

title: Feature Drift-Guided Adaptive ML Retraining: An MLOps Approach for Big Data Analytics
journal: Proceedings of the IEEE International Conference on Big Data, BigData
reason: The paper proposes an MLOps framework for adaptive ML retraining guided by feature drift, directly addressing model monitoring and maintenance in production environments.
deployment_context: Generic AI Implementation (Big Data Analytics, real-time analytics, dynamic production environments)
operational_constraints: (1) Establishing a direct causal link between detected data drift and model decay is challenging due to the complexity of real-world data dynamics. (2) Standard drift detection methods like the Kolmogorov-Smirnov (KS) statistic may miss gradual or widespread changes in data and do not explain which data points cause failure. (3) Existing drift detection methods often lack explainability regarding how drift drives model performance decay or the extent of actual feature drift. (4) No structured methodology currently exists for systematically identifying drift, determining when retraining is necessary, and selecting optimal strategies for model updates in MLOps.
mlops_practices: (1) Feature Drift Detection: Uses a combination of the Kolmogorov-Smirnov (KS) statistic, a confidence-based model performance indicator (CMP), and quantitative measures of feature-level distributional shift (Percent Mean Shift (PMS) or Percent Variance Shift (PVS)) to detect feature drift. (2) Adaptive Retraining Decision-Making: A systematic framework determines when retraining is necessary by checking three conditions: if a feature has drifted based on KS statistic, if CMP has dropped below a predefined threshold (CMPT), and if PMS and/or PVS have reached their respective thresholds (PMST, PVST). (3) Model Performance Monitoring: Model confidence (CMP) is used as a baseline for performance monitoring, calculated as the highest probability within the probability distribution produced by the model across all possible classes. (4) Threshold Estimation for Drift Indicators: A simulation-based methodology is used to estimate thresholds for PMS and PVS for individual features, defined as the lowest value of PMS/PVS when CMP just drops below an expected level. (5) Retraining Strategy: ML models are retrained only if the retraining decision algorithm yields a positive signal across the three conditions and sufficient drifted data is acquired. Retraining uses both original training data and the drifted data.
gaps_identified: (1) Current drift detection methods (e.g., KS statistic) primarily focus on when drift occurs but offer limited insight into why it happens, which is critical for guiding retraining decisions. (2) Existing methods often lack explainability regarding how drift drives model performance decay or the extent of the actual feature drift. (3) There is a lack of structured methodology for systematically identifying drift, determining when retraining is necessary, and selecting optimal strategies for model updates in MLOps.
key_findings: The paper introduces an MLOps framework for adaptive ML retraining guided by feature drift, integrating the KS statistic with a confidence-based model performance indicator (CMP) and quantitative measures like Percent Mean Shift (PMS). Experimental results demonstrate that PMS shows a stronger correlation with CMP than the KS statistic in most scenarios, significantly enhancing model performance monitoring and explainability of performance decay. The proposed methodology for determining PMS thresholds optimizes retraining frequency, leading to more robust models that maintain performance even under severe drift conditions. The framework achieves comparable or superior performance to existing drift detectors while providing better explainability and decision-making optimization.
method: Experimental evaluation using multiple real-world datasets (Heart Diseases, Loan Approval, Diabetes, Electricity, Digits08, Digits17, Wine, CovType) and diverse ML algorithms (Random Forest Classifier, Support Vector Machine Classifier). The method involves inducing feature drift through mean and variance shifts, quantifying these shifts with PMS and PVS, monitoring model performance with CMP, and developing a simulation-based approach to estimate drift thresholds. Statistical analysis (correlation, R-squared, adjusted R-squared) is used to evaluate the relationships between drift indicators and model performance. Benchmarking against existing drift detectors (FBDD, DDM, EDDM, PCA-FDD, KS, P-H, MD3, MD3-RS) is also performed.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Model Monitoring, Model Retraining, Model Maintenance
future_research: Develop an automated retraining decisional algorithm based on the proposed framework to monitor feature drift in production environments.

title: Demonstration of Geyser: Provenance Extraction and Applications over Data Science Scripts
journal: Proceedings of the ACM SIGMOD International Conference on Management of Data
reason: The paper describes Geyser, a provenance extraction system for data science workloads, and demonstrates its application in industrial contexts like model compliance, linting, versioning, and poisoning detection. This directly addresses operational aspects of ML/AI models in production.
evidence_level: Demonstration of a system with industrial applications and quantitative performance metrics (precision/recall).
deployment_context: Enterprise-grade data science workloads, including auditing, model debuggability, reproducibility, and explainability. Specific applications include model compliance, linting, versioning, and poisoning detection.
operational_constraints: (1) Data science pipelines invoke a variety of external libraries with a plethora of operations, making it challenging to systematically encode the semantics of operations for provenance extraction. (2) Provenance applications have different extraction requirements in terms of fidelity (fine- vs. coarse-grained) and storage (volatile vs. non-volatile, optimized for read/write/both). (3) Static provenance extraction is often imprecise and limited to coarse-grained provenance due to no access to runtime information (e.g., dynamic control and data flows complicate correct extraction). (4) WIR (Workflow-based Intermediate Representation) verbosity for fine-grained provenance extraction is unnecessary and inconvenient, as logical operators can span multiple WIR nodes and edges, making direct extraction laborious and error-prone.
mlops_practices: (1) Provenance Extraction: Geyser extracts both static and dynamic provenance over data science scripts, driven by a knowledge base of data science APIs. (2) Model Compliance: Uses coarse-grained provenance to determine if a model relies on sensitive data (e.g., PII) by tracing model features back to input dataset columns and checking for PII classifications in a catalog like Microsoft Purview. (3) Model Linting: Employs dynamic provenance to deduce whether a target column is inadvertently used as a feature, addressing potential target leakage. (4) Model Versioning: Analyzes differences in both coarse- and fine-grained provenance across runs to explain why models are different or identical (e.g., input file changes, filter changes). (5) Poisoning Detection: Utilizes fine-grained provenance to trace feature matrix rows back to their source hospitals in the input CSV, propagate trust annotations, and then apply a poisoning detection algorithm. (6) Configurable Provenance Storage: Supports in-memory, Apache Arrow, database, and blob storage backends to meet diverse application requirements (e.g., immediate access for debugging vs. long-term storage for auditing). (7) Provenance Querying: Exposes point, backward, and forward queries on provenance graphs, and interfaces for defining and propagating annotations through these graphs.
gaps_identified: (1) Existing provenance extractors struggle to produce meaningful output without systematically encoding the semantics of operations from diverse external libraries. (2) Current systems lack the extensibility to support various provenance extraction methods (fidelity, storage) to meet diverse application needs. (3) Static provenance extraction is insufficient for precise and fine-grained provenance, as it cannot access runtime information (e.g., variable values, branches taken, command-line arguments) needed for accurate decisions and to infer correct and complete provenance graphs. (4) The verbosity of intermediate representations (like WIR) makes direct fine-grained provenance extraction for logical operators (e.g., selection, aggregation) laborious and error-prone, requiring an automated condensing step.
key_findings: Geyser is an extensible provenance system for data science workloads that supports both static and dynamic provenance extraction, driven by a knowledge base of data science APIs. It addresses limitations of static provenance by incorporating runtime information, enabling precise coarse-grained and fine-grained provenance capture. The system achieves >98% precision and recall on a collection of ~100K Kaggle and internal scripts, outperforming its predecessor Vamsa by up to ~80%. Geyser's capabilities are demonstrated across industrial applications including model compliance, linting, versioning, and poisoning detection, highlighting its role in enabling enterprise-grade data science.
method: System design and demonstration. The paper describes the architecture of Geyser, detailing its static and dynamic provenance extraction components, knowledge base (KB) driven approach, code synthesis for instrumentation, and provenance storage/querying capabilities. It then outlines a demonstration plan showcasing Geyser's functionality with a running example and four industrial applications.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Model Deployment, Model Monitoring, Model Governance, Model Maintenance (debugging, versioning, compliance, auditing).
future_research: The paper implicitly suggests future work in expanding the knowledge base to cover more logical operators and their expressions in WIR, and potentially integrating with more diverse MLOps platforms and tools.

title: Enhancing Collaboration and Agility in Data-Centric AI Projects
journal: Communications in Computer and Information Science
reason: The paper proposes a Git workflow and development methodology for data-centric AI projects, focusing on MLOps principles for collaboration, agility, and managing the ML lifecycle in production-like settings. It includes a case study with two real-world applications.
deployment_context: Generic AI Implementation (specifically Active Learning in medical image/ECG classification, but the methodology is presented as general for data-centric AI)
operational_constraints: (1) Data volatility: ML artifacts (trained models) often need to be recreated more frequently than in conventional software projects due to evolving data, requiring additional computational resources. (2) Computing intensity: Producing ML artifacts can be sluggish due to high computational demands, requiring additional resources. (3) Siloed work: MLOps is often hampered by siloed work, necessitating measures for better collaboration. (4) Development dataset creation: Creating a representative development dataset can be challenging due to heterogeneous data sources and may require maintaining multiple datasets of varying sizes. (5) Initial overhead: Setting up the necessary infrastructure and familiarizing team members with code and data version control systems represents a substantial initial investment and learning curve. (6) Configuration management: Feature branches likely require different configurations (e.g., fewer training epochs) than the best model, requiring additional management of these configurations.
mlops_practices: (1) Data versioning: Dedicated tools (e.g., DVC) are used to ensure efficient and traceable versioning of data, configurations, and ML models, facilitating reproducibility. (2) Development datasets: Creation of small, representative, reproducible, and version-safe development datasets for local development and rapid CI feedback, reducing computation time and resource usage. (3) Modular code structure: Splitting ML pipeline steps into separate, automatically executable modules to enable parallel development and efficient work distribution. (4) Automated CI/CD/CT runners: Branch-based Git workflow triggers runners for continuous integration (code quality, unit tests, integration tests with dev dataset), continuous delivery, and continuous training (retraining models on full datasets, periodically or by triggers). (5) Experiment tracking: Use of tools like MLflow to track and manage experimental endeavors, enabling structured comparisons of experiments and monitoring models in production. (6) Model monitoring: Strategies for early detection of concept drift through continuous evaluation and validation, allowing timely intervention and model updating. (7) Robust infrastructure: Support for end-to-end processes, including data collection, preparation, model development, validation, deployment (e.g., via Kubernetes), and monitoring, with considerations for scalability, performance, and security. (8) Feature flags: Used to manage different implementations and settings of ML pipeline stages, enabling systematic comparison when multiple developers work on the same stage. (9) Caching of artifacts: Data versioning tools cache ML pipeline artifacts (preprocessed data, trained models, evaluation reports) to restore them if an execution with identical code and data occurred, saving computation time.
gaps_identified: The paper implicitly highlights that standard software engineering practices and tools are insufficient for AI projects due to data volatility and the need for data versioning, continuous training, and specific monitoring for concept drift. It also points to the challenge of managing heterogeneous data sources for development datasets and the initial overhead of MLOps setup.
key_findings: The proposed development methodology, incorporating a Git workflow with specific branching namespaces and CI/CD/CT runners, significantly enhances agility and team collaboration in data-centric AI projects. Case studies in skin image analysis and ECG classification demonstrated that using development datasets and different runners led to substantial reductions in CI pipeline execution times (e.g., factor of 28-156 faster for failed jobs compared to experiments), enabling rapid iteration and considerable resource savings. While the approach improves traceability and transparency, it requires a significant initial investment in infrastructure setup and team training in data version control systems.
method: The paper proposes a development methodology and Git workflow, then evaluates it through a case study applying the concepts to two real-world Active Learning projects (skin image analysis and ECG classification). The evaluation includes quantitative analysis of CI pipeline execution times and qualitative discussion of benefits and challenges.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Data Management, Model Development, Model Training, Model Deployment, Model Monitoring, Model Retraining, Experimentation, Collaboration
future_research: Future work could focus on optimizing the methodology to reduce the setup and maintenance requirements for developers, particularly concerning the development and management of development datasets.

key_findings: This action design research study successfully designed and validated an end-to-end industrial AIOps architecture, Job Prophet (JP), for ML-driven memory allocation in a production HPC workflow. method: Action Design Research (ADR) methodology, aligned with the CRISP-DM (Cross Industry Standard Process for Data Mining) framework. The study involved iterative prototyping, deployment, and evaluation in a live industrial environment (NXP Semiconductor N.V.).

Further evaluations are needed to assess the system's performance under extreme computational loads. Optimizing the inference latency of some ML models could ensure seamless integration with job schedulers. Incorporating additional job parameters and tools into the model to improve prediction accuracy. Optimizing the application to handle larger datasets and more extensive HPC environments without performance degradation. title: MLPilot: An Intelligent CI/CD Flight Controller for Auto-Tuning Resource Allocation in MLOps Kubernetes Pipelines
journal: Proceedings - 2025 IEEE International Conference on Advances in Data-Driven Analytics and Intelligent Systems, ADACIS 2025
reason: The paper directly addresses the operational challenge of resource allocation for ML workloads in production Kubernetes CI/CD pipelines and proposes a novel, learning-based solution. It discusses deployment, monitoring, and optimization of ML models in an operational context.
deployment_context: Generic AI Implementation (Kubernetes-based MLOps CI/CD pipelines for NLP, computer vision, and tabular models)
operational_constraints: ["(1) Traditional Kubernetes resource schedulers (HPA, VPA) are designed for stateless microservices and do not account for the volatile, stage-specific requirements of ML pipelines, leading to inefficient resource utilization, increased costs, and unpredictable performance.", "(2) ML workloads exhibit distinct runtime characteristics (e.g., bursty GPU usage during training, unexpected memory spikes for feature engineering, low-jitter execution for inference latency) that static resource allocation fails to address, resulting in under-provisioning or over-provisioning.", "(3) Existing scheduling optimization efforts often rely on rule-based heuristics or historical averages, which fail to adapt in real-time to dynamic workload patterns.", "(4) Current CI/CD automation systems (Jenkins, GitLab CI/CD, Tekton, Argo Workflows) typically treat each pipeline stage as independent, black-box jobs, ignoring performance and resource variability across tasks.", "(5) Tools like Kubeflow Pipelines and MLflow offer pipeline-level abstraction but do not directly optimize underlying infrastructure allocation.", "(6) Inference-serving tools (KServe, Seldon) only support inference workloads, leaving training and preprocessing stages statically configured.", "(7) In highly unpredictable multi-tenant environments, policy learning may require additional safety constraints to prevent resource starvation.", "(8) The RL agent requires an initial bootstrapping phase, during which performance may be suboptimal.", "(9) MLPilot currently assumes a single-cluster setup; multi-cluster or hybrid cloud deployments may require more sophisticated scheduling logic."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) Telemetry-driven monitoring: Continuously collects runtime metrics via a Telemetry Collector module interfacing with Prometheus, Grafana Loki, and OpenTelemetry agents, capturing CPU/GPU utilization, memory usage, pod I/O rates, network latency, and ML-specific KPIs (batch training time, inference latency).", "(2) Dynamic resource allocation: An RL-Based Optimizer (Deep Q-Networks) dynamically adjusts compute, memory, and GPU configurations, replica counts, pod priority class, and re-schedules pods to different nodes based on real-time telemetry and learned policies.", "(3) CI/CD integration: A CI/CD Integration Layer interfaces with Jenkins, GitLab CI/CD, and ArgoCD to extract pipeline stage information, artifact versions, and trigger events, enabling context-aware policy generation.", "(4) Kubernetes-native control: A Kubernetes Controller (custom operator using CRDs) oversees pipeline stage deployment, manages pod lifecycles, and injects MLPilot-generated resource specifications into pod manifests, acting on RL optimizer commands.", "(5) Policy enforcement with safety constraints: A Policy Enforcer module applies optimized resource policies by patching live Kubernetes objects, while checking tenant- and namespace-level constraints (hard caps, minimum guaranteed quotas, SLO-aware preemption blocks) to prevent unsafe actions.", "(6) Multi-objective optimization: The reward function for the RL agent is designed to jointly minimize latency, reduce infrastructure cost, and enhance deployment stability, while penalizing safety violations.", "(7) Warm-starting and conservative exploration: MLPilot supports warm-starting from historical execution logs and performs conservative exploration using decaying epsilon values to accelerate convergence and avoid pipeline instability during the learning phase.", "(8) Generalization and scalability: The policy engine is transferable across different clusters and workloads due to its abstracted state-action design, using workload encoding and normalization layers to support different pipeline structures without retraining the RL agent from scratch."]
gaps_identified: ["Traditional Kubernetes resource schedulers (HPA, VPA) are not tailored for ML workloads' volatile, stage-specific requirements.", "Existing efforts to optimize scheduling often rely on rule-based heuristics or historical averages, which fail to adapt in real-time to dynamic ML workload patterns.", "CI/CD automation systems typically treat pipeline stages as independent, black-box jobs, ignoring performance and resource variability.", "Kubeflow Pipelines and MLflow do not directly optimize underlying infrastructure allocation.", "Inference-serving tools (KServe, Seldon) only support inference workloads, leaving training and preprocessing stages statically configured.", "Few reinforcement learning approaches in cluster scheduling integrate with CI/CD processes or account for multi-stage pipelinedynamics.", "Existing learning-based resource optimization systems (Google's Borg, Facebook's Horizon) are designed for internal use and not exposed as MLOps-native, general-purpose solutions, lacking tight integration with CI/CD tools and the Kubernetes ecosystem.", "TunePod, while using contextual bandits for resource settings, does not support multi-objective optimization nor integrate with the complete ML lifecycle in CI/CD pipelines."]
key_findings: MLPilot, an intelligent CI/CD flight controller for Kubernetes-based MLOps pipelines, leverages a telemetry-driven deep reinforcement learning engine to dynamically auto-tune resource allocation. Through extensive experiments on real-world ML workloads (NLP, computer vision, tabular models), MLPilot achieved up to 32% faster pipeline execution, 27% lower resource cost, and 20% higher GPU utilization compared to state-of-the-art baselines. This demonstrates the importance of intelligent, learning-based orchestration in future MLOps deployments for enhancing efficiency, responsiveness, and resilience.
method: The paper proposes MLPilot, a novel CI/CD-aware flight controller. Its core methodology is based on a deep reinforcement learning (DRL) engine, specifically Deep Q-Networks (DQN), to model the CI/CD pipeline tuning process as a Markov Decision Process (MDP). The system collects real-time telemetry (CPU/GPU utilization, memory, I/O, ML KPIs) to form state representations, defines an action space for resource adjustments (CPU, memory, GPU limits, replica counts, pod priority, rescheduling), and uses a multi-objective reward function (latency, cost, stability, safety violations). The DRL agent is pre-trained and fine-tuned online with an epsilon-greedy policy, and its actions are enforced via a Kubernetes custom operator with safety constraints. The approach was evaluated through extensive experiments on a 3-node Kubernetes cluster with various ML workloads, comparing against Manual Tuning, VPA, and AutoKube.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Deployment, Operations, Monitoring, Maintenance (specifically resource optimization during CI/CD pipeline execution)

title: Stream-based Active Learning for Speech Emotion Recognition via Hybrid Data Selection and Continuous Learning
journal: Lecture Notes in Computer Science
reason: The paper explicitly discusses stream-based active learning within an MLOps context for continuous adaptation of speech emotion recognition models in real-world scenarios, addressing challenges like continuous data generation and annotation.
deployment_context: Speech Emotion Recognition (SER) in real-life scenarios with continuous data generation (e.g., call centers), requiring continuous model adaptation to different domains.
operational_constraints: (1) Lack of large-labeled data for novel deep learning models, increasing economic costs for labeling and training. (2) Difficulty and high expense of data annotation, particularly for Speech Emotion Recognition (SER). (3) Challenge in retrieving representative emotional data. (4) Need for continuous adaptation of SER models to different domains due to continuous data generation. (5) Poorly studied strategies for managing a constant streaming of data to be labeled, posing a challenge to industrial scenarios based on MLOps principles and human-in-the-loop methods, affecting continuous integration and deployment processes.
mlops_practices: (1) Stream-based Active Learning (AL): A methodology leveraging MLOps principles and human-in-the-loop methods to continuously adapt previously trained deep learning models. (2) Hybrid Data Selection: Combines semi-supervised (e.g., entropy of prediction scores, uncertainty, min-max margin) and unsupervised (e.g., clustering techniques like K-means, Affinity Propagation, HDBSCAN) approaches to select both informative and representative samples for annotation. (3) Continuous Learning Strategies: Includes 'No stream-based' (pre-trained model for entropy computation remains static), 'Stream-Based' (pre-trained model is constantly retrained with selected samples), and 'Pocket stream-based' (model is updated only if performance improves over the previous model). (4) Iterative Model Training: Downstream classifier is trained iteratively using different AL strategies. (5) Carbon Footprint Estimation: Quantifying the environmental impact of training processes (kgCO2eq) as an operational metric.
gaps_identified: (1) Strategies to manage a constant streaming of data to be labeled have been poorly studied, posing a challenge to industrial scenarios based on MLOps principles and human-in-the-loop methods, affecting continuous integration and deployment processes of trained models. (2) Existing stream-based AL and MLOps system architectures have not been incorporated yet for speech processing applications like SER.
key_findings: The proposed hybrid stream-based Active Learning (AL) approach, particularly with a pocket stream-based strategy, achieved competitive results for Speech Emotion Recognition (SER) with just 20% of the original training data. Leveraging unsupervised methodologies like HDBSCAN significantly reduced training times by over threefold and diminished the carbon footprint by fourfold, with less than a 1% reduction in accuracy. The results suggest that adopting a stream-based AL approach is the most effective way to manage a model in alignment with MLOps principles for continuous learning in production environments.
method: The study proposes a novel stream-based Active Learning (AL) approach for Speech Emotion Recognition (SER). It combines hybrid data selection (using PCA, clustering with K-Means, Affinity Propagation, or HDBSCAN, and entropy-based uncertainty sampling) with continuous learning strategies (No stream-based, Stream-Based, and Pocket stream-based). Experiments were conducted across in-domain, out-domain, and multi-domain scenarios using IEMOCAP, MELD, and MSP-Podcast corpora, evaluating model performance (accuracy), training time, and carbon footprint.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Data Management (Active Learning for data selection/annotation), Model Training (Continuous Learning, Retraining), Model Deployment (adapting models in production environments), Model Monitoring (implicit in continuous adaptation and performance evaluation).
future_research: (1) Exploiting data selection strategies focusing on the individual influence of both unsupervised and semi-supervised approaches. (2) Exploring other stream-based methods, such as filtering non-relevant data, to enable more rapid improvement of a pocket model.
