title: RelAI: an automated approach to judge pointwise ML prediction reliability
journal: International Journal of Medical Informatics
reason: This paper introduces RelAI, a tool for assessing the pointwise reliability of ML predictions in clinical settings, directly addressing the operational challenges of deploying and monitoring AI models in production.
evidence_level: Case Study (Multiple Sclerosis patient outcomes), Synthetic Dataset Validation
deployment_context: Clinical settings, specifically for predicting Multiple Sclerosis disease worsening. Focus on high-stakes, safety-critical domains.
operational_constraints: (1) Logistical issues due to lack of cohesive data models in Electronic Health Records (EHRs) and poor interoperability, hindering ML training and integration into clinical practice. (2) Regulatory compliance with acts like the EU AI Act, which classifies AI-based medical devices as high-risk and mandates 'technical robustness and safety,' requiring mechanisms to identify potential AI misclassifications during deployment. (3) Lack of user and stakeholder trust in AI predictions due to poor generalization ability, algorithmic bias, dataset shift problems, and low interpretability. (4) Need for pointwise reliability assessment, as average performance metrics (e.g., accuracy) fail to provide actionable information about a model's correctness for a specific patient. (5) Requirement to assess reliability without access to the original training data during deployment due to privacy concerns or logistical constraints.
mlops_practices: (1) Pointwise reliability assessment: RelAI uses Autoencoders (AEs) to detect distributional shifts (Density principle) and a proxy model to encode local performance (Local Fit principle) for individual predictions. (2) Model-agnostic integration: RelAI is designed to work seamlessly with any pre-trained ML model and is provided as a Python package for easy integration into existing ML pipelines. (3) Automated threshold selection: An automated method is proposed to select the Mean Squared Error (MSE) threshold for the Density principle, balancing reliability and classifier performance using a validation set. (4) Continuous monitoring: RelAI can signal the need for re-training the model with updated data if a decrease in reliability over time indicates concept drift. (5) Federated learning support: The method allows sharing the reliability pipeline among cooperating institutions without disclosing the actual training set, supporting federated scenarios.
gaps_identified: (1) Current evaluation metrics for AI/ML fail to provide actionable information about the model’s correctness for a specific patient, only offering 'average' performance. (2) Existing uncertainty measures may not be trusted under dataset shift, as miscalibration increases. (3) Many reliability assessment approaches constrain AI developers to specific types of ML architecture (e.g., ensembles, models with reject options) that intrinsically allow for reliability assessment but may not be the best performing for a specific application. (4) Previous reliability assessment methods often require access to the original training data even during deployment, which is not always feasible due to privacy or logistical reasons. (5) The 'attribute-by-attribute' implementation of the Density principle in prior work fails to consider feature correlations, treating each feature independently. (6) Current work primarily deals with structured clinical data and does not investigate the whole spectrum of information relevant to predictive AI models, such as text, images, and signals. (7) The assumption that all out-of-distribution cases correspond to unreliable predictions can lead to overly conservative assessments, not accounting for situations where a model might generalize properly to out-of-bound data subspaces.
key_findings: RelAI is an automated, model-agnostic tool for pointwise ML prediction reliability assessment, utilizing Autoencoders for distributional shift detection and a proxy model for local performance encoding. It effectively identifies unreliable predictions, outperforming alternative uncertainty-based and instance selection methods, with a 17.4% higher difference in balanced accuracy between reliable and unreliable sets on a simulated dataset. In a Multiple Sclerosis case study, RelAI successfully identified incorrect predictions and provided insights into individual-level characteristics associated with misclassification, such as sex, residence, and eye symptoms, demonstrating its value in high-stakes clinical settings for regulatory compliance and fostering user trust.
method: The paper proposes RelAI, a novel model-agnostic method for pointwise reliability assessment based on two principles: Density and Local Fit. The Density principle is implemented using Autoencoders (AEs) to detect distributional shifts, with an automated method for MSE threshold selection. The Local Fit principle is implemented by training a proxy model to learn regions of 'low' and 'high' performance of the original ML classifier, using synthetic points generated from the training set. The method was validated on a synthetic 2D dataset and a real-world Multiple Sclerosis patient outcome dataset, comparing its performance against uncertainty-based and instance selection methods.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Deployment, Monitoring, Maintenance
future_research: Future work will focus on extending RelAI and further validating it across various clinical data types, including imaging and time-series data. Additionally, research will explore methods to address the limitation that RelAI currently assumes all out-of-distribution cases correspond to unreliable predictions, investigating scenarios where models might generalize properly to out-of-bound data subspaces.

title: Machine Learning in CNC Machining: Best Practices
journal: Machines

deployment_context: Industrial manufacturing (CNC machining for tool wear detection)
operational_constraints: ["(1) Data collection limitations: Manual data collection for CNC machines resulted in significantly less data than desired (data collected for 27 days over a year, missing 79 tool failures).", "(2) Data quality and 'messiness' in real-world industrial environments: CNC dataset reflects real-world manufacturing conditions with varying operator thresholds for tool changes and diverse part materials/dimensions, making it harder for models to achieve high results compared to controlled lab data.", "(3) Limited signal availability: The industrial CNC model could only use spindle motor current, whereas the lab milling dataset had six different signals, limiting information for the ML model.", "(4) Class imbalance: Both CNC and milling datasets are highly imbalanced, with far more 'healthy' samples than 'failed' ones, which can lead to problems in training machine learning models.", "(5) Data leakage: Unintentional introduction of target domain information into the training dataset, often subtle and indirect, making it hard to detect and eliminate, leading to overly optimistic and useless results in production.", "(6) Computational resource access: While HPCs are common in academia, individuals in industrial settings might not always have access to such resources for extensive parameter searches, though consumer GPUs can be leveraged."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) Data infrastructure focus: Emphasizing building a robust data foundation for reliable collection, transformation, and storage as a prerequisite for ML tasks, aligning with 'data-centric AI'.", "(2) Simple model preference: Starting with classical ML models (e.g., Random Forests, Naïve Bayes) due to ease of implementation, lower data requirements, strong performance, lower training costs, and quicker iteration times.", "(3) Data preprocessing: Applying scaling, normalization, and over-/under-sampling (e.g., SMOTE, ADASYN, SMOTE-TOMEK) only after splitting data into training and testing sets to prevent data leakage.", "(4) Feature engineering: Automated feature extraction using open-source libraries like tsfresh to generate a wide variety of time-series features (e.g., statistical, FFT coefficients, wavelet transforms).", "(5) Feature selection: Using methods like random selection and the 'FRESH' algorithm (tsfresh's inbuilt method based on scalable hypothesis tests) to identify and select the most beneficial features, performed only on the training dataset.", "(6) Cross-validation: Employing k-folds cross-validation (7-fold for milling, 10-fold for CNC) with stratification and grouping by 'case' to ensure independence between samples and minimize overfitting, especially for time-series data.", "(7) Hyperparameter optimization: Utilizing random search for determining optimal model parameters, recognized as more effective than deterministic grid search.", "(8) Open-source software adoption: Leveraging open-source libraries (Python, scikit-learn, XGBoost, NumPy, SciPy, Pandas, PyPHM, Matplotlib, tsfresh, imbalanced-learn) to improve productivity, save development time, and benefit from community contributions.", "(9) Leveraging computational power: Utilizing High-Performance Computers (HPCs) for extensive parameter searches and noting the increasing accessibility of consumer GPUs for parallelizing model training (e.g., XGBoost on GPUs, RAPIDS)."]
gaps_identified: ["Current ML systems often lack robust autonomous data collection mechanisms in manufacturing, leading to insufficient data for training production-ready models.", "Standard ML approaches struggle with the 'messiness' and heterogeneity of real-world industrial data, which includes varying operator behaviors and diverse operating conditions, unlike controlled lab environments.", "Many publicly available industrial datasets are small, making it difficult for researchers to produce generalizable results.", "The challenge of data leakage is often overlooked or difficult to detect in peer-reviewed literature and practice, leading to overly optimistic and ultimately useless model performance in production."]
key_findings: The study successfully developed an ML system for tool wear detection in CNC machining, achieving a true positive rate of 90.3% and a true negative rate of 98.3% on a real-world CNC dataset using a Random Forest model. It highlights five best practices for building ML systems in manufacturing: prioritizing data infrastructure, starting with simple models, being aware of data leakage, utilizing open-source software, and leveraging computational power. The paper emphasizes that the primary constraint to creating production-ready ML systems is often the lack of quality data, not the algorithm itself, and that real-world industrial data presents unique challenges compared to controlled laboratory datasets.
method: Empirical study involving the development and evaluation of machine learning models (eight classical ML algorithms) for tool wear detection on two datasets: a proprietary industrial CNC dataset and a public UC Berkeley milling dataset. The methodology includes detailed data preprocessing, feature engineering using tsfresh, feature selection, handling class imbalance with various over-/under-sampling techniques, hyperparameter optimization via random search, and evaluation using PR-AUC and F1-score.

(3) Automated CI processes: For projects at the prototype level, automated Continuous Integration (CI) pipelines are required to build and test the model. (4) Rudimentary monitoring features: MVP should contain rudimentary monitoring features. (5) Integrated monitoring: Monitoring is an integral part of the product at the production level to ensure ongoing reliability and spot potential problems like drifts or errors. (6) Decision records: AI audit requirements are translated into structured decision records (ID, status, decision group, context/issue, decision, arguments, method, related requirements, related artifacts, project maturity level) to guide development teams, ensure traceability of Trustworthy AI decisions, and support auditability.

key_findings: The paper presents a method for integrating AI audit catalog requirements into a company's MLOps processes, specifically within the healthcare sector. By translating 242 audit requirements into 82 consolidated decision records and assigning them to project maturity levels, the approach provides a guideline for project teams to prioritize Trustworthy AI decisions, minimize additional effort, and prepare products for audits. A case study involving two new AI development projects demonstrated the usability of this approach, showing how early consideration of Trustworthy AI aspects (e.g., AI autonomy and human control) influenced architectural decisions and risk mitigation strategies.
method: Case study: The authors analyzed an AI audit catalog and translated its requirements into tasks for a healthcare IT service provider's MLOps process. This involved consolidating 242 requirements into 82 tasks, assigning them to project maturity levels, and converting them into structured decision records. The approach was then evaluated by implementing the results in two recently started AI development projects at the partner company.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Design, Exploration, Development, Continuous Integration, Continuous Deployment, Operations (all stages are covered by the project maturity levels, with Trustworthy AI considerations integrated throughout).
future_research: Further research is needed on theoretical and practical challenges regarding the integration of trustworthiness into MLOps processes. key_findings: This paper presents StreamMLOps, an architecture for operationalizing online learning models for high-velocity streaming data in real-time industrial applications, particularly in banking. operational_constraints: ["(1) Patient variability: ML models need to adapt to individual patient EEG characteristics, which vary significantly and require patient-specific models.", "(2) Real-time prediction requirement: The system must provide predictions with minimal inference time (below one second) for early intervention in clinical settings.", "(3) Data quality and artifacts: EEG signals are complex, nonlinear, and often contaminated by artifacts (eye movements, muscle activity, power line interference) requiring robust preprocessing.", "(4) Imbalanced datasets: Preictal segments are rare compared to interictal segments, necessitating data augmentation and class balancing techniques.", "(5) Scalability and reliability: The system must handle intensive concurrent usage (multiple patient models, multiple requests per second) and remain fully operational without degradation.", "(6) Interpretability and trust: Clinicians need to interpret and trust AI outputs, requiring explainability features in the MLOps workflow.", "(7) Infrastructure compatibility: Challenges related to the compatibility of MLOps tools and environment scalability for developing automated pipelines.", "(8) Generalization to unseen patients: Models trained on specific patients need to perform well on new, unseen patients for broader clinical applicability.", "(9) Long-term robustness and scalability: Deployed systems require continuous monitoring, anomaly detection, and periodic updates to maintain performance over time."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) CRISP-ML(Q) methodology: Used to manage the full ML lifecycle, ensuring structured, quality-driven pipelines from business understanding to deployment and monitoring.", "(2) Automated preprocessing pipeline: ZenML orchestrates steps for data ingestion, noise removal (notch, Butterworth bandpass, FIR filters), artifact removal, feature extraction (MSE, RMSE, mean, variance, skewness, kurtosis, entropy, SNR, FDR, entropy difference), and channel selection.", "(3) Patient-specific model training: Models are tailored to individual patient EEG patterns, with automated selection of optimal configurations (high-cut frequency, segment length, overlap ratio, channel selection features) for each patient.", "(4) Hyperparameter optimization: Optuna framework is used for automated tuning of CNN-BiLSTM model parameters (kernel size, number of CNN layers, LSTM units, dropout, batch size, epochs) to enhance precision and reduce training time.", "(5) Stratified k-Fold Cross-Validation with hold-out: Training strategy to ensure balanced class distributions across folds, prevent overfitting, and evaluate generalization on unseen data.", "(6) Experiment tracking and logging: MLFlow tracks all experiments, runs, artifacts, configurations, hyperparameters, and performance metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, F1-score, FPR, FPH) for full traceability and reproducibility.", "(7) Model versioning and registration: Trained models are packaged with TensorFlow Extended, versioned, and registered in MLFlow, including architecture, weights, and metadata, to manage evolution and enable rollbacks.", "(8) Containerized deployment: Models are deployed as separate containerized REST API endpoints using Docker with NVIDIA support (cuDNN) and TensorFlow Serving, enabling parallel request handling and efficient inference.", "(9) Real-time monitoring and alerting: Prometheus collects inference metrics (health state, RPS, latency, error rates), and Grafana provides real-time dashboards for visualization and anomaly detection, triggering alerts for performance degradation or drift.", "(10) Data augmentation and class balancing: Overlapping time windows (e.g., 1/6 overlap for 30s segments) are used to increase preictal segment count and balance classes, effectively doubling dataset size.", "(11) Early stopping and model checkpointing: During training, early stopping prevents overfitting, and the best-performing model based on validation accuracy is saved.", "(12) Learning rate scheduling: ReduceLROnPlateau callback adjusts the learning rate when validation loss plateaus to refine model performance."]
gaps_identified: ["Most existing approaches for EEG-based epilepsy prediction lack adaptability to patient variability or remain confined to experimental contexts, relying on manual pipelines with limited automation.", "Existing research often does not support deployment and is seldom validated under real-world conditions, limiting scalability and clinical applicability.", "Many MLOps studies in healthcare lack complete coverage of the ML lifecycle, particularly in deployment monitoring, robustness, governance, and retraining.", "No existing research addresses EEG-based seizure prediction through a full MLOps pipeline, highlighting a gap in operationalizing AI for this specific medical application.", "Traditional EEG diagnostics rely on visual observation, which is error-prone and time-consuming, reducing the possibility of early intervention."]
key_findings: The proposed end-to-end MLOps framework for EEG-based epilepsy prediction, leveraging CRISP-ML(Q) and a CNN-BiLSTM-Attention architecture, achieved an average accuracy of 99.0% and sensitivity of 99.4% on the CHB-MIT EEG dataset. The automated pipeline, including patient-specific optimization of preprocessing and model parameters, improved performance by over 15% compared to static baselines. In production, the deployed system maintained full operational availability under intensive concurrent usage, with consistent inference latency below one second, demonstrating its robustness, scalability, and clinical readiness for real-time seizure prediction.
method: The study proposes an MLOps framework and evaluates its performance through empirical testing on the CHB-MIT EEG dataset. It involves systematic experimentation with different preprocessing configurations, model architectures, and deployment scenarios, with results tracked and analyzed using MLFlow, Prometheus, and Grafana. Performance metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, FPR, FPH) are reported for cross-validation, hold-out testing, and real-time inference.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Data Engineering, Model Engineering, Model Deployment, Model Monitoring
future_research: Future work should focus on incorporating explainable AI (XAI) techniques to bridge the gap between neurologist interpretations and deep learning decisions, providing patient-by-patient explainable information to increase clinical adoption and trust. Additionally, efforts should be directed towards reducing model complexity using lightweight architectures, pruning, quantization, and knowledge distillation to support deployment on low-power edge devices for faster response, better privacy, and continuous monitoring in remote or mobile healthcare.

title: MLOps in Practice: Applications, Challenges, and Insights Across Domains
journal: 2025 5th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Signal Processing, AISP 2025
reason: This paper is a systematic review of MLOps applications, challenges, and insights across various domains, including industrial ones like manufacturing, IoT, and freight rail. It directly addresses the deployment, operation, and maintenance of ML/AI models in production.
evidence_level: Review of existing applications and challenges
deployment_context: Diverse domains including Healthcare, Finance & Business, Industry & Manufacturing (predictive maintenance, smart factories, fault detection, cyber-physical systems), Telecommunications and Networks (IoT/IoT edge computing, 6G Networks), Public Sector and Society (smart city, electricity market forecasting), Transportation and Maritime (maritime domain, freight rail transport), Defense & Security, Data, Emerging Technologies and Big Data, Metaverse.
operational_constraints: ["(1) Secure model certification and communication mechanisms are required for edge deployments (Dias and Peltonen, 2023).", "(2) Limited functionality for edge devices: restricted to structured data, no native batch inference, and inference currently bound to Python (Dias and Peltonen, 2023).", "(3) Inefficient inference API container reloads imputation functions for each request instead of preloading them at startup on edge devices (Dias and Peltonen, 2023).", "(4) Data restricted to one company’s operations, reducing generalizability for freight rail prediction (Pineda-Jaramillo and Viti, 2023).", "(5) Workflow not yet extended to other railway tasks (e.g., real-time scheduling, maintenance, external location) or integrated with real-time sensor data (e.g., weather, track conditions) (Pineda-Jaramillo and Viti, 2023).", "(6) Overall accuracy of sentiment models remains modest, particularly for Russian, in smart city applications (Mussina et al., 2025).", "(7) Reliance on manual data labeling, which is resource-intensive, and lacks more advanced aspect-based sentiment analysis (Mussina et al., 2025).", "(8) Performance dependent on social media and online sources, making it sensitive to data quality and availability for smart city applications (Mussina et al., 2025).", "(9) Evaluation limited to specific datasets (e.g., CIDDS for intrusion detection), with little validation on other benchmarks, reducing generalizability (Babu et al., 2025).", "(10) Heavy reliance on GPU-optimized inference (TensorRT, TorchServe) may hinder deployment in resource-constrained environments (Babu et al., 2025).", "(11) Coupling issues and demanding coordination to maintain alignment across teams in microservices-based MLOps systems (Ferreira et al., 2025).", "(12) Communication with diverse technical stakeholders was challenging, given the need to explain detailed technical aspects to varying backgrounds (Ferreira et al., 2025).", "(13) The system has not yet fulfilled all functional and non-functional research-driven and state-of-the-practice advancements in maritime anomaly detection (Ferreira et al., 2025)."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) End-to-end ML lifecycle automation with codeless modeling, monitoring, deployment, and versioning (Pelekis et al., 2024).", "(2) Docker-based packaging and versioning for model deployment to edge devices (Dias and Peltonen, 2023).", "(3) On-device performance monitoring and retraining to adapt to changing data conditions for IoT edge deployments (Dias and Peltonen, 2023).", "(4) Automated data ingestion, model development, and deployment through CI/CD/CT integration for freight rail operations (Pineda-Jaramillo and Viti, 2023).", "(5) Four automated pipelines for data collection, feature engineering, experimentation, and deployment/maintenance for opinion mining in smart cities (Mussina et al., 2025).", "(6) Full MLOps pipeline for intrusion detection systems supporting real-time data streaming, multi-model serving, inference optimization, continuous monitoring, drift detection, and automatic retraining (Babu et al., 2025).", "(7) Microservices-based architecture with contract-based and domain-driven design (DDD) principles for anomaly detection, ensuring clearer specifications and communication across code, model, and data components (Ferreira et al., 2025)."]
gaps_identified: ["Standard MLOps solutions lack platform-agnostic deployment for time-series forecasting, with limited database support and interoperability frameworks (Pelekis et al., 2024).", "Current edge MLOps solutions have limited functionality, restricted to structured data, lacking native batch inference and being bound to specific programming languages like Python (Dias and Peltonen, 2023).", "MLOps implementations often rely on limited datasets and single-model approaches, restricting generalizability and comparative evaluation (Pineda-Jaramillo and Viti, 2023; Babu et al., 2025).", "Insufficient support for unstructured data and advanced aspect-based sentiment analysis in current MLOps frameworks for smart city applications (Mussina et al., 2025).", "Lack of domain-agnostic solutions and generalizable benchmarks for evaluating MLOps systems across diverse contexts (Babu et al., 2025).", "Organizational barriers such as team alignment, stakeholder communication, and coordination remain significant challenges for MLOps adoption (Ferreira et al., 2025).", "Concerns around security, data privacy, and regulatory compliance are insufficiently addressed in current MLOps practices.", "The need for more resilient, generalizable, and domain-agnostic MLOps solutions, including tailored tools and frameworks for specific needs like LLMOps."]
key_findings: The paper finds that MLOps has transitioned from theoretical discussions to concrete implementations across diverse domains, enhancing automation, scalability, and reproducibility through CI/CD/CT practices, containerized deployments, and monitoring frameworks. Key contributions include full-stack frameworks for time-series forecasting, edge infrastructures for IoT, and end-to-end pipelines for real-time delay prediction in freight rail. However, significant challenges persist, such as reliance on limited datasets, restricted support for unstructured data, modest model accuracy in specific contexts, and organizational barriers like team coordination and communication. The review emphasizes the necessity for more resilient, generalizable, and domain-agnostic MLOps solutions.
method: Systematic analysis of recent academic works (journal articles and conference papers up to 2025) that implement and evaluate MLOps pipelines in real-world application domains. Search conducted across IEEE Xplore, ACM Digital Library, ScienceDirect, SpringerLink, Web of Science (WoS), Google Scholar, and Scopus.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Development and experimentation, Continuous Integration, Continuous Delivery, Automated triggering (Continuous Training), Model deployment, Monitoring.
future_research: Development of tailored MLOps tools and frameworks to meet domain-specific needs (e.g., LLMOps for large language models). Addressing security, data privacy, and regulatory compliance more robustly. Ensuring all stages of the MLOps lifecycle function effectively in an integrated manner. Designing more effective systems leveraging a holistic perspective spanning data science, ML, AI, programming languages, database management, software tools, frameworks, software engineering practices, cloud-native infrastructures, scalable deployment environments, system architecture, governance, and project management.

title: MLOps-Enabled Security Strategies for Next-Generation Operational Technologies
journal: ACM International Conference Proceeding Series
reason: The paper directly addresses the application of MLOps methodologies in operational technologies (OT) with a specific focus on enhancing security measures. It discusses challenges unique to securing ML implementations in OT and proposes MLOps strategies to overcome them.
deployment_context: Industrial AI / Operational Technology (OT) environments, including manufacturing, energy, and transportation, where ML models control and monitor physical processes.
operational_constraints: (1) OT systems are often created and put into operation without strong security protocols, leading to enduring weaknesses. (2) Insufficient authentication methods and ineffective access controls can be manipulated to obtain unauthorized access to OT systems. (3) Integration of OT into IT networks broadens the scope of vulnerability, creating possible avenues for cyber threats. (4) OT systems often cannot be disconnected for updates and patching, leading to delayed security updates and leaving systems vulnerable to identified weaknesses. (5) Conventional OT environments might not have robust security monitoring and incident response capabilities, hindering prompt identification and resolution of security breaches. (6) ML models require a unique and robust infrastructure for training, evaluation, and implementation, which frequently expands in size and complexity, requiring scalable infrastructure. (7) Managing resources for ML models requires specific hardware and software to operate effectively. (8) ML projects commonly rely on a variety of external libraries, frameworks, and outsourced components, each bringing possible vulnerabilities.
mlops_practices: (1) Data encryption: Employing strong encryption algorithms like Advanced Encryption Standard (AES) with large key sizes (e.g., 256 bits) for data at rest. (2) Secure data transmission protocols: Adopting HTTPS (HTTPSecure) with SSL/TLS certificates, secure database connections using SSL/TLS, Virtual Private Networks (VPNs) for network-level encryption, and SSH for secure file exchange. (3) Access control and data anonymization: Establishing rigorous access control policies based on roles and duties, and incorporating data anonymization methods to protect privacy. (4) Model versioning and tracking: Implementing robust versioning mechanisms for ML models using tools like Git or dedicated ML versioning platforms to track changes, improvements, and historical performance. (5) Secure model storage and access: Storing models in protected databases with strong access restrictions to prevent unauthorized changes and ensuring models are sourced only from reliable sources. (6) CI/CD pipelines with integrated security: Utilizing CI/CD platforms (e.g., Jenkins, GitHub Actions) to automate testing and deployment, with security measures like Static Application Security Testing (SAST) integrated early in the development lifecycle. (7) DevSecOps collaboration: Fostering a cooperative environment among development, operations, and security teams to embed security throughout the ML deployment cycle. (8) Containerization for model isolation: Using technologies like Docker to isolate ML models and their dependencies from the underlying infrastructure, ensuring uniformity and replicability. (9) Orchestration platforms: Employing platforms like Kubernetes to simplify deployment by automatically managing scaling, load distribution, and resource allocation. (10) Infrastructure as Code (IaC): Embracing IaC methodologies to maintain reproducibility and uniformity in ML task implementation, allowing for easy duplication of complete environments. (11) Real-time monitoring and logging: Utilizing tools like DataDog and Elasticsearch to monitor ML model performance in real-time, identify irregularities, and react swiftly to security incidents. (12) Routine security audits: Conducting regular security audits to methodically assess ML models and deployment settings to detect and rectify vulnerabilities.
gaps_identified: (1) Conventional security protocols employed in IT are insufficient to address the unique security issues of OT systems. (2) Many OT systems are deployed without strong security protocols, leading to inherent vulnerabilities. (3) OT systems' inability to be disconnected for updates creates delays in security patching. (4) Lack of robust security monitoring and incident response capabilities in conventional OT environments. (5) The need for specialized hardware and software for ML models in OT, which may not be readily available or scalable within existing OT infrastructure. (6) Challenges in fostering effective collaboration among data science, IT operations, and business analysis teams in MLOps.
key_findings: The paper highlights the critical role of MLOps in enhancing security for next-generation Operational Technologies (OT), which face unique vulnerabilities due to their integration with AI/ML and control over physical processes. It identifies key challenges such as inherent security weaknesses in OT, difficulties in patching, and the need for specialized infrastructure. To address these, the research advocates for robust MLOps practices, including strong data encryption, secure model management, containerization, IaC, and continuous monitoring and auditing. These strategies are crucial for protecting sensitive information, maintaining model integrity, and ensuring the adaptability and security of ML deployments in OT systems.
method: Survey study to gain insight into current practices and identify areas for improvement, combined with a literature review to explore MLOps practices and security strategies.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Data Security, Model Security, Deployment (DevOps Practices), Infrastructure Management, Monitoring and Auditing
future_research: Future research should explore adaptive MLOps frameworks to counter evolving cyber threats, integrate emerging technologies for enhanced security, and foster collaboration for the development of standardized MLOps practices.

title: ML-Enabled Systems Model Deployment and Monitoring: Status Quo and Problems
journal: Lecture Notes in Business Information Processing
reason: The paper directly addresses the deployment and monitoring of ML models in production, identifying current practices and problems through an international survey of practitioners. It focuses on the operational aspects of ML-enabled systems.
deployment_context: Generic AI Implementation (software-centric ML deployment contexts across various industries)
operational_constraints: (1) Difficulty in designing the architecture of the infrastructure for production deployment, considering scalability and financial constraints. (2) Challenges in integrating ML models with legacy applications. (3) Lack of appropriate monitoring practices in place for many models in production. (4) Need to create custom monitoring tools due to the absence of suitable off-the-shelf solutions. (5) Difficulties in selecting appropriate metrics for model monitoring.
mlops_practices: (1) Model deployment as separate services (59.457% of respondents). (2) Embedding models within the consuming application (42.719% of respondents). (3) Using Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS) solutions for deployment (23.826% of respondents). (4) Limited adoption of MLOps principles, with only 29.089% of organizations following them. (5) Some organizations build their own automated retraining pipelines on continuous delivery tools (e.g., GitLab CI/CD, Azure DevOps) or use ML-specific platforms (e.g., BentoML, MLFlow, AWS Sagemaker MLOps). (6) Monitoring of model inputs and outputs (62.675% of respondents). (7) Monitoring of model decisions (62.082% of respondents). (8) Monitoring of interpretability output (28.034% of respondents). (9) Monitoring of fairness (12.965% of respondents).
gaps_identified: (1) A significant gap exists between ML model development in testing environments and successful deployment in real-world settings, particularly in integration, monitoring, and updating. (2) Traditional Software Engineering practices are often insufficient for ML-enabled systems, revealing new challenges. (3) Many models in production are not monitored at all. (4) There is a need for a holistic framework that addresses ML deployment and monitoring challenges, as current strategies and techniques are numerous but fragmented. (5) Lack of MLOps adoption, despite proven benefits, suggests a gap in understanding or ease of implementation for practitioners. (6) The need for developing custom monitoring tools indicates a lack of standardized, comprehensive, and readily available solutions for ML model monitoring. (7) Difficulty in choosing appropriate monitoring metrics highlights a gap in guidance or established best practices for ML-specific monitoring.
key_findings: The study, based on an international survey of 188 practitioners, found that ML model deployment and monitoring are perceived as relevant but challenging phases. Most models are deployed as separate services, with limited adoption of MLOps principles and automated retraining pipelines. Key problems include difficulties in infrastructure architecture design, legacy system integration, and the need for custom monitoring tools and metric selection. A significant finding is that less than 50% of deployed ML projects have their models actively monitored, highlighting a critical gap in operationalizing ML systems.
method: International questionnaire-based online survey with 188 complete responses from 25 countries. Data analysis involved bootstrapping with confidence intervals for quantitative questions and open/axial coding procedures from grounded theory for qualitative open-text responses.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Model Deployment, Model Monitoring
future_research: (1) Develop robust and scalable deployment frameworks that accommodate a wide range of ML models and applications, focusing on better specific infrastructure management and seamless integration to other services. (2) Advance methodologies for comprehensive and real-time monitoring, through incisive metrics discovery and ML-ready monitoring tools. (3) Research on how MLOps could influence the work of professionals, given its low adoption despite proven benefits. (4) Further exploration of monitorability to identify aspects, detect metrics, and create new tools to increase the quality attributes of ML models.

title: Scalable earthquake magnitude prediction using spatio-temporal data and model versioning
journal: Scientific Reports
reason: The paper describes an MLOps-driven pipeline for earthquake magnitude prediction, including automated data ingestion, preprocessing, model versioning, tracking, and deployment using MLflow. It explicitly discusses operational challenges like real-time data streaming and computational costs.
deployment_context: Natural calamity prediction (earthquake magnitude prediction) in a scientific/research setting, with a focus on real-world applicability.
operational_constraints: ["(1) Real-time data streaming: Ensuring seamless ingestion of seismic and environmental data with minimal latency requires robust data pipelines and scalable infrastructure.", "(2) Computational costs: Deploying machine learning models on resource-intensive cloud platforms or edge devices can be expensive, requiring efficient model retraining strategies and optimized hyper-parameter tuning.", "(3) System reliability under operational conditions: Model performance can degrade over time due to data drift or unexpected anomalies, necessitating automated monitoring, model retraining, and anomaly detection mechanisms."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) Automated data ingestion and preprocessing: The pipeline automatically ingests seismic records from the Indian government seismology department and weather data from VisualCrossing.com, followed by preprocessing steps like missing value imputation, outlier removal, noise reduction (using Empirical Mode Decomposition), and feature engineering (temporal attributes, logarithmic transformation, standardization, PCA, SMOTE).", "(2) Model training and evaluation: Multiple ML algorithms (Gradient Boosting, LightGBM, XGBoost, Random Forest, Linear Regression) are trained and evaluated on different dataset sizes (20%, 35%, 65%, 100%) using metrics like MAE, MSE, RMSE, and R2.", "(3) Model versioning and tracking (MLflow Tracking and Registry): MLflow is used to log model parameters, performance metrics, and dataset versions, enabling transparent model comparisons and maintaining past iterations for reproducibility. The MLflow Registry stores model versions and facilitates deployment of the best-performing model.", "(4) Automated retraining: The system is designed to trigger automated retraining when new seismic data becomes available, ensuring models remain up-to-date and adapt to the nonstationary nature of seismic activity.", "(5) Dynamic model selection: The MLOps pipeline includes a framework for dynamically selecting the most suitable algorithm for deployment based on real-time data characteristics and performance metrics, adapting to varying data availability and quality.", "(6) Automated hyper-parameter optimization: The framework employs grid search and cross-validation for automated hyper-parameter tuning to minimize prediction errors without manual intervention.", "(7) Scalable deployment: The pipeline supports distributed computing and can be deployed across various platforms, including cloud services and on-premises infrastructure, to accommodate diverse operational requirements."]

This highlights a gap for models that provide robust outputs from low and noisy inputs.", "Steidl et al. key_findings: This research introduces a novel MLOps-driven pipeline using MLflow for scalable earthquake magnitude prediction, integrating spatio-temporal seismic and weather data. It demonstrates that model performance varies significantly with dataset size, with Gradient Boosting performing optimally on smaller datasets and LightGBM excelling with larger datasets. The study's core contribution is a dynamic model selection framework within the MLOps pipeline, which allows for efficient detection and deployment of the most suitable algorithm based on real-time data properties, enhancing predictive accuracy and adaptability in natural calamity forecasting. Practical challenges such as real-time data streaming, computational costs, and system reliability are identified as critical for real-world deployment.
method: The study employs a comprehensive methodology including data collection from government seismology departments and weather services, magnitude scale transformation (ML to Mwg), extensive data preprocessing (missing value imputation, outlier removal, noise reduction using EMD, feature engineering, standardization, PCA, SMOTE), and training of multiple machine learning models (LightGBM, XGBoost, Gradient Boosting, Random Forest, Linear Regression). Model performance is evaluated using MAE, MSE, RMSE, and R2 across different dataset sizes (20%, 35%, 65%, 100%). An MLOps framework, implemented with MLflow, is used for experiment tracking, model versioning, automated retraining, and dynamic model selection. Feature importance is analyzed using Permutation Importance.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Data Ingestion, Data Preprocessing, Model Training, Model Evaluation, Model Versioning, Model Tracking, Model Deployment, Model Retraining, Monitoring (implied by reliability discussion)
future_research: Future work plans to integrate advanced MLOps tools such as automated feature engineering and hyper-parameter optimization frameworks to further enhance model performance. Additionally, incorporating real-time data pipelines into the MLOps framework will enable faster adaptation to new data, ensuring models remain accurate and relevant in rapidly evolving conditions. Optimizing real-time data streaming and computational efficiency is also highlighted.

title: Enabling AI-Driven Customer Experiences in Fashion E-Commerce through an End-to-End ML Software Development Framework
journal: ICSIE 2024 - 2024 13th International Conference on Software and Information Engineering
reason: The paper describes an end-to-end ML software development framework used at Zalando for deploying AI-driven customer experiences in fashion e-commerce. It details various stages from project setup to post-rollout learnings, including specific MLOps practices and challenges encountered in a large-scale production environment.
deployment_context: Fashion E-Commerce (Zalando), customer-facing AI-heavy solutions, large-scale (millions of customers, millions of articles, 25 countries)
operational_constraints: (1) Uncertainty around infrastructure needs for AI/ML solutions. (2) Difficulty in estimating potential business impact of AI/ML models. (3) Challenges in understanding unexpected model behavior influenced by data changes, as ML components are often treated as black boxes. (4) Difficulty in quantifying acceptable risk for a deployed ML product. (5) Silent failures (e.g., due to code bugs, data drifts, limited generalization) often go unnoticed until too late. (6) Lack of pipeline and system testing leads to system failures even if the model gives correct results. (7) Achieving a minimal detectable effect (MDE) during A/B tests can take significant time, leading to prolonged unequal customer experiences. (8) Transitioning from serving a small portion of users to a larger user base can present scaling challenges for infrastructure. (9) Data lag: it can take several days for a product to be returned, creating a delay between customer feedback and the availability of up-to-date article data for the algorithm.
mlops_practices: (1) Multi-stage framework: Project Setup, Training and Validation Experiments, Backtesting, Simulation Testing, Shadow Testing, A/B Testing, Experimental Rollout, Full-Scale Rollout, Post-Rollout Learnings. (2) Backward Walk planning: Imagining the target picture and systematically working backward to identify uncertainties, dependencies, and non-essential stages. (3) Forward Walk implementation: Iteratively reducing uncertainty through experimentation and well-defined stages. (4) Experiment tracking and monitoring software: Used to log experiment data, configurations, hyper-parameters, metrics, and results. (5) Formalized data pipelines: To transform raw data into the format ingested by ML models, ensuring reproducibility. (6) Code repositories and automated scripts: Moving from notebook environments to modular, reusable components for maintainability and reproducibility. (7) Business metric-driven evaluation: Shifting from algorithmic metrics to business metrics (e.g., acceptance, coverage, return rate) from the Backtesting stage onwards. (8) Load testing (Simulation Testing): Measuring readiness of ML solution in a production-like environment by monitoring serving infrastructure under artificially generated high-load user traffic. (9) Shadow Testing (dark launching): Validating end-to-end performance of new ML systems under real production conditions without affecting primary user experience, comparing new model to production counterpart on algorithmic, business, and engineering metrics. (10) A/B Testing: Randomized controlled experiments to accurately estimate the impact of the model on business metrics with live data and statistical significance. (11) Experimental Rollout: Exposing the model to a carefully selected subset of customers to prioritize scientific and product learning while reducing risk. (12) Gradual rollout: Gradually ramping up user traffic to the new model’s production endpoint while monitoring infrastructure and system health. (13) Continuous monitoring: Post-rollout monitoring and analysis of real-world performance, including checking for data and concept drift. (14) Periodic re-training: Necessary to ensure model predictions are not stale due to changes in data or user behavior.
gaps_identified: (1) MLOps currently suffers from complexity and lack of standardization, requiring significant effort and resources for implementation. (2) Existing MLOps frameworks often prioritize technical aspects (tooling, infrastructure) over actual business value, lacking strong connections to specific experimentations. (3) Limited literature on time-tested and flexible frameworks that can be adopted by practitioners without extensive technology teams. (4) Fragmented knowledge across academic and industrial communities regarding the end-to-end journey of ML models from problem definition to product.
key_findings: The paper proposes a flexible, multi-stage ML software development framework that enables multidisciplinary teams to take AI/ML models from initial experimentation to live customer-facing products at scale. It emphasizes reducing uncertainty through iterative experimentation and strategic stage skipping or re-iteration. Through two real-world case studies in fashion e-commerce (personalized size recommendation and article-based size advice), the authors demonstrate the framework's effectiveness in navigating challenges like infrastructure scaling, business impact measurement, and model performance validation, ultimately leading to successful deployment of AI-powered solutions serving millions of customers.
method: Experience-based framework development and validation through real-world case studies.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: End-to-end lifecycle, covering Project Setup, Data Preparation, Model Training, Model Validation, Model Deployment, Model Monitoring, and Model Maintenance.
future_research: The paper outlines potential improvements for the article-based size advice approach to be incorporated in future iterations. It also suggests continuously collecting data to monitor and analyze the time taken to detect article sizing issues for different articles and garment categories, to further improve the algorithm.

title: A Cross-Disciplinary Knowledge Management Framework for Artificial Intelligence in Business Projects
journal: Proceedings of the European Conference on Knowledge Management, ECKM
reason: The paper proposes a knowledge management framework for designing, monitoring, and optimizing the business value of intelligent services, explicitly discussing MLOps adaptation for continuous monitoring and CausalML for root-cause analysis in production.
deployment_context: Generic AI Implementation (marketing communication optimization in a financial company)
operational_constraints: (1) Difficulty in designing an appropriate business case and monitoring the real business value generated by intelligent services due to the complexity of machine learning methods and techniques. (2) Narrow specialization within data science teams, IT engineers, and business representatives makes it difficult to develop and implement 'horizontal' solutions that integrate competencies and needs. title: Data drift-resilient inference: Maintaining model integrity via novel lightweight distribution correction
journal: Future Generation Computer Systems
reason: The paper directly addresses maintaining ML model integrity in production environments under data drift, proposing and evaluating lightweight distribution correction methods as a preprocessing step during inference. It discusses operational constraints, MLOps practices, and identifies gaps in existing solutions.
deployment_context: Real-world production environments, sensor-based systems, medical imaging, finance, natural language processing, IoT, edge computing, mobile devices, real-time services, serverless/FaaS, cloud GPU/on-prem batch.
operational_constraints: ["(1) Requirement for label-free operation: Standard solutions like retraining or test-time adaptation (TTA) often require labeled data, which is costly or infeasible to obtain in production.", "(2) Restricted model access: TTA methods require access to the model's internal structure and permit gradient computation, which is not applicable in scenarios where model access is restricted (e.g., proprietary models, deployed as black boxes).", "(3) Low computational latency: Solutions must be computationally efficient for online inference in resource-constrained environments like IoT and edge computing, where complex methods are prohibitive.", "(4) Online inference capability: Methods must be suitable for continuous, real-time adaptation of models in production.", "(5) Model-agnosticism: The solution should not require modifications to the underlying ML model, allowing integration into existing pipelines with various classifier types (e.g., tree-based models common in tabular domains).", "(6) Privacy constraints: Source data cannot always be retained or shared due to privacy regulations, making methods that require source data access problematic.", "(7) High-dimensional data: Drift detection and correction in high-dimensional data can be computationally expensive (e.g., MMD's O(N^2) cost).", "(8) Mixed data types: Existing methods may struggle with datasets containing both numerical and categorical features.", "(9) Small reference batches: Non-parametric methods like CDF Matching may not represent the true distribution well with few samples, leading to instability."]
mlops_practices: ["(1) Inference-time preprocessing: Proposed methods (Mean-Std, Quantile Mapping, CDF Matching) are applied as preprocessing steps immediately upstream of the classifier during inference, without altering the model.", "(2) Fixed-reference evaluation protocol: Batch 1 is used as the reference domain (source domain) for training the classifier, and subsequent batches (2-10) are evaluated as target domains after correction, reflecting typical real-world deployment scenarios.", "(3) Standardization: Applied to both training and test data (fit on training, transform on both) as a preprocessing step to ensure scale-invariant evaluation.", "(4) Hyperparameter optimization: Optuna framework is used to automatically determine classifier and correction method hyperparameters by maximizing average 5-fold cross-validation F1 score across datasets.", "(5) Efficiency-Weighted F1 (EWF1) metric: A tunable metric (EWF1) is proposed to combine F1 performance with computational cost (runtime and memory) for hardware- and scenario-aware method selection, supporting deployment decisions for edge IoT, mobile, real-time services, serverless, and cloud GPU contexts.", "(6) Drift detection (implicit): The proposed correction methods mitigate accuracy degradation under covariate shift without explicit prior drift detection, aligning live data with the reference distribution.", "(7) Model versioning, A/B testing, rollback (mentioned as complex MLOps processes introduced by retraining approaches, which the proposed methods aim to avoid)."]
gaps_identified: ["(1) Lack of thorough comparison of lightweight distribution correction methods that work without model access or labels.", "(2) Absence of a standard way to measure both performance and efficiency for such methods.", "(3) Existing drift-adaptation solutions (retraining, TTA, classical batch-domain adaptation) are typically label-dependent, tightly model-coupled, or computationally prohibitive for real-world, online tabular inference.", "(4) Most existing methods assume immediate availability of labels, which is often not the case in production environments.", "(5) In unsupervised scenarios, predicting in advance whether a distributional change will actually affect performance remains an open problem.", "(6) Neural networks continue to underperform tree-based methods on tabular data, and in-context learning approaches need further validation.", "(7) Detecting data drift that cannot be shared due to privacy constraints without disclosing it to a central authority remains a significant research gap.", "(8) Existing studies in data/sensor drift correction have often been validated on a single data type, lacking comprehensive comparison across multiple datasets and classifiers.", "(9) None of the existing adaptation paradigms (Retraining, TTA, Classical Domain Adaptation) simultaneously satisfies all six operational constraints: model access, label availability, latency tolerance, online operation, model-agnosticism, and tabular applicability.", "(10) Univariate correction methods cannot handle changes in inter-feature correlation structures (multivariate drift)."]
key_findings: The study introduces three lightweight, model-agnostic distribution correction methods (Mean-Std, Quantile Mapping, CDF Matching) as inference-time preprocessing steps to address covariate shift. Mean-Std correction ranked first in F1 score (0.7357) while being 150 times faster (0.4 ms, 0.9 MB) than its closest competitor, KMM, demonstrating superior efficiency-performance trade-off. The proposed Efficiency-Weighted F1 (EWF1) metric confirmed Mean-Std's lead, highlighting its practicality for resource-constrained environments. All proposed methods consistently outperformed the 'Source Only' baseline across diverse datasets and classifiers, with CDF Matching and Quantile Mapping excelling in scenarios with prominent distributional shape changes. The findings suggest that simple statistical alignment can match or outperform more complex distribution correction methods with significantly less computational overhead, making them practical, plug-and-play solutions for production ML systems, especially for tabular sensor data where TTA methods often underperform.
method: The study proposes three lightweight, model-agnostic distribution correction methods: Mean-Standard Deviation (Mean-Std), Quantile Mapping, and Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) Matching. These methods are applied as preprocessing steps during inference to align new input data with a reference distribution without altering the model or requiring retraining. An extensive empirical evaluation was conducted across 4 benchmark datasets (Gas Sensor, Electricity, Weather, Digits), 25 correction methods (including baselines from distribution correction, output-adjustment TTA, and neural network-based TTA), and 4 classifiers (SVM, KNN, Random Forest, MLP). A total of 3213 experiments were run. Performance was measured using F1 score, accuracy, computational time, and peak memory usage. A novel Efficiency-Weighted F1 (EWF1) metric was introduced to combine F1 performance with computational cost for deployment-aware evaluation. Hyperparameters for all classifiers and correction methods were optimized using Optuna for reproducibility.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Deployment, Operations, Monitoring, Maintenance (specifically, drift adaptation during inference).
future_research: ["(1) Develop multivariate methods that include covariance alignment to handle changes in inter-feature relationships.", "(2) Create a meta-learning framework that can automatically detect drift types and choose the best correction method.", "(3) Investigate methods for updating the reference distribution for streaming data.", "(4) Test the generalization capacity of these methods to different data types beyond tabular data."]

title: Introducing MLOps to Facilitate the Development of Machine Learning Models in Agronomy: A Case Study
journal: IEEE Access
reason: The paper explicitly discusses the integration of MLOps practices (ClearML, Docker) into an existing ML project in agronomy, focusing on reproducibility, transparency, and design choices, even before deployment. It details how these tools facilitate experiment tracking, data versioning, pipeline automation, and preparation for production.
deployment_context: Agronomy research (pre-production ML model development for crop trait prediction)
operational_constraints: (1) Traditional ML research in agronomy lacks standardized protocols for reproducibility, leading to potential issues in experiment replication and informed model design. (2) Configuration differences between machines and sub-package dependencies lead to inconsistencies when replicating ML environments without containerization. (3) Manual linking of hyper-parameter performance data with specific hyper-parameters is cumbersome and limits insights. (4) Overloading git repositories with large dataset files is impractical for data versioning. (5) Complex ML workflows without orchestration make it difficult to maintain insights into individual execution pieces. (6) Mismatches between dataset versions across different tasks or environment inconsistencies can arise in complex ML pipelines if not carefully designed. (7) Memory usage can quickly grow if unnecessary model weights are saved, especially from HPO agents.
mlops_practices: (1) Containerization with Docker: Used to encapsulate the entire application and its dependencies, ensuring a consistent and reproducible execution environment across different machines for both Python and R scripts. (2) Dataset Versioning with ClearML: Dataset instances are created and versioned, allowing precise tracking of changes, preventing data corruption from parallel runs, and ensuring past executions are documented with the exact dataset version. (3) Experiment Tracking with ClearML Tasks: Each stage of the ML workflow (data preprocessing, model training, HPO, evaluation) is wrapped as a 'task' to log and track parameters, plots, output metrics, configuration details, and git commits for reproducibility. (4) Hyper-parameter Optimization (HPO) Automation with ClearML Agent: Automates the search for optimal hyper-parameters by cloning tasks with new parameters and logging results, enabling analysis of effectiveness and supporting advanced search strategies like Bayesian optimization. (5) Pipeline Automation with ClearML: Orchestrates complex workflows by chaining or executing tasks in parallel based on dependencies, automating end-to-end processes from data preprocessing to training and evaluation. (6) Training Diagnostics and Visualization: ClearML automatically logs and visualizes learning curves, parallel coordinate plots for HPO, and dynamically updated tables of executed tasks, providing real-time insights into model behavior and hyper-parameter impact. (7) Model Saving and Accessibility: Trained models are saved and linked to their execution, making them accessible for inference, further predictions, or fine-tuning. (8) Production Readiness (ClearML Serving): ClearML provides native support for model deployment via RESTful APIs, dynamic model updates, and prediction monitoring, facilitating integration into external systems for production use.
gaps_identified: (1) The current agronomic literature, despite increasing ML/DL adoption, shows scarce use of MLOps frameworks during the research stage. (2) Existing ML research in agronomy lacks standardized protocols for ML applications, leading to potential issues in experiment reproducibility and informed model design. (3) Traditional methods of specifying project dependencies (e.g., 'requirements.txt') do not account for configuration differences and sub-package inconsistencies between machines, hindering reproducibility. (4) Manual analysis of HPO results is inefficient and limits insights into the impact of different hyper-parameters. (5) The effectiveness of model robustness assessment is reduced when replicates assess performance on the same test data split, as it only captures variability from model initialization and HPO procedure, not different hold-out splits. (6) Evaluations on a unique holdout split may present a biased estimate of model performance compared to assessments on different data splits (e.g., KFold CV).
key_findings: This study demonstrates the transformative potential of MLOps frameworks, specifically ClearML and Docker, in enhancing the reproducibility, transparency, and design of ML projects in computational agronomy research. By integrating these tools into an existing deep learning study for crop trait prediction, the authors achieved an enhanced level of reproducibility through containerization and detailed experiment tracking. The modular and structured approach allowed for multi-level analysis of results, leading to more informed experiment design and higher quality research workflows. The findings underscore that MLOps practices can significantly benefit ML-based research even before the deployment stage, providing step-by-step guidelines for efficient integration.
method: Case study: Integration of ClearML and Docker into an existing deep learning study in foodomics (predicting agronomic traits from SNP markers). The original workflow was modified to incorporate MLOps practices, focusing on experiment tracking, dataset management, pipeline automation, result visualization, and production readiness. The MLP model for 'Plant Height 2014' trait was selected for demonstration.
mlops_lifecycle_stage: Experimentation & Development (Data Preparation, Model Training, Hyper-parameter Optimization, Model Evaluation, Workflow Orchestration), with readiness for Deployment.
future_research: The paper does not explicitly state future research directions, but implicitly suggests further exploration of MLOps adoption in agronomic research and industrial applications, potentially including more sophisticated HPO approaches like Bayesian search and alternative evaluation schemas like Monte Carlo CV or nested KFold CV for more robust model assessment.
